single.metaTitle: Single Bet Calculator Official Returns UK 2026
single.metaDescription: Free Single Bet Calculator for UK punters. Enter stake and odds (fractional, decimal, American) to see profit and return. Official tool, football and each-way ready 2026.
single.intro: Work out profit and total return on any UK single bet in seconds. Enter stake and odds in fractional, decimal, or American formats, toggle win/lose/void, and optionally set each-way terms. Optimised for football singles and horse racing, free to use, updated for 2026.
single.howItWorks: This tool lets you enter your stake, choose an odds format (fractional, decimal, or American), and select the outcome to instantly see profit and total return. It supports UK markets, football singles, and horse racing, including each-way options and non-runner outcomes. Calculations are exact; displayed decimals may be rounded to two decimal places.
single.articleHtml:
How do I use the Single Bet Calculator?

This tool lets you enter your stake, choose an odds format (fractional, decimal, or American), and select the outcome to instantly see profit and total return. It supports UK markets, football singles, and horse racing, including each-way options and non-runner outcomes. Calculations are exact; displayed decimals may be rounded to two decimal places.

What stake, odds, and result do I enter?

Enter the amount you plan to stake on the selection, choose your odds format, and pick the settlement result. For each-way bets, remember that your total outlay is doubled (win part + place part), and you should still enter the “per-part” stake (e.g., £10 each-way = £10 win + £10 place, total £20).

  • Stake: Enter your unit stake (e.g., 10 for £10). For each-way, the calculator treats this as “per part”.

Odds:

  • Fractional: Enter as A/B (e.g., 7/2, 10/11, 100/30).
  • Decimal: Enter as a number ≥ 1.01 (e.g., 4.00, 1.91, 5.50). Decimal includes your stake.
  • American: Use + for underdogs and − for favourites (e.g., +300, -110, +150).

Result:

  • Single: Win, Lose, or Void (non-runner/refund).
  • Each-way: Win, Placed, Lose, or Void. If “Win” is selected, both the win and place parts settle; if “Placed”, only the place part returns.
ExampleInputStakeProfitTotal Return
Fractional3/1£10.00£30.00£40.00
Decimal4.00£10.00£30.00£40.00
American+300£10.00£30.00£40.00
Void (NR)Any price£10.00£0.00£10.00

Can I switch between fractional, decimal, and American?

Yes. Switching formats preserves the underlying price and stake, and the calculator converts automatically using standard formulas. Returns are identical regardless of format.

Conversions:

  • Fractional (A/B) → Decimal = 1 + A/B
  • Decimal (D) → Profit = Stake × (D − 1); Return = Stake × D

American:

  • For +X: Profit = Stake × (X / 100); Return = Stake + Profit
  • For -Y: Profit = Stake × (100 / Y); Return = Stake + Profit

Implied probability (optional display):

  • Fractional A/B → B / (A + B)
  • Decimal D → 1 / D
  • American +X → 100 / (X + 100); American -Y → Y / (Y + 100)
FractionalDecimalAmericanImplied Probability
1/21.50-20066.67%
10/111.91-11052.38%
Evens (1/1)2.00+10050.00%
6/42.50+15040.00%
7/42.75+17536.36%
5/23.50+25028.57%
4/15.00+40020.00%

Does it handle each-way and non-runner outcomes?

Yes. Toggling “Each-Way” splits your bet into two equal parts: a win bet and a place bet. You can set standard UK place terms (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4) and the applicable number of places. Settlement is automatic:

  • Win: Both win and place parts return.
  • Placed (not first): Win part loses; place part returns at reduced “place odds”.
  • Lose (unplaced): Both parts lose.
  • Non-Runner/Void: Stake is returned on both parts.

Formulas:

  • Place fractional odds = (A/B) × (place fraction). Example: 8/1 at 1/5 → 8/5.
  • Place decimal odds = 1 + (D − 1) × (place fraction).
  • Profit (any format) = Return − Total Stake.
BetTermsOutcomeWin ReturnPlace ReturnTotal StakeTotal ReturnProfit
£10 each-way @ 8/11/5, 3 placesWin£90.00£26.00£20.00£116.00£96.00
£10 each-way @ 8/11/5, 3 placesPlaced£0.00£26.00£20.00£26.00£6.00
£10 each-way @ 8/11/5, 3 placesLose£0.00£0.00£20.00£0.00-£20.00
£10 each-way @ 8/11/5, 3 placesNon-Runner (Void)£0.00£0.00£20.00£20.00£0.00

Note: In UK racing, non-runners in singles are void and stakes are returned. If applicable, industry deductions (e.g., Rule 4) or dead-heats affect settled returns; use the calculator’s advanced options where provided.

How are returns and profits calculated?

The calculator follows standard UK settlement rules. For win-only singles, profit equals stake multiplied by the odds margin, and total return equals stake plus profit. For decimal odds, the stake is already included in the price; for fractional odds, the stake is added after calculating profit. Each-way singles are split into two equal parts (win and place) and settled separately. Where applicable, Rule 4 deductions and dead-heat rules are applied to winnings (not stake), and void selections return the stake.

Fractional vs decimal: how do we convert and compute?

Fractional odds A/B express net profit B-stake yields A profit units (profit = stake × A/B; return = stake + profit). Decimal odds D express total return per £1 (return = stake × D; profit = stake × (D − 1)). Conversion is direct: decimal = 1 + A/B; fractional = (D − 1) as a fraction. Returns are identical whichever format you use.

  • Fractional A/B → Decimal D = 1 + A/B.
  • Win-only fractional profit = stake × (A/B); return = stake + profit.
  • Win-only decimal return = stake × D; profit = return − stake = stake × (D − 1).
  • Rounding: UK operators typically display to two decimals for currency, applying rounding at the end of the calculation.
FractionalDecimalStakeProfitTotal Return
1/21.50£10.00£5.00£15.00
10/111.91£10.00£9.09£19.09
1/1 (Evens)2.00£10.00£10.00£20.00
6/42.50£10.00£15.00£25.00
100/304.33£10.00£33.33£43.33
4/15.00£10.00£40.00£50.00

Example: £10 at 6/4 (decimal 2.50) returns £25.00 and profits £15.00. The same selection at 2.50 decimal gives the same figures because D − 1 = 1.50 equals 6/4 as a decimal margin.

How are each-way singles calculated: place terms and reductions?

An each-way (EW) single splits your stake into two equal bets: a win part and a place part. The place part is settled at reduced “place terms” (a fraction of the win odds) and for a set number of places, which vary by race conditions and bookmaker rules. Your total outlay is double the per-part stake (e.g., £10 EW = £20 total).

Place odds:

  • Fractional: place fractional = (A/B) × place fraction (e.g., 8/1 at 1/5 → 8/5).
  • Decimal: place decimal = 1 + (D − 1) × place fraction.

Common UK place terms (can vary; check market rules):

  • 2–4 runners: win only (no place part offered).
  • 5–7 runners: 1/4 odds, places 1–2.
  • 8+ runners (non-handicaps): 1/5 odds, places 1–3.
  • Handicaps 12–15 runners: 1/4 odds, places 1–3.
  • Handicaps 16+ runners: 1/4 odds, places 1–4.

Settlement outcomes:

  • Win: both win and place parts pay.
  • Placed (not first): win part loses; place part pays.
  • Unplaced: both parts lose.
  • Void/Non-runner: stake returned on both parts.

Worked example: £10 each-way at 8/1 with 1/5 place terms. Total stake £20. If the selection wins: win return = £10 × (8/1) + £10 = £90. Place return = £10 × (8/5) + £10 = £26. Total return £116; profit £96. If the selection is placed (not first): total return £26; profit £6.

Note: Any industry deductions (e.g., Rule 4) are applied to the winnings portion of each part. Bookmakers may also advertise “extra places” promotions; when offered, they change only the place count, not the place fraction, unless stated.

What about Rule 4, dead heats, and void bets?

These adjustments follow well-established UK rules. The calculator applies them to the winnings part of your bet according to the parameters you enter.

Rule 4 (non-runner after betting formed):

  • A per-£1 deduction (e.g., 5p–90p) is applied to winnings based on the withdrawn horse’s price at withdrawal; stake is not reduced.
  • Adjusted decimal margin = (original decimal − 1) × (1 − deduction rate). Return = stake × (1 + adjusted margin).
  • Example: £10 at 4/1 (margin 4.00). With a 10p Rule 4, adjusted margin = 4.00 × 0.90 = 3.60 → return £46.00; profit £36.00.

Dead heats:

  • For an N-way tie, divide the stake by N; settle one portion as a winner at full odds, treat the remainder as losers.
  • Win example (2-way): £10 at 6/1 → settle £5 at 6/1 (return £35), £5 loses → total return £35; profit £25.
  • Place example (last paying place tied 2-way): place stake £10 at place odds 2/1 → settle £5 at 2/1 (return £15), £5 loses → place return £15.

Void bets and non-runners:

  • Singles that are void or non-runners return the stake in full.
  • Football markets may specify push conditions (e.g., Draw No Bet if drawn). The calculator’s “Void” result reflects this by returning the stake.

Always check the market rules published by your operator; settlement conventions can vary by event and promotion. For dispute resolution in Great Britain, the independent ADR provider will be named in the bookmaker’s terms, and regulatory oversight is by the UK Gambling Commission.

Single bet calculator for football: what should I know?

Most football markets in the UK settle on “90 minutes plus injury time” only, excluding extra time and penalties unless the market name clearly says otherwise (e.g., To Qualify, Lift the Trophy, Extra Time Result). The Single Bet Calculator lets you set the correct market context so your potential profit and return reflect how the bet will actually be graded by bookmakers.

Do football singles include extra time or penalties?

By default, no. Standard markets settle on the regulation result at the end of 90 minutes plus injury time. Extra time and penalties are only included in markets that explicitly reference them or in qualification-type markets. Use the examples below to align your calculator inputs with how the market is settled.

  • “90-minute” settlement includes: Full Time Result (1X2), Correct Score, Half-Time/Full-Time, Both Teams To Score, Over/Under goals (unless the market title states otherwise).
  • Includes extra time and/or penalties: To Qualify, Lift the Trophy, Extra Time Result, Extra Time Goals markets, Penalty Shootout Winner.
  • Tip: For cups and playoffs, always read the market title. If you backed “Team A to Win” (1X2) and it’s 1-1 after 90 minutes but Team A wins in extra time, your 1X2 bet loses; a “To Qualify” bet would win.

How are markets like BTTS, Over/Under, Draw No Bet settled?

Settlement follows clear, market-specific rules. The calculator mirrors these by letting you choose the market logic and enter the final score at 90 minutes (unless your market explicitly includes ET/pens).

Both Teams To Score (BTTS):

  • BTTS: Yes wins if both teams score at least once in 90 minutes (own goals count).
  • BTTS: No wins if at least one team fails to score in 90 minutes.
  • If a match is abandoned, settlement depends on bookmaker rules; commonly void if not completed on the day or within a specified window.

Over/Under Goals:

  • Settled on total goals at FT. For lines ending in .5 (e.g., 2.5), no push is possible.
  • For integer lines (e.g., 2.0), “push” may apply: exactly 2 goals → stake returned.
  • Example: Over 2.5 wins at FT scores of 2-1, 3-0, etc.; loses at 1-1, 2-0.

Draw No Bet (DNB):

  • Settled on the 90-minute result: if your team wins, bet wins; if draw, stake is void/refunded; if your team loses, bet loses.
  • Example at FT: Team A 1-1 Team B → DNB on either side is void.

Corner/Bookings markets (if supported by your bookmaker):

  • Typically 90-minute settlement. Player bookings and corners accrued in ET do not count unless stated.

How does cash out affect your potential return?

Cash Out converts a live bet into an immediate, fixed payout offered by the bookmaker. Once accepted, your original potential return no longer applies. Partial Cash Out splits your stake: one part is settled instantly at the offer price, and the remainder stays active at the original odds.

Full Cash Out:

  • Payout equals the Cash Out offer; finalised immediately regardless of the eventual match result.
  • Example: £10 at 2.50 (potential £25). If offered £14.50 to Cash Out and you accept, your return is £14.50; your bet is closed.

Partial Cash Out (proportional split):

  • If you cash out fraction f of your stake, immediate payout ≈ f × (full Cash Out offer). Remaining live stake ≈ (1 − f) × original stake at original odds.
  • Example: Original £10 at 2.50; offer £14.50. Partial Cash Out 50%: receive ~£7.25 now; £5 continues at 2.50 (potential remaining return £12.50).

Availability and rules:

  • Cash Out is not guaranteed; markets may be suspended during key events or priced off.
  • Free bet stakes: if your bet was placed with a free bet, the “stake not returned” rule usually applies; Cash Out calculations reflect this (check operator terms).
  • Settlement disputes follow the operator’s published rules and applicable regulations in Great Britain.

Single vs Double vs Patent: which calculator do I need?

Choose the calculator that matches your bet structure. A Single is one selection settled once. A Double is two selections multiplied together; both must win for a full payout (void legs reduce to a Single). A Patent covers three selections as seven bets (3 Singles, 3 Doubles, 1 Treble), returning something from just one winner. Using the correct calculator ensures accurate returns, especially when each-way terms, Rule 4 deductions, or voids apply.

Single vs double: how does the payout differ?

A Single settles on one selection only. A Double multiplies two selections; both must win for the combined return. If one leg of a Double is void (e.g., non-runner), the bet settles as a Single on the remaining leg at its odds. If either leg loses, the Double loses.

Single (win-only):

  • Decimal: Return = Stake × D; Profit = Stake × (D − 1).
  • Fractional A/B: Profit = Stake × (A/B); Return = Stake + Profit.

Double (win-only):

  • Decimal: Return = Stake × D1 × D2; Profit = Return − Stake.
  • If one leg is void: Return = Stake × Dw (the winning leg’s decimal); Profit = Return − Stake.

Each-way:

  • Single EW: win part + place part (at reduced place odds) settle separately.
  • Double EW: four parts (win-win, win-place, place-win, place-place). Use an Acca/EW-capable calculator for accuracy.
Bet TypeSelections (Decimal)Unit StakeTotal StakeOutcomeTotal ReturnProfit
SingleA @ 3.00 (2/1)£10.00£10.00A wins£30.00£20.00
DoubleA @ 3.00 × B @ 2.50 (6/4)£10.00£10.00Both win£75.00£65.00
PatentA @ 3.00, B @ 2.50, C @ 3.50 (5/2)£1.00 per bet£7.00Two winners (A,B)£13.00£6.00
PatentA @ 3.00, B @ 2.50, C @ 3.50£1.00 per bet£7.00All three win£62.00£55.00

Key takeaway: Singles provide linear risk/return on one outcome. Doubles compound odds for higher potential but require two winners. Patents trade higher total stake for broader coverage across three picks.

When should I use a Patent bet calculator instead?

Use a Patent calculator whenever you have three selections covered as 7 bets: 3 Singles, 3 Doubles, and 1 Treble (unit stake × 7 total). It’s ideal if you want a return even when only one selection wins, and scaled returns as more selections win. A standard Single calculator won’t apportion stakes across the seven components or apply each-way terms consistently across all legs.

Best for:

  • Three correlated or confidence-graded picks where at least one is expected to win.
  • Racing each-way strategies using place terms across multiple selections.

What a Patent calculator handles:

  • Breakdown by component bet (S×3, D×3, T×1) with exact returns.
  • Each-way Patents (14 bets): place fractions and place counts per event.
  • Adjustments: void legs (reducing affected components), Rule 4 on impacted races, dead-heat rules per component.

Alternatives (for reference):

  • Trixie (3 selections, 4 bets) excludes Singles; needs at least two winners.
  • Yankee (4 selections, 11 bets) and Canadian/Super Yankee (5 selections, 26 bets) follow similar principles at larger scales.

Is this comparable to the William Hill bet calculator?

Yes. Any accurate calculator using standard UK settlement logic should produce the same returns for identical inputs. That includes decimal/fractional conversions, each-way place fractions, Rule 4 deductions applied to winnings, dead-heat stake division, and void/push handling (e.g., Draw No Bet in football). Differences are typically interface and the range of supported bet types, not the maths.

Alignment points:

  • Returns: Single = Stake × Price (decimal), Double = Stake × D1 × D2, Patent sums across 7 components.
  • Each-way: Win and place parts settled independently using declared place fractions and places.
  • Adjustments: Rule 4 as a per-£1 deduction to winnings; dead-heat by dividing stake across ties; void legs reduce multiples accordingly.
  • Rounding: Currency rounded to two decimals at the end of the calculation, which matches common UK operator practice.

Check your inputs:

  • Odds format and each-way terms (1/5 or 1/4, places) must match the market.
  • For free bets, many operators settle as “stake not returned”; ensure your calculator has this toggle if applicable.
  • For football pushes (e.g., Draw No Bet draw), the selection is void and multiples reduce accordingly.

Trust, history, and risks

Accurate bet calculators are built on published settlement rules used by licensed UK bookmakers. They convert odds, apply each-way terms, and factor in adjustments such as Rule 4 and dead heats. Understanding where these tools came from—and their limitations—helps you use them confidently and avoid common mistakes, especially in football and horse racing markets.

How did bet calculators evolve from slips to apps?

Before digital tools, punters estimated returns manually or asked shop staff to price up multiples. As online betting grew, web-based calculators standardised the maths, added each-way logic, and automated Rule 4 and dead-heat adjustments. Modern apps now mirror operator settlement conventions and let you toggle football market contexts (90 minutes vs To Qualify), free-bet stakes, and advanced racing rules.

Paper era:

  • Punters used fractional odds tables and mental arithmetic; shop tills produced final figures only after a bet was struck.

Early web calculators:

  • Introduced consistent conversion between fractional, decimal, and American odds; automated Singles and basic multiples.

Modern calculators:

  • Support each-way structures, Rule 4 scales, dead-heat splits, void/push handling, and football market-specific settlement.
  • Quality-of-life features: stake-not-returned (free bets), rounding controls, and partial/early cash-out scenarios.

The other side of the coin: When can a calculator mislead?

Even the best calculator can yield the wrong answer if inputs don’t match the market’s settlement rules. The maths is precise; mismatched assumptions are the usual culprit. Use the checklist below to avoid pitfalls.

Market context mismatch:

  • Football 90 minutes vs To Qualify/Lift the Trophy. AET/penalties rarely count for 1X2 markets; entering the “winner after pens” as a 90-minute win will overstate returns.

Each-way terms entered incorrectly:

  • Wrong place fraction (1/5 vs 1/4) or wrong number of places (e.g., extra places promo not reflected) changes place returns materially.

Adjustments not applied:

  • Rule 4 after a withdrawal or a dead heat at the last paying place can cut winnings; forgetting to apply them inflates results.

Free bets and stake return:

  • Many free bets pay “stake not returned.” If you forget to toggle SNR, your total return will be overstated by the stake amount.

Rounding and displayed prices:

  • Operators round currency to two decimals at settlement. Minor pence differences can occur if you round mid-calculation.

Voids/pushes in multiples:

  • Void legs reduce an accumulator component (e.g., Double to Single). Treating a void as a winner or loser will distort totals.

Cash Out vs potential:

  • Once you Cash Out, your potential return is replaced by the offer value. Calculating “what might have been” using original odds is no longer relevant to your settled payout.

Where can I find responsible betting tools and guidance in the UK?

Use official resources for impartial help, safer-gambling tools, and regulatory guidance in Great Britain. These services provide self-exclusion, budgeting aids, treatment pathways, and clear explanations of consumer protections under licence conditions.

Regulation and consumer information:

  • The UK Gambling Commission sets and enforces licence conditions for operators in Great Britain.

Help and treatment:

  • National Gambling Helpline (operated by GamCare) is free and available 24/7.
  • BeGambleAware signposts to support and provides safer gambling advice.
  • NHS services offer clinical support pathways for gambling-related harms.

Online self-exclusion:

  • GAMSTOP lets you block online gambling accounts with participating GB-licensed operators for 6 months, 1 year, or 5 years.

If you believe a bet has been settled incorrectly, first contact your operator’s customer support using the details in your account. If unresolved, follow their complaints procedure; as a last resort, an independent ADR listed in the operator’s terms can review the case. Always keep records of your bet slip, market rules, and any communications.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are “official returns” in UK racing and when is a single bet settled?

A single is settled when the race result is declared official (“weighed in”), and the official starting price (SP) or your taken price is used to compute the return. If you took SP, your payout is stake × SP (decimal) or stake + stake × SP margin (fractional); settlement typically occurs within minutes of the weigh-in and uses industry-standard Rule 4 and dead-heat conventions where relevant. The SP is the industry benchmark for settlement; for example, a £10 SP single at 5/1 returns £60.00 (profit £50.00) once the result is official.

How do I calculate a free bet (stake not returned) single versus a cash bet?

For stake-not-returned (SNR) bets, only winnings are paid; the original stake is excluded from the return. In decimal odds D, a cash bet returns stake × D, while an SNR free bet returns stake × (D − 1). For example, £10 at 2.50 pays £25.00 with a cash stake but £15.00 with SNR; at 5.00 it pays £50.00 cash versus £40.00 SNR. In fractional A/B, profit is stake × A/B for both, but only a cash bet adds the stake back to make the total return.

Are gambling winnings taxable for UK punters in 2026?

Individual punters in Great Britain pay 0% tax on gambling winnings; returns are paid gross and are not reportable as income. Operators, not customers, pay duties such as Remote Gaming Duty (historically 21% for remote casino games) or General Betting Duty (historically 15% on fixed-odds bets), but these taxes are not deducted from your bet settlement. A £10 single at 3/1 still settles at £50.00 return (profit £40.00) to the customer.

Which Rule 4 band applies and how do I adjust my payout?

Rule 4 deducts a fixed pence-in-the-pound from winnings based on the withdrawn runner’s price; you multiply your winnings by (1 − deduction). Common bands are 90p for 1/9 or shorter, 85p for 2/11–2/17, 80p for 1/4–1/5, 75p for 3/10–2/7, 70p for 2/5–1/3, 65p for 8/15–4/9, 60p for 8/13–4/7, 55p for 4/5–4/6, 50p for 20/21–5/6, 45p for Evens–6/5, 40p for 5/4–6/4, 35p for 13/8–7/4, 30p for 15/8–9/4, 25p for 5/2–3/1, 20p for 10/3–4/1, 15p for 9/2–11/2, 10p for 6/1–9/1, 5p for 10/1–14/1, and no deduction at 16/1 or bigger. For example, £10 at 4/1 has £40.00 winnings; a 20p Rule 4 pays £40.00 × 0.80 = £32.00 winnings, so total return is £42.00.

How do dead heats affect a single each-way bet payout?

In a dead heat, divide the relevant stake by the number of tied runners, settle one portion as a winner at full odds, and treat the remainder as losers. If £10 each-way at 10/1 (1/5, 3 places) ties three-way for second, the place stake £10 is split into £3.33 winners and £6.67 losers; place odds are 10/1 × 1/5 = 2/1, so place return is £3.33 × 2 + £3.33 ≈ £10.00, while the win part loses unless your selection is first. The total each-way return in this scenario is approximately £10.00 on a £20.00 outlay (loss ≈ £10.00).

What’s the difference between Ante-Post and NRNB for single bets if my horse is a non-runner?

Ante-post singles generally forfeit the stake if your selection does not run, while Non-Runner No Bet (NRNB) singles refund the stake when the selection is a non-runner. On a £20 ante-post single at 8/1, a non-runner returns £0.00; the same £20 at 8/1 under NRNB returns £20.00. For winners and placers, both markets settle at the taken odds and applicable terms; only the non-runner treatment differs.

Can I model Asian handicap or quarter-goal lines (-0.25, +0.75) on a single?

Yes, split the stake across the two adjacent half lines and apply push or win/lose rules to each portion. For -0.25, treat it as half on 0 (push if draw) and half on -0.5 (loses if draw); for example, £20 at 1.90 on -0.25 returning a draw yields £10 pushed and £10 lost, so total return £10.00. For +0.75, split into +0.5 and +1.0; a one-goal defeat returns half the stake on +1.0 and loses the +0.5 half, while a draw wins both halves at the quoted price.

How do UK bookmakers round payouts on single bets?

Most GB operators round currency at settlement to the nearest penny, with 0.5p rounding up, after completing all calculations and deductions. This end-rounding can create 1p differences versus mid-calculation rounding; for example, a computed return of £19.995 rounds to £20.00, while rounding earlier steps might show £19.99. Consistent end-rounding avoids cumulative rounding drift across adjustments such as Rule 4 or dead heats.

What happens to single bets if a football match is postponed or abandoned?

If a match is not completed, many GB operators void singles unless the event is finished within a defined window; common policies require completion the same day or within 24–72 hours, but exact cut-offs vary by bookmaker. Practically, a £25 Over 2.5 Goals single is usually voided and £25 returned if the match stops at 1-0 after 30 minutes and is not resumed within the operator’s time window; if later completed within policy, settlement uses the final 90-minute result.

How does Best Odds Guaranteed (BOG) change a single’s return and how should I input it?

BOG replaces your taken early price with the higher of early price or SP, so returns increase only if SP is bigger. Enter the final settled odds that apply under BOG; for example, £10 at 8/1 with SP 10/1 pays £110.00 under BOG instead of £90.00, adding £20.00 profit. If SP shortens to 6/1, you still receive the taken 8/1 and would input 8/1.

What is the break-even win rate for my single at given odds?

Break-even equals the implied probability of your odds: for decimal D it is 1/D, for fractional A/B it is B/(A + B), and for American +X it is 100/(X + 100) while for −Y it is Y/(Y + 100). Concretely, 2.00 needs 50.00%, 1.91 needs about 52.36%, 2.50 needs 40.00%, and 4.00 needs 25.00% to break even over time; winning more frequently than this threshold yields positive expected value.

How do I account for betting exchange commission on a single bet return?

Apply commission to net winnings, not to the stake, then add the stake back to get total return. With 5% commission at decimal 3.00, £10 wins £20.00 gross, commission is £1.00, net winnings are £19.00, and total return is £29.00; this is equivalent to an effective price of 2.90. At shorter prices, the effect is smaller in absolute pounds but identical in percentage terms, reducing profit by the commission rate.

double.metaTitle: Double Bet Calculator Work Out Returns Live 2026

double.intro: Free Double Bet Calculator for UK bettors. Enter stake and two prices to see total return, profit, and Each Way outcomes in seconds. Supports football, horse racing, NBA, fractional/decimal/American odds, and quick-check. No sign-up.
double.howItWorks: A Double combines two selections into one bet. The return from Selection 1 (including the original stake) rolls on to Selection 2. If either leg loses, the overall return is £0. Using decimal odds, the total return is Stake × (Decimal 1) × (Decimal 2). Using fractional odds A/B, first convert to decimal as 1 + A/B and then multiply through. Voids/pushes act as odds of 1.00 for that leg, effectively turning the Double into a Single on the other leg.
double.articleHtml:
How do you calculate a Double bet return?

A Double combines two selections into one bet. The return from Selection 1 (including the original stake) rolls on to Selection 2. If either leg loses, the overall return is £0. Using decimal odds, the total return is Stake × (Decimal 1) × (Decimal 2). Using fractional odds A/B, first convert to decimal as 1 + A/B and then multiply through. Voids/pushes act as odds of 1.00 for that leg, effectively turning the Double into a Single on the other leg.

Stake, odds and settlement: what inputs does the calculator need?

To price a Double accurately, the calculator needs the monetary stake, odds for both legs, and the settlement status of each selection. Optional fields account for special settlement cases (e.g., dead-heats). Here is the minimum and recommended input set:

  • Stake: total amount staked on the Double (e.g., £10).
  • Odds format: choose fractional (e.g., 5/4), decimal (e.g., 2.25), or American (e.g., +125).
  • Selection 1 odds and status: price plus settlement outcome (Won, Lost, Void/Push, Dead Heat with number of ties).
  • Selection 2 odds and status: price plus settlement outcome (Won, Lost, Void/Push, Dead Heat with number of ties).

Settlement conventions: apply standard UK fixed-odds rules:

  • Won: multiply by full odds (converted to decimal, includes stake).
  • Lost: overall return becomes £0.
  • Void/Push/Non-runner: treat as decimal 1.00 for that leg.
  • Dead Heat (n-way tie on the win market): the effective factor for that leg is decimal odds divided by n.
  • Convert Selection 1 odds to decimal (if needed).

Apply settlement for Selection 1:

  • Won: Stage 1 return = Stake × Decimal 1.
  • Void/Push: Stage 1 return = Stake × 1.00.
  • Dead Heat (n-way): Stage 1 return = Stake × (Decimal 1 ÷ n).
  • Lost: Final return = £0 (stop).
  • Convert Selection 2 odds to decimal (if needed).

Apply settlement for Selection 2 to Stage 1 return:

  • Won: Final return = Stage 1 return × Decimal 2.
  • Void/Push: Final return = Stage 1 return × 1.00.
  • Dead Heat (n-way): Final return = Stage 1 return × (Decimal 2 ÷ n).
  • Lost: Final return = £0.
  • Profit = Final return − Stake.
Leg statusDecimal factor appliedEffect on the DoubleExample (Stake £10, Decimal 2.50)
WonDecimal priceStake and winnings roll to next legStage return = £10 × 2.50 = £25.00
Lost0.00Final return is £0£0
Void/Push/Non-runner1.00Acts like a Single on the other legStage return = £10 × 1.00 = £10.00
Dead Heat (2-way, win market)Decimal ÷ 2Stake effectively halved for that legStage return = £10 × (2.50 ÷ 2) = £12.50

Fractional, decimal and American: how does format affect returns?

Returns are identical once odds are converted to decimal. Conversions: Fractional A/B → Decimal = 1 + A/B. American +M → Decimal = 1 + (M/100). American −M → Decimal = 1 + (100/M). The Double return is Stake × Decimal 1 × Decimal 2 regardless of the original formats.

FractionalDecimalAmericanNotes
1/51.20-500Low return favourite
1/21.50-200Common short price
10/111.909...-110Near pick’em
1/1 (Evens)2.00+100Doubles return stake
5/42.25+125Odds-against
7/24.50+350Bigger price
9/110.00+900Longshot
  • Fractional example: £10 on 5/4 and 7/2 → Decimal 2.25 × 4.50 → Return = £10 × 2.25 × 4.50 = £101.25; Profit = £91.25.
  • American example: £10 on +120 and −150 → Decimal 2.20 and 1.6667 → Return ≈ £10 × 2.20 × 1.6667 = £36.67; Profit ≈ £26.67.

Quick check and app: how fast can you price two picks?

Computationally, a Double is an instant O(1) calculation once odds are parsed. A well-built web tool or app can compute locally on-device without network calls: convert formats (if needed), apply settlement factors, multiply, and display return and profit immediately.

Quick-check flow:

  • Enter stake.
  • Input odds for Selection 1 and 2 (any format).
  • Pick settlement for each leg (Won, Lost, Void/Push, Dead Heat n-way).
  • Calculator converts to decimal, applies factors, and outputs return and profit.

Speed enablers:

  • Local arithmetic (no external API dependency).
  • Immediate format conversion and validation.
  • Optional installable PWA for offline use on mobile and desktop.

Accuracy guardrails:

  • Auto-detect and normalise odds (e.g., “evens”, “1/1”, “2.00”).
  • Explicit handling of void/push and dead-heats.
  • Clear profit vs return breakdown.
StakeSelection 1Selection 2Combined decimalTotal returnProfit
£204/5 (1.80) Won11/10 (2.10) Won1.80 × 2.10 = 3.78£20 × 3.78 = £75.60£55.60
£152.25 (+125) WonVoid/Push2.25 × 1.00 = 2.25£15 × 2.25 = £33.75£18.75
£103.50 (7/2) Dead Heat (2-way)1.50 (1/2) Won(3.50 ÷ 2) × 1.50 = 2.625£10 × 2.625 = £26.25£16.25

Which sports and markets can you use a Double on?

You can place a Double on most fixed-odds sports markets offered by UK bookmakers, including football, horse racing, and basketball (NBA). A Double multiplies the decimal prices of both legs. If either leg loses, the overall return is £0; if a leg is void/push, it settles at 1.00 for that leg. Markets with push potential (e.g., Asian handicaps, spreads, whole-number totals) can reduce variance in Doubles because a push keeps the bet alive on the other leg.

Football Double: which markets work best?

For football, aim for markets with clearly defined settlement and reasonable prices so the Double’s combined price remains realistic. Use independent matches to avoid related-contingency restrictions unless using a book’s same-game builder that prices correlation explicitly.

  • Match Result (1X2): simple win/draw/win market; no push condition. Doubles here can be high variance due to the draw outcome.
  • Draw No Bet (DNB): reduces variance; a draw pushes (factor 1.00) and rolls the stake to the other leg only. Price is shorter than 1X2.
  • Double Chance (e.g., Home/Draw): highest hit rate but much shorter odds; useful as a stabiliser leg.
  • Both Teams To Score (BTTS): binary outcome with no push; combines well with result-based markets from different matches.

Over/Under Goals:

  • Whole-number lines (e.g., Over 2.0) can push if the exact total lands, acting as 1.00.
  • Half-lines (e.g., Over 2.5) avoid push but increase variance.
  • Asian goal lines (e.g., Over 2.25) can split stakes and partially push.
  • Asian Handicap: push possible on whole numbers (e.g., -1.0); quarter lines split stakes and can create partial wins/pushes.
  • Example (independent matches): £10 Double on 1X2 @ 2.10 and BTTS @ 1.80 → Return = £10 × 2.10 × 1.80 = £37.80; Profit = £27.80.
  • Example with push safety: £10 Double on DNB @ 1.75 and Over 2.0 @ 1.95; if Over 2.0 pushes, Return = £10 × 1.75 × 1.00 = £17.50; Profit = £7.50.

Horse racing Double and Each Way: how are place terms applied?

An Each Way (E/W) Double is two bets: a Win Double and a Place Double of equal stakes. The win part requires both horses to win. The place part multiplies using “place odds” derived from race place terms (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4 the win odds) and pays if each horse places (a win also counts as a place). A non-runner is a void leg (factor 1.00). Dead-heats reduce the effective odds by the number of tied horses at the relevant market (win or place).

Race typeRunnersPlaces paidPlace fraction
All (flat & jumps)2–4Win Only—
All (flat & jumps)5–71st–2nd1/4
Non-handicap8+1st–3rd1/5
Handicap8–111st–3rd1/5
Handicap12–151st–3rd1/4
Handicap16+1st–4th1/4

Place odds are computed from fractional win odds A/B using the fraction f (e.g., 1/5): place fractional odds = (A × f) / B; place decimal = 1 + (A/B × f). Example: 10/1 at 1/5 → place fractional 2/1 (decimal 3.00). In an E/W Double, returns are calculated separately and then added.

Example (Stake £5 E/W; total £10): Horse 1 at 10/1 (f=1/5), Horse 2 at 3/1 (f=1/5).

  • Both WIN: Win Double = £5 × 11.00 × 4.00 = £220.00; Place Double = £5 × 3.00 × 1.60 = £24.00; Total Return = £244.00; Profit = £234.00.
  • H1 PLACES only, H2 WINS: Win Double = £0; Place Double = £5 × 3.00 × 1.60 = £24.00; Profit = £24.00 − £10.00 = £14.00.
  • Any LOSER: that leg settles at 0.00 for the relevant part (win or place); a non-runner is 1.00 for that leg.

NBA Double: moneyline, spread or totals?

For NBA Doubles, choose markets based on your tolerance for push risk and price. UK books usually settle NBA moneyline “including overtime,” whereas spreads and totals can push on whole numbers. Player props may void if the player does not take the court (bookmaker rules vary), which would act as 1.00 for that leg in a Double.

  • Moneyline (incl. OT): no push; higher prices on underdogs, shorter on favourites. Good anchor leg when confident in the matchup.
  • Spread (handicap): pushes on whole numbers (e.g., -3.0) settle 1.00; half-point lines (e.g., -2.5) avoid pushes but change the price.
  • Totals (game or team): pushes on whole numbers; quarter/half markets behave the same way.
  • Player props: check house rules; commonly void if the player records no minutes, acting as 1.00 in the Double.
  • Example (independent games): £20 Double on Moneyline @ 1.62 and Over 224.5 @ 1.91 → Return = £20 × 1.62 × 1.91 = £61.88; Profit = £41.88.
  • Spread push scenario: £20 Double on -3.0 @ 1.91 (push) and ML @ 1.80 (win) → Return = £20 × 1.00 × 1.80 = £36.00; Profit = £16.00.

How many Doubles are in 3 or 4 selections, and how are they settled?

With n independent selections, the number of distinct Doubles is the combination C(n, 2) = n × (n − 1) ÷ 2. Each Double is a separate bet that pairs two selections; settlement is independent across pairs. Returns from winning pairs are added together. A losing leg settles that Double at £0; a void/push/non‑runner leg settles at factor 1.00 for that Double, effectively turning it into a Single on the other leg at its original odds.

Doubles with 3 selections: how are the 3 pairings settled?

With three selections A, B, C, you have C(3, 2) = 3 separate Doubles: AB, AC, BC. You can stake a unit amount per Double (e.g., £2 per Double; total stake £6) or specify a total stake to be split evenly across the three bets. Each Double settles on its own outcomes.

Pairings:

  • AB (pairs Selection A with Selection B)
  • AC (pairs Selection A with Selection C)
  • BC (pairs Selection B with Selection C)

Key properties:

  • If exactly two of the three selections win, you’ll have exactly one winning Double (the pair of winners); the other two include the loser and return £0.
  • If all three win, all three Doubles win and returns are summed.
  • If a selection is void/push, any Double containing it reduces to a Single on the other leg at its original odds.

Example (unit stake £5 per Double; total £15): A @ 2.10, B @ 1.80, C @ 3.00 (decimal). Outcomes:

  • A and C win; B loses → Winning Double: AC only → Return = £5 × 2.10 × 3.00 = £31.50; Profit = £31.50 − £15 = £16.50.
  • If all three win → AB = £5 × 2.10 × 1.80 = £18.90; AC = £31.50; BC = £5 × 1.80 × 3.00 = £27.00; Total Return = £77.40; Profit = £62.40.

Doubles with 4 selections: what about voids and non‑runners?

With four selections A, B, C, D you have C(4, 2) = 6 Doubles: AB, AC, AD, BC, BD, CD. Each selection appears in (n − 1) = 3 Doubles. Voids/pushes/non‑runners (NR) settle at factor 1.00 for any Double they touch, converting that Double to a Single on the remaining leg. If both legs of a Double are void/push/NR, that Double returns the unit stake. In UK horse racing, if a runner is withdrawn and the race goes ahead, a Tattersalls Rule 4 deduction (expressed as pence per £1) may reduce the winnings part on affected legs; the deduction applies per affected leg and does not reduce the stake portion.

Impact of voids/non‑runners:

  • One NR (e.g., D): AD, BD, CD become Singles on A, B, C respectively; AB, AC, BC remain Doubles.
  • Two NRs (e.g., C and D): AC, AD, BC, BD become Singles on A or B; CD is void (stake returned); AB remains a Double.
  • Pushes (e.g., spreads/totals landing on the line) behave the same as voids for that leg (factor 1.00).
  • Rule 4 illustration (winnings-only deduction): If a winning leg has decimal price D and a 15p/£1 deduction applies, the adjusted single-leg return is Stake × [1 + 0.85 × (D − 1)]. In a Double, apply the adjusted factor for that leg before multiplying by the other leg.

Example (unit stake £5 per Double; total £30): A @ 2.20, B @ 1.73, C @ 2.60, D @ 3.20. If D is NR:

  • AD → Single on A: £5 × 2.20 = £11.00 (if A wins).
  • BD → Single on B: £5 × 1.73 = £8.65 (if B wins).
  • CD → Single on C: £5 × 2.60 = £13.00 (if C wins).
  • AB, AC, BC remain full Doubles and settle as usual.
  • Rule 4 example within a Double: Suppose AB wins but A’s race has a 10p/£1 deduction and A @ 3.50 (decimal). Adjusted A factor = 1 + 0.90 × (3.50 − 1) = 1 + 0.90 × 2.50 = 3.25. If B @ 2.00, unit £5 → Return = £5 × 3.25 × 2.00 = £32.50.

Cross doubles vs standard doubles: what changes in payout?

Standard Doubles perm: choose any two from a single list of selections, giving C(n, 2) bets. Cross Doubles: take at least two separate events/groups and back multiple selections in each, then form all pairings with exactly one selection from each group. If Group 1 has g₁ selections and Group 2 has g₂, you have g₁ × g₂ Cross Doubles. Settlement and per‑bet payouts use the same multiplication rule; the difference is coverage and the number of bets.

When to use Cross Doubles:

  • Horse racing across two races: multiple picks in Race 1 and Race 2; only one horse can win per race, so a Double pays if you find the winner in each race.
  • Football across two matches: cover several outcomes in Match A and Match B, forming all cross-pairings across matches.

Counting bets and stakes:

  • Standard Doubles with n selections: C(n, 2) bets; each selection appears in (n − 1) Doubles.
  • Cross Doubles with groups g₁ and g₂: g₁ × g₂ bets; each selection in Group 1 appears in g₂ Doubles, and vice versa.

Example (Cross Doubles, unit £2): Race 1 picks R1a @ 4.00, R1b @ 6.00; Race 2 picks R2a @ 3.00, R2b @ 5.00 → 2 × 2 = 4 Doubles:

  • R1a–R2a, R1a–R2b, R1b–R2a, R1b–R2b.
  • If winners are R1b (6.00) and R2a (3.00), only R1b–R2a returns: £2 × 6.00 × 3.00 = £36.00; Total stake £8; Profit £28.00.
Bet setupSelections/groupsNumber of DoublesUnit stakeTotal stakeNotes
Standard Doubles (3 selections)n = 3C(3, 2) = 3£5£15Pairs: AB, AC, BC
Standard Doubles (4 selections)n = 4C(4, 2) = 6£5£30Each selection appears in 3 Doubles
Cross Doubles (2 groups)g₁ = 2, g₂ = 32 × 3 = 6£2£12Exactly one pick from each group per Double
Void/NR impact—Per affected Double only—Stake returned or Single appliedVoid/push = factor 1.00; both legs void = stake returned

Double vs Treble and related multiples: which calculator should you use?

A Double multiplies the prices of two independent selections; a Treble multiplies three. Both bets settle at £0 if any leg loses; a void/push/non‑runner (NR) acts as a factor of 1.00 for that leg. Value comes from finding edges on individual legs; compounding increases both potential return and variance. Use a Double calculator for exactly two legs, and switch to a Treble (or Acca) calculator for three or more.

Double vs Treble: which offers better value?

Neither is “better” in isolation—the expected value (EV) depends on the quality of each leg. With decimal odds d and true per‑leg win probability p (legs independent), EV on a £1 stake is p²·d² − 1 for a Double and p³·d³ − 1 for a Treble. For equal‑priced legs, the per‑leg break‑even probability is p = 1/d for both Double and Treble; the difference is variance and hit rate. Trebles deliver higher upside when all edges land together, but they cash less often. Doubles pay more frequently and smooth bankroll swings.

Bet typeLegsDecimal per leg (d)Combined decimalImplied win probabilityReturn on £10Profit on winBreak‑even per‑leg p
Double21.801.80² = 3.241/3.24 = 30.86%£32.40£22.401/1.80 = 55.56%
Treble31.801.80³ = 5.8321/5.832 = 17.15%£58.32£48.321/1.80 = 55.56%

Practical takeaways: if you’re pricing edges similarly across legs, a Treble amplifies both your edge and your variance; a Double offers a higher cash rate and lower drawdown risk. If any leg has a high push probability (e.g., whole‑number spread/total), your realized variance drops because pushes reduce the multiplier to 1.00 for that leg.

Doubles and Trebles calculator: when to switch tools?

Use a Double calculator for two selections, including settlement cases like voids, dead‑heats, and Rule 4 deductions (when applicable in horse racing). Move to a Treble/Acca calculator when you have three or more legs, when you’re mixing markets with partial pushes (Asian lines), or when you’re handling system bets that include a Treble component.

  • Exactly two legs (any sports/markets) → Double calculator handles win/loss/push and dead‑heat logic.
  • Exactly three legs (pure accumulator) → Treble calculator for win/loss/push and per‑leg adjustments.

System bets containing Doubles and a Treble:

  • Trixie (3 selections): 4 bets = 3 Doubles + 1 Treble.
  • Patent (3 selections): 7 bets = 3 Singles + 3 Doubles + 1 Treble.
  • Yankee (4 selections): 11 bets = 6 Doubles + 4 Trebles + 1 Fourfold.

In these cases, use a system/multiples calculator that itemises each component (including the Treble) and totals the return.

  • Each Way (E/W) across three legs (rare, racing only) → dedicated E/W Acca calculator to separate Win and Place parts per leg before compounding.
  • Rule of thumb: if your slip shows any Treble or higher‑fold, switch from a Double‑only tool to a Treble/Acca or System calculator to ensure full settlement accuracy.

2 singles and a Double: how does the combined bet pay out?

“2 Singles and a Double” is three bets on two selections A and B: Single A, Single B, and the Double AB (often staked at the same unit). It’s a simple way to keep a return if one leg wins while still compounding upside if both win.

  • Stake structure: total stake = 3 × unit stake (one per Single and one for the Double).

Settlement:

  • Both win → both Singles win + the Double wins (all three returns are added).
  • Exactly one wins → only the winning Single returns; the other Single and the Double lose.
  • Void/push on one leg → affected Single returns stake; the Double becomes a Single on the other leg at its odds.
  • Both void/push → all three stakes returned.

Worked example (unit £10; total £30): A @ 2.10, B @ 1.80

  • Both win: Single A = £10 × 2.10 = £21.00; Single B = £10 × 1.80 = £18.00; Double AB = £10 × 2.10 × 1.80 = £37.80; Total return = £76.80; Profit = £46.80.
  • Only A wins: Return = £21.00; Profit = £21.00 − £30.00 = −£9.00.
  • Only B wins: Return = £18.00; Profit = £18.00 − £30.00 = −£12.00.
  • B void, A wins: Single A = £21.00; Single B stake returned £10; Double becomes Single on A = £21.00; Total return = £52.00; Profit = £22.00.

Expert corner: evolution, app options and the strongest counterargument

Double bet calculators have moved from manual arithmetic to instant, on-device tools that handle mixed odds formats, dead-heats, pushes, and Each Way logic. Today, you can calculate returns offline with a native app or a Progressive Web App (PWA), and verify settlement edge cases that once required rulebooks. Below we trace that evolution, explain offline options, and examine a key risk: whether slick calculators can foster overconfidence.

From pen-and-paper to live tools: how did calculators evolve?

Before online tools, bettors converted fractional prices by hand, multiplied leg-by-leg, and adjusted for voids or dead-heats using shop rules. Early web pages in the late 1990s–2000s introduced simple server-side calculators. By the 2010s, client-side JavaScript made instant, interactive calculators standard. Modern PWAs, documented on [MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Progressive_web_apps), use service workers ([Service Worker API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Service_Worker_API)) to enable offline use and background updates.

  • Manual era: paper coupons and pocket calculators; fractional-to-decimal conversion and multiplication done by hand; Rule 4 deductions applied from shop charts in horse racing.
  • Early web: basic forms posted to a server; returned totals but offered little help for special cases.
  • Client-side calculators: instant conversion (fractional/decimal/American), push logic for handicaps/totals, and dead-heat handling.
  • PWAs and native apps: offline-first arithmetic, installable experiences, and support for Each Way doubles, cross-doubles, and system bets.

What modern tools compute precisely:

  • Mixed-odds multiplication: decimal = Stake × d₁ × d₂ (void/push = 1.00 factor).
  • Dead-heats: effective factor per tied position (e.g., decimal ÷ n for a 2-way dead-heat on the win market).
  • Each Way place terms: apply 1/5 or 1/4 fractions per race type and field size; multiply place parts separately.
  • Rule 4 (horse racing): winnings-only deduction per affected leg before compounding with the other leg.

Double bet calculator app and PWA: can you use it offline?

Yes. All arithmetic for a Double is deterministic and can run fully offline once the code is installed locally. A PWA caches assets via a service worker and stores preferences in IndexedDB, while a native app bundles the logic at install. Limitations: without connectivity you cannot fetch live prices, Rule 4 deductions, or team news—these must be entered manually. Web push notifications require browser/OS support (on iOS/iPadOS, Web Push is supported from 16.4+).

Works offline:

  • Odds conversion (fractional/decimal/American) and multiplication.
  • Settlement factors (win/loss/push, dead-heats, Each Way place fractions).
  • Profit vs return breakdown; quick scenario toggling.

Requires online data (optional):

  • Auto-odds retrieval from bookmakers’ APIs or pages.
  • Dynamic Rule 4 schedules and real-time non-runner updates.
  • Price history and sync across devices.
CapabilityStandard web page (no service worker)PWA (service worker)Native app (iOS/Android)
Offline calculation after first useNo (unreliable caching)Yes (cached assets + local data)Yes (bundled code)
Add to Home Screen / installShortcut onlyInstallable app-like experienceStore install
UpdatesOn page reloadBackground update on next visitVia App Store/Play updates
Local storageLimited (localStorage/cookies)IndexedDB/Cache StorageNative storage/SQLite
Web Push/NotificationsNoYes (desktop/Android; iOS/iPadOS 16.4+)Yes

For responsible gambling guidance in Great Britain, see the regulator’s site at the [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk/).

Devil’s advocate: do calculators encourage overconfidence or risk?

They can, if users mistake precision for edge. Calculators make compounding returns look effortless, which can mask the steep drop in combined hit rate as legs are added. Even with “fair” prices, a Double or Treble has a lower chance of success than a Single; without a genuine edge in each leg, the expected value is negative after the margin.

Main risks:

  • Illusion of control: clean outputs may nudge larger stakes without improving selection quality.
  • Compounding volatility: a small error per leg is magnified across multiple legs.
  • Misread pushes: assuming a push “saves” a bet, while it still erodes upside compared to a full win.

Built-in guardrails that help:

  • Show combined implied probability and break-even per leg alongside returns.
  • Flag pushes and dead-heats clearly, separating return and profit.
  • Offer stake sizing presets (e.g., fixed unit, percentage) without suggesting amounts.
  • Link to independent safer gambling resources such as the [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk/).

Example of context that tempers overconfidence:

  • Two legs at 1.80 decimal each → combined 3.24 → implied hit rate ≈ 30.86%.
  • Three legs at 1.80 → combined 5.832 → implied hit rate ≈ 17.15%.
  • A calculator that shows these probabilities next to returns makes risk transparent.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I calculate a Double with a free bet (stake not returned)?

Treat the free bet as removing the stake from the final return: Return = Free‑bet stake × (d1 × d2 − 1), where d1 and d2 are decimal odds after any settlements. Example: £10 free bet at 2.50 and 1.80 gives combined 4.50, return = £10 × (4.50 − 1) = £35.00; if one leg is void so combined becomes 2.50, return = £10 × (2.50 − 1) = £15.00; if both legs void so combined is 1.00, return = £0.00.

Does the order of selections change a Double’s payout or settlement?

No, order does not change the payout because multiplication is commutative and the same factors apply. If Leg 1 settles first the stage return is Stake × d1 and then multiplied by d2, if Leg 2 settles first it is Stake × d2 then multiplied by d1; for Stake £20 with 1.80 and 2.10 both ways give £20 × 1.80 × 2.10 = £75.60.

How are Asian handicaps and quarter lines applied inside a Double?

Split the leg into equal sub‑stakes per quarter component, apply each outcome factor, then recombine before multiplying by the other leg. For −0.25, a draw gives half push and half lose so the effective factor is 0.50; with £20 on −0.25 @ 1.95 and Over 2.5 @ 1.90 that draws, the combined factor is 0.50 × 1.90 = 0.95 and return is £19.00, while for −0.75 winning by exactly 1 the factor is 0.5 × 1.95 + 0.5 × 1.00 = 1.475 which then multiplies the other leg.

What happens to a Double if one or both legs are void or push?

One void or push turns the Double into a Single on the other leg with factor 1.00 for the voided leg; two voids or pushes return the stake. Example: £15 on 2.20 and 1.80 where the first leg is void and the second wins returns £15 × 1.00 × 1.80 = £27.00, while both void return £15.00.

How are Each Way Doubles settled when one leg wins and the other only places or dead‑heats?

An E/W Double is two equal stakes, a Win Double and a Place Double; the win part pays only if both win and the place part uses place odds per race terms, with dead‑heats dividing the affected decimal by the tie count. Example: £10 E/W total £20 on A 8/1 (place fraction 1/5 so place decimal 2.60) and B 3/1 (place decimal 1.60), if A places in a two‑way dead‑heat and B wins then Win Double is £0.00 and Place Double returns £10 × (2.60 ÷ 2) × 1.60 = £20.80, a net profit of £0.80.

What is a Rule 4 deduction and how does it change a Double return?

Rule 4 reduces only the winnings part on the affected horse by a fixed pence‑per‑pound rate, so the adjusted decimal is 1 + (1 − r) × (d − 1) where r is the deduction rate. Example: £10 Double with A 3.50 subject to 15p/£ (r = 0.15) and B 2.20 gives adjusted A = 1 + 0.85 × 2.50 = 3.125 and combined 3.125 × 2.20 = 6.875 so return £68.75, versus £77.00 without the deduction, a reduction of £8.25.

Are Double winnings taxed in the UK in 2026?

No, UK bettors pay 0% tax on gambling winnings; betting duty is levied on operators, not customers. Since 2001, UK customers have not paid income or capital gains tax on bet winnings, so a calculator’s return figure is the amount you receive before any optional fees like payment withdrawals.

How do UK bookmakers round Double returns and deductions?

Returns are settled to two decimal places to the nearest penny (£0.01), with intermediate steps sometimes rounded at leg level per house rules. For example, £10 × 1.9091 × 2.10 = £40.091 is paid as £40.09; if a bookmaker rounds each leg first to four decimals and then multiplies, the total can differ by 1p, so always check stated rounding rules.

What is a fair cash‑out estimate for a Double and how can I compute it?

Multiply the current combined decimal of all unresolved parts by the live stage stake; if one leg has already won, use Stage return × remaining leg decimal. Example: £20 Double after the first leg wins at 1.80 gives a stage return of £36.00; if the remaining leg is trading at 1.91, a fair, fee‑free estimate is £36.00 × 1.91 = £68.76, noting that bookmaker offers may discount this for margin and risk.

Can I place a Double on correlated outcomes from the same match?

Standard Doubles on related contingencies are usually restricted because the events are not independent; only a same‑game builder or parlay priced for correlation is typically allowed. For example, Team A to win and Over 2.5 goals in the same match is often blocked as a standard Double but may be offered as a priced same‑game parlay where the combined decimal already embeds correlation.

How many Doubles are in n selections and how should I split a total stake?

The count is n × (n − 1) ÷ 2 and an even split per Double is Total stake ÷ count. Example: with n = 5 there are 10 Doubles, so a £50 total becomes £5 per Double; with n = 8 there are 28 Doubles, so a £56 total becomes £2 per Double.

Do in‑play or live odds change how a Double is calculated?

No, the formula is unchanged; you still multiply the live decimal factors after applying any pushes or voids. The only practical differences are that legs may settle at different times and push‑prone markets like whole‑number spreads or totals still apply a factor of 1.00, so £25 × 1.75 (live) × 1.00 on a push returns £43.75.

How do odds boosts and accumulator bonuses apply to a Double?

A boost on a leg replaces that leg’s decimal before multiplication, while a Double bonus usually adds a percentage to the winnings portion only. Example: £20 at 2.10 and 1.80 with a 10% winnings bonus returns £75.60 + 10% of £55.60 = £75.60 + £5.56 = £81.16; boosting the 2.10 leg to 2.30 increases the return by £20 × (2.30 − 2.10) × 1.80 = £7.20.

How are non‑runners handled in horse racing Doubles and Each Way Doubles?

A non‑runner is a void leg that settles at 1.00 in the Double, so it becomes a Single on the other horse, and if both legs are non‑runners the stake is returned. In an E/W Double, both the win part and the place part for the non‑runner are void, so only the remaining horse’s corresponding part counts; for instance, £10 E/W where one horse is a non‑runner and the other wins at 4.00 yields Win return £10 × 1.00 × 4.00 = £40.00 and Place return depends on its place odds, then both are added.
