trixie.metaDescription: Free Trixie bet calculator for UK punters. Enter odds, stake, each way, Rule 4 and dead heat. Instant doubles and treble returns. Football and Irish Lotto ready.
trixie.intro: Work out Trixie returns in seconds — free, fast, and built for UK markets. Enter fractional, decimal, or American odds, add each way, Rule 4, or dead heat, and see doubles, treble, and total profit instantly. Optimised for football and Irish Lotto Trixies, no sign‑up required.
trixie.howItWorks: A Trixie is a full-coverage multiple built from three selections and contains four equal-stake bets: three doubles and one treble. There are no singles. In a win-only Trixie, you need at least two winning selections to receive a return. The calculator splits your total stake equally across the four component bets (or uses a per-bet unit stake), converts odds from the format you enter, and then multiplies them across each double and the treble to show total return and profit. Optional settlement settings such as each way, dead heat, and Rule 4 deductions can be applied to mirror UK settlement practices.
trixie.articleHtml:
What is a Trixie bet and how does this calculator work?

A Trixie is a full-coverage multiple built from three selections and contains four equal-stake bets: three doubles and one treble. There are no singles. In a win-only Trixie, you need at least two winning selections to receive a return. The calculator splits your total stake equally across the four component bets (or uses a per-bet unit stake), converts odds from the format you enter, and then multiplies them across each double and the treble to show total return and profit. Optional settlement settings such as each way, dead heat, and Rule 4 deductions can be applied to mirror UK settlement practices.

How is a Trixie structured: 3 doubles + 1 treble

A Trixie uses three selections (A, B, C) to generate four bets. Each component bet must win in full for a return on that component. If you enter a total stake, the calculator divides it equally across all four bets; if you enter a unit stake, that amount is staked on each component bet.

  • Selections: three picks (A, B, C)
  • Component bets: 3 doubles (AB, AC, BC) and 1 treble (ABC)
  • Stake allocation: total stake ÷ 4 per bet, or fixed unit stake per bet
  • Minimum winners (win-only): 2 winners (to land at least one double)
ComponentCombinationsNumber of BetsMinimum Winners NeededStake Allocation Logic
DoublesAB, AC, BC32Unit stake s per bet, or total T ⇒ T/4 on each bet
TrebleABC13Unit stake s per bet, or total T ⇒ T/4 on the treble

Can we walk through a Trixie bet example?

Example (win-only). Three selections with fractional odds and decimal equivalents: A at 2/1 (3.00), B at 5/4 (2.25), C at 4/5 (1.80). Use a unit stake of £2 per bet (total stake £8 across 4 bets). Returns are calculated in decimal as stake × product of odds for the legs in that component.

Component BetOdds (Decimal)StakeReturn if A, B, C all winReturn if C loses (only A and B win)
Double AB3.00 × 2.25£2£2 × 3.00 × 2.25 = £13.50£13.50
Double AC3.00 × 1.80£2£2 × 3.00 × 1.80 = £10.80£0.00 (loses)
Double BC2.25 × 1.80£2£2 × 2.25 × 1.80 = £8.10£0.00 (loses)
Treble ABC3.00 × 2.25 × 1.80£2£2 × 3.00 × 2.25 × 1.80 = £24.30£0.00 (loses)
Totals—£8.00£56.70 return, £48.70 profit£13.50 return, £5.50 profit
  • When all three win, each component pays; total return is the sum of all four components.
  • If only two win, exactly one double pays; the other two doubles and the treble lose.
  • If fewer than two win (win-only), no return is generated in a Trixie.

Each way Trixies place a win and a place Trixie simultaneously (doubling the number of bets from 4 to 8). Place returns use the bookmaker’s place terms and the place part of the odds; the calculator applies these automatically when each way is toggled on.

What inputs and formats are supported (fractional, decimal, American)?

The calculator accepts fractional, decimal, and American odds, and converts them consistently for calculation. You can enter either a total stake (auto-split into four equal parts) or a unit stake per bet. Optional inputs include each way, Rule 4 deductions, dead heat fractions, void legs, and custom place terms for racing.

  • Fractional (UK): enter as a/b (e.g., 5/2). Decimal conversion: 1 + a/b.
  • Decimal (EU): enter as d (e.g., 3.50). American conversion: if d ≥ 2.0 ⇒ +100 × (d − 1); if 1.01–1.99 ⇒ −100 ÷ (d − 1).
  • American (US): enter as +x or −x. Decimal conversion: +x ⇒ 1 + x/100; −x ⇒ 1 + 100/|x|.
FormatExampleDecimal EquivalentAmerican Equivalent
Fractional2/13.00+200
Fractional5/42.25+125
Fractional4/51.80-125
Decimal1.501.50-200
American+1502.50+150
American-1101.9091-110
  • Rounding: returns are typically displayed to 2 decimal places in GBP for UK users.
  • Settlement options: dead heats apply fractional reductions; Rule 4 deductions reduce affected legs as per the deduction band.
  • Validation: the tool checks for zero/negative odds, incompatible formats, and splits total stake accurately across the four bets.

How do you place a Trixie bet and read your returns?

Placing a Trixie in practice means entering three selections, choosing how you want to stake (a single unit stake per component bet or a total stake split across them), and deciding whether to make it each way. Reading the returns is then about understanding that your Trixie comprises three doubles and one treble: if all three selections win, all four components settle and pay; if exactly two win, one double pays; if fewer than two win in a win-only Trixie, there is no return. The calculator shows per-component returns and the combined total return and profit, applying settlement adjustments such as dead heats, Rule 4, and voids where relevant.

Stake options: total vs per bet, each way explained

You can fund a Trixie using either a unit stake per component bet or a single total stake that is divided equally across all component bets. A win-only Trixie has 4 bets; an each-way Trixie doubles that to 8 bets (a win and a place part for each component). Each way is most relevant to events with each-way place terms (e.g., horse racing, golf outrights); it is typically not used for regular football match markets.

Stake ModeTrixie TypeNumber of BetsStake Per BetTotal Outlay (Example)How It’s Applied
Unit stakeWin-only4s (e.g., £2)4 × s (e.g., £8)Same unit stake goes on each double and the treble.
Unit stakeEach way8s on win + s on place (e.g., £2 + £2)8 × s (e.g., £16)Each component has a win part and a place part at the defined place terms.
Total stakeWin-only4T ÷ 4 (e.g., £20 ÷ 4 = £5)T (e.g., £20)Total stake is split equally across the 3 doubles and 1 treble.
Total stakeEach way8T ÷ 8 (e.g., £20 ÷ 8 = £2.50)T (e.g., £20)Total stake is split equally across the win and place parts of all components.

Key points when reading returns: the “win” and “place” parts of an each-way Trixie settle independently; place returns use the bookmaker’s stated place terms and the place fraction of the odds. Profit equals total return minus total outlay. For UK users, returns are typically displayed in GBP to two decimal places.

  • Win-only Trixie: minimum of two winners required for any return.
  • Each-way Trixie: the place part can return even if the win part loses, subject to place terms.
  • Total stake mode simplifies budgeting; unit stake mode gives fine control over staking per component.

Settlement rules: dead heats, Rule 4, voids

UK settlement conventions affect how component bets in your Trixie are calculated. The calculator mirrors these rules per selection and propagates them to affected doubles and the treble.

  • Dead heats: when selections tie for a paying position and cannot be split, the affected part of the bet is settled at full odds on a reduced stake. For example, in a two-way dead heat for first on a win market, half the stake is settled at the full odds (the other half is lost). For each-way bets, apply dead-heat reductions separately to the win part (if tied for the win) and/or the place part (if tied for a place).
  • Rule 4 deductions (horse/greyhound racing): if a runner is withdrawn after bets are placed and before the off, a deduction in pence per pound may apply to winning returns (stake is not reduced). The deduction band depends on the withdrawn runner’s price at the time of withdrawal (e.g., a strong favourite such as 4/6 typically produces a higher deduction than a 6/1 chance). The calculator lets you enter the Rule 4 figure (e.g., 55p in the pound) per affected selection, and applies it to the returns of any component bet including that selection.

Voids: a void selection is treated as having odds of 1.00. In a Trixie:

  • A double containing a voided selection becomes a single on the remaining selection.
  • The treble becomes a double on the two remaining selections.
  • If two selections are void, the treble becomes a single on the remaining live selection; any doubles with two voids are void.
  • Order of operations: apply voids first (structural change), then dead heat adjustments and Rule 4 deductions to any impacted live selections.
  • Non-runner with no Rule 4 (e.g., after the off or in sports outside racing) typically settles as void.
  • The calculator shows per-component outcomes so you can verify how each rule changed the return.

Football and Irish Lotto Trixie: any differences?

The Trixie structure is identical across sports and specials, but market conventions differ. Football Trixies commonly use win-only markets (e.g., Match Result, Over/Under, Both Teams To Score). Each way is unusual for regular match markets but can apply to outrights (e.g., league winner each way) with specified place terms. Irish Lotto Trixies, where permitted, use fixed-odds number betting; there are typically no each-way or Rule 4 adjustments on lotto markets, and dead heat concepts do not apply in number draws. Bookmaker rules may restrict lotto multiples, especially within the same draw; many require selections to be from different draws or markets—always check the specific operator’s rules.

Football:

  • Commonly win-only Trixies on match or totals markets.
  • No Rule 4; dead heats rare except in player or outright markets.
  • Each way mainly for outrights or top-scorer style markets (if offered).

Irish Lotto:

  • Fixed-odds number betting; no each-way, no Rule 4, dead heats not applicable.
  • Voids can occur if a draw is cancelled/postponed; affected legs settle as void.
  • Multiples may be restricted within the same draw; cross-draw Trixies may be required by some bookmakers.

Calculator inputs:

  • Football: enter odds in fractional/decimal/American; usually win-only.
  • Irish Lotto: use the bookmaker’s fixed odds (often large fractional prices) and set each selection’s market/draw to comply with rules.

Advanced calculator settings for real‑world betting

Real‑world settlements rarely follow a tidy textbook pattern. Prices move, runners come out, ties occur, and bookmakers sometimes offer cash‑out on live bets. This section explains how an advanced Trixie calculator treats edge cases you will meet in practice, how to interpret the coverage of doubles and the treble, and how to measure overall performance (ROI). It also outlines practical ways to run the tool like an app on your mobile.

Does the app handle cash‑out and partial wins?

Cash‑out is a proprietary price offered by a bookmaker on the whole bet at a given moment; it depends on internal models and margins and cannot be predicted exactly by third‑party calculators. As a result, an advanced Trixie calculator will not “simulate” or fetch live cash‑out automatically. Instead, you can record or approximate outcomes in a transparent way.

What the calculator can do:

  • Settle standard outcomes precisely (win, lose, void) and apply dead‑heat fractions and Rule 4 deductions per affected selection.
  • Record a taken cash‑out as a final total return for audit purposes (if a “custom total return” field is provided), bypassing further component settlement.
  • Approximate “partial success” on a selection by using dead‑heat fractions when applicable (e.g., ties for places) — this is the only regulated form of a “partial win.”

What the calculator cannot do:

  • Reconstruct or guarantee a bookmaker’s live cash‑out offer, since algorithms and margins vary by operator and time.
  • Apply a single “effective” price to one leg that exactly replicates a whole‑bet cash‑out across all Trixie components — there is no unique solution because each selection appears in multiple components with different exposures.
  • If you accept a bookmaker cash‑out, treat the bet as closed. Record the actual payout as the final return; do not also settle legs later, to avoid double counting.
  • Use dead‑heat inputs only when a legitimate tie exists and settlement rules specify a stake split at full odds.
  • If a selection is voided (e.g., non‑runner), mark it void; the calculator will automatically transform affected doubles/treble as per standard rules (see settlement rules).

Accumulator coverage: doubles, treble, and overall ROI

A Trixie creates three doubles and one treble from three selections. Your exposure and return come from the sum of those four components. For win‑only Trixies with unit stake s, total outlay is 4s; for each‑way, outlay is 8s (win and place parts). Measure performance with profit (total return − total stake) and ROI = profit ÷ total stake × 100%.

ScenarioSelections (Decimal)Live ComponentsComponent Returns (£, unit stake £1)Total StakeTotal ReturnProfitROI
All three winA 2.50, B 1.80, C 3.20AB, AC, BC, ABCAB 4.50; AC 8.00; BC 5.76; ABC 14.40£4.00£32.66£28.66716.50%
Exactly two win (A, B win; C loses)A 2.50, B 1.80, C 3.20ABAB 4.50£4.00£4.50£0.5012.50%
One or zero winAny——£4.00£0.00−£4.00−100.00%

The table assumes win‑only with £1 unit stakes per component (total £4). For each‑way Trixies, compute win and place parts separately using the place terms, then sum both totals before calculating ROI.

  • Coverage: a Trixie pays with two winners (at least one double) and maximises upside when all three win (all four components pay).
  • Sensitivity: ROI is highly sensitive to one short‑priced selection losing because it knocks out two doubles and the treble at once.
  • Discipline: compare expected value (EV) selection‑by‑selection; the product structure magnifies both edge and overround.

Is there a Trixie bet calculator app?

Most modern Trixie calculators are web‑based and mobile‑friendly. You can run them like an app by adding them to your home screen. This creates an app‑style launcher, quick access, and (for progressive web apps) local caching of assets. Functionality is the same as the website: you can enter odds in fractional/decimal/American, toggle each way, and apply dead‑heat, Rule 4, and void settings.

iOS (Safari):

  • Open the calculator page.
  • Tap Share, then Add to Home Screen.
  • Confirm the name and Add.

Android (Chrome):

  • Open the calculator page.
  • Tap the menu (⋮), choose Add to Home screen (or Install app if offered).
  • Confirm Install.

Desktop (Chrome/Edge):

  • Open the calculator page.
  • Click the Install icon in the address bar (if available), or use More tools → Create shortcut (Open as window).

Tip: Always verify settlement settings (dead heat, Rule 4, voids) match your market before saving presets. Web apps cannot access your bookmaker account, so they do not place bets or fetch live cash‑out quotes; they are calculation tools only.

Trixie vs Patent and other multiples: which should you choose?

Choosing between a Trixie and its neighbours (Patent, plain doubles/trebles, or large perms like a Heinz) is a trade‑off between coverage, cost, variance, and your edge on each selection. A Trixie focuses your stake on three doubles and one treble (no singles), while a Patent adds singles for more coverage at a higher outlay. Larger perms scale exposure quickly and require robust selection quality and bankroll discipline.

Patent vs Trixie: which is better for your strategy?

A Trixie is 4 bets (3 doubles + 1 treble) from 3 selections, requiring at least two winners for any return. A Patent is 7 bets (3 singles + 3 doubles + 1 treble), so it can return with just one winner, but the extra coverage costs more stake. The better choice depends on your strike rate, average odds, and tolerance for variance.

Bet TypeBetsComponentsMinimum Winners for Any ReturnOutlay at £1 Unit StakeReturn if only A wins (A=3.00)Return if A & B win (A=3.00, B=2.25)Return if A, B, C win (A=3.00, B=2.25, C=1.80)
Trixie4AB, AC, BC; ABC2£4£0.00£6.75 (AB)£28.35 (AB+AC+BC+ABC)
Patent7A, B, C; AB, AC, BC; ABC1£7£3.00 (A)£12.00 (A + B + AB)£35.40 (A+B+C+AB+AC+BC+ABC)

Interpretation with £1 unit stakes: A single winner at 3.00 returns £3.00 on a Patent but still loses overall (profit −£4.00) because the outlay is £7; a Trixie would return £0.00 in the same case. With two winners at 3.00 and 2.25, the Trixie returns £6.75 (profit +£2.75 vs £4 outlay), while the Patent returns £12.00 (profit +£5.00 vs £7 outlay). Break‑even guides: Trixie with exactly two winners breaks even when d1 × d2 ≥ 4.00 (for £1 unit stakes); Patent with exactly one winner breaks even only if the winning single’s decimal odds ≥ 7.00. If you often hit two winners at modest odds, Trixie concentrates stake efficiently; if you hit lone winners regularly or value reduced variance, Patent’s singles can justify the extra cost.

Double and Treble calculators: when are they better?

Sometimes you do not want full Trixie coverage. A Double calculator is ideal when you only want exposure to one specific pair (e.g., AB) without staking on AC or BC. A Treble calculator fits a high‑conviction three‑leg view where you accept all‑or‑nothing risk for a higher potential return. These tools also help sanity‑check settlement on a subset of legs (e.g., a single related double) without the extra permutations a Trixie carries.

Use a Double when:

  • You have two strong, independent selections and want to avoid extra pairs.
  • Your edge is concentrated in the two picks, and adding a third would dilute EV.
  • You need tighter risk control than a Trixie’s three doubles provide.

Use a Treble when:

  • You have three strong selections and prefer maximum payout over partial coverage.
  • You explicitly accept higher variance and the possibility of returning £0 unless all three win.
  • You want a clean benchmark for the three‑leg product price (to compare against a Trixie’s blended outcome).

Notes:

  • A Trixie’s “coverage premium” is the extra stake spread over three doubles; a single Double avoids paying for unwanted combinations.
  • Related contingencies are typically not allowed in multiples; ensure legs are independent under bookmaker rules.

Example check: With A=2.20 and B=2.00, a £1 Double returns £4.40 if both win; a Trixie including C adds two more doubles and a treble — useful only if C adds genuine EV. If C is marginal, isolating AB as a Double often produces a higher EV per staked pound.

Heinz and above: when does scale beat coverage?

Large perms like a Heinz multiply coverage across many fold sizes but escalate cost and variance. They suit bettors with a consistent edge across multiple selections and a bankroll plan that tolerates swings.

  • Heinz (6 selections, 57 bets): 15 doubles, 20 trebles, 15 fourfolds, 6 fivefolds, 1 sixfold.
  • Super Heinz (7 selections, 120 bets): 21 doubles, 35 trebles, 35 fourfolds, 21 fivefolds, 7 sixfolds, 1 sevenfold.
  • Goliath (8 selections, 247 bets): 28 doubles, 56 trebles, 70 fourfolds, 56 fivefolds, 28 sixfolds, 8 sevenfolds, 1 eightfold.

When scale can beat smaller coverage: (1) your selections are genuinely value (positive EV) and broadly independent; (2) your strike rate sustains multiples so that higher‑fold winners appear often enough to offset the many losing components; (3) staking is sized to bankroll (e.g., small unit stakes) so variance is survivable. If your edge is narrow or concentrated in few legs, or you prefer tighter control, smaller structures (Trixie, Doubles, Trebles) are typically superior. Remember that bookmaker overround compounds through multiples; only real edge can overcome that in the long run.

How did Trixie multiples evolve — and when is using one a bad idea?

Trixies are part of the UK “full-cover” family of multiple bets designed to spread risk across combinations of the same set of selections. They evolved alongside other named perms such as the Yankee, Canadian (Super Yankee), Heinz, and Patent. A Trixie uses three selections to create three doubles and a treble, deliberately omitting singles to focus stake on combination payouts. This section outlines where Trixies sit historically, the main argument against using them, and how to quantify bankroll impact and variance so you can decide when a Trixie is a smart choice — and when it is not.

From Yankees and Patent bets to modern Trixies: a brief history

Full-cover combinations became staples of British bookmaking as a way to balance risk and reward across several selections without having to place each combination manually. Names distinguish the number of selections and whether singles are included. A Trixie (3 selections, 4 bets) is the lean, combination-only option; a Patent (3 selections, 7 bets) adds singles for softer variance; larger sets (Yankee, Canadian, Heinz) expand the grid of doubles through higher folds as the number of selections grows.

  • Trixie: 3 selections, 4 bets (3 doubles + 1 treble), no singles.
  • Patent: 3 selections, 7 bets (3 singles + 3 doubles + 1 treble).
  • Yankee: 4 selections, 11 bets (6 doubles + 4 trebles + 1 fourfold), no singles.
  • Canadian (Super Yankee): 5 selections, 26 bets, no singles.
  • Heinz: 6 selections, 57 bets, no singles.

What’s the strongest argument against Trixies?

The strongest argument is stake dilution without sufficient edge on all three selections. Because a Trixie spreads your money across three doubles and one treble, one weak or negative‑EV leg can destroy overall value: if that leg loses, it can wipe out two doubles and the treble at once. If you regularly hit only one winner from three, a Trixie returns nothing, whereas a Patent at least has singles that can recoup part of the outlay. In short, Trixies reward clustered success (two or three winners) but punish isolated winners.

  • Minimum winners: win‑only Trixies need at least two winners for any return.
  • Edge concentration: adding a marginal third selection can reduce overall EV versus staking more on your best two as a single Double.
  • Overround compounding: bookmaker margin compounds across legs; only genuine value can overcome this in multiples.
  • Use‑case fit: Trixies suit bettors who expect two or more winners reasonably often at sensible prices; otherwise, variance and negative EV can dominate.

Bankroll and variance: what’s the cost of error?

Cost of error shows how much a single miss hurts a Trixie. The table below uses a win‑only Trixie with £1 unit stakes per component (total outlay £4) and the same three decimal prices across scenarios. It quantifies returns and ROI when different numbers of selections win. These figures are mechanical outcomes from standard multiple-bet settlement.

ScenarioSelections (Decimal)Paying ComponentsTotal StakeTotal ReturnProfitROI
All three winA 2.50, B 2.00, C 3.00AB=£5.00; AC=£7.50; BC=£6.00; ABC=£15.00£4.00£33.50£29.50737.50%
Exactly two win (A & B win; C loses)A 2.50, B 2.00, C 3.00AB=£5.00£4.00£5.00£1.0025.00%
Exactly two win (A & C win; B loses)A 2.50, B 2.00, C 3.00AC=£7.50£4.00£7.50£3.5087.50%
Exactly two win (B & C win; A loses)A 2.50, B 2.00, C 3.00BC=£6.00£4.00£6.00£2.0050.00%
One or zero winA 2.50, B 2.00, C 3.00—£4.00£0.00−£4.00−100.00%

Reading the numbers: with three winners, upside is strong because all components land. With exactly two winners, returns vary by which pair lands, reflecting different price products (AC pays more than AB or BC here). The “cost of error” is clearest when one or zero wins: the entire Trixie returns £0, so your outlay becomes the loss. Mitigation tactics include improving selection quality (positive EV), avoiding marginal third legs, or reallocating some stake to a Double on the strongest pair or to a Patent if you want singles for variance control.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I calculate each‑way Trixie returns step by step?

An each‑way Trixie is 8 bets: settle the 4 win parts at win odds and the 4 place parts at reduced place odds, then add both totals. Use place decimal = 1 + f × (d − 1), where d is win decimal and f is the place fraction (for 1/5, f=0.20). Example: A 3.50, B 2.75, C 5.00, terms 1/5, £1 each‑way unit stake (so £2 per component, £16 total). Place decimals are A 1 + 0.2×2.5 = 1.50, B 1 + 0.2×1.75 = 1.35, C 1 + 0.2×4.0 = 1.80. If all three win, win‑part returns: AB £1×3.50×2.75=£9.63, AC £1×3.50×5.00=£17.50, BC £1×2.75×5.00=£13.75, ABC £1×3.50×2.75×5.00=£48.13 (total £89.01). Place‑part returns: AB £1×1.50×1.35=£2.03, AC £1×1.50×1.80=£2.70, BC £1×1.35×1.80=£2.43, ABC £1×1.50×1.35×1.80=£3.65 (total £10.81). Combined return £99.82; profit £83.82.

How are Rule 4 deductions applied in a Trixie when there are multiple withdrawals?

Apply Rule 4 to the profit part of each affected leg before multiplying: replace each affected leg’s decimal d with d′ = 1 + (1 − D) × (d − 1), where D is the total deduction (e.g., 0.30 for 30p). For multiple withdrawals in the same race, add deductions and cap at the bookmaker’s limit (commonly 90p in the pound; check rules). Example: A 3.50 with a 30p deduction becomes 1 + 0.70×2.50 = 2.75; if two withdrawals total 50p, A becomes 1 + 0.50×2.50 = 2.25. In a Trixie, use the adjusted price for every component containing that selection; unaffected legs remain at their original odds.

How is a dead heat settled inside a Trixie?

Settle the affected part at full odds on a reduced stake: for an n‑way dead heat, use stake/n at the original odds. Equivalent shortcut for a single leg is an effective decimal of d/n for the impacted part. Example 1 (win market, two‑way tie): £2 at 4.00 becomes £1×4.00 = £4.00 return (the other £1 is lost). Example 2 (each‑way, three‑way dead heat for 2nd with 1/5 terms, d=6.00): place decimal is 1 + 0.2×5 = 2.0; with a three‑way tie on the place part, £2 place stake settles as £2/3 at 2.0 = £1.33 return; the win part is unaffected unless it also tied for the win.

What happens if one leg is void or a non‑runner in a Trixie?

Treat a void as decimal 1.00 and reduce folds accordingly: any double containing the void becomes a single, and the treble becomes a double on the remaining live legs. Example: Unit stake £2 per bet, A 3.00, B 2.00, C void. Settlements: AB double £2×3.00×2.00=£12.00; AC becomes single on A £2×3.00=£6.00; BC becomes single on B £2×2.00=£4.00; ABC treble becomes another AB double £2×3.00×2.00=£12.00. Total return £34.00 from £8.00 staked; profit £26.00.

Can I place a Trixie on related markets from the same match or event?

Generally no: most bookmakers prohibit related contingencies in multiples, so markets that are causally linked (e.g., Team to Win and Player to Score in the same match) cannot form a Trixie. If the operator offers a same‑game multi/bet‑builder, it is usually a single bespoke bet and cannot be combined into an external Trixie; check house rules before placing.

What are the break‑even odds for a win‑only Trixie with unit stakes?

With unit stake s on each component (4s total), a two‑winner outcome breaks even when d1×d2 ≥ 4.00. Example: if your two winners are 2.00 and 2.10, their product is 4.20, so a single paying double returns s×4.20, which exceeds the £4s outlay by 5% of s; if it were 2.00×2.00=4.00, you exactly break even on the whole Trixie.

How do I calculate the chance of any return and the expected value (EV) of a Trixie?

For independent legs with win probabilities pA, pB, pC, the chance of any return (win‑only) is pA pB (1−pC) + pA pC (1−pB) + pB pC (1−pA) + pA pB pC. EV with unit stake s per component is s[dA dB pA pB + dA dC pA pC + dB dC pB pC + dA dB dC pA pB pC]. Example: A d=3.00 p=0.40, B d=2.25 p=0.50, C d=1.80 p=0.60 gives P(return)=0.08+0.12+0.18+0.12=0.50 (50%); EV per s is 6.75×0.20 + 5.40×0.24 + 4.05×0.30 + 12.15×0.12 = 5.319, so EV on a £1 unit stake Trixie is £5.319 return vs £4 outlay (illustrative only; real edges vary).

How do free‑bet (stake not returned) promotions affect a Trixie calculation?

For a stake‑not‑returned free bet, each winning component returns stake × (product decimal − 1) instead of stake × product decimal. Example: a £2 free‑bet unit stake on a double priced 6.75 returns £2×(6.75−1)=£11.50, not £13.50. If you apply one free‑bet token as a total Trixie stake, it is split equally across the 4 components and the SNR rule applies to each winning component; always confirm the promo permits perms.

How are rounding and pennies handled on UK Trixie settlements?

Operators usually display returns to 2 decimal places and round at the final bet level, though some round components first; the difference is typically ≤£0.01 per component. Example: if three components each compute to £10.005, rounding components first yields £30.03; rounding after summing £30.015 yields £30.02. Your calculator should match your bookmaker’s published rounding policy.

Are Trixie winnings tax‑free in the UK and Ireland?

Yes for customers: as of 2026, UK and Irish fixed‑odds winnings are not taxed at the point of payout; operators pay duties, not bettors. If you bet from other jurisdictions, tax treatment can differ; verify local law before assuming net‑of‑tax returns.

Do maximum payout limits apply to Trixies, and can they cap my return?

Yes, bookmakers publish per‑sport and per‑event maximum payout limits, and a Trixie is usually treated as one bet against that cap. Example: if your calculated return is £280,000 but the sport’s maximum payout is £250,000, settlement is capped at £250,000 even if component math is higher; check the operator’s limits before staking.

Why does my Trixie show £0 return even though two selections "won"?

Most often one "win" was a place‑only outcome on a win‑only Trixie, a dead‑heat or Rule 4 reduction was mis‑set, or a leg was voided and no remaining pair actually formed a winning double. Quick audit: confirm Trixie type (win‑only vs each‑way), verify each leg’s result flag (win/lose/void), recheck dead‑heat fractions and Rule 4 inputs, and ensure at least one double genuinely contains two full winners.

Can I build a Trixie from Irish Lotto selections, and what should I watch for?

You can only if your bookmaker permits lotto multiples across different draws/markets; same‑draw combinations are often restricted. Example: if a firm prices a 1‑number pick at 6/1 (decimal 7.00) and you choose three different draws, a £1 unit‑stake Trixie has doubles returning £7.00×7.00=£49.00 each and the treble £7.00×7.00×7.00=£343.00 if all land; confirm eligibility before relying on these figures.

How should pushes and Asian handicap lines be handled in a Trixie?

A push settles that leg as void (decimal 1.00) and reduces folds as usual. For split Asian lines (e.g., +0.25), settlement splits the stake across two sub‑lines; to mirror this in a standard Trixie calculator, duplicate the selection into two legs with half stakes (one at 0 and one at +0.5) and settle each outcome, then sum the components.

How can I sanity‑check odds inputs to avoid calculation errors in a Trixie?

Convert all odds to decimals and spot‑check: fractional a/b should become 1 + a/b; American +x becomes 1 + x/100; American −x becomes 1 + 100/x. Example: 5/4→2.25, +150→2.50, −110→1.9091. If any converted decimal is ≤1.00 or negative, the input is invalid and will produce £0 or nonsensical returns.

patent.howItWorks: A Patent is a 7-line multiple across three selections: 3 singles, 3 doubles, and 1 treble. The calculator totals returns and profit for win-only or each-way (EW) settlements, applying built-in rules such as Rule 4 deductions, dead-heats, and void legs. Enter your three selections, choose odds format, set a unit or total stake, and the tool itemises every line so you can see exactly where the return comes from.
patent.articleHtml:
How do I calculate a Patent bet with this calculator?

A Patent is a 7-line multiple across three selections: 3 singles, 3 doubles, and 1 treble. The calculator totals returns and profit for win-only or each-way (EW) settlements, applying built-in rules such as Rule 4 deductions, dead-heats, and void legs. Enter your three selections, choose odds format, set a unit or total stake, and the tool itemises every line so you can see exactly where the return comes from.

How do I enter odds, stake, and each-way options step by step?

Follow these steps to get an accurate result for a Patent in seconds. You can use fractional, decimal, or American prices and switch formats at any time without changing the underlying odds.

  • Select odds format: choose Fractional, Decimal, or American in the format switcher.
  • Add three selections: for each, enter the price and choose the result (Won, Lost, Void, Dead-Heat). For EW bets, also enter place terms where applicable (e.g., 1/5 odds, 3 places).

Stake entry:

  • Unit stake: the amount per line (e.g., £1 makes a £7 total for win-only; £14 total for EW).
  • Total stake: the calculator will derive the unit stake by dividing by 7 (win-only) or 14 (EW).
  • Each-Way option (optional): tick EW if your market supports places. Enter place fraction (e.g., 1/5, 1/4) and places paid. The calculator doubles the stake (win part + place part) and settles each part independently.
  • Advanced settlement (optional): if notified by the bookmaker, add Rule 4 deductions (e.g., 10p, 20p per £1) on affected legs, mark dead-heats, or set void/non-runner legs. These settings apply automatically across all relevant singles, doubles, and the treble.
  • Calculate: the tool lists returns and profit for each of the 7 lines and the overall total, including EW place returns if selected.

Worked example (win-only, unit stake £1, total stake £7): Selections at 2/1 (A), 5/2 (B), and Evens 1/1 (C). Decimal equivalents are 3.0, 3.5, and 2.0. If all three win, each line settles as below.

LineLegsUnit Stake (£)Decimal ProductReturn (£)
SingleA (2/1)1.003.003.00
SingleB (5/2)1.003.503.50
SingleC (1/1)1.002.002.00
DoubleA × B1.003.00 × 3.50 = 10.5010.50
DoubleA × C1.003.00 × 2.00 = 6.006.00
DoubleB × C1.003.50 × 2.00 = 7.007.00
TrebleA × B × C1.003.00 × 3.50 × 2.00 = 21.0021.00
TotalAll 7 linesStake £7—£53.00 (Profit £46.00)
  • EW example guidance: if EW at 1/5 odds for places, each line is duplicated into a place part at one-fifth of the win odds. The calculator sums win and place returns for the total.

What settlement rules are built in (Rule 4, dead-heat, voids)?

The calculator follows standard UK fixed-odds settlement. You can apply Rule 4 deductions to affected markets, handle dead-heats correctly, and mark void/non-runner legs. These rules flow through all 7 lines of the Patent automatically.

Rule 4 (UK horse racing):

  • Applied when a horse is withdrawn after betting has formed a market.
  • Deduction is a pence-per-£1 factor taken from winnings (profit), not from the returned stake.
  • Multiple withdrawals are applied sequentially to the winnings portion.
  • Place part of EW bets is also subject to Rule 4 when applicable.

Dead-heat:

  • For a win dead-heat between _k_ runners, settle as if stake is divided by _k_ at full odds; the remainder of the stake is lost.
  • For place dead-heats, if _k_ runners share the final paying place across _m_ available places in that tie, the effective stake fraction is _m ÷ k_.

Voids/non-runners:

  • A void single returns the stake.
  • In multiples, a void leg is removed: a double with one void becomes a single on the remaining leg; a treble with one void becomes a double on the other two legs.
  • In a Patent, one void selection redistributes stakes across remaining valid lines; duplicate lines that result (e.g., two AB doubles) are summed.

Worked Rule 4 and dead-heat examples:

ScenarioInputSettlement LogicReturn (£)
Rule 4 on a single£10 at 2/1, Rule 4 = 20p/£Winnings £20 × (1 − 0.20) = £16; return = £10 + £16£26.00
Win dead-heat of 2£10 at 2/1, two-way dead-heatEffective stake £10 ÷ 2 = £5 at 2/1; other £5 lost£5 × 3.0 = £15.00
Void in a PatentUnit stake £1; C is voidSingles: A £1, B £1; C £1 returned. Doubles: AC→A £1, BC→B £1, AB stays £1. Treble ABC→AB £1.Active lines: A×2, B×2, AB×2; C stake returned

Which odds formats are supported (fractional, decimal, American)?

The calculator supports fractional, decimal, and American odds. You can enter prices in any format, switch formats instantly, and the tool will convert and settle consistently. Conversions use standard formulas below.

  • Fractional (a/b) to Decimal: 1 + a/b
  • Decimal (d) to Fractional: d − 1 expressed as a simplified fraction

American to Decimal:

  • Positive +X: 1 + (X ÷ 100)
  • Negative −X: 1 + (100 ÷ X_abs)

Decimal to American:

  • If d ≥ 2.00: +100 × (d − 1)
  • If 1.01 ≤ d < 2.00: −100 ÷ (d − 1)
FractionalDecimalAmericanNotes
1/41.25-4001 + 0.25 = 1.25; American = -100 ÷ (1.25 − 1) = -400
1/21.50-2001 + 0.5 = 1.5; American = -100 ÷ 0.5 = -200
1/1 (Evens)2.00+1001 + 1 = 2.0; American = +100 × (2.0 − 1) = +100
2/13.00+2001 + 2 = 3.0; American = +100 × 2 = +200
4/15.00+4001 + 4 = 5.0; American = +100 × 4 = +400
  • Tip: For multiples (doubles/treble), the calculator multiplies decimal prices across legs and then applies any Rule 4, dead-heat, or EW adjustments before summing all 7 lines.

What is a Patent bet and what does it include?

A Patent is a full-cover multiple across three selections that includes singles. It breaks into seven separate lines: three singles (one on each selection), three doubles (every pair), and one treble (all three). Because it contains singles, a Patent can return money with just one winner, while still giving you the compounding upside of doubles and the treble. Stakes are staked per line (unit stake). For each-way (EW) betting, every line is duplicated into a win part and a place part, doubling the total outlay.

  • Number of selections: 3
  • Number of bets (win-only): 7 (3 singles, 3 doubles, 1 treble)
  • Stake multiplier: 7× unit stake (win-only) or 14× unit stake (each-way)
  • Minimum winners for a return: 1 (because singles are included)

How does a Patent split into 7 bets with a real-money example?

The seven lines in a Patent are the complete set of outcomes across three picks with singles coverage. This protects your bank if just one selection wins and gives scalable returns as more legs land.

  • Single A
  • Single B
  • Single C
  • Double AB
  • Double AC
  • Double BC
  • Treble ABC

Real-money example (win-only): Unit stake £2 (total stake £14). Prices: A at 2/1 (decimal 3.00), B at 5/2 (3.50), C at Evens 1/1 (2.00).

All three win:

  • Singles: A £2×3.00=£6.00; B £2×3.50=£7.00; C £2×2.00=£4.00
  • Doubles: AB £2×3.00×3.50=£21.00; AC £2×3.00×2.00=£12.00; BC £2×3.50×2.00=£14.00
  • Treble: ABC £2×3.00×3.50×2.00=£42.00
  • Total return £106.00; Profit £92.00

Two winners (A and B win; C loses):

  • Singles: A £6.00; B £7.00
  • Doubles: AB £21.00
  • Other lines lose; Total return £34.00; Profit £20.00

One winner (A wins only):

  • Singles: A £6.00
  • Other lines lose; Total return £6.00; Loss £8.00

Each-way variant: if a market offers EW at, for example, 1/5 odds for places, each of the seven lines is duplicated into a place line settled at one-fifth of the win odds. The calculator sums win and place sides to show the overall return.

Trixie vs Patent vs Yankee: which suits your strategy?

These are all “full cover” style multiples, but they differ in how much cover they provide and how much stake they require. A Patent adds singles to a three-selection full cover (more protection, higher stake). A Trixie uses only doubles and a treble (no singles, lower stake, higher variance). A Yankee extends to four selections without singles, increasing lines and potential upside at the cost of a larger stake.

Bet typeSelectionsBets (win-only)Stake multiplier (WO / EW)Includes singles?Min winners for a returnTypical use case
Patent37 (3 singles, 3 doubles, 1 treble)7× / 14×Yes1Balanced cover with protection from one winner; popular for horses and football props
Trixie34 (3 doubles, 1 treble)4× / 8×No2Lower outlay than a Patent; higher variance aiming for 2+ winners
Yankee411 (6 doubles, 4 trebles, 1 fourfold)11× / 22×No2Four solid fancies without singles; strong upside if 3–4 land

Strategy tips: Choose a Patent if you value a safety net from singles or your selections include some shorter prices that can grind a return. Opt for a Trixie if you expect at least two legs to win and want a leaner stake. Use a Yankee when you have four selections with attractive prices and are comfortable needing multiple winners to unlock the bigger compounding returns.

Why did the Patent format emerge and replace older permutations?

The Patent gained popularity in UK fixed-odds betting because it adds singles coverage to a three-selection full cover, creating a clearer balance between protection and upside than older no-singles permutations. While Trixies and other perms are still widely available, many recreational bettors prefer Patents because they can get money back with one winner, understand exactly how lines are built, and use straightforward settlement rules across all seven components.

  • Practical protection: Singles reduce the “all or nothing” feel of no-singles perms, smoothing bankroll swings.
  • Straightforward structure: Three selections produce an intuitive 3–3–1 split that is easy to track and audit.
  • Each-way friendly: Duplicates cleanly into win/place parts for racing, keeping settlement consistent.
  • Calculator- and app-ready: Modern tools show line-by-line returns instantly, minimizing disputes.

Trade-offs remain: a Patent requires a higher total stake than a Trixie, and covering singles dilutes peak upside when all legs are big prices. That said, for many bettors the steadier return profile and transparency explain why the Patent became a go-to three-selection format alongside, and often preferred to, older no-singles permutations.

Patent bet calculator for football: how do I enter markets?

The calculator is market-agnostic: it needs an odds price for each selection and its final settlement status (Won, Lost, Void, Dead-Heat). For football, you can enter standard 90-minute markets (match result, BTTS, totals), as well as player and team props. If a leg is later voided (e.g., abandoned match) or adjusted after VAR, record the final official outcome and the calculator will update all seven Patent lines accordingly.

Are BTTS, match result, and player props supported?

Yes. You can include common football markets as individual selections within a Patent. Enter each selection’s odds and, after the event is official, mark the result. The calculator then settles all singles, doubles, and the treble automatically.

Supported examples (as independent selections):

  • Match Result (1X2), Draw No Bet, Double Chance
  • BTTS (Yes/No), Over/Under Goals (e.g., Over 2.5)
  • Player to Score (Anytime/First/Last), Shots on Target, Assists
  • Team totals (goals/corners), Cards markets (player or team), Corners

How to enter:

  • Pick your three markets (e.g., BTTS Yes at 1.95, Home Win at 2.60, Player to Score Anytime at 3.40).
  • Input the odds in your preferred format (fractional, decimal, or American).
  • After settlement, set each leg as Won/Lost/Void (e.g., if VAR cancels a goal, BTTS may become Lost).
  • Correlation note: Some bookmakers restrict multiples built from the same match if selections are related (e.g., Home Win + Player to Score). If you placed a priced “Bet Builder” as a single, treat that as one selection in the calculator, not multiple separate legs.

How are extra time, VAR, and abandoned matches handled?

Football settlement typically follows official match records. Unless the market explicitly includes extra time or penalties, standard “90-minute” markets settle on regulation time plus stoppage time only. VAR decisions are included once the match is completed and the official result is confirmed. Abandoned or postponed matches are governed by bookmaker house rules; if a leg is voided by your bookmaker, mark it as Void so the calculator correctly reduces multiples (e.g., a double with one void becomes a single on the remaining leg).

MarketStandard settlement scopeExtra time/penalties included?VAR impact
Match Result (1X2)90 minutes + stoppage timeNo (separate ET/Pens markets exist)Yes, settled on official final result after VAR
Draw No Bet / Double Chance90 minutes + stoppage timeNoYes
BTTS (Yes/No)90 minutes + stoppage timeNoYes, goals awarded/disallowed by VAR affect outcome
Over/Under Goals90 minutes + stoppage timeNoYes
To Qualify / Lift the TrophyMatch outcome including ET and, if needed, penaltiesYes (by definition)Yes
Player to Score (Anytime)90 minutes + stoppage timeNo (unless explicitly priced for ET)Yes, scorer changes after VAR are reflected
First Goalscorer90 minutes + stoppage time; own goals do not countNoYes, goals re-designated by VAR update settlement
Cards (player/team)90 minutes + stoppage timeNo (unless specified for ET)Yes, disciplinary changes post-VAR are included
Corners (match/team)90 minutes + stoppage timeNoSettled on official stats provider’s record

Abandonment guidance: If a match is abandoned or postponed, bookmakers apply their own house rules by market (e.g., voiding unsettled markets, or settling those already determined). Reflect your bookmaker’s decision for the leg by selecting Won/Lost/Void in the calculator so downstream Patent lines adjust correctly.

Can you mix sports in one Patent (football, tennis, horses)?

Yes. A Patent is defined by the number of selections, not by sport. You can combine, for example, a football BTTS pick, a tennis match winner, and a horse racing selection. The calculator settles each leg according to the status you set for that sport and applies standard multiple rules across the seven Patent lines.

Mixing examples:

  • Leg A: Football BTTS Yes at 1.95 (90-minute settlement).
  • Leg B: Tennis Player A to Win at 1.80 (apply your bookmaker’s retirement rule outcome when settling).
  • Leg C: Horse at 5/1 (6.00) win-only, or each-way with stated place terms (e.g., 1/5 odds, 3 places).

Each-way note:

  • An EW Patent duplicates all seven lines into win and place parts.
  • Non-EW-eligible legs (e.g., football BTTS) will have their place-part lines voided per bookmaker rules; the calculator marks those lines as Void if you set them accordingly.

Sport-specific settlement cautions:

  • Tennis retirements and walkovers: settlement varies by bookmaker and market; record the final leg status (e.g., Void) as per your bookmaker’s rule.
  • Racing Rule 4, dead-heats, and non-runners: apply on affected legs only; the calculator propagates adjustments through all connected lines.
  • Same-event correlation: avoid building separate legs from highly related markets in the same match unless your bookmaker prices them as a single “Bet Builder” selection.

Is there a Patent bet calculator app for iOS and Android?

Yes—while there is no mandatory app store download, you can install the calculator to your home screen as a Progressive Web App (PWA). This gives you an app-like icon, full-screen experience, and offline-style use once the page is cached locally. Your inputs are processed on-device, so you can quickly compute Patent returns even when connectivity is patchy.

How do I add it to my home screen for offline-style use?

Install the calculator as a home screen app to enable fast launch, full-screen layout, and caching for offline-style calculations. The first visit must be online so the app can store essential files locally; after that, most calculations work without a network.

On iOS/iPadOS (Safari):

  • Open the calculator in Safari.
  • Tap the Share icon, then choose “Add to Home Screen.”
  • Confirm the name and tap “Add.” Launch it from the home screen for the full-screen experience.
  • Updates: pull-to-refresh on the home-screen version or open the site in Safari when online to fetch updates.
  • Remove/clear: delete the home-screen icon to remove the app shell; clear website data in Settings → Safari → Advanced → Website Data if you want to wipe cached files.

On Android (Chrome/Edge/Firefox):

  • Open the calculator in your browser.
  • Chrome/Edge (Chromium): you may see an “Install app” prompt; if not, open the browser menu and tap “Install app” or “Add to Home screen.”
  • Firefox for Android: open the menu and choose “Add to Home screen.”
  • Launch from the home screen for full-screen mode and fast local caching.
  • Updates: when online, reopen the app or refresh to fetch the latest version.
  • Remove/clear: long-press the icon to uninstall the shortcut; clear site storage in browser Settings → Site settings → Storage if you want to remove cached data.

Offline-style behaviour:

  • Works after the first successful online load, using a service worker to cache core assets.
  • Live content (e.g., remote odds feeds if you choose to import them) requires connectivity; local calculations on entered odds do not.
  • If storage is purged by the OS (low space) or you manually clear data, you’ll need to reconnect once to recache.

Do you store my stakes or selections, or is it private?

By design, calculations run entirely on your device and do not require an account or cloud sync. Any optional “remember my inputs” feature uses local storage (e.g., localStorage or IndexedDB) in your browser so your stakes and odds remain private to your device. You stay in control of persistence and can clear it at any time.

Default behaviour:

  • No server round-trips are needed to compute returns; maths is executed locally.
  • No inputs are transmitted unless you explicitly use a share/export feature.

Optional persistence:

  • Toggle “remember inputs” to store your latest odds, stakes, and settings locally.
  • Clear storage from the calculator settings or via your browser’s site data controls.

Privacy notes:

  • If you follow external links (e.g., to bookmaker help pages), those sites have their own policies.
  • If your browser or OS syncs browsing data across devices, that is governed by your platform settings, not the calculator.

What accessibility features help power users and screen readers?

The calculator is built to meet common accessibility practices so everyone can compute Patent returns efficiently. It supports keyboard-only workflows, clear focus indicators, and semantic markup for assistive technologies. Screen readers receive live updates when results change, while users sensitive to motion can opt for reduced animations.

Screen reader support:

  • Form controls have associated labels and descriptions.
  • Results area uses an aria-live region to announce recalculated totals.
  • Headings provide a logical outline for quick navigation.

Keyboard and power-user features:

  • Logical Tab order; Esc closes modals; Enter triggers calculate; Arrow keys adjust numeric inputs where appropriate.
  • Skip-to-content link at the top for fast access to inputs.

Visual clarity:

  • High-contrast theme options meet commonly referenced contrast targets for text and controls.
  • Respects user preferences such as prefers-reduced-motion to minimise animation.
  • Responsive layout ensures large touch targets on mobile.

Error prevention and feedback:

  • Inline validation with clear text, not just colour, to indicate issues.
  • Descriptive error summaries are linked to the affected fields.

Which Patent bet calculator is best in the UK in 2026?

The best Patent bet calculators for UK users in 2026 combine accurate UK settlement logic (Rule 4, dead-heats, voids), full cover across all seven lines, fast mobile performance, privacy-by-default, and clear line-by-line breakdowns. Established UK tools (for example, AceOdds and Free Bet Calculator) are highly accurate; differentiation often comes from user experience, PWA/offline support, privacy, exportability, and accessibility. Always verify an operator’s or calculator publisher’s compliance, and prefer tools that explain their settlement math transparently.

How do speed, accuracy, and coverage compare to alternatives?

Across leading UK calculators, settlement logic is broadly aligned with industry standards. Practical differences are feature depth, offline/app-like use, and the clarity of per-line reporting. The table contrasts an industry baseline with a privacy-first, PWA-enabled calculator.

DimensionIndustry baseline (leading UK calculators)This calculator (2026)

Odds formatsFractional, Decimal (American varies)Fractional, Decimal, American
Per-line breakdown and totalsCommonAlways on; exportable summary
PWA/home-screen installVaries by siteAvailable (offline-style caching)
PrivacyVaries (some use analytics)On-device only; optional local persistence
AccessibilityCore supportARIA live results, keyboard-first, high contrast

Reference points: Well-known UK calculators such as [AceOdds](https://www.aceodds.com) and [Free Bet Calculator](https://www.free-bet-calculator.co.uk) offer robust Patent, EW, Rule 4, and dead-heat handling. If you need app-like use, look for explicit PWA support; if you need auditability, prefer calculators that show line-by-line math and let you export or copy results for records or disputes (e.g., with ADR providers such as [IBAS](https://www.ibas-uk.com)).

When does a Patent underperform: what’s the strongest counterargument?

The strongest argument against using a Patent is that you “pay for protection” via singles. In scenarios where you expect multiple legs to land (and especially when prices are bigger), a no-singles perm like a Trixie concentrates stake into doubles/treble and can yield higher returns for the same total outlay.

Two-winner scenario (equal total stakes):

  • Setup: A=3.00 (2/1), B=3.50 (5/2), C=2.00 (Evs). A and B win; C loses. Total outlay £14.
  • Patent (£2 unit over 7 lines): returns £34.00 (AB double + A and B singles); profit £20.00.
  • Trixie (£3.50 unit over 4 lines): only AB double wins → £3.50 × (3.00 × 3.50) = £36.75; profit £22.75.

All-three-winners (equal total stakes):

  • Patent (£14 total): returns £106.00.
  • Trixie (£14 total): returns £36.75 + £21.00 + £24.50 + £73.50 = £155.75.

Trade-off summary:

  • Patent smooths variance with singles and can return with one winner.
  • Trixie amplifies upside when 2–3 win but returns nothing on one-winner days.
  • Choose Patent for protection and clearer cash-back potential; choose Trixie when you’re targeting clustered winners and can tolerate dry spells.

What should UK bettors know about limits, taxes, and 1xBet?

For UK residents, consumer protection, taxation, and operator licensing shape where and how you should bet. Your winnings are not taxed in the UK for recreational bettors; operators pay duties to HMRC. Always use UK-licensed bookmakers and verify license status before depositing.

Limits and maximum payouts:

  • Bookmakers set per-bet, per-event, and per-sport maximum payouts; multiples may be capped separately from singles.
  • Stake limits and related-selection rules can restrict combining markets from the same event unless priced as a single “Bet Builder.”
  • Affordability/safety checks and staking controls (deposit limits, time-outs, self-exclusion) are provided by UK-licensed firms under UKGC oversight.

Taxes and duties (UK):

  • Player winnings: 0% tax for individuals (recreational betting).
  • Operator duties: General Betting Duty and Pool Betting Duty apply to bookmakers; Remote Gaming Duty applies to casino-style gaming. See HMRC guidance: [HMRC: Gambling duties](https://www.gov.uk/guidance/gambling-duties).

Licensing and 1xBet:

  • Only bet with operators licensed by the UK Gambling Commission; check a firm’s status on the public register: [UKGC Public Register](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk/public-register).
  • As of 2024, 1xBet does not hold a UKGC licence. UK consumers using unlicensed sites are not protected by UK consumer safeguards or UKGC dispute/AML standards.
  • For disputes with UK-licensed operators, independent adjudication is available via [IBAS](https://www.ibas-uk.com).

Bottom line for UK bettors: use UKGC-licensed brands, understand your bookmaker’s max-payout tables and multiple rules, keep records of your bets and calculator outputs, and know that your personal winnings are not taxed—operators pay duties at source.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I calculate a Patent bet with a free bet (stake not returned)?

Treat each of the seven lines as an SNR bet where the stake is not returned, so a line with decimal odds D returns u × (D − 1) instead of u × D. For a full Patent with all three winners at 3.00, 3.50, and 2.00 and unit stake u = 1 from a free bet, the total is (2 + 2.5 + 1) for the three singles, plus (3×3.5 − 1) + (3×2 − 1) + (3.5×2 − 1) for the three doubles, plus (3×3.5×2 − 1) for the treble, which sums to £46.00. With a cash stake the same slip returns £53.00, so the SNR reduces the overall by exactly £7.00, the sum of the seven line-stakes.

Can I mix a cash stake and a free bet in one Patent?

Yes, split the bet into two Patents with proportional unit stakes and add the results. For example, a £10 total outlay made up of £3 cash and a £7 SNR free bet becomes a cash unit of £3 ÷ 7 ≈ £0.428571 per line and a free-bet unit of £7 ÷ 7 = £1 per line; if all three selections at 3.00, 3.50, and 2.00 win, the cash part returns 0.428571 × £53 ≈ £22.71 and the SNR part returns 1 × £46 = £46.00, for a combined £68.71 before per-line rounding.

How are rounding differences handled on Patent returns?

UK settlement rounds each completed line to the nearest penny, then sums, which can differ from rounding once at the end. The maximum discrepancy versus single total rounding is bounded by the number of active lines, typically up to £0.07 on a win-only Patent or £0.14 on an each-way Patent; this comes from at most one penny of rounding variance per line across 7 or 14 lines.

What odds or hit-rate are needed to break even on a Patent?

With equal decimal odds d and independent per-leg win probability p, the expected return is 3pd + 3p²d² + p³d³ and break-even occurs when this equals 7. At fair odds (p = 1/d), pd = 1 and the expected return is exactly 7, so the expected profit is zero; scenario break-evens are d ≥ 7.00 with exactly one winner and d ≥ about 1.83 with exactly two winners since 2d + d² ≥ 7.

How can I back-solve the unit stake to hit a target return if all three win?

Divide the target by the sum of all seven decimal products. For selections with decimals d1, d2, d3, the unit stake u equals Target ÷ (d1 + d2 + d3 + d1d2 + d1d3 + d2d3 + d1d2d3); for 3.00, 3.50, and 2.00 this denominator is 53, so a £100 target implies u = £100 ÷ 53 ≈ £1.8868 and a total stake of about £13.21.

How do handicap or totals pushes affect a Patent?

A push settles as void, which returns the stake on that line and reduces any multiple containing the leg by one selection. For example, if leg A is a −1.0 handicap push, all A singles return stake only, AB and AC doubles become singles on B and C, and the ABC treble becomes the BC double; you should mark the pushed leg as Void so the calculator cascades the reduction through all seven lines.

Do place-term changes after non-runners affect an each-way Patent, and how should I enter them?

Yes, revised place terms change both the number of places paid and sometimes the place fraction, which can turn a previously paid place into a loser. A common case is 8 runners at 1/5 odds 3 places becoming 7 runners at 1/5 odds 2 places; a selection finishing 3rd would now lose on the place part, so enter the final official place terms and the finishing position to settle each place line correctly.

Can I apply commission or boosts to a Patent, and how do I model them?

Apply boosts by increasing the affected leg’s decimal odds or the final multiple’s decimal product by the stated percentage, while commission reduces winnings only. For three legs each at 3.00 with unit 1, the cash return is £63 and winnings are £56; a 5% commission gives £56 × 0.95 = £53.20 in winnings and a £60.20 total return, whereas a 10% treble-only boost multiplies the treble from 27.00 to 29.70, lifting the total from £63.00 to £65.70.

Does a Patent support banker selections or variable stakes per line?

No, a true Patent uses equal unit stakes across 3 singles, 3 doubles, and 1 treble and has no banker concept. If you want different exposure, place three separate bets instead: one on singles, one on doubles, and one on the treble with the individual stakes you want; for instance, £2 on each single, £1 on each double, and £0.50 on the treble mimics a weighted permutation rather than a Patent.

How do Best Odds Guaranteed (BOG) and odds boosts change Patent settlement?

BOG replaces the taken price with the higher SP for winning racing legs on both singles and multiples that include that leg, increasing every affected line by the same multiplier. If a leg taken at 5.00 returns at 6.50 SP, its effective decimal becomes 6.50, so any line containing it rises by 30% for that leg’s factor; for example, a double AB increases from 5.00×B to 6.50×B, and the treble ABC increases by the same 30% on that leg’s contribution.

How do multiple Rule 4 deductions apply across a Patent?

Convert each affected leg’s decimal D to an adjusted D′ = 1 + (D − 1) × Π(1 − r_i), where r_i are the sequential deductions, then use D′ everywhere that leg appears. A 10p and 15p Rule 4 on a 5.00 leg gives D′ = 1 + 4 × 0.90 × 0.85 = 4.06; paired with an unaffected 3.50 leg, the double’s decimal becomes 4.06 × 3.50 = 14.21, so a £1 unit returns £14.21 on that line.

How is a place dead-heat settled in an each-way Patent with numbers?

Divide the place stake by the number of tied runners across the last paying place, then settle at full place odds. With 1/5 place terms and 8 runners paying 3 places, if three horses dead-heat for third, the effective stake fraction is 1 ÷ 3; a £10 each-way at 6/1 has a £10 place part settled at 6/5 decimal 2.20, so the place return is (£10 × 1/3) × 2.20 = £7.33, while the remaining two-thirds of the place stake is lost.

Can a calculator replicate cash-out values on a Patent?

No, cash out uses a bookmaker’s proprietary model with margin, volatility, and hedging inputs, so it cannot be derived from public odds alone. If you cashed out the entire Patent, use the bookmaker’s cash-out figure as the final result; if you partially cashed out a component, treat the cashed line as a fixed settled return and remove its future correlation, because remaining lines no longer follow standard multiple math.

Does currency or odds precision change Patent results?

The math is currency-agnostic; only rounding rules change the pennies. Using two-decimal rounding per line, switching from pounds to euros or setting decimal odds to three or four places alters only the last cent or penny and typically shifts a win-only Patent by no more than ±0.07 in the settlement currency due to per-line rounding accumulation.

yankee.intro: Calculate Yankee returns, profit, and total stakes in seconds. Supports fractional or decimal odds, Each Way, Rule 4, dead-heats, and void legs. Ideal for football and Irish Lottery bets, with clear examples and guidance on 50p or £1 stakes.
