Bet type Count in a Yankee Notes Doubles 6 All 2-selection combinations from 4 picks (C(4,2) = 6) Trebles 4 All 3-selection combinations from 4 picks (C(4,3) = 4) Fourfold 1 Accumulator of all 4 selections Total bets 11 Win-only; Each Way doubles to 22 parts (win + place)
yankee.articleHtml:
How do I calculate Yankee returns and stakes?

A Yankee is a 4-selection multiple containing 11 bets: 6 doubles, 4 trebles, and 1 fourfold accumulator. There are no singles. To calculate returns, the calculator multiplies the decimal odds of each winning leg across every winning combination, then sums the returns from all winning doubles, trebles, and the fourfold. Total stake equals unit stake multiplied by the number of bets (11 for win-only; 22 for each way). Each way (EW) Yankees split the unit stake into two equal parts (win and place) and settle each part separately using the relevant odds/terms.

Bet typeCount in a YankeeNotes
Doubles6All 2-selection combinations from 4 picks (C(4,2) = 6)
Trebles4All 3-selection combinations from 4 picks (C(4,3) = 4)
Fourfold1Accumulator of all 4 selections
Total bets11Win-only; Each Way doubles to 22 parts (win + place)

What inputs does the calculator need?

Enter one set of stake settings and four selection lines. The calculator uses these to build and settle all 11 component bets automatically.

Stake settings:

  • Stake mode: Stake per bet (unit stake) or Total stake (the tool will back-calculate the unit stake).
  • Amount: e.g., £0.50, £1, £2.
  • Each Way toggle: On/Off. When On, the calculator creates two parts (win and place) for every component bet.

Odds and format:

  • Odds format: Fractional (e.g., 5/2), Decimal (e.g., 3.50). Many tools also accept American, but UK Yankees are typically fractional or decimal.
  • Per selection odds entry: one price per selection; for EW, place odds are derived from place terms.

Result per selection:

  • Win, Lose, Place (for EW markets), Void/Non-runner.
  • Dead-heat factor (if applicable): e.g., 1/2 for a two-way tie, 1/3 for a three-way tie.

Each Way (if EW is On):

  • Place terms: e.g., 1/5 or 1/4 the odds, and number of places paid (race/market dependent).

Optional settlement adjustments:

  • Rule 4 deduction per affected selection (horse/greyhound racing): pence-in-the-pound deduction applied to winnings if a competitor is withdrawn after betting.
  • Commission (exchange bets only, if supported): percentage applied to winnings.

Step-by-step: enter odds, stake, each way

Follow these steps to produce accurate Yankee returns. The same process works for football, horse racing, and other sports; for Each Way, ensure the market actually supports EW (commonly racing).

  • Select odds format (Fractional or Decimal).

Choose stake mode:

  • Stake per bet: enter the unit stake (e.g., £0.50). Total stake = unit stake × 11 (win-only) or × 22 (EW).
  • Total stake: enter the overall amount (e.g., £11) and let the calculator infer the unit stake.
  • Toggle Each Way if required, then set place terms (e.g., 1/5, places 1–3).
  • Enter odds for each of the 4 selections.
  • Set settlement outcomes for each selection (Win/Lose/Place/Void). Add any Rule 4 deduction and dead-heat splits if relevant.

Calculate to view:

  • Returns and profit per component (doubles, trebles, fourfold) and in total.
  • Stake breakdown and EW split (if enabled).
Common stake setupsWin-only total stakeEach Way total stake
£0.50 unit stake£5.50 (11 × £0.50)£11.00 (22 × £0.50)
£1.00 unit stake£11.00 (11 × £1.00)£22.00 (22 × £1.00)

Worked example (win-only, ignoring Rule 4/dead-heats/voids): Selections A 2/1 (3.00), B 5/2 (3.50), C 1/2 (1.50), D 3/1 (4.00). Unit stake £0.50. Winning combos are AB, AD, BD (doubles) and ABD (treble). Returns: AB double = £0.50 × 3.00 × 3.50 = £5.25; AD double = £0.50 × 3.00 × 4.00 = £6.00; BD double = £0.50 × 3.50 × 4.00 = £7.00; ABD treble = £0.50 × 3.00 × 3.50 × 4.00 = £21.00. Total return = £39.25. Total stake = £5.50. Profit = £33.75.

Rule 4, dead-heat, void legs: how are they handled?

The calculator applies industry-standard settlement logic used by UK bookmakers so your Yankee reflects the same adjustments you would see on your bet slip.

Rule 4 (racing withdrawals):

  • Applied as a pence-in-the-pound deduction to the winnings part of affected selections. The stake is not reduced.
  • Implementation: Adjust the winnings portion by the deduction rate, then add back the original stake. Example: £1 at 2/1 (decimal 3.00) returns £3.00 without deductions. With a 10p Rule 4, the winnings (£2.00) are reduced by 10% to £1.80, so return becomes £1.00 + £1.80 = £2.80.
  • Multiple withdrawals can lead to multiple deductions; calculators apply them sequentially to the winnings component.
  • Rule 4 can affect both win and place parts in EW bets if the withdrawn runner changes the market terms.

Dead-heats:

  • Win market: Divide the win stake by the number of tied competitors, settle one part as a full winner at the original odds, and treat the remainder as lost. Example: £1 at 3/1 in a two-way dead-heat returns £0.50 × (1 + 3) = £2.00.
  • Each Way place market: If the dead-heat extends the number of placed horses beyond places paid, the place stake is split by the number of tied runners occupying the final paid place(s). Example: £1 EW, 3/1, 1/5 terms; two-way dead-heat for the last paid place settles the place stake as £0.50 at place decimal 1.60, returning £0.80.
  • Dead-heat factors propagate through all affected combinations inside the Yankee (doubles, trebles, fourfold) that include the tied selection.

Void legs / Non-runners:

  • Avoided selection is settled at odds of 1.00 (stake returned) within any combination; the combination’s fold reduces by one. Example: a double with one void leg settles as a single on the remaining selection.

In a Yankee, combinations containing the void selection are downgraded:

  • Affected doubles become singles on the other selection.
  • Affected trebles become doubles on the remaining two selections.
  • The fourfold becomes a treble on the other three selections.
  • If all legs in a component bet are void, that component returns the stake for that component.

Notes: Each Way is typically offered on racing and selected markets only; football Yankees are usually win-only. Rule 4 does not apply to fixed-odds lotteries (e.g., Irish Lottery) but dead-heat and void logic still follow operator rules. Always check the bookmaker’s published settlement terms for the specific market.

Which sports, formats, and stake sizes does it support?

The Yankee bet calculator supports win-only multiples across most sports (e.g., football, tennis, golf) and Each Way (EW) where EW terms exist (primarily horse racing and, with some firms, greyhounds). It also supports fixed-odds number betting such as the Irish Lottery (win-only). You can enter fractional or decimal odds, set settlement outcomes (win/lose/place/void), and apply adjustments (e.g., Rule 4 for racing). Common unit stakes include £0.50 and £1.00, but any valid currency amount is supported; the calculator scales totals across the 11 component bets of a Yankee.

Football Yankee: practical setup and tips

A football Yankee is typically win-only (no EW). Standard football settlement is 90 minutes plus injury time (extra time and penalties do not count unless explicitly stated by the bookmaker). To build a sound four-selection Yankee across football matches, ensure each selection is from a different game or a non-related market to avoid related-contingency restrictions.

Practical setup:

  • Select four independent markets (ideally from four different matches), e.g., Match Result (1X2), Draw No Bet (DNB), Both Teams To Score (BTTS), Over/Under Goals.
  • Confirm settlement scope: 90 mins only (most markets) unless the market title explicitly references Extra Time or Penalties.
  • Enter odds for each selection (fractional or decimal) and set outcomes when known (Win/Lose/Void).
  • If a DNB leg ends in a draw, it is typically settled “void” by bookmakers; in a Yankee, combinations including that leg are downgraded (e.g., doubles become singles).

Compliance and correlation:

  • Avoid same-game related picks (e.g., Home Win and Home -1 Handicap in the same fixture) unless your bookmaker explicitly allows a same-game multi; these are usually not permitted inside standard Yankees.
  • Player props (goals/cards/shots) are often restricted from cross-match multiples at many firms or must be built via a dedicated bet-builder; check operator rules.

Risk control:

  • Spread selections across leagues and kick-off times to reduce postponement/abandonment correlation.
  • Track “void” rules for postponements (operator-specific) and remember that any void football leg becomes a 1.00 multiplier inside each affected combination in the Yankee.

Irish Lottery Yankee: numbers, odds, and limits

Irish Lottery “numbers betting” with UK bookmakers is fixed-odds betting on outcomes of the Irish Lotto draws. The Irish Lotto main draw selects 6 numbers from 1–47 (plus a Bonus Ball drawn from the same pool); bookmakers price specific number outcomes (e.g., “one number to be drawn”), and you can place multiples across different draws when allowed. Yankees on Irish Lottery bets are win-only (no EW) and do not use Rule 4 deductions.

Numbers and markets:

  • Number range: 1–47. Bookmakers typically offer “1 number to be drawn,” “2 numbers to be drawn,” etc., as fixed-odds selections.
  • Multiple rules: Most bookmakers require multiples to be placed across different draws to avoid related-contingency issues; same-draw selections are often restricted from being combined.

Odds handling:

  • Odds are fixed by each bookmaker and shown at selection time; enter the quoted price into the calculator. Odds can differ between operators and may change between draws.
  • No EW and no Rule 4. Dead-heat concepts do not apply in standard number betting because the draw outcomes are discrete and fully defined.

Limits and settlement:

  • Stake limits and maximum payout caps are operator-specific; always check your bookmaker’s published limits before staking.
  • If a draw is canceled or declared void per operator rules, affected legs in the Yankee are settled as void and combinations are downgraded accordingly.

Practical tip:

  • Pick four independent selections across different draw times/days (e.g., lunchtime/teatime or different dates) to ensure your Yankee is accepted and to avoid correlation blocks.

How much is a 50p or £1 Yankee today?

The cost of a Yankee is determined by the unit stake multiplied by the number of component bets. A win-only Yankee contains 11 bets; an Each Way Yankee contains 22 parts (win and place). Therefore, the total outlay scales linearly with your unit stake.

Unit stakeNumber of bets (win-only)Total stake (win-only)Number of parts (Each Way)Total stake (Each Way)
£0.5011£5.5022£11.00
£1.0011£11.0022£22.00

Notes: Football and Irish Lottery Yankees are typically win-only, so the relevant total stake is 11 × unit stake. If your calculator has a “Total Stake” input mode, it back-solves the unit stake by dividing by 11 (win-only) or 22 (EW). Always confirm whether EW is genuinely available for your sport/market before toggling EW in the calculator.

Yankee vs alternatives: what should you pick?

All three multiples—Yankee, Lucky 15, and Super Yankee (Canadian)—combine your selections into many smaller bets to spread risk and amplify returns when several legs win. The key differences are whether singles are included, how many combinations are created, how much your total stake grows per unit stake, and how many winners are needed for any payout. Choosing between them is mainly about variance tolerance and bankroll planning.

Yankee or Lucky 15: which suits your risk profile?

A Yankee (4 selections, 11 bets) excludes singles, so you typically need at least two winners for any return. A Lucky 15 (4 selections, 15 bets) adds four singles, which cushions variance because one winner can still generate a payout, but it also increases total stake by 36% versus a Yankee at the same unit stake. If you prefer a steadier return profile and are willing to pay more stake for that stability, Lucky 15 fits better; if you want a leaner stake with higher concentration on multiples, choose a Yankee.

Bet typeSelectionsSingles includedDoublesTreblesFourfoldsFivefoldsTotal bets (win-only)EW parts totalMin. winners for any returnTotal stake at £1 unit (win-only)
Yankee4No641011222£11
Lucky 154Yes (4)641015301£15
Super Yankee (Canadian)5No10105126522£26

Notes: Some bookmakers offer settlement bonuses on Lucky 15s (e.g., “one-winner” or “all-correct” bonuses), but terms vary by operator and are not universal. Use calculator toggles only if your bookmaker’s exact bonus terms are supported and enabled; otherwise, settle at standard odds.

  • Pick a Yankee if you’re comfortable needing two or more winners and want lower total stake versus a Lucky 15.
  • Pick a Lucky 15 if you value singles as variance control and accept the higher outlay.
  • Fix your total budget first; then choose the bet type and unit stake so the combined outlay fits your bankroll.

Super Yankee/Canadian: how do 26 bets change returns?

A Super Yankee (also called a Canadian) uses 5 selections to create 26 bets: 10 doubles, 10 trebles, 5 fourfolds, and 1 fivefold. Compared with a Yankee, the Canadian increases both coverage and stake. With more combinations, partial success (e.g., two or three winners) still returns something via the many doubles and trebles, but your stake outlay rises proportionally (26 × unit stake for win-only; 52 × for Each Way).

Combination coverage by number of winners among 5 selections:

  • Exactly 2 winners: C(2,2) = 1 winning double.
  • Exactly 3 winners: C(3,2) = 3 winning doubles; C(3,3) = 1 winning treble.
  • Exactly 4 winners: C(4,2) = 6 winning doubles; C(4,3) = 4 winning trebles; C(4,4) = 1 winning fourfold.
  • All 5 winners: 10 winning doubles; 10 winning trebles; 5 winning fourfolds; 1 winning fivefold.

Stake planning:

  • At £1 unit stake, a Canadian costs £26 (win-only) or £52 (EW). Adjust unit stake down if you need to match a fixed total budget.
  • Voids reduce fold size within affected combinations (e.g., a treble with one void becomes a double).

Use cases:

  • Five correlated events are generally not allowed; choose independent markets across different matches/races to comply with bookmaker rules.
  • Useful when you expect several selections to win but want more ways to cash even if one or two lose.

Best Yankee bet calculator: what features matter in 2026?

Accuracy and transparency are critical. A modern calculator should mirror UK settlement rules and clearly show how every component bet is built, priced, and adjusted. The following capabilities help ensure reliable results and an efficient workflow in 2026.

Core settlement accuracy:

  • Support for fractional and decimal odds with precise conversion and rounding to the nearest penny.
  • Each Way handling with configurable place terms (e.g., 1/5, 1/4) and number of places.
  • Dead-heat settlement (stake split by number of ties) for win and place parts.
  • Rule 4 deductions applied to winnings components, including sequential deductions when multiple withdrawals occur.
  • Void/non-runner logic that automatically reduces fold size within affected combinations.

Input and validation:

  • Stake per bet vs total stake modes, with back-solved unit stake and clear stake breakdown across 11 (Yankee), 15 (Lucky 15), or 26 (Canadian) bets.
  • Outcome selectors (Win/Lose/Place/Void) per leg and optional dead-heat factors.
  • Safeguards against related contingencies (warnings for same-game conflicts in football or same-draw lottery multiples where prohibited).

Transparency and reporting:

  • Component-level ledger showing returns from each double, treble, and accumulator, with subtotals and overall totals.
  • Rounding policy disclosure (component-level vs aggregate rounding) and calculation formulae references.
  • Export/share options (CSV/JSON) and shareable links to replicate inputs.

UK context and compliance:

  • GBP currency formatting, 90-minute football settlement notes, and guidance on EW availability (primarily racing).
  • Irish Lottery number-betting support (win-only; no Rule 4) with draw identifiers and operator-style market labels.

Performance and accessibility:

  • Fast, client-side calculation with offline-capable progressive web app behaviour.
  • Mobile-first numeric inputs, keyboard navigation, and accessibility aligned to WCAG guidance.

Advanced options (if supported by your bookmaker’s terms):

  • Optional toggles for bookmaker-specific Lucky 15 bonuses and EW place term variations (documented and user-controlled).
  • Commission input for exchange scenarios and per-leg settlement notes for auditability.

How do you place a Yankee with UK bookmakers?

UK bookmakers let you place a Yankee on desktop sites, mobile websites, and native apps. The flow is the same: add four independent selections to your bet slip, open the Multiples/Full Cover section, choose “Yankee (11 bets)”, set your unit stake (or total stake), optionally toggle Each Way (if the market supports EW), and confirm. Always check market settlement rules (e.g., football is usually 90 minutes only) and that your operator is licensed in Great Britain by the UK Gambling Commission. You can verify licences on the UKGC public register at [https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk/public-register](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk/public-register).

Bookie interfaces: desktop, mobile site, and app

While layouts differ by brand, the core controls are consistent across devices. Look for “Multiples”, “Full cover”, or a dedicated “Yankee” option once four selections are on your slip. Verify the bet count (11 for win-only; 22 parts for EW) before you place the bet.

Desktop:

  • Navigation: Sportsbook → Markets → add 4 selections → Bet Slip → Multiples/Full Cover → Yankee.
  • Controls: Stake per bet vs Total stake, EW toggle (if offered), odds format switch (Fractional/Decimal).
  • Audit: Expand component list to preview doubles, trebles, and the fourfold if the site supports breakdown previews.

Mobile site:

  • Navigation: Hamburger menu → Sport/Competition → add 4 selections → Floating Bet Slip → Multiples tab → Yankee.
  • Controls: Numeric keypad for stake entry, quick toggles (EW, odds format) optimised for touch.
  • Checks: Ensure no related-contingency conflicts (same-game combos often blocked in standard Yankees).

App:

  • Navigation: Home → Sport → Markets → add 4 selections → Bet Slip → Yankee.
  • Extras: Biometric login, saved payment methods, and stake presets; some apps remember your last odds format.
  • Compliance: KYC may be required before bet placement; deposit and loss limits can be managed in-app. For dispute resolution guidance, see the Independent Betting Adjudication Service (IBAS) at [https://www.ibas-uk.com](https://www.ibas-uk.com).

Yankee bet example: four selections, settlement walkthrough

Example (win-only, football 90-minute markets): Four selections with decimal odds A 2.50, B 1.80, C 3.20, D 2.10. Outcomes: A Win, B Win, C Lose, D Win. Unit stake £1 per bet (total stake £11). Winning combinations are AB, AD, BD (doubles) and ABD (treble). Returns are the product of the odds in each winning combination multiplied by the unit stake.

ComponentSelectionsDecimal multiplierUnit stakeReturn
DoubleAB2.50 × 1.80 = 4.50£1.00£4.50
DoubleAD2.50 × 2.10 = 5.25£1.00£5.25
DoubleBD1.80 × 2.10 = 3.78£1.00£3.78
TrebleABD2.50 × 1.80 × 2.10 = 9.45£1.00£9.45
Total return£22.98
Total stake (11 × £1)£11.00
Profit£11.98

Notes: If a leg is void (e.g., match postponed), combinations including that leg reduce by one fold (a treble with one void becomes a double). UK bookmakers round to the nearest penny at settlement; calculators should mirror this and disclose whether rounding occurs at component or aggregate level.

Each way Yankees: when does EW make sense?

Each Way (EW) Yankees are primarily for horse racing (and sometimes greyhounds) where place terms apply. An EW Yankee doubles the number of parts (win and place), so the total outlay is 22 × unit stake. EW can reduce variance when selections are at bigger prices and you believe they can finish in the places even if they may not win. Always use the exact place terms shown on the race (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4 the odds, with the stated number of places), which are set by the bookmaker for that market.

When EW may be appropriate:

  • Competitive fields where your selections have strong place chances at double-digit fractional odds.
  • Handicap races with more places paid (per the race card terms) versus small fields that are win-only.
  • When you prefer smoothing returns across the 22 parts rather than concentrating solely on win lines.

Operational tips:

  • Confirm that EW is offered on the race; small fields are often win-only.
  • Enter the declared place fraction (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4) and the number of places in the calculator so the place part prices correctly.
  • Remember that Rule 4 deductions (racing withdrawals) can apply to both win and place parts if the market terms are affected.

Responsible betting: set deposit and loss limits with your operator, and verify licence status on the UKGC public register at [https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk/public-register](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk/public-register). If you cannot resolve a dispute with a licensed operator, you can seek guidance from IBAS at [https://www.ibas-uk.com](https://www.ibas-uk.com).

How did Yankee calculators evolve—and what’s the catch?

Yankee calculators have moved from pen-and-paper and bookmaker settlement tables to precise, real-time tools that mirror UK settlement logic (including Each Way, Rule 4 deductions, dead-heats, and voids). This evolution reduced arithmetic errors and made complex multiples easier to plan. The catch is that frictionless calculation can create false confidence, potentially leading to oversized stakes, overlooking bookmaker restrictions (e.g., related contingencies), or ignoring variance. The best use of a calculator is to verify maths, clarify total outlay and liability, and pressure-test scenarios—then size stakes prudently.

From pen-and-paper to live calculators: a brief history

Before online tools, punters combined odds manually using fractional-to-decimal conversions and combination counts (C(n,k)). Early spreadsheets automated multiplication but still required careful input. Modern calculators model full-cover multiples, place terms, Rule 4, dead-heats, and void logic in one view, with transparent component-by-component results and rounding to the penny. Below is a high-level, factual timeline of capabilities.

PeriodTypical toolsCapabilities addedCommon limitations
Pre-2000sPen-and-paper, pocket calculators, printed settlement tablesManual combination counts, basic returns for singles/doubles/accasTime-consuming, error-prone; minimal support for dead-heats/Rule 4
2000sDesktop spreadsheets and early web calculatorsAutomated combination building for Yankees, Lucky 15s, etc.Limited EW handling; inconsistent rounding; little transparency per component
2010sResponsive web tools, mobile-friendly interfacesEW place terms (1/5, 1/4), dead-heat factors, void downgrades, basic Rule 4Inconsistent support for sequential Rule 4; sparse audit trails/export
2020s–presentReal-time calculators, progressive web appsFractional/decimal parity, per-leg outcomes, sequential Rule 4 on winnings, component ledgers, share/exportUser oversight still needed for related-contingency bans and operator-specific limits

The other side of the coin: can tools encourage overbetting?

Yes—by removing friction and presenting neat totals, calculators can nudge users toward higher stakes or more selections than their bankroll and variance tolerance justify. They may also create an illusion of “edge” where none exists if users conflate accurate arithmetic with positive expected value. Typical failure modes include misreading “stake per bet” versus “total stake,” ignoring correlation bans, and chasing losses after quick recalculations. Countermeasures are straightforward and evidence-based.

Build productive friction:

  • Use pre-set daily/weekly budgets and reality checks. UK-licensed operators offer deposit and loss limits; independent help is available from [https://www.gamcare.org.uk](https://www.gamcare.org.uk) and [https://www.begambleaware.org](https://www.begambleaware.org).
  • Treat the calculator as a verifier, not a green light. Verify outlay/liability before considering the odds merit.

Separate maths from value:

  • Accurate returns do not imply an edge. Expected value depends on true probabilities versus odds—not on the neatness of the calculation.
  • Avoid adding legs solely to “boost returns”; more legs increase variance and the chance of zero payout.

Prevent input traps:

  • Label and double-check Stake per bet vs Total stake; confirm EW doubles your outlay (22 parts for a Yankee).
  • Run a worst-case scenario (e.g., only two winners) to see potential drawdowns before staking.

Bankroll and liability: how to set sensible stakes

Define liability first, then back-solve the unit stake. For a Yankee, liability equals unit stake × 11 (win-only) or × 22 (EW). If you cap total outlay per bet to a fixed percentage of bankroll, you can size stakes consistently regardless of odds. The table below shows how to translate a budget into a unit stake, with rounding notes.

Example bankrollRisk budget per multipleBet typeParts (win-only / EW)Target unit stake (win-only)Target unit stake (EW)Notes
£3001% = £3.00Yankee11 / 22£3.00 ÷ 11 ≈ £0.27 (total ≈ £2.97)£3.00 ÷ 22 ≈ £0.14 (total ≈ £3.08)Round down to stay under budget (e.g., £0.13 → £2.86 EW)
£5001% = £5.00Yankee11 / 22£5.00 ÷ 11 ≈ £0.45 (total ≈ £4.95)£5.00 ÷ 22 ≈ £0.23 (total ≈ £5.06)Round to nearest penny; monitor cumulative daily outlay
£5001% = £5.00Lucky 1515 / 30£5.00 ÷ 15 ≈ £0.33 (total ≈ £4.95)£5.00 ÷ 30 ≈ £0.17 (total ≈ £5.10)Singles add stability but increase outlay
£5001% = £5.00Super Yankee (Canadian)26 / 52£5.00 ÷ 26 ≈ £0.19 (total ≈ £4.94)£5.00 ÷ 52 ≈ £0.10 (total ≈ £5.20)High coverage; ensure stake fits limits

Operational guidance:

  • Keep a consistent percentage-of-bankroll budget per multiple (e.g., 0.5%–2%). Higher variance structures (Yankee/Canadian) favour the lower end of that range.
  • Record every bet’s total outlay, not just unit stake, to prevent accidental oversizing.
  • Rebase stake sizes after meaningful bankroll changes; round down to the penny to avoid creep.
  • Use operator tools such as deposit limits, time-outs, and reality checks; independent support is available via [https://www.gamcare.org.uk](https://www.gamcare.org.uk) and [https://www.begambleaware.org](https://www.begambleaware.org).

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the break‑even odds for a Yankee with exactly two, three, or four winners?

Two winners must average about 3.317 decimal each to cover the 11-unit outlay; three winners break even around 1.555 decimal; four winners profit at any odds above 1.000. This comes from solving s·d^2 = 11s for two winners (d ≈ √11 = 3.317), s·(3d^2 + d^3) = 11s for three winners (d ≈ 1.555), and s·(6d^2 + 4d^3 + d^4) = 11s for four winners (d = 1.000), where d is the common decimal price and s is unit stake.

How do I convert fractional odds and EW place terms to decimal in a Yankee?

Use decimal = 1 + (numerator ÷ denominator), and for places use place‑decimal = 1 + (numerator ÷ denominator × place‑fraction). For example, 10/3 becomes 4.3333; under 1/5 EW terms the place price is 1 + (10/3 × 1/5) = 1.6667; under 1/4 terms it is 1 + (10/3 × 1/4) = 1.8333.

Will rounding per component change my total Yankee return?

Yes—penny rounding per double/treble/fourfold can move the total by a few pence on small stakes. Example: six identical £0.10 doubles at 1.83 × 1.83 return £0.33489 each; rounding per component pays 6 × £0.33 = £1.98, while aggregating first gives £2.00934 → £2.01, a 3p difference.

How is a Rule 4 deduction applied inside a multiple?

Adjust the affected leg’s decimal to d′ = 1 + (d − 1) × (1 − r), where r is the Rule 4 rate (e.g., 0.10 for 10p). For instance, a 4.00 leg with a 20p Rule 4 becomes 1 + 3 × 0.8 = 3.40; in a £1 double with an unchanged 3.00 partner the return is £1 × 3.40 × 3.00 = £10.20 instead of £12.00.

What happens to a Yankee if one leg is void or a DNB push?

Each affected component reduces by one fold and the voided leg is settled at 1.00, so its stake is simply carried through. A £1 double at 2.10 with the other leg void becomes a single returning £2.10; trebles with one void become doubles, and the fourfold becomes a treble.

Do I pay tax on Yankee winnings in the UK in 2026?

No—customer winnings are paid tax‑free in the UK; the rate charged to bettors is 0%. Since 2001, general betting duties are levied on operators, not on recreational customers.

Can I enter American odds or exchange prices in a UK Yankee calculator?

Convert American to decimal as 1 + (A/100) for positives and 1 + (100/|A|) for negatives; for example, +250 → 3.50 and −150 → 1.6667. Betting exchanges do not offer Yankees; if you model exchange commission on singles, apply it to winnings only (e.g., 5% of £10 winnings = £0.50 deducted).

Why was my football Yankee rejected by the bookmaker?

Most rejections are for related contingencies such as multiple selections from the same match that cannot be combined in standard full‑cover bets. Other causes include market‑scope conflicts (e.g., mixing 90‑minute and extra‑time outcomes) or operator‑specific multiples rules; Draw No Bet legs that end drawn are usually settled void rather than causing rejection.

How do dead‑heats affect EW Yankees in practice?

The relevant stake is split by the number of tied runners and settled at place odds for the paid places. Example: £1 EW component at 10/1 with 1/5 terms in a two‑way dead‑heat for the final place settles the place side as £0.25 at decimal 3.00, returning £0.75, while the win side loses.

How does a “Total stake” input back‑solve the unit stake for Yankees and EW Yankees?

Unit stake is Total ÷ 11 for win‑only or ÷ 22 for EW, then rounded to the nearest penny without exceeding the budget. For £10 total, a win‑only Yankee uses £10 ÷ 11 = £0.909… which is typically set to £0.90 (total £9.90); for EW it is £10 ÷ 22 = £0.4545… set to £0.45 (total £9.90).

Do bookmaker payout caps change Yankee returns compared with the calculator?

Yes—operators apply maximum‑payout limits that can override the raw calculation. If a calculator shows £300,000 but your bookmaker cap is £250,000 for that sport/market, the payout is limited to £250,000 even though the arithmetic return is higher.

How can I estimate the expected value (EV) of a Yankee before placing it?

Sum s × P(all legs win) × product(decimals) over all 11 components and subtract the total stake 11s. As a check, four independent evens selections (each 2.00 decimal with 50% win probability) yield expected return 6 × 0.25 × 4 + 4 × 0.125 × 8 + 1 × 0.0625 × 16 = 6 + 4 + 1 = 11 per 11s outlay, so EV ≈ 0.

Can I place an Irish Lottery Yankee on numbers from the same draw?

Usually no—most bookmakers bar same‑draw combinations to avoid related‑contingency risk. Build the Yankee across different draws; for example, spread four selections over separate lunchtime and teatime draws on different days so all legs are independent.

Are free‑bet tokens usable on Yankees, and how are SNR returns calculated?

Most UK operators do not allow tokens on full‑cover bets like Yankees; where a token is allowed on a single accumulator, stake‑not‑returned pays s × (product(decimals) − 1). For example, a £10 SNR token on a 2.50 × 3.00 acca returns £10 × (7.50 − 1) = £65.00 instead of £75.00 with a cash stake.

super-yankee.articleHtml:
How do you use our Super Yankee calculator?

A Super Yankee (also known as a Canadian) is a 26‑bet multiple built from five selections: 10 doubles, 10 trebles, 5 fourfolds and 1 fivefold (no singles). Our calculator lets you enter odds in fractional, decimal or American formats, choose each‑way terms, and apply non‑runners, dead‑heats and Rule 4 deductions to get instant, line‑by‑line returns and totals. Stakes can be entered as total or per‑line (unit) stake, with results rounded to the nearest penny.

  • Select bet type: Super Yankee (Canadian).
  • Choose odds format (fractional, decimal or American) and enter odds for five selections.
  • Set the result for each leg: Won, Lost, Placed (for each‑way), or Void/Non‑runner as needed.
  • If applicable, toggle Each‑Way (sets 52 bets: 26 win + 26 place) and enter place terms (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4 the odds).
  • Apply Rule 4 deductions (e.g., 20p in the £) to affected legs and mark dead‑heats where relevant.
  • Enter stake: either a total stake (auto‑split across 26 or 52 bets) or a unit stake per bet.
  • Calculate to see per‑fold breakdown, total return, profit and effective ROI.

Step-by-step example: odds, stake, each-way, Rule 4

The worked examples below use UK settlement conventions and round each line to two decimal places.

**Example A — Win only (no each‑way, no Rule 4)** Five selections with a total stake of £26 (26 bets at £1 unit stake each). Outcomes and prices:

  • Total stake: £26.00
  • Total return: £301.00
  • Profit: £275.00

**Example B — Each‑Way with Rule 4** Each‑Way Super Yankee: 52 bets (26 win + 26 place) at £1 unit stake per part (total stake £52). Place terms 1/5 the odds on all five races. Selection A is subject to a 20p Rule 4 deduction (20% off winnings) due to a late withdrawal.

  • Rule 4 application: effective decimals = 1 + (original decimal − 1) × (1 − deduction). For A win: 1 + (4.00 − 1) × 0.8 = 3.40. For A place: 1 + (1.60 − 1) × 0.8 = 1.48.
  • Win part pays only where all legs in the line are winners; place part pays where all legs are win or place.

What odds formats are supported (fractional, decimal, American)?

The calculator accepts fractional (e.g., 5/2), decimal (e.g., 3.50) and American (e.g., +250 or −110) odds. You can switch formats at any time; conversions are exact to the entered precision. Conversion rules:

  • Fractional a/b → Decimal = 1 + a/b
  • Fractional a/b → American: if a ≥ b, +100 × a/b; if a < b, −100 × b/a
  • Decimal D → American: if D ≥ 2.00, +100 × (D − 1); if D < 2.00, −100 ÷ (D − 1)
FractionalDecimalAmericanNotes
5/23.50+250Win returns include stake
6/42.50+150Common UK price
1/41.25−400Short‑priced favourite
4/15.00+400Longer price

7/24.50+350Also written 3.5/1

Can I factor in non-runners, dead-heats and each-way terms?

Yes. The calculator models UK settlement accurately:

  • **Non‑runner/Void:** Any line containing a non‑runner is settled with that leg at 1.00 (void). Doubles with one non‑runner become singles; trebles become doubles; fourfolds become trebles; the fivefold becomes a fourfold.
  • **Dead‑heat (win part):** Stake on the tied selection is divided by the number of tied runners. In multiples, you can treat the affected leg’s decimal factor as divided by the number of ties (for a two‑way dead‑heat, divide by 2).
  • **Each‑way terms (place part):** Place odds = original fractional odds × place fraction (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4). Winners count as placed for the place part.
  • **Dead‑heat (place part):** If runners tie for the final place(s), the place return is proportionally reduced (e.g., two‑way dead‑heat for one remaining place → half returns on the place part).

**Non‑runner example** — If A wins at decimal 3.00 and E is a non‑runner, the A×E double at £1 becomes a single on A and returns £3.00.

Original LineOutcomeSettlementReturn (£1 unit)
A × E (double)A won, E non‑runnerBecomes single on A3.00

**Dead‑heat example (win part)** — £1 treble with legs at 5.00 (dead‑heat two‑way), 2.00 and 3.00. Apply 2‑way dead‑heat to the 5.00 leg by dividing by 2: effective factor 2.50.

LegsAdjustmentCombined DecimalReturn (£1 unit)
5.00 (DH 2‑way) × 2.00 × 3.005.00 → 2.502.50 × 2.00 × 3.00 = 15.0015.00

**Each‑way place odds reference** — Convert win odds to place odds using the place fraction:

Win FractionalPlace TermsPlace FractionalPlace Decimal
4/11/54/51.80
5/11/51/12.00
7/21/47/81.875
2/11/52/51.40
  • Rounding: UK bookmakers typically round to the nearest penny per line; totals may differ by a penny due to cumulative rounding.
  • Responsible gambling: 18+. For support in the UK, visit [BeGambleAware](https://www.begambleaware.org).

What is a Super Yankee (Canadian) bet and how does it work?

A Super Yankee, also called a Canadian, is a full-coverage multiple built from five selections that excludes singles. It spreads your stake across all doubles, trebles, fourfolds and the fivefold so you are not relying on one accumulator to land. Returns are calculated line by line: each winning line multiplies the decimal odds of its legs, and void legs (e.g., non-runners) settle at 1.00 within any affected line. If placed each-way, the bet duplicates into win and place parts with the place odds derived from the stated each-way terms (for example, 1/5 or 1/4 the win odds), and both parts settle independently.

How many bets are in a Super Yankee?

A Super Yankee consists of 26 separate bets formed from five selections. The breakdown follows combinations of selections taken 2, 3, 4 and 5 at a time. Each stake unit is placed on every line; a total stake is simply the unit stake multiplied by the number of lines.

  • 10 doubles: C(5,2) = 10
  • 10 trebles: C(5,3) = 10
  • 5 fourfolds: C(5,4) = 5
  • 1 fivefold: C(5,5) = 1
  • Total lines: 10 + 10 + 5 + 1 = 26
  • Win-only stake example: £1 per line = £26 total stake.
  • Each-way example: £1 win + £1 place per line = 52 lines, £52 total stake.
  • Minimum winners for any return: 2 winners (to land at least one double). Profit depends on prices.

Super Yankee vs Lucky 31: which should you choose?

Both products cover the same five selections but allocate stake differently. A Lucky 31 adds five singles, giving you a safety net when only one pick wins, at the cost of higher total stake. A Super Yankee skips singles, concentrating the stake on multiples for higher upside when several selections win.

FeatureSuper Yankee (Canadian)Lucky 31
Selections55
Total bets (win-only)2631
Composition10 doubles, 10 trebles, 5 fourfolds, 1 fivefold5 singles, 10 doubles, 10 trebles, 5 fourfolds, 1 fivefold
Singles includedNoYes (5)
Min. winners for any return2 winners (a double)1 winner (a single)
Stake at £1 per line (win-only)£26£31
Each-way lines and stake at £1 win + £1 place52 lines, £52 total62 lines, £62 total
Risk/varianceHigher (no singles safety net)Lower (singles cushion poor runs)
When it suitsConfident in multiple winners or bigger pricesMixed confidence; want returns with one winner
Upside potentialGreater concentration on multiplesSlightly diluted by singles stake

Rule of thumb: choose a Super Yankee if your selections are at healthier odds and you expect at least two or three to win. Choose a Lucky 31 if you want more consistent strike rates and protection from a day where only one selection lands.

Football vs horse racing: settlement nuances

Settlement rules vary by sport and market. The core multiple mechanics are the same, but how each leg is graded can differ. Below are common UK-facing conventions; always check your bookmaker’s specific rules before placing a bet.

**Horse racing**

  • Each-way widely available; standard place terms depend on race type and field size. Typical industry terms: non-handicaps with 8+ runners pay 1/5 odds 1–2–3 places; 5–7 runners pay 1/4 odds 1–2; fewer than 5 runners win-only. Handicaps typically pay 1/5 odds 1–2–3 for 8–11 runners, 1/4 odds 1–2–3 for 12–15, and 1/4 odds 1–2–3–4 for 16+.
  • Rule 4 deductions apply to winning returns when a horse is withdrawn after final declarations and the market shortens. The deduction is a pence-in-the-pound scale based on the withdrawn horse’s price at time of withdrawal.
  • Dead-heats: stake is divided by the number of tied horses for the affected place or win, then settled at full odds.
  • Non-runner: leg is void (factor 1.00) and affected lines reduce in fold count accordingly.

**Football**

  • Most 1X2 markets settle on 90 minutes plus stoppage time, excluding extra time and penalties unless otherwise stated.
  • Asian handicaps and totals can “push” (tie with the line), which voids that leg; quarter lines may split the stake across two adjacent lines.
  • Player props often require participation for action (e.g., must start or must play); otherwise the leg is void.
  • Postponements: if not played within the bookmaker’s stated window, the leg is void; if rescheduled within the window, it usually stands.
  • Dead-heats are rare but can occur in outright or seasonal markets (e.g., Top Goalscorer), applying proportional reductions similar to racing.

Key multiple takeaway: in both sports, a void leg settles at 1.00 within any multiple, so a treble with one void becomes a double on the remaining winners. For help and safer gambling resources in the UK, visit [BeGambleAware](https://www.begambleaware.org) or the regulator at [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk). 18+ Please gamble responsibly.

Which calculator do you need for similar multiples?

Multiples that look similar often settle very differently because of how many lines they contain and whether singles are included. Use the Canadian (Super Yankee) calculator when you have five selections and want full coverage without singles. Choose the Lucky 31 calculator if you want the same five selections but with five singles added. For larger or smaller sets of selections (e.g., Heinz, Super Heinz, Goliath, Trixie), pick the corresponding calculator to match the exact bet composition, line count and each-way behaviour.

Canadian vs Lucky 31: what changes in returns?

Both cover five selections, but a Lucky 31 adds five singles. This increases the total stake and can generate a return even when only one leg wins. The Canadian concentrates stake on doubles and upwards, boosting upside when multiple legs win. The example below assumes win-only stakes of £1 per line with all selections priced the same at decimal 3.00 (2/1). Figures show total return per bet type; profits subtract the total stake (26 for Canadian, 31 for Lucky 31).

Number of winners (out of 5)Canadian (26 lines) Return (£)Canadian Profit (£)Lucky 31 (31 lines) Return (£)Lucky 31 Profit (£)
00-260-31
10-263-28
29-1715-16
354286332
4243217255224
510089821023992

Interpretation: Lucky 31 provides a small cushion at low strike rates thanks to singles, but it also commits more stake. When you expect multiple winners or are chasing bigger prices, the Canadian concentrates exposure on the higher-paying lines, often improving ROI at the same per-line unit stake.

Super Heinz: how many bets and when to use it?

A Super Heinz uses seven selections and contains 120 win-only bets with no singles. The breakdown is 21 doubles, 35 trebles, 35 fourfolds, 21 fivefolds, 7 sixfolds and 1 sevenfold. Minimum winners for any return is two (to land a double). Each-way duplicates the bet into win and place parts, creating 240 lines in total. Use a Super Heinz when you have a strong, broad shortlist and want wide coverage without paying for singles, accepting higher variance in exchange for significant upside if several picks oblige.

  • Win-only stake example: £1 per line = £120 total stake.
  • Each-way example: £1 win + £1 place per line = 240 lines, £240 total stake.
  • Best suited to bettors confident of at least 3–4 winners at decent odds across the seven selections.

Accumulator calculator alternatives (Heinz, Goliath, Trixie)

Select the calculator that matches your exact bet structure. Line counts below are for win-only; each-way doubles the number of lines because the win and place parts settle independently.

  • Accumulators (folds): a standalone accumulator is a single line (e.g., a fivefold), so use a dedicated accumulator calculator when you want just one multiple rather than full coverage.
  • Void legs in any of the above settle at 1.00, reducing the fold depth of affected lines; dead-heats and Rule 4 (racing) adjust returns according to standard UK rules.
  • For safer gambling guidance in the UK, visit [BeGambleAware](https://www.begambleaware.org). 18+.

From paper slips to live bet calculators: how we got here

Bet settlement moved from manual tallies on paper and shop till rolls to real-time engines that model every line in a multiple with accurate rounding, rule handling and sport-specific conventions. Historically, clerks combined odds by hand, applied Rule 4 deductions from printed scales, and worked through dead-heats and void legs across dozens or even hundreds of lines—an error-prone and time-consuming process. Modern calculators automate combinations, convert odds precisely across formats, and apply UK-facing settlement rules consistently, improving accuracy and transparency for bettors and operators alike.

Old-school settlement methods and common pitfalls

Traditional settlement involved working line by line with a calculator and printed rule sheets. The workflow and where errors commonly crept in are outlined below.

  • List selections and prices, usually in fractional odds.
  • Generate combinations (e.g., for a Canadian: 10 doubles, 10 trebles, 5 fourfolds, 1 fivefold).
  • Convert odds where needed (fractional to decimal) and multiply factors per line.
  • Apply each-way place fractions (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4) and duplicate lines for win/place parts.
  • Adjust for non-runners (void legs), dead-heats and Rule 4 deductions where applicable.
  • Round each line to the nearest penny; sum totals for return and profit.

Common pitfalls:

  • Missing a combination or double-counting a line in large perms (e.g., Heinz, Goliath).
  • Incorrect application of dead-heats (should divide stake by number of ties, not halve odds arbitrarily).
  • Forgetting that void legs reduce fold depth (treble with one void becomes a double).
  • Applying place terms incorrectly (confusing 1/5 vs 1/4 or wrong place counts by race type/field size).
  • Mishandling Rule 4 (deduction applies to winnings only; decimals adjust by 1 + (D − 1) × (1 − deduction)).
  • Rounding inconsistencies (per-line rounding vs aggregate rounding can differ by a penny or more).
  • Sport-specific settlement differences (e.g., football “90 mins only” vs markets that include extra time).
ScenarioHow it settles (UK conventions)Worked example (unit stake £1)

Dead-heat for win (two-way)Divide stake by number of ties; settle at full oddsSingle at 4/1 (decimal 5.00), 2-way DH: (£1 ÷ 2) × 5.00 = £2.50
Dead-heat for final place (two-way, one place left)Place part proportionally reduced (half the place return)Place odds 2.00 → half return: £1 × 2.00 ÷ 2 = £1.00

Why modern calculators reduce errors and save time

Automated calculators encode combinatorics and settlement logic, ensuring consistent treatment of edge cases and precise rounding per line. They also give transparent breakdowns, so users can audit returns quickly without reworking every fold.

  • Automatic generation of all combinations for the chosen bet type (e.g., 26 lines for a Canadian, 247 for a Goliath).
  • Accurate odds conversions (fractional, decimal, American) and per-leg adjustments (Rule 4, dead-heats, voids).
  • Each-way handled correctly by duplicating win and place parts with correct place fractions and place counts.
  • Per-line rounding to the penny and cumulative totals, reducing arithmetic drift.
  • Instant scenario testing: toggle leg outcomes and see immediate impact on ROI and variance.
  • Sport-aware settlement notes (e.g., football 90-minute rules, Asian handicap pushes) to prevent misgrading.
  • Clear audit trail: line-by-line returns allow verification against bookmaker rules.

Dead-end approaches: static tables and manual prorating

Before dynamic tools, bettors relied on printed payout tables for fixed odds and fold counts, or improvised spreadsheets. These approaches break down when any leg deviates from the template—different prices, each-way terms, non-runners, Rule 4, or sport-specific pushes—forcing manual prorating that introduces rounding drift and inconsistent logic across lines. Static tables cannot encode all permutations and quickly become obsolete as market practices evolve.

  • Static payout tables assume uniform prices and no adjustments; they cannot handle live Rule 4 or dead-heats.
  • Manual prorating (e.g., “halve the odds” in a dead-heat) often misapplies correct method (should split stake).
  • One-size spreadsheets hide assumptions (rounding order, stake allocation), leading to penny errors compounded across dozens of lines.
  • Different bookmakers’ terms (settlement windows, place terms, push rules) render static references unreliable.
  • Outcome: inconsistent settlements, slower reconciliation, and poor transparency for customers and shops.

For official guidance and safer gambling resources, visit the regulator at [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk) and [BeGambleAware](https://www.begambleaware.org). 18+ Please gamble responsibly.

The other side of the coin: strongest argument against Super Yankees

The strongest counterargument to a Super Yankee (Canadian) is that a singles-first approach often makes better risk-adjusted sense for many bettors. Because a Super Yankee splits your bankroll across 26 lines and excludes singles, you need at least two winners to see any return and typically three to four winners (depending on prices) to show a profit. If your edge per selection is modest or uncertain, concentrating stake on singles can reduce variance, improve capital efficiency, and make results easier to audit against your pricing. Complex multiples amplify correlation risk between selections and can mask negative expected value behind occasional large payouts.

The singles-first strategy: pros and context

A singles-first plan prioritises clarity, control, and lower variance. It is widely preferred by risk-managed bettors, particularly when edges are small or markets are efficient.

  • Lower variance and smoother bankroll curve: each selection stands alone, so one winner still returns cash.
  • Transparent performance tracking: easier to attribute profit/loss to specific edges and closing line value.
  • Flexible staking: you can size each bet to confidence and price without being locked into equal unit stakes across 26 lines.
  • Reduced correlation exposure: related outcomes (e.g., same meeting going, same team news cycle) don’t cascade through many combined lines.
  • Operational simplicity: fewer settlement edge cases (dead-heats, voids, Rule 4) to propagate through dozens of lines.

Best contexts:

  • Limited bankroll or strict risk limits.
  • Small but frequent edges where compounding over time matters more than rare windfalls.
  • Sports with high variance per selection (e.g., football props) where strings of results can be choppy.
  • When you need clear auditability for compliance or personal record-keeping.

Risk concentration, variance and stake dilution

In a Super Yankee, your total outlay is dispersed across 26 lines. You earn nothing from a single winner and rely on doubles or better, which concentrates risk into combination outcomes. If picks are correlated (same jockey, same trainer form, same weather assumptions), poor information can torpedo many lines at once. Conversely, when only one pick wins, you realise no benefit despite being partially “right.” Stake dilution also reduces your effective stake on the best ideas compared to targeted singles or a smaller perm.

  • Stake dispersion: each line receives the same unit stake; your highest-confidence picks cannot be upweighted within the structure.
  • Correlation drag: adverse news on a card or team can sink multiple legs, wiping out many lines simultaneously.
  • Return threshold: two winners are required for any return; three or more typically needed to approach breakeven at shorter odds.
  • Rounding and deductions: Rule 4, dead-heats and voids propagate across numerous lines, compounding penny differences.

Assumptions for the table: win-only stakes of £1 per line, uniform decimal odds of 2.00 (evens) on all selections; totals per bet type are therefore £5 (singles), £26 (Canadian) and £31 (Lucky 31). Combinatorics and returns follow standard UK settlement.

When should you avoid complex multiples?

Complex perms can be powerful when you expect multiple winners at value prices, but there are clear cases where they are counterproductive.

  • Your assessed edges are small or uncertain; capital is better deployed on carefully sized singles.
  • Bankroll is tight or subject to daily/weekly limits; higher variance from perms can accelerate drawdowns.
  • Selections are positively correlated (same meeting conditions, team news, or market drivers), magnifying downside.
  • You require predictable cash flow or strict maximum liability (e.g., trading around positions).
  • Events prone to Rule 4 and dead-heats (large-field handicaps) where adjustments proliferate across many lines.
  • You need granular record-keeping for self-assessment or safer gambling commitments.

For independent guidance, see the regulator at [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk) and support at [BeGambleAware](https://www.begambleaware.org). 18+ Please gamble responsibly.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many winners do I need in a Super Yankee to show a profit at common odds?

With uniform decimal odds of 3.00 (2/1), profit typically starts at 3 winners; with evens (2.00), you generally need 4 winners. For £1 per line (total £26), three winners at 3.00 return about £54 (profit +£28), while three winners at 2.00 return about £20 (loss −£6) and four winners at 2.00 return about £72 (profit +£46).

How does the Super Yankee calculator split a total stake across 26 or 52 lines?

Your total stake is divided equally across all lines, so unit stake = total stake ÷ 26 (win‑only) or ÷ 52 (each‑way). For example, a £20 win‑only total implies £20 ÷ 26 = £0.77 per line to the nearest penny; because £0.77 × 26 = £20.02, the effective spend can differ by up to a penny or two after per‑line rounding, and the calculator reports ROI from the actual spend shown.

How are Rule 4 deductions applied across doubles, trebles and bigger folds?

Apply the deduction to each affected leg independently, then multiply the adjusted decimal factors within the line. Using the UK convention, adjusted decimal = 1 + (original − 1) × (1 − deduction), so a 20p Rule 4 turns 4.00 into 3.40 and 3.00 into 2.60; a £1 double that was 12.00 becomes 8.84, a 26.3% reduction in that line’s return.

How do you settle a two‑way or three‑way dead‑heat inside a Super Yankee?

Divide the stake on the tied leg by the number of ties, then settle at full odds; in decimal terms this is equivalent to dividing that leg’s factor by the number of ties. For example, a £1 single at 5.00 in a two‑way dead‑heat returns (£1 ÷ 2) × 5.00 = £2.50; a treble with a two‑way dead‑heat leg 5.00 and other legs 2.50 and 3.00 pays 2.50 × 2.50 × 3.00 = £18.75 per £1.

Does the calculator handle football pushes, Asian handicaps and postponements?

Yes; a push voids that leg at 1.00 and the affected line reduces in fold depth. For instance, a double where AH 0 at 1.95 pushes and the other leg at 2.10 wins returns £2.10 per £1; a treble with one push and winners at 1.80 and 2.20 settles as a double paying 1.80 × 2.20 = £3.96 per £1, while postponed matches outside the bookmaker’s settlement window void that leg.

Are Super Yankee winnings taxed in the UK?

No; UK punters pay 0% tax on betting winnings, and operators, not customers, are liable for Remote Gaming Duty. Since 2001, the old punter betting duty has not applied to customer payouts, so your calculated returns are gross to you.

Can I weight certain selections with higher stakes inside a Super Yankee?

Not within the structure itself; all 26 lines carry the same unit stake by definition. To overweight a selection you must add separate bets that include it, for example extra singles or additional perms such as targeted doubles or a Trixie involving that selection, thereby increasing its total staked exposure by a known amount.

How does each‑way settlement work in a Super Yankee with winners and placers mixed?

The win part pays only when all legs in the line are winners, while the place part pays when all legs are placed or winners, with winners also counting as placed. For example, at place terms 1/5 on 3/1 (decimal win 4.00), the place decimal is 1.60, so two placed selections return 1.60 × 1.60 = £2.56 per £1 on each place‑part double even if neither won.

How is ROI calculated on the results screen and what do the percentages mean?

ROI% is computed as (Total return − Total stake) ÷ Total stake × 100. Using the worked examples, ROI for Example A is (301.00 − 26.00) ÷ 26.00 × 100 = 1,157.7%, while ROI for Example B each‑way with Rule 4 is (22.17 − 52.00) ÷ 52.00 × 100 = −57.4%.

What happens to each‑way place terms if the field size changes after non‑runners?

Settlement uses the bookmaker’s final terms at the off, so both the place fraction and the number of places can change with withdrawals. A common case is a 16‑runner handicap at 1/4 odds, 4 places becoming 15 runners at 1/4 odds, 3 places; your place part is then settled at the revised 3‑place terms across all affected lines.

Can the calculator model a dead‑heat for the final place on the each‑way part?

Yes; if two tie for one remaining place, the place return for that leg is halved in each affected line. For example, if a leg’s place decimal is 2.00 and it dead‑heats two‑way for the last paying place, its effective factor becomes 1.00, so a place double with another leg at 1.80 returns 1.00 × 1.80 = £1.80 per £1.

How should returns be rounded to match UK bookmaker settlement?

Round each line to the nearest penny before summing totals, which can create a 1p difference versus rounding only at the end. For instance, three lines at £1.335 each round to £1.34 and sum to £4.02, while the unrounded sum £4.005 would round to £4.01; per‑line rounding mirrors shop settlement.

Super Yankee vs Yankee: what changes in line count, stake and return profile?

A Super Yankee uses 5 selections and 26 lines, while a Yankee uses 4 selections and 11 lines; at £1 per line the win‑only stakes are £26 versus £11. Both need at least 2 winners for any return, but the Super Yankee concentrates more stake into higher folds, increasing variance and upside when 3–5 legs win compared with a smaller 4‑selection Yankee.

How are quarter‑line Asian handicaps (±0.25, ±0.75) treated in multiples?

A quarter line splits the line stake across two adjacent handicaps, and each sub‑line settles independently before recombining. For example, AH −0.25 splits into AH 0 and AH −0.5; if the match is drawn, half the stake pushes and half loses, so a double with a winning partner at 2.10 returns 0.5 × 2.10 = £1.05 per £1 unit on that line.

heinz.howItWorks: A Heinz is a 57-bet multiple built from 6 selections; a Super Heinz is a 120-bet multiple built from 7 selections. heinz.articleHtml:
How do I calculate a Heinz bet return online?

A Heinz is a 57-bet multiple built from 6 selections; a Super Heinz is a 120-bet multiple built from 7 selections. Online calculators automate the combinatorics, convert fractional or decimal odds, and apply rules such as each-way terms, voids, and dead heats. To get accurate UK returns, you set the stake (unit or total), define outcomes for each leg, and the tool sums all winning components across doubles to accumulators.

Step-by-step: enter odds, stake, each-way terms

Follow these steps to compute a Heinz or Super Heinz accurately:

  • Select bet type: Heinz (6 selections, 57 bets) or Super Heinz (7 selections, 120 bets).
  • Choose odds format: fractional (e.g., 5/2, 1/1) or decimal (e.g., 3.50, 2.00). The calculator will internally convert as needed.

Enter each selection’s odds and result:

  • Result options typically include Won, Lost, Void/NR (non-runner), and Dead Heat.
  • For football, use markets like Match Result, BTTS, or Over/Under with their quoted odds.

Set stake model:

  • Unit stake (per bet): the amount staked on each component (e.g., £1 per bet). Total outlay = unit stake × number of bets.
  • Total stake: enter the total outlay; the calculator back-solves the implied unit stake.

If Each-Way, toggle each-way and set place terms:

  • Place fraction (commonly 1/5 or 1/4) and number of places (market dependent).
  • Each-way doubles the stake (win part + place part) and settles the place part at the place fraction of odds for placed selections.
  • Optional settlement adjustments (if provided): Rule 4 deductions (horse racing), dead heat places, or commission (exchanges).

Press Calculate to see:

  • Total stake, total return (including stakes), and profit.
  • Breakdown by bet type (doubles, trebles, fourfolds, etc.).
Bet typeSelectionsNumber of betsComponent breakdownEach-Way stake effect
Heinz65715 doubles, 20 trebles, 15 fourfolds, 6 fivefolds, 1 sixfoldStake is doubled (win + place)
Super Heinz712021 doubles, 35 trebles, 35 fourfolds, 21 fivefolds, 7 sixfolds, 1 sevenfoldStake is doubled (win + place)

Example: what do 7 football picks return on a Super Heinz?

Worked example (win-only) using simple, verifiable numbers. Assumptions: 7 football selections, all priced at Evens (fractional 1/1, decimal 2.00). Exactly 5 selections win and 2 lose. Unit stake = £1 per bet. A Super Heinz contains 120 bets, so the total outlay is £120.

ComponentTotal components in Super HeinzWinning components given 5 winnersDecimal return per £1 (all legs at 2.00)Total return (£)
Doubles2110 (choose 2 from 5 winners)4.0010 × 4.00 = 40.00
Trebles3510 (choose 3 from 5 winners)8.0010 × 8.00 = 80.00
Fourfolds355 (choose 4 from 5 winners)16.005 × 16.00 = 80.00
Fivefolds211 (choose 5 from 5 winners)32.001 × 32.00 = 32.00
Sixfolds70—0.00
Sevenfold10—0.00
Totals12026—£232.00

Summary: Total stake £120 (120 bets × £1). Total return £232.00. Profit £112.00. Note: If you toggle Each-Way, your total stake doubles (win + place parts), and place returns are settled at the place fraction (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4) for placed selections.

Is there a Heinz bet calculator app for mobile?

Yes. Most modern calculators are fully responsive and work smoothly on iOS and Android. You can add the calculator to your home screen for app-like access without installing anything from an app store.

  • iOS (Safari): open the calculator page, tap Share, then Add to Home Screen.
  • Android (Chrome): open the calculator page, tap the menu (⋮), then Add to Home screen.
  • Benefits: quick launch, offline-friendly if supported, auto-updates when you revisit, and no sign-in required for basic calculations.
  • What to look for: support for each-way terms (1/5, 1/4), Rule 4 and dead-heat handling, per-selection result toggles (Won/Lost/Void), unit vs total stake entry, fractional and decimal odds, and export/share of results.

Tip: If you often calculate on the go, set default preferences (odds format, stake model, each-way toggle) so your mobile workflow is one tap to calculate.

What is a Heinz bet vs Super Heinz vs Lucky 63?

These are full-cover multiple bets used across UK horse racing and football. A Heinz combines all doubles up to a sixfold from 6 selections (57 bets). A Super Heinz does the same from 7 selections (120 bets). A Lucky 63 is a Heinz with singles added for 6 selections (63 bets). If placed each-way, the stake is doubled because you are placing both a win and a place version of every component.

How much does a Super Heinz bet cost?

The cost is the number of bets multiplied by your unit stake, and doubled if each-way.

  • Formula (win-only): Cost = 120 × unit stake.
  • Formula (each-way): Cost = 120 × unit stake × 2.
  • Unit stake £0.10: win-only £12.00; each-way £24.00.
  • Unit stake £0.25: win-only £30.00; each-way £60.00.
  • Unit stake £0.50: win-only £60.00; each-way £120.00.
  • Unit stake £1.00: win-only £120.00; each-way £240.00.

Tip: Minimum unit stakes vary by bookmaker and channel (online vs shop). Always check your operator’s staking rules before placing a Super Heinz.

Heinz or Lucky 63: which is better and when?

Both use 6 selections. The difference is singles: a Lucky 63 adds 6 singles to the 57 Heinz components, increasing coverage and cost. Choose based on risk tolerance, expected strike-rate, and whether singles provide meaningful protection for your prices.

Choose Lucky 63 when:

  • You want a return even if only one or two selections win (singles can soften losing sequences).
  • Your selections are shorter prices, so singles can meaningfully recoup outlay.
  • You value potential bookmaker concessions sometimes associated with “Lucky” bets (e.g., one-winner consolations or all-winners bonuses where offered; terms vary by firm).

Choose Heinz when:

  • You prefer to concentrate stake on multiples for higher potential upside.
  • You’re comfortable with no singles safety net (zero return if fewer than two winners).
  • You want to keep cost about 10.5% lower than Lucky 63 at the same unit stake (57 vs 63 bets).

Example intuition: If exactly one of your six selections wins at, say, 2.50 (6/4), a Lucky 63 returns the single (less overall loss), while a Heinz returns nothing. If three or more win—especially at bigger prices—the Heinz’s heavier weighting toward multiples can yield stronger returns for the same unit stake.

Heinz vs Canadian vs Yankee: key differences

Here is a side-by-side of three popular full-cover without singles (Yankee, Canadian, Heinz). Costs shown are for win-only; each-way doubles the cost.

Bet typeSelectionsTotal bets (win-only)Singles included?Component breakdownTotal cost at £0.10 unitTotal cost at £1 unit
Yankee411No6 doubles, 4 trebles, 1 fourfold£1.10£11.00
Canadian (Super Yankee)526No10 doubles, 10 trebles, 5 fourfolds, 1 fivefold£2.60£26.00
Heinz657No15 doubles, 20 trebles, 15 fourfolds, 6 fivefolds, 1 sixfold£5.70£57.00

Notes: Lucky 63 is a Heinz plus singles (63 bets total). A Super Heinz (7 selections) contains 120 win-only bets (21 doubles, 35 trebles, 35 fourfolds, 21 fivefolds, 7 sixfolds, 1 sevenfold). For each-way versions of any of the above, multiply the total cost by two.

Does the calculator handle each-way, Rule 4, and dead heats?

Yes. A modern Heinz/Super Heinz calculator applies Rule 4 deductions to the affected legs only, settles dead heats correctly for win and place parts, and prices each-way components using the stated 1/4 or 1/5 place terms. In multiples, adjustments are applied leg-by-leg, and the calculator recomputes every winning component (doubles to accumulators) using the adjusted odds or stakes.

How are Rule 4 deductions applied in multiples?

Tattersalls Rule 4 is a deduction to winnings (not to the stake) when a horse is withdrawn after you’ve placed your bet and prices are reformed. In a multiple, the deduction applies only to components that include the affected selection. The standard mechanics are:

  • Identify the bookmaker-declared deduction (e.g., 10p, 20p, 30p per £1 of winnings).
  • Convert each affected selection’s price to decimal (D). For fractional a/b, D = 1 + a/b.

Reduce the winnings part by the deduction r (e.g., r = 0.20 for 20p):

  • Adjusted decimal D′ = 1 + (D − 1) × (1 − r).
  • Example: 3/1 (D = 4.00) with a 20p deduction → D′ = 1 + 3 × 0.8 = 3.40.
  • Recalculate each component bet that contains the leg using D′. Other legs remain at their original odds.
  • If multiple legs incur deductions, apply each leg’s D′ independently; the combined price is the product of all legs’ decimals.
Original oddsDecimal (D)Deduction (r)Adjusted decimal (D′)£1 return after Rule 4
1/21.5020p1 + (0.50 × 0.80) = 1.40£1.40
Evens (1/1)2.0020p1 + (1.00 × 0.80) = 1.80£1.80
5/23.5020p1 + (2.50 × 0.80) = 3.00£3.00
5/16.0020p1 + (5.00 × 0.80) = 5.00£5.00

Worked multiple: Double with £1 stake; Leg A 3/1 subject to 20p Rule 4 → D′ = 3.40; Leg B 2/1 → D = 3.00. Return = £1 × 3.40 × 3.00 = £10.20 (vs £12.00 without the deduction). For independent adjudication guidance on settlement disputes, see [IBAS](https://www.ibas-uk.com). Licensing and consumer rights information is available via the UK regulator at [Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk).

Dead-heat settlement: worked example

Dead heats split the winning stake across the number of tied runners. In a win market dead heat with N ties, your effective return for that leg is (stake ÷ N) settled at full odds. In decimal terms, you can treat the leg’s effective decimal as D/N for compounding in multiples. For place parts, if the dead heat spans the final available places, settlement is proportionate to the number of places available within the tie.

ScenarioMarketInputsSettlement rule£1 stake return
Win DH (2-way)Win only3/1 (D = 4.00), 2 tiedStake halved (1/2) at full odds(£1 ÷ 2) × 4.00 = £2.00
Double with DH first legWin doubleLeg 1: 3/1, 2-way DH → effective D = 4.00/2 = 2.00; Leg 2: 6/4 (D = 2.50)Multiply adjusted decimals£1 × 2.00 × 2.50 = £5.00
EW place DH for final placeEach-way place partTerms 1/5, 3 places; 10/1 (Dwin = 11.00); 2 tie for 3rdPlace odds decimal = 1 + (10 × 1/5) = 3.00; apply 1/2 DH factor£1 place stake × 3.00 × 1/2 = £1.50

In a Heinz/Super Heinz, the calculator applies the dead-heat factor only to components that include the dead-heating selection. Other components are unaffected and are summed into the overall return.

What do 1/4 and 1/5 each-way terms mean in a Heinz?

Each-way duplicates your multiple into two parallel bets: a win Heinz and a place Heinz. The place part settles at a fraction of the win odds (commonly 1/4 or 1/5) provided the selection finishes within the stated places. In football, each-way is rare; in horse racing, the fraction and number of places depend on race type and field size published by the bookmaker.

  • Place fraction: 1/4 or 1/5 of the win odds becomes the place odds.
  • Places paid: e.g., 1–2–3 in handicaps with 8–15 runners; extended places may apply in promotions but follow the operator’s stated terms.
  • EW stake: total outlay doubles (win + place components for every bet in the Heinz).

Settlement interaction:

  • Win part requires the selection to win.
  • Place part pays if the selection finishes in the paid places; dead heats for place are settled proportionately.

Quick conversion examples (per £1 unit in the place part):

  • 8/1 at 1/5 terms → place decimal = 1 + (8 × 1/5) = 2.60; return £2.60.
  • 5/1 at 1/4 terms → place decimal = 1 + (5 × 1/4) = 2.25; return £2.25.
  • 9/4 at 1/5 terms → place decimal = 1 + (2.25 × 1/5) = 1.45; return £1.45.

In a Heinz, each selection’s place price is used to price all place-side components (doubles to the sixfold). The total return is the sum of win-side and place-side components, minus the total each-way stake.

How should you use a Heinz bet for football and horse racing?

A Heinz (6 selections) and Super Heinz (7 selections) spread your stake across many combinations, which smooths outcomes compared with a single accumulator but still carries significant variance. Use them when you have multiple independent selections and a clear staking plan. Football Heinz bets are typically win-only; horse racing Heinz bets are often each-way to reduce downside volatility. Always confirm bookmaker rules on settlement (voids, dead heats, Rule 4) and each-way terms before placing.

How to win a Super Heinz: staking and variance control

You cannot guarantee a profit from a Super Heinz, but you can manage variance and give yourself a clearer risk–reward profile by controlling stake sizing, correlation, and average prices.

Stake planning:

  • Total outlay = unit stake × 120 (win-only) or × 240 (each-way).
  • Size the unit stake so the total outlay is a small, affordable fraction of your bankroll. Avoid increasing unit stakes after losses; use flat staking for transparency.

Control variance:

  • Shorter average odds (e.g., decimals 1.70–2.10) raise your per-leg hit rate and reduce swings but cap upside.
  • Higher odds increase upside but require more winners to cover the outlay. Mix price bands thoughtfully.

Keep selections independent:

  • Avoid correlated outcomes (e.g., multiple markets from the same match) which can inflate risk or be restricted by bookmakers.
  • Diversify by league/meeting and kickoff/post time to reduce common shocks (team news, weather).

Benchmark scenarios:

  • With w winners from 7 selections, winning components equal combinations of those w winners (see the table below). More winners unlock higher-fold returns disproportionately.
  • Example EV intuition (identical decimal odds D and per-leg win probability p): the expected return for one k-fold is stake × D^k × p^k; the Super Heinz has C(7,k) such k-folds for k=2…7.

Football Heinz examples: match odds, BTTS, over/under

Set up your slip with one selection per match to avoid correlation: for instance, 3× Match Result (1X2), 2× BTTS, 2× Over/Under. Enter odds in decimal or fractional, choose win-only, and apply results leg-by-leg. The calculator multiplies the relevant winners for each double, treble, and accumulator and sums the returns.

Winners (w) out of 7Winning doublesWinning treblesWinning fourfoldsWinning fivefoldsWinning sixfoldsWinning sevenfoldTotal winning components
00000000
10000000

33100004
464100011
51010510026
615201561057
72135352171120

Illustrative return with even-ish football prices: suppose all winners are around 1.90 decimal (common for Match Result/BTTS/Over–Under). If 5 of 7 win, each winning double returns 1.90² = 3.61; each treble 1.90³ = 6.859; each fourfold 1.90⁴ = 13.0321; fivefold 1.90⁵ = 24.761. With unit stake £1 per bet: returns = 10×3.61 + 10×6.859 + 5×13.0321 + 1×24.761 = £194.61. Total outlay £120 → profit £74.61. Exact results will differ with your specific odds and which legs win.

Practical football tips:

  • Avoid mixing markets from the same match; pick different fixtures to reduce correlation and comply with bookmaker rules.
  • Model line value: compare your assessed probabilities to implied probabilities (1/decimal odds) and only include selections with perceived edge.
  • Mind kickoff times: early winners can enable optional cashing strategies on remaining singles or side bets (if permitted), but the Heinz itself settles as placed.

Horse racing Heinz: each-way strategy and field size tips

Each-way Heinz bets split your outlay between win and place components for every combination, offering smoother returns when runners place without winning. The value of the place side depends on field size and race type because those determine places paid and the place fraction.

When each-way helps:

  • Open handicaps with 12+ runners where place terms are more generous (often 1/4 the odds and 3–4 places).
  • Selections at medium-to-big prices where a place offers meaningful compensation.

When win-only may be better:

  • Small fields (≤7 runners) where each-way places are limited or absent.
  • Very short-priced favourites where the place fraction yields minimal uplift relative to the doubled stake.

The following table shows commonly used UK each-way terms. Exact terms are set by bookmakers per race; promotional “extra places” may differ.

Race typeField sizeStandard placesTypical place fraction
All (non-handicap/handicap)2–4Win only (no EW)—
All (non-handicap/handicap)5–72 places1/4
Non-handicap8+3 places1/5
Handicap12–153 places1/4
Handicap16+4 places1/4

Strategy pointers: check the day’s declared places and fractions before toggling each-way in your calculator; target races where your selection is fairly priced and the place terms are favourable; and remember that each-way doubles your Heinz stake, so adjust unit size accordingly.

The other side of the coin: Is a Heinz bet right for you?

A Heinz (6 selections, 57 bets) and a Super Heinz (7 selections, 120 bets) spread your stake across many combinations. That diversification does not eliminate risk; it redistributes it. Your bankroll can face sharp swings because most of your total outlay still sits in higher-fold multiples that depend on multiple legs landing. Before choosing a Heinz-style multiple, assess variance, staking, correlation between selections, and whether a different full-cover bet (Yankee, Canadian, Goliath) better matches your risk tolerance and budget.

The volatility problem: bankroll swings and exposure

Variance in full-cover bets comes from two forces: the number of high-fold components (which are volatile) and the average price of your selections. Even when you land several winners, returns can disappoint if your winners are short-priced or spread across components that don’t stack together in the same folds.

Exposure math:

  • Heinz: 57 unit bets; Super Heinz: 120; Goliath: 247. Your total outlay scales linearly with the unit stake.
  • At £1 unit stake, a Super Heinz outlay is £120 win-only (or £240 each-way). A downswing of a few losing days can materially dent a small bankroll.

Why swings feel larger than on singles:

  • Stake weighting skews toward higher folds as selection count increases; these components have higher payoff but lower hit rate.
  • Correlation traps: multiple picks tied to the same underlying event (e.g., same match) can all fail together.

Practical controls:

  • Size the unit stake so total outlay is a small fraction of bankroll (e.g., 0.5–2%).
  • Prefer one market per event to keep legs independent.
  • Mix prices: include some shorter odds to lift base hit rate, while retaining a few bigger prices for upside.
  • Track results by fold size to see where your edge (if any) actually resides.

When is a Yankee, Canadian, or Goliath the smarter play?

Choose the structure that matches your budget, strike-rate, and appetite for variance. Fewer selections (Yankee/Canadian) concentrate more stake in doubles and trebles, reducing volatility. More selections (Heinz/Goliath) push more stake into higher folds—bigger peaks and deeper troughs. Costs below are win-only; each-way doubles the outlay.
