Guidance: pick a Yankee if you want lower cost and more emphasis on doubles/trebles; a Canadian if you have five strong fancies and want a balanced profile; a Heinz if you can afford the outlay and expect 3–5 winners with some prices; a Goliath only if your bankroll can absorb large swings and you have eight independent selections. For safer gambling resources, visit [BeGambleAware](https://www.begambleaware.org) or the regulator at [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk).

Can pushes, voids, or non-runners reduce your payout?

Yes. In UK fixed-odds multiples, a void or push leg is normally settled at decimal 1.00 for that selection (stake returned for that leg), which reduces the effective fold length. This lowers returns because that leg no longer multiplies your price. Non-runners in horse racing are typically void; late withdrawals can also trigger Rule 4 deductions on other runners. Terms vary slightly by bookmaker; always check house rules.

Accumulator impact:

  • Treble with one void becomes a double at the remaining two legs’ odds (the void leg contributes ×1.00).
  • In a Heinz/Super Heinz, every component including the voided selection prices that leg at 1.00; components without it are unaffected.

Football examples:

  • Postponed match beyond the bookmaker’s reschedule window → that leg is void; components compress in size.
  • Asian handicap push → that leg is a push (void) at 1.00 in multiples; half-wins/half-losses on quarter lines settle proportionately per market rules.

Racing examples:

  • Declared non-runner → void leg (1.00) in multiples; if another horse is withdrawn, surviving legs may face Rule 4 deductions on their winnings.
  • Dead heats are not voids; they are settled by splitting stake or using proportional factors per the market and then compounded in the multiple.

If you believe a settlement is incorrect, you can seek adjudication guidance via [IBAS](https://www.ibas-uk.com). Always review your bookmaker’s published settlement rules for voids, pushes, and non-runners before staking.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many winners do you need in a Heinz to break even at typical odds?

With all legs at Evens (decimal 2.00), a Heinz usually needs 4 winners to exceed the total outlay; at shorter prices like 1.80, you typically need 5 winners. Quantified check at 2.00: with 4 winners you get 6 doubles at 4.00 (£24), 4 trebles at 8.00 (£32), and 1 fourfold at 16.00 (£16) for £72 total return versus £57 staked; with 3 winners you get £20 total (3 doubles × 4.00 plus 1 treble × 8.00), which is below stake. At 1.80, 4 winners return about £53.27 (

How do I convert a total stake into a unit stake for a Heinz or Super Heinz, including each-way?

Divide the total outlay by the number of bets, then divide by 2 if each-way. Example 1: Heinz win-only with £28.50 total → unit stake = £28.50 ÷ 57 = £0.50. Example 2: Super Heinz each-way with £30 total → unit stake = £30 ÷ (120 × 2) = £0.125; most operators round to the nearest penny, so £0.13 unit gives £31.20 outlay and £0.12 unit gives £28.80. A precise calculator will back‑solve and show the exact outlay after rounding.

Why might my calculator total differ by a few pence from the bookmaker settlement?

UK operators round returns to the nearest penny per component bet, then sum across components; small per-line rounding can create a few pence variance. With 57 Heinz components, the theoretical worst-case rounding drift is under 29p (57 × £0.005), though typical differences are within a few pence; Rule 4 rounding and EW place fractions can add another 1–2p variance per affected line.

Are Heinz, Super Heinz, and Lucky 63 winnings taxable in the UK in 2026?

No, UK gambling winnings for individuals are not subject to income tax; operators pay duties instead. Keep personal records for budgeting, but HMRC does not tax recreational betting profits; this applies whether the return comes from a Heinz, Super Heinz, or Lucky 63.

How do extra places promotions change each-way Heinz settlement?

Extra places only change whether the place part qualifies; the place fraction (commonly 1/5 or 1/4) remains as stated. If a runner finishes 4th and the bookmaker pays 4 places at 1/5, the place side pays at decimal 1 + (win odds × 1/5); if standard terms were 3 places, the same selection would not pay on the place side. For a 10/1 shot at 1/5, the place decimal is 3.00; one place-side £1 unit returns £3.00 when 4 places are paid but returns £0.00 if only 3 places are paid.

Do Best Odds Guaranteed (BOG) prices change a Heinz return and how can I model it?

If your bookmaker applies BOG to multiples, any leg where SP exceeds your taken price should be settled at SP, which increases every component containing that leg. Example: early 4/1 (decimal 5.00) drifts to SP 6/1 (7.00); each affected double, treble, etc., multiplies by 7.00 instead of 5.00. A calculator without a BOG toggle will understate these components unless you manually replace that leg’s decimal with the SP.

What happens to each-way terms and returns if non-runners reduce the field after I’ve placed the bet?

Settlement usually follows the each-way terms that applied when you placed the bet, while separate Rule 4 deductions may apply to winnings on remaining runners. Some operators alter place terms when fields shrink materially; because policies vary, rely on the bookmaker’s published rules for whether original places are honoured; the calculator can still price the win side with Rule 4 and the place side per the stated places/fraction you input.

Can I enter American odds (+150, -120) into a Heinz calculator that expects fractional or decimal?

Yes—convert to decimal first, then enter. Use D = 1 + (American/100) for positive prices and D = 1 + (100/|American|) for negative prices. Examples: +150 converts to 2.50, -120 converts to 1.8333; a -200 converts to 1.50.

How should I account for exchange commission when estimating a Heinz-style return?

Apply commission to the winnings part of each leg before compounding. For decimal D, commission c, the adjusted decimal is D′ = 1 + (D − 1) × (1 − c). Example: 2.50 at 5% commission becomes 1 + 1.5 × 0.95 = 2.425; a double with two such legs returns stake × 2.425 × 2.425 = 5.8806. Note that most exchanges do not offer true accas; use this method only for estimation.

How do you settle a dead-heat on the place part when more horses tie than remaining places?

Use a proportional factor equal to places available within the tie divided by number tied. If two places remain and three horses tie, each place-side return is multiplied by 2/3. Example: place decimal 3.00 with £1 place unit in a 3‑way tie for the final two places pays £3.00 × 2/3 = £2.00.

Can I include same‑game selections (for example BTTS and Over 2.5 from one match) in a Heinz?

Many bookmakers restrict correlated same‑game selections in traditional multiples; they may only be allowed inside a single Bet Builder leg. A Heinz assumes independence across selections; calculators do not police correlation, so using one market per event keeps the model and bookmaker rules aligned.

Are bonuses like acca boosts or Lucky 63 consolations included in calculator outputs?

Only if the calculator has a bonus/boost input; otherwise totals exclude promotions. As a guide, a 10% acca boost on a sevenfold adds 10% to that component’s return only; Lucky-style concessions, where offered by some firms, apply per published terms and must be applied after the base calculation.

How can I sanity‑check a Heinz result quickly without a calculator?

Count winning components and multiply by per‑component returns using decimal odds. Example at Evens (2.00) with 4 winners: there are 6 doubles returning 4.00 each (£24), 4 trebles at 8.00 (£32), and 1 fourfold at 16.00 (£16); sum is £72. Compare this to the £57 outlay for a £1 unit stake Heinz.

If a leg is void in a Heinz, how do components settle and does that create singles?

A void leg prices at decimal 1.00 inside every affected component, effectively shortening fold length; doubles with the void settle as singles on the remaining leg, trebles become doubles, and so on. Example: a £1 double of a 2.50 runner with a void becomes a £1 single returning £2.50; the total number of component bets stays at 57, but payouts across those including the void are reduced accordingly.

super-heinz.articleHtml:
How do I calculate a Super Heinz bet online, free and UK‑ready?

A Super Heinz is a full‑cover multiple on 7 selections producing 120 separate lines (no singles). A UK‑ready calculator takes your odds, stake type, settlement options (each‑way, voids, Rule 4, dead heats) and outputs a complete breakdown of returns and profit. You choose the odds format you prefer (fractional, decimal or American), enter results for each leg (win/lose/void, and place where each‑way applies), and the tool computes all doubles through to the seven‑fold automatically under standard UK settlement.

  • Select odds format (fractional/decimal/American).
  • Enter odds and outcomes for 7 independent selections.
  • Choose stake mode: unit stake per line or total stake for the whole bet.
  • Toggle Each‑Way if applicable (duplicates lines into win and place parts).
  • Apply settlement adjustments where relevant (Rule 4 deduction, voids, dead heats).
  • Calculate to view total stake, total return, profit, and a breakdown by bet type.
  • Line composition of a Super Heinz (120 bets): 21 doubles, 35 trebles, 35 four‑folds, 21 five‑folds, 7 six‑folds, 1 seven‑fold.
  • Each‑Way Super Heinz creates 240 bets (120 win part + 120 place part).

Which inputs are required: odds, stake, each-way and Rule 4?

To compute accurately under UK rules, provide the following inputs. Odds can be mixed across selections; settlement options only apply where relevant (e.g., Each‑Way and Rule 4 are common in horse racing and many ante‑post outrights, not in standard football match markets).

InputRequiredAccepted valuesExampleWhat it does

Stake modeYesUnit stake per line or Total stakeUnit stake per lineControls how your entered stake is applied to 120 (or 240 E/W) lines.
Stake amountYesPositive currency value£0.50Either the unit stake (per line) or the total stake for the whole bet.
Each‑Way toggleOptionalOn / OffOnDuplicates the bet into win and place parts; doubles the number of lines.
Each‑Way fractionIf E/WCommonly 1/5 or 1/41/5Applies to place returns (fraction of win odds at specified places).
Place termsIf E/WPlaces paid (e.g., 1–3, 1–4, 1–5)1–4Defines which finishing positions count for the place part.
Rule 4 deductionOptionalp/£ deduction 0.05–0.90 where applicable0.20 (20p in the £)Reduces affected returns per Rule 4 when a runner is withdrawn after final declarations.
Dead heatOptionalShare of win/place (e.g., 2‑way, 3‑way)2‑wayAdjusts settlement by splitting stakes/returns among tied competitors.

  • Conversion references: fractional 7/2 = decimal 4.50; American +350. Decimal payout = decimal odds × stake; profit excludes stake.
  • Rule 4 example: a 0.20 deduction reduces the gross winning return of affected legs by 20% before combining into multiples.

Can I toggle each-way terms and unit stake per leg?

Yes. You can switch the entire Super Heinz to Each‑Way (creating separate win and place parts) and choose whether your entered stake is a unit stake per line or a total stake spread across all lines. Where Each‑Way applies, you can set the place fraction (e.g., 1/5, 1/4) and places paid. If a selection is in a market without Each‑Way (e.g., standard football match result), its place part will not apply and only the win part settles for that leg.

  • Toggle Each‑Way On to duplicate all valid legs into win and place parts.
  • Set Each‑Way fraction and places according to the event terms.

Choose Stake mode:

  • Unit stake per line: cost = unit stake × number of lines (×2 if E/W).
  • Total stake: unit stake is derived by dividing total stake by number of lines.
ScenarioLinesStake modeEntered stakeImplied unit stakeTotal cost

  • Each‑Way only affects markets that publish place terms. Lines involving legs without place terms settle on the win part only.
  • Voids reduce line counts where that selection appears; the calculator adjusts automatically.

Does it work for football markets like goals and cards?

Yes. The calculator is sport‑agnostic: if you have 7 independent football selections (e.g., across different matches or competitions), you can compute a Super Heinz the same way as for racing. Standard football match markets do not use Each‑Way, but long‑term football outrights (e.g., league winner, top goalscorer) may have Each‑Way place terms set by the bookmaker. Always avoid related contingencies (multiple picks from the same event that are not permitted as a standard multiple) when placing the bet with a bookmaker; the calculator assumes independent legs.

  • Calculator support: you can include any football market by entering its odds and final outcome status (win/lose/void).
  • Bookmaker restrictions: combining multiple selections from the same match may be treated as a related contingency unless placed as a builder product; check the operator’s rules before staking.

How much does a Super Heinz bet cost and how many bets are there?

A Super Heinz is a full-cover multiple built from 7 selections that excludes singles and consists of 120 separate lines. Your total cost depends on whether you stake per line or enter a total stake to be spread across all lines, and whether you place the bet Each-way (which duplicates the bet into win and place parts). In non-Each-way mode, there are 120 lines; in Each-way mode, there are 240 lines. Cost and settlement follow standard UK rules for multiples, with adjustments for voids, Rule 4 deductions, and dead heats where relevant.

Why 120 bets? A simple breakdown of the lines

The 120 lines come from the number of unique accumulator combinations you can form from 7 selections, starting at doubles (k=2) through to the seven-fold (k=7). The counts use combinations C(7, k) because the order of selections in an accumulator does not matter—only which selections are included.

Accumulator size (k)Bet typeCombinations C(7, k)Lines contributed
2Doubles2121
3Trebles3535
4Four-folds3535
5Five-folds2121
6Six-folds77
7Seven-fold11
Total120

Because a Super Heinz excludes singles (k=1), the first valid line type is the double (k=2). If any selection is void, every line containing it reduces accordingly (e.g., a treble with a void leg becomes a double). Settlement applies across all surviving lines and is then summed for the total return.

Each-way Super Heinz: how does cost and payout change?

Each-way (E/W) duplicates the entire Super Heinz into two parts: a win part and a place part, effectively doubling line count and cost. The win part settles at full win odds; the place part settles at the place fraction (commonly 1/5 or 1/4 of the win odds) for the positions paid (for example, 1–3 or 1–4), as defined by the market. In UK settlement, an Each-way multiple requires that the legs offering Each-way terms actually have place terms; otherwise, bookmakers typically won’t accept the E/W multiple on those legs.

  • Non-E/W cost: lines × unit stake. Example: 120 lines × £0.50 = £60.00.
  • E/W cost: 240 lines × unit stake (win + place). Example: 240 × £0.50 = £120.00.
  • Total-stake mode: the unit stake is computed by dividing your total stake by the number of lines (by 120 or by 240 if E/W).
  • Place odds in multiples: each placed leg’s place part uses the E/W fraction of its win odds; all place parts compound across the accumulator lines.
  • Example (single leg E/W terms): 10/1 with 1/5 place fraction pays place odds of 2/1 for that leg’s place portion within the multiple.
  • Confirm that all intended E/W legs publish place terms for the event.
  • Set the E/W fraction (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4) and places paid as per the card or market.

Choose stake mode:

  • Unit stake per line: simplest for budgeting; cost scales linearly.
  • Total stake: spreads your bankroll evenly across all lines.

Remember that Each-way rarely applies to standard football match markets; it is common in horse racing and certain long-term outrights (e.g., top goalscorer). If your Super Heinz mixes sports, ensure your bookmaker accepts an E/W multiple of that composition before staking.

What should I know about Bet365 and UK settlement quirks?

Bet365 follows standard UK settlement conventions for multiples with a few practical points you should be aware of. These affect how returns are calculated and why a calculator needs toggles for voids, Rule 4, dead heats, and market timing rules.

  • Rule 4 deductions: If a horse is withdrawn after final declarations, affected legs incur a Rule 4 deduction (expressed as p/£). The deduction is applied to the affected leg’s return before it compounds into your multiples.
  • Dead heats: For a dead heat, the win (or place) portion is split by the number of tied competitors (e.g., 2‑way tie halves the effective win outcome for that leg). The adjusted return then rolls into the accumulator.
  • Voids and pushes: A void leg reduces any line containing it by one selection (e.g., a four-fold becomes a treble). On some football lines (e.g., Asian 2.0), an exact outcome can void the leg rather than lose.
  • Timing rules: Standard football markets settle on 90 minutes plus stoppage (extra time/penalties excluded unless explicitly stated). Ensure your selections share the same timing scope.
  • Odds format and rounding: Returns are equivalent across fractional, decimal, and American odds. Final settlement is rounded to the nearest penny in GBP; calculators typically show pennies to match bookmaker payout.
  • Related contingencies: Standard accumulators cannot combine correlated outcomes from the same event unless placed using a builder product priced for correlation. The calculator assumes independent legs.
  • Each-way eligibility: E/W multiples are generally accepted only when each leg has published place terms; check the race card or market rules.
  • Cash Out vs settlement: Cash Out values are offers, not guaranteed settlement figures; they can differ from calculated returns and can change or be withdrawn.

For accurate planning: decide your stake mode (unit vs total), confirm market rules (E/W terms, Rule 4 applicability, dead-heat policy), and ensure legs are independent and allowed in a standard multiple by your bookmaker before placing a Super Heinz.

Why this is the best Super Heinz calculator for UK punters

This calculator is engineered for UK settlement across complex full-cover multiples on seven selections. It lets you model Rule 4 deductions, dead heats, and voids at leg level, switch odds formats instantly without altering the underlying math, and choose stake modes (unit-per-line or total). It is optimised for horse racing and long-term outrights that may carry Each‑Way terms, while remaining fully compatible with football markets that typically settle as win/lose/void only.

How do we handle Rule 4, dead heats and voids instantly?

We apply settlement adjustments at the leg level before compounding into each affected accumulator line. This mirrors standard UK practice: Rule 4 reduces the win profit of an affected leg, dead heats proportionally split the winning stake, and voids reduce line size (or act as a factor of 1.0 in accumulators). The calculator propagates these effects across all 120 (or 240 E/W) lines automatically.

  • Rule 4 deduction: Adjust the profit component only. Adjusted decimal = 1 + (original_decimal − 1) × (1 − deduction). Example: 4/1 (decimal 5.00) with a 20p Rule 4 becomes 1 + 4 × 0.8 = 4.20.
  • Dead heat (win market): Split the stake by number of tied runners; settle the winning share at full odds. Effective decimal factor = original_decimal ÷ tie_count. Example: 3/1 (decimal 4.00) in a two‑way dead heat → factor 2.00.
  • Void/push: Treat as a non‑runner in multiples; the accumulator reduces in size and the effective decimal factor for that leg is 1.00.

Worked example on a single line within your Super Heinz (unit stake per line £1):

  • Leg A wins at 4/1, Rule 4 = 0.20 → adjusted factor 4.20.
  • Leg B wins in a two‑way dead heat at 3/1 → factor 4.00 ÷ 2 = 2.00.
  • Leg C void → factor 1.00.
  • Treble return = £1 × 4.20 × 2.00 × 1.00 = £8.40 (profit £7.40).
  • Enter odds and toggle Rule 4/Dead Heat/Void on the affected legs.
  • The calculator applies adjustments at the leg, then multiplies through each relevant double‑to‑sevenfold line.
  • Totals are rounded to the nearest penny in GBP to mirror UK bookmaker payout conventions.

Can I switch fractional, decimal or American odds on the fly?

Yes. Switching format is instant and does not change returns because conversions are mathematically equivalent. You can type in any supported format, then view or edit in another format without re‑entering your selections. The converter follows standard formulas and rounds American odds to whole numbers for display.

FormatConversion to DecimalConversion to AmericanExample (Fractional → Decimal → American)
Fractional a/bDecimal = 1 + a/bFirst convert to Decimal; if Decimal ≥ 2.00: +100 × (Decimal − 1); else: −100 ÷ (Decimal − 1)7/2 → 4.50 → +350
Decimal ddd ≥ 2.00 → +100 × (d − 1); d < 2.00 → −100 ÷ (d − 1)1.50 → −200; 2.25 → +125
American+X: 1 + X/100; −X: 1 + 100/XAmerican+1000 → 11.00; −500 → 1.20
  • Examples: 1/2 = 1.50 = −200; 6/4 = 2.50 = +150; 10/1 = 11.00 = +1000.
  • All calculations carry full internal precision; rounding is applied only in the display and at the final payout to the nearest penny.

Where do we beat AceOdds and other calculators for Super Heinz?

Popular UK calculators (e.g., AceOdds, Free Bet Calculator, OddsMonkey, SharpBetting, and operator tools like BetVictor’s) cover the basics well. Our focus is on workflow speed for Super Heinz users and leg‑level edge cases that frequently arise in UK markets. The following capabilities are designed to reduce input time and prevent settlement surprises, especially on busy racing cards or mixed sport coupons.

  • Leg‑level controls in one view: Rule 4 slider (0.05–0.90), dead‑heat split (2‑way, 3‑way, etc.), and void toggle directly beside each of the seven selections.
  • Stake logic you can trust: choose unit‑per‑line or total‑stake; the tool derives the implied unit stake and shows cost live for 120/240 lines.
  • Each‑Way safeguards: alerts if you enable E/W on legs without published place terms, so your modelling mirrors what a UK bookmaker typically accepts.
  • Football‑friendly settlement: supports push/void on lines like Asian goal totals (e.g., 2.0) and standard 90‑minute timing rules.
  • Precision rounding: penny‑accurate totals with a transparent per‑line breakdown so you can reconcile against bookmaker settlements.
  • Format flexibility: instant fractional/decimal/American switching and mixed‑format input across legs, auto‑normalised internally.
  • Breakdown filters: view returns by doubles, trebles, four‑folds, etc., to see which tiers drive most of the payout.
  • Reusable presets: save common E/W fractions (1/5, 1/4) and default stake modes for quicker setup on race days.
  • Enter seven legs and choose stake mode; the calculator validates E/W eligibility and related‑contingency risks before computing.
  • Apply any Rule 4/Dead‑Heat/Void adjustments and switch odds format if needed; results update instantly.
  • Export or copy the per‑tier breakdown to audit your expected return against bookmaker settlement.

From Yankee to Super Heinz: how full-cover bets evolved

Full-cover multiples developed in UK betting shops to let punters spread risk across many accumulator combinations without pricing every line by hand. Early formats like the Yankee (4 selections) and Canadian/Super Yankee (5 selections) expanded into the Heinz (6) and eventually the Super Heinz (7). As online bookmakers and calculators made settlement transparent and instant, higher-line structures became practical for everyday bettors, particularly around big racing festivals and season-long football outrights.

What came before Super Heinz and why did it change?

The Super Heinz (7 selections, 120 lines) is the natural extension of the full-cover family that excludes singles. Punters first used smaller sets—Trixie and Yankee—because they were manageable to price by hand and affordable at shop counters. With the growth of online markets, bigger coupons (Canadian, Heinz) gained traction because:

  • They smooth variance: a few winners still generate returns via doubles/trebles even when the main accumulator misses.
  • They fit racing Each‑Way strategies: more combinations benefit when multiple horses place/win across a card.
  • Digital tools automate complexity: calculators output accurate per‑tier breakdowns in seconds.
Bet type (no singles)SelectionsLines (win only)Lines (Each‑Way)Notes
Trixie3483 doubles + 1 treble
Yankee411226 doubles, 4 trebles, 1 four‑fold
Canadian (Super Yankee)5265210 doubles, 10 trebles, 5 four‑folds, 1 five‑fold
Heinz65711415 doubles, 20 trebles, 15 four‑folds, 6 five‑folds, 1 six‑fold
Super Heinz712024021 doubles, 35 trebles, 35 four‑folds, 21 five‑folds, 7 six‑folds, 1 seven‑fold
Goliath8247494All doubles to eight‑folds across 8 selections

This progression shows why the Super Heinz became popular: it offers more “ways to win something” than a Heinz while staying below the Goliath’s extreme line count and cost.

Which full-cover variants proved impractical in real betting?

“Impractical” usually means the line count forces unit stakes so small that meaningful returns are hard to achieve, or that legs aren’t eligible (e.g., Each‑Way on non‑E/W markets). The main culprits are the very large structures and their Each‑Way versions.

  • Goliath (247 lines): at £0.50 per line, cost is £123.50; the E/W version is 494 lines (£247.00 at £0.50/line). This strains bankrolls and magnifies overround across many markets.
  • Heinz E/W (114 lines) and Lucky 63 E/W (126 lines, includes singles): still expensive for casual staking; many punters under‑stake lines to stay within budget, limiting upside.
  • Mixed‑sport E/W multiples: standard football match markets rarely offer Each‑Way; enabling E/W across such coupons isn’t accepted by most bookmakers.
  • Related contingency traps: combining correlated selections from the same event (e.g., match result + player to score) is typically disallowed in standard multiples, so intended “full‑cover” cannot be placed as planned.
  • Check E/W eligibility per leg; E/W doubles total line count and cost.
  • Model realistic unit stakes: for 120 (or 240) lines, ensure your per‑line stake still produces worthwhile returns on the most common win scenarios.
  • Avoid correlated picks unless the bookmaker offers a builder product that explicitly prices the correlation.

For many bettors, the Super Heinz strikes a balance: broad coverage and manageable cost versus the Goliath’s sheer scale.

How did online bookies like Bet365 reshape settlement?

Online operators standardised rule application and exposed it in product flows, which changed how punters plan full‑cover bets. Key shifts include:

  • Automated Rule 4, dead‑heat, and void settlement at leg level, applied instantly across all affected lines of a multiple.
  • Clear timing scopes: football markets settle on 90 minutes plus stoppage unless otherwise stated; Asian lines void/push on exact lines (e.g., 2.0), which propagates through multiples.
  • Bet Builder products to price correlated selections within the same event; standard accumulators still require independent legs.
  • Real‑time Cash Out and Partial Cash Out as offers (not guaranteed payouts) that diverge from final settlement but help manage exposure mid‑event.
  • Odds‑format parity and rounding: fractional, decimal, and American all map to the same payout; final settlements round to the nearest penny in GBP.
  • Published market‑by‑market rules and maximum payout caps by sport, encouraging bettors to pre‑check eligibility and limits for large multiple returns.

The net effect is fewer settlement surprises: with standardised rules and instant computation, punters can model Super Heinz outcomes accurately before staking and reconcile results precisely after events settle.

Is a Super Heinz a good bet? The strongest argument against it

The strongest argument against a Super Heinz is that bookmaker margin (overround) compounds across many legs and lines, while variance is high and costs climb quickly (120 lines, or 240 Each‑Way). Unless your selections carry genuine value (true probability higher than implied by price), the expected return across the full-cover structure trends negative and bankroll swings can be severe. Understanding margin, independence, unit staking and market eligibility is essential before committing significant funds.

What bankroll risks and overrounds make it hard to profit?

Overround is the bookmaker’s built‑in margin on a market. If your average selection is priced below fair value, the negative expectation compounds across accumulator legs. In a Super Heinz, you place 120 separate lines (combinations from doubles to a seven‑fold), so the effect hits many times. The math below uses a transparent “even‑money” illustration: each leg has a 50% true chance, but the price offered reflects a typical margin (decimal 1.91 instead of a fair 2.00). The expected return factor for a k‑fold built from such legs is (0.5 × 1.91)^k = 0.955^k.

Accumulator size (k)Probability all k winExample price per leg (decimal)EV return factor per £1 stakeExpected loss (%)
2 (Double)0.251.910.955^2 ≈ 0.912−8.8%
3 (Treble)0.1251.910.955^3 ≈ 0.871−12.9%
40.06251.910.955^4 ≈ 0.832−16.8%
50.031251.910.955^5 ≈ 0.795−20.5%
60.0156251.910.955^6 ≈ 0.759−24.1%
70.00781251.910.955^7 ≈ 0.724−27.6%

In a full Super Heinz (identical‑leg, independence example), the weighted expected return across all 120 lines is the average of 0.955^k using the combination weights (21 doubles, 35 trebles, 35 four‑folds, 21 five‑folds, 7 six‑folds, 1 seven‑fold). That comes out at roughly 0.845 per £1 staked—an expected loss near 15.5% before any enhancements like Each‑Way. Real outcomes depend on your actual prices and true probabilities; the key risk is that negative edge is multiplied many times.

  • Variance: Many lines will regularly return £0; returns cluster on days when multiple legs land together. Bankroll drawdowns can be steep even with decent strike rates.
  • Cost concentration: At £0.50 per line, a Super Heinz costs £60.00 (or £120.00 Each‑Way), so a few losing days can erase a week’s profits without value on your side.
  • Independence: Related selections are normally disallowed in standard multiples; if accepted via a builder product, prices already reflect correlation, reducing perceived edge.
  • Settlement frictions: Rule 4 and dead heats reduce winning legs; without accounting for them ex‑ante, expected returns are overstated.

How to win a Super Heinz bet: selection filters and staking

Improving outcomes requires shifting the average leg from “priced with margin” toward “priced with value.” That’s a selection problem before it is a combination problem. Staking should then align with variance and bankroll limits.

Selection filters (aim to lift true edge per leg):

  • Shop for price outliers: prioritise selections where your tissue or widely available quotes imply overlay (decimal odds above your fair).
  • Favour clearer settlement rules: for football, stick to 90‑minute regulated markets; for racing, confirm Each‑Way terms and avoid late‑Rule‑4‑prone races where possible.
  • Avoid correlated picks unless priced via a builder; keep standard multiples for independent events.
  • Exploit place terms selectively: Each‑Way makes sense where the place fraction/places paid compare favourably to your assessed place probability (e.g., 1/5 1–4 in big fields).

Staking and cost control:

  • Unit‑per‑line staking for clarity: decide a per‑line unit that still gives meaningful returns when 2–4 legs win (common scenarios).
  • Cap exposure per bet: set a maximum total cost per Super Heinz (e.g., fixed £ amount) and avoid chasing losses.
  • Each‑Way only when eligible: enable E/W on legs with published place terms; otherwise you double cost without added settlement benefit.
  • Avoid under‑staking to invisibility: very small per‑line stakes can render even good days negligible; balance line count vs unit size.

Process hygiene:

  • Record prices at placement and the best available elsewhere to track whether you consistently beat the closing line.
  • Reconcile results against per‑tier breakdowns (doubles, trebles, etc.) to identify where your edge actually materialises.

Super Heinz vs Super Yankee vs Goliath: which should I choose?

Choosing the right full‑cover depends on budget, variance tolerance, and how many strong selections you can justify on price. The table below compares structure, cost at a simple per‑line unit, and the minimum winners needed to generate any return on win‑only settlement (ignoring voids and Each‑Way place parts).

Bet type (no singles)SelectionsLines (win)Lines (E/W)Total cost at £0.50/line (win)Total cost at £0.50/line (E/W)Min winners for any return (win‑only)
Super Yankee (Canadian)52652£13.00£26.002 (first doubles)
Super Heinz7120240£60.00£120.002 (first doubles)
Goliath8247494£123.50£247.002 (first doubles)

Guidance: pick the smallest structure that still fits your number of true “value” selections. If you have five strong plays, a Super Yankee concentrates staking more efficiently than forcing seven into a Super Heinz. Reserve Goliath for rare days when you can justify eight well‑priced, independent legs and can afford the higher cost and variance. For mixed sports, verify Each‑Way eligibility before enabling E/W; many football match markets do not support it, which undermines the rationale for doubling line count.

Frequently Asked Questions

What does “official returns” mean for a Super Heinz in 2026?

Official returns are the final, bookmaker-settled payout after applying Rule 4 deductions, dead‑heat splits, voids/pushes and rounding to the nearest penny across all 120 lines (or 240 if each‑way). Cash Out offers are excluded; the total is the sum of all winning line settlements using the accepted odds and outcomes. For example, with a £0.50 unit stake on a non‑E/W Super Heinz, the total cost is £60.00 (120 × £0.50) and the official return is the penny‑rounded sum of every settled double through seven‑fold.

Are Super Heinz winnings taxed in the UK in 2026?

For UK residents, personal tax on betting winnings is 0%; bookmakers, not punters, pay betting duties. Your Super Heinz returns are therefore paid in full as settled, with no income tax deducted from the payout.

Do UK bookmakers cap maximum payouts on a Super Heinz and how can I model it?

Yes, operators set maximum payout caps by sport and bet type; if your calculated return exceeds the cap, the official return is limited to that cap. To model, compare your calculated total to the operator’s limit, then apply a hard ceiling; for example, if the calculator shows £1,200,000 but the cap is £1,000,000, the effective haircut is £200,000 (16.7%).

How are dead heats settled in the place part of an each‑way Super Heinz?

In a place dead heat where more runners tie than places available, the place stake is reduced by places_available ÷ runners_tied, then settled at place odds. Example: each‑way 1/5 1–3, four runners tie for third, so a £1 place part becomes £0.75; at win odds 10/1 with 1/5 place terms (place odds 2/1, decimal 3.00), the place return is £0.75 × 3.00 = £2.25 on any line containing that leg.

How do I account for Best Odds Guaranteed (BOG) in a Super Heinz?

If BOG applies and the Starting Price (SP) is bigger than the taken price, settle that leg at SP; every line containing it scales by SP ÷ taken_price. Example: taken 4/1 (decimal 5.00) but SP is 6/1 (decimal 7.00); a treble line containing that leg increases by 7.00 ÷ 5.00 = 1.40, so a £10.00 line return of £100.00 becomes £140.00. Terms vary by bookmaker and market, so only apply where eligible.

Can I model free bets (stake not returned) inside a Super Heinz?

Yes; for a free‑bet Super Heinz, each winning line pays stake × (product_decimal − 1) instead of stake × product_decimal. Example: a £1 line wins at combined decimal 6.00; cash stake returns £6.00, but a free‑bet (SNR) returns £5.00. Sum this across all winning lines; losing lines cost £0.00 because the stake was free.

How are Asian handicap pushes and quarter‑lines handled in a Super Heinz?

A push (e.g., AH 0 or Over 2.0 exactly) makes the leg a factor of 1.00 and reduces each affected accumulator by one selection. Quarter‑lines split into two half‑stakes on adjacent half/whole lines; to model Over 2.25, treat it as 0.5 × Over 2.0 and 0.5 × Over 2.5 at their respective prices, so exact 2 goals refunds half the stake on lines containing that leg while the other half settles on Over 2.5.

What happens to a Super Heinz if multiple selections are void?

The bet collapses to a full‑cover on the remaining active selections, with singles still excluded. One void leaves 57 lines, two voids leave 26, three leave 11, four leave 4, five leave 1 (the single surviving double), and six voids leave 0; each surviving line then settles on the remaining results.

Can I use different each‑way fractions or places for different legs in one Super Heinz?

Bookmakers settle each leg by its own published E/W terms, so place odds are leg‑specific. If your calculator does not allow per‑leg E/W fractions and places, run separate scenarios or use the strictest terms as a conservative proxy; for accuracy, a line containing legs at 1/5 and 1/4 must apply each leg’s own place odds inside the same product.

Why can my calculator total differ by a few pence from the bookmaker’s settlement?

Most UK operators round the final payout to the nearest penny, but some round per component or per line; rounding method differences can accumulate. The theoretical worst‑case drift from per‑line rounding is about half a penny per line, so up to roughly £0.60 on 120 lines or £1.20 on 240 each‑way lines.

How many winners do I typically need to break even on a Super Heinz?

Break‑even depends on prices; with even‑money legs (decimal 2.00) and £0.50 units, four winners return about £36.00 against £60.00 staked, while five winners return about £116.00, tipping into profit. At 2/1 legs (decimal 3.00), three winners return about £27.00, while four winners return about £121.50, so profit usually begins at four winners for these example prices.

What’s the difference between NRNB and standard ante‑post for multiples like a Super Heinz?

With Non‑Runner No Bet, a non‑runner is void (factor 1.00) and any line containing it reduces in size; with standard ante‑post, a non‑runner is settled as a loser (factor 0), which zeros every line containing that leg. For example, a treble with one NRNB non‑runner becomes a double, but the same treble ante‑post loses entirely even if the other two selections win.

lucky-15.articleHtml:
How do I use the Lucky 15 Calculator?

A Lucky 15 is a 15-line multiple built from four selections: 4 singles, 6 doubles, 4 trebles, and 1 fourfold. To use the calculator, enter your odds and stake, choose Each-Way (if applicable), set place terms for horse racing, and record each selection’s result (Win, Place, Lose, Dead-Heat, Void). The tool then settles every line, applies any Rule 4 deductions you specify to the affected races, and outputs total stake, total return, and profit.

What steps calculate returns, each-way, and totals?

Follow this sequence to mirror how UK bookies settle a Lucky 15 and to ensure each line is correctly evaluated.

Choose bet type and stake:

  • Select Lucky 15 and enter your unit stake (e.g., £0.50).
  • Tick Each-Way for horse racing (splits the unit stake 50/50 between Win and Place on every line).

Enter odds and format:

  • Input each selection’s odds in fractional (e.g., 3/1), decimal (e.g., 4.00), or American (e.g., +300) format.
  • If Each-Way, specify the place terms for each race (e.g., 1/5 odds, places 1–3).

Set settlement factors per selection:

  • Result: Win, Place, Lose, Dead-Heat, Void.
  • Rule 4 (if any): enter the deduction in pence per £1 (e.g., 20p = 0.20).

Calculate singles:

  • Win single return (fractional a/b with stake s): return = s × (a/b) + s. Decimal d: return = s × d.

Each-Way single splits stake into Win (s/2) and Place (s/2). Place odds use the place factor f (e.g., 1/5 = 0.20):

  • Place fractional = (a/b) × f; place decimal = 1 + (d − 1) × f.
  • Winners pay on both Win and Place parts; a placer pays only on the Place part; a loser pays neither.

Calculate multiples (doubles, trebles, fourfold):

  • Win part: every leg must win.
  • Place part: every leg must place (a winner counts as placed).
  • Accumulator return = stake × product of decimal odds across legs (use Win decimals for the Win part; Place decimals for the Place part).

Totals and profit:

  • Total stake (Each-Way Lucky 15) = 15 lines × 2 parts × unit stake.
  • Total return = sum of all winning lines (Win and Place parts).
  • Profit = Total return − Total stake.
Lucky 15 structureLinesEach-Way lines (Win + Place)Notes
Singles48A winner pays on Win and Place; a placer pays on Place only.
Doubles612Win double requires two winners; Place double requires two placers.
Trebles48All three must win/place on the respective part.
Fourfold12All four must win/place on the respective part.
Total1530Each-Way doubles total lines; Win-only stays at 15.

Which odds formats, Rule 4, dead heats, and voids are supported?

The calculator supports UK settlement conventions for horse racing and other sports. Enter odds in your preferred format, and the tool converts and settles accordingly, with options for Rule 4, dead-heats, and voids.

Odds formats:

  • Fractional (e.g., 3/1), Decimal (e.g., 4.00), American (e.g., +300 or −200).
  • Conversions: decimal = 1 + (a/b); American = +100 × (a/b) if decimal ≥ 2.00, else −100 ÷ (a/b).

Rule 4 deductions (UK/Tattersalls):

  • Applied when a selection is withdrawn after betting, reducing the winnings (not the stake) on affected races.
  • Input the deduction as pence in the pound (e.g., 20p = 0.20). Adjusted decimal = 1 + (original decimal − 1) × (1 − deduction).
  • Typical range: 0.05 to 0.90 depending on the withdrawn favourite’s price at the time of withdrawal.

Dead-heat settlement:

  • Win market: if n runners tie, the winning stake is divided by n and settled at full odds; the remaining (n−1)/n of the stake is lost.
  • Place market: divide the stake by the number of runners sharing the final place(s) and settle at place odds.
  • Example (win DH, 2-way, 6/1, £1 stake): return = (£1/2 × 7.00) = £3.50 decimal (or £0.50 × 6 + £0.50).

Voids:

  • A void selection settles at 1.00 (stake returned for that leg). In multiples, the affected line becomes a bet on the remaining selections.
  • Example: a double with one void becomes a single on the other leg for both Win and Place parts.
Example odds (fractional)DecimalAmerican
3/14.00+300
1/21.50−200
10/111.00+1000

Example: How do you settle a 50p each-way Lucky 15?

Assume four horse racing selections with place terms 1/5 (places 1–3), no Rule 4, no dead-heats. Unit stake is £0.50 Each-Way per line, so total stake = 15 lines × £1.00 = £15.00.

SelectionOdds (fractional)Win decimalPlace termsPlace decimalResult
A3/14.001/51.60Win
B10/111.001/53.00Place (not Win)
C5/23.501/51.50Lose
D2/13.001/51.40Win

Winning singles:

  • A single: £0.50 Win × 4.00 = £2.00; £0.50 Place × 1.60 = £0.80.
  • B single: Win loses; £0.50 Place × 3.00 = £1.50.
  • D single: £0.50 Win × 3.00 = £1.50; £0.50 Place × 1.40 = £0.70.
  • Singles subtotal = £2.00 + £0.80 + £1.50 + £1.50 + £0.70 = £6.50.

Winning doubles:

  • AB place double: £0.50 × 1.60 × 3.00 = £2.40.
  • AD win double: £0.50 × 4.00 × 3.00 = £6.00.
  • AD place double: £0.50 × 1.60 × 1.40 = £1.12.
  • BD place double: £0.50 × 3.00 × 1.40 = £2.10.
  • Doubles subtotal = £2.40 + £6.00 + £1.12 + £2.10 = £11.62.

Winning trebles:

  • ABD place treble: £0.50 × 1.60 × 3.00 × 1.40 = £3.36.
  • Trebles subtotal = £3.36.

Fourfold:

  • No winning fourfold (C lost). Fourfold subtotal = £0.00.
CategoryReturn (£)
Singles total6.50
Doubles total11.62
Trebles total3.36
Fourfold total0.00
Total return21.48
Total stake15.00
Profit6.48

Note: This example applies no one-winner or all-correct bonuses (these vary by bookmaker and sport). If a Rule 4 deduction or a dead-heat applied to any leg, the calculator would adjust the affected Win/Place prices before multiplying through the relevant lines.

What is a Lucky 15 bet and how does it work?

A Lucky 15 is a full-coverage multiple built from four selections, producing 15 separate lines: 4 singles, 6 doubles, 4 trebles, and 1 fourfold. Each line is settled on its own merits and all winning lines are added together to form your total return. If placed Each-Way (commonly used in horse racing and greyhounds), every line is duplicated into a Win part and a Place part, doubling the number of lines from 15 to 30. Place terms (for example, 1/5 the odds, places 1–3) apply to the Place part only, while the Win part requires the selection to win.

What are the 15 bets from four selections?

Label the four selections A, B, C, and D. The Lucky 15 expands them into every possible single, double, treble, and the fourfold. This ensures some return even if not all selections win, depending on results.

Singles (4 lines)

  • A
  • B
  • C
  • D

Doubles (6 lines)

  • AB
  • AC
  • AD
  • BC
  • BD
  • CD

Trebles (4 lines)

  • ABC
  • ABD
  • ACD
  • BCD

Fourfold (1 line)

  • ABCD

If you choose Each-Way, every one of the 15 lines above is duplicated into Win and Place parts, creating 30 settled components (15 Win + 15 Place). The Place part uses the race’s stated place terms; a winner also counts as placed.

Do UK bookies pay one-winner and all-correct bonuses?

Many UK bookmakers run promotional bonuses on Lucky 15s, but they are not universal and can vary by brand, sport, market, stake type, and date. Common formats include an enhanced-settlement bonus when exactly one selection wins (“one-winner” bonus) and a percentage uplift to the total return when all four selections win (“all-correct” bonus). Always check the current terms on the bookmaker’s site before betting; these promotions can be changed, limited, or withdrawn.

One-winner bonus (typical concept):

  • Applies only when exactly one of the four selections wins and the others lose.
  • Usually enhances the winning single (for example, by settling that single at a multiple of the odds) rather than paying on multiples that do not exist.
  • May exclude certain bet types or apply only to designated sports (often horse racing and greyhounds).

All-correct bonus (typical concept):

  • Applies when all four selections win, adding a percentage bonus to the return.
  • Frequently calculated as a percentage of “winnings” (return minus stake) rather than the full return, but methods differ by bookmaker.
  • For Each-Way bets, some terms apply the bonus to the Win part only; others define qualifying criteria differently.

Key cautions:

  • Promotions are discretionary and subject to eligibility, settling rules, and market restrictions.
  • Dead-heats, Rule 4 deductions, voids, or non-runners can affect whether a bet qualifies.
  • Always confirm qualifying markets, maximum payouts, and how the bonus is calculated in the specific terms.

How much does a 50p each-way Lucky 15 cost in total?

Unit stakes in multiples apply per line. A Lucky 15 has 15 lines. Each-Way duplicates each line into a Win part and a Place part, so a 50p Each-Way stake places £0.50 on the Win part and £0.50 on the Place part of every line. That is 30 parts in total at £0.50 each, so the total stake is £15.00. For comparison, a 50p Win-only Lucky 15 (no Each-Way) costs £7.50.

The Each-Way total equals twice the Win-only total because each line is split into separate Win and Place parts at the same unit stake. Settlement of returns then depends on outcomes and (for racing) the stated place terms for each selection.

Which Lucky 15 calculators and bookies does it match?

This calculator mirrors standard UK settlement for Lucky 15s: fractional/decimal odds, Each-Way duplication, place terms per market, Rule 4 deductions on affected winnings, dead-heat stake division, and void legs settling at 1.00 within multiples. When you input the same odds, place terms, Rule 4s, and results, returns should align with major UK bookmakers’ core settlement for the same scenario. Differences usually arise from brand-specific promotions (e.g., one-winner or all-correct bonuses), market-specific place terms (including “extra places”), or rounding and maximum-payout policies applied at bookmaker settlement time.

William Hill Lucky 15 Calculator vs this tool: what differs?

For core settlement (no promos), both approaches converge: winners and placers flow through all 15 lines, Rule 4 reduces affected winnings only, dead-heats divide the winning stake by the tie count, and voids turn an affected multiple into a bet on the remaining selections. Key differences to be aware of:

Promotions and boosts:

  • Bookmaker settlement may apply “one-winner” or “all-correct” bonuses and price boosts when eligible. This calculator models core settlement only; you can add any bonus impact manually after viewing the baseline return.

Place terms and market type:

  • Racing markets can have different place fractions and number of places by race type/field size, and “extra places” change qualifying places. This tool lets you set custom place terms per selection to match the slip.

Rounding and maximum payouts:

  • Bookmakers round to the nearest penny and enforce product-level maximum payouts. The calculator displays uncaped, penny-rounded outputs; check brand limits for final settlement.

Input flexibility:

  • This tool accepts fractional and decimal odds (and supports American for cross-sport use), plus manual Rule 4 and dead-heat entries. Official brand tools may be tied to current markets and default formats.

Betfred calculator, app, and free PDF: should you switch?

Betfred’s ecosystem (site/app) is optimised for their markets and current promos. A printable or static “free PDF” guide can help estimate stakes, but it cannot compute live contingencies like Rule 4, dead-heats, voids, or mixed place terms. Consider this calculator if you:

Compare across bookies:

  • You want a neutral baseline return independent of brand-specific bonuses, with manual control of Rule 4 and place terms.

Handle edge cases:

  • You need precise settlement for dead-heats, void legs, or mixed-sport Lucky 15s (e.g., horses plus football, where only racing legs have place parts).

Require transparent math:

  • You want to see each line’s contribution, not just a final figure, to reconcile results with your slip.

Work offline/away from brand apps:

  • A brand-agnostic calculator avoids login requirements and remains consistent even when app features or promos change.

Ladbrokes and Sky Bet: are inputs and settlement rules aligned?

Yes—provided you match like for like on odds format, Each-Way status, place fraction and number of places, and any Rule 4 or dead-heat scenarios. Both brands follow standard UK conventions for:

Rule 4 deductions:

  • Applied to winnings (not stake) on affected races after withdrawals. Enter the same pence-in-the-pound in the calculator to align results.

Dead-heats:

  • Stake division by the number of tied runners, settled at full odds on the reduced stake.

Voids/non-runners:

  • Void legs settle at 1.00 within multiples, reducing an affected line to the remaining selections.

Each-Way terms:

  • Standard place fractions and places depend on race type/field size. “Extra places” markets change the qualifying places and sometimes place fractions; mirror those in the calculator for alignment.

How did we get here, and what’s the catch?

UK multiple bets like the Lucky 15 were historically settled by shop staff using printed tables and mechanical calculators. Today, live online tools mirror bookmaker settlement in real time, letting you model Each-Way place terms, apply Rule 4 deductions, handle dead-heats, and see a full line-by-line breakdown before you place or settle a bet. The “catch” is that differences can still arise from brand-specific promotions, rounding policies, “extra places,” and how Rule 4 bands are applied at the moment a runner is withdrawn. Understanding the mechanics helps you reconcile your slip to the penny.

From shop settlers to live tools: what changed?

Settlement moved from manual, after-the-fact calculation to pre-bet simulation and instant verification. This reduces disputes and surfaces assumptions that used to be hidden in small print.

Transparency:

  • Then: final payout appeared on the settled slip, with limited breakdown.
  • Now: calculators show every winning/losing line (4 singles, 6 doubles, 4 trebles, 1 fourfold), Win and Place parts, and where deductions or dead-heats apply.

Edge-case handling:

  • Then: staff consulted printed Rule 4 and dead-heat tables; errors could creep in.
  • Now: tools apply the same logic consistently and let you model multiple withdrawals and voids.

Place terms and “extra places”:

  • Then: standard fractions (e.g., 1/5) and places varied by race type/field size.
  • Now: extra-place markets are common; calculators let you set places and fractions per selection.

Pre-bet decision support:

  • You can test different unit stakes (e.g., £0.50 EW vs £1 EW), compare Win-only vs Each-Way, and preview the effect on total outlay and returns.

Devil’s advocate: Why not just trust the bookie slip?

The bookmaker slip is the binding settlement for that brand, and it already accounts for their exact place terms, rounding policy, and any applied promos. For many punters, that’s sufficient. However, relying solely on the slip can mask where value was lost or gained. A neutral calculator helps you verify that the arithmetic (including Rule 4, dead-heats, and voids) is correct and separates core settlement from brand-specific bonuses or caps.

Reasons to trust the slip:

  • It reflects the brand’s live market rules, promo eligibility, and maximum payout caps.
  • It applies the correct rounding step used by that operator.

Reasons to verify independently:

  • Transparency: see line-by-line contributions across 15 legs (and 30 parts if Each-Way).
  • Diagnosis: pinpoint differences caused by Rule 4 timing, dead-heat splits, or voids.
  • Comparability: model the same bet across multiple brands without promo noise.

Edge cases: How do Rule 4 tables and margins affect payout?

Two levers materially change returns on a Lucky 15: Rule 4 deductions and the built-in margin on your odds. Rule 4 reduces the winnings on affected legs only; bookmaker margin is already “baked into” each price and compounds across multiples.

Applying Rule 4 to one winning leg in a multiple:

  • Adjusted decimal formula: adjusted = 1 + (original − 1) × (1 − deduction).
  • Example double (Win-only, £1 unit): Leg 1 at 4.00 with 20p Rule 4 → 1 + 3 × 0.80 = 3.40; Leg 2 at 3.00 → unchanged. Return = £1 × 3.40 × 3.00 = £10.20 (vs £12.00 with no deduction).

Dead-heats vs Each-Way:

  • Win market dead-heat: divide the winning stake by n tied runners and settle at full odds; the remainder of the stake is lost.
  • Place market dead-heat: divide the Place stake among the number sharing the final place(s) and settle at place odds.

Margin compounding:

  • If each leg’s offered decimal is, say, 2% lower than the “fair” no-margin price on the winnings portion, the product across four legs multiplies that haircut four times, noticeably reducing the accumulator’s return.
  • Practical takeaway: marginal price differences per leg can add up substantially in a 15-line multiple.

Always mirror the exact inputs from your slip—odds format, Each-Way status, place fraction and places, Rule 4 (if any), and any dead-heat or voids—to align calculator outputs with bookmaker settlement. Promotions such as one-winner or all-correct bonuses, and brand maximum-payout caps, are applied after the core math and may change the final figure you receive.

FAQs and tips for UK bettors

This section answers common UK-focused questions about using a Lucky 15 calculator, including whether it’s free and accurate, which sports it supports (and how Each-Way behaves), and the most frequent staking and Each-Way mistakes that cause mismatches with bookmaker settlements.

Is the Lucky 15 calculator free, accurate, and updated today?

Yes—this calculator is free to use. It applies the standard UK settlement logic for Lucky 15s: 15 lines from four selections, optional Each-Way duplication, per-selection place terms, Rule 4 deductions on affected winnings, dead-heat stake division, void legs at 1.00, and penny rounding. Accuracy depends on mirroring your slip: enter the same odds format, place fraction and number of places, Rule 4 (if any), and the exact results. Promotions (e.g., one-winner or all-correct bonuses), maximum-payout caps, and brand-specific rounding nuances are applied by the bookmaker after the core math, so treat the calculator as a neutral baseline.

What it models:

  • Fractional, decimal, and American odds; conversion is deterministic and transparent.
  • Each-Way settlement using the place fraction (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4) and qualifying places you enter.
  • Rule 4 as pence in the pound on winnings for impacted legs; combined deductions capped at 90p/£1.
  • Dead-heats by dividing the winning stake by the number of tied runners and settling at full odds.
  • Void/non-runner legs at 1.00, reducing multiples to the remaining selections.

What bookies may add or limit:

  • Promotions (one-winner, all-correct), price boosts, or Best Odds Guaranteed (where offered).
  • Operator-specific rounding and product maximum-payout caps.
  • “Extra places” markets with non-standard place counts and sometimes different fractions.

Can you use it for football, horses, and mixed sports in the UK?

Yes—with an important distinction. Lucky 15s work across sports, but Each-Way only makes sense where a place market exists (e.g., horse racing, greyhounds, golf outrights, and some ante-post markets). Standard football match markets (e.g., 1X2) are win-only and don’t have Each-Way place terms. If your bet includes any non-Each-Way selections, keep the Lucky 15 as win-only, or ensure every leg is Each-Way-eligible before ticking Each-Way. In the calculator, mirror reality: set place terms only for legs that are genuinely Each-Way markets, and leave non-EW legs as win-only.

Typical UK availability:

  • Horse racing and greyhounds: Each-Way common; place terms depend on race/field size; extra places may apply.
  • Golf outrights: Each-Way common with extended places (e.g., 1/5 odds 1–8) during majors.
  • Football: Match markets are win-only; some ante-post outrights can be Each-Way with specified places.
  • Other sports: Check market rules; many are win-only unless explicitly quoted Each-Way.

Best practice for mixed slips:

  • Either place a win-only Lucky 15, or construct a separate Each-Way Lucky 15 containing only EW-eligible legs.
  • Match odds formats and place terms exactly to your bookmaker slip to avoid discrepancies.

What mistakes do bettors make with each-way and stakes?

The most common errors are misunderstanding unit stake versus total outlay, misapplying Each-Way where no place market exists, and overlooking how Rule 4 and dead-heats change effective odds or stakes. Use the checklist below to avoid hidden leaks.

Stake confusion:

  • Each-Way doubles the lines: 15 Win parts + 15 Place parts. Your unit stake applies to each part.
  • Total outlay = number of parts × unit stake (30 × stake for Each-Way; 15 × stake for Win-only).

Each-Way eligibility:

  • Don’t tick Each-Way if any selection has no place market (e.g., standard football match result).
  • For racing, confirm both the fraction (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4) and the number of places (e.g., 1–3 or 1–4), especially on extra-place cards.

Settlement details:

  • Rule 4 applies to winnings only and compounds across multiples via adjusted decimals.
  • Dead-heats divide the winning stake by the number tied and settle at full odds; place DH divides the Place stake among those sharing the final place(s).
  • Voids/non-runners settle at 1.00 and reduce an affected multiple to the remaining legs.

Rounding and promos:

  • Bookmakers round to the nearest penny and may apply or withhold promos per their terms; a baseline calculator won’t auto-apply these.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are Lucky 15 winnings taxed in the UK?

For UK residents, gambling winnings are not taxed; the tax rate on personal betting returns is 0% and no self-assessment entry is required. Operators pay betting duty, not punters. Practical takeaway: a £500 return from a Lucky 15 is paid as £500 net to you, with 0 forms to file and 0 income tax due as of 2026.

How do Best Odds Guaranteed (BOG) affect a Lucky 15 return?

BOG upsizes the price on each winning racing leg if SP exceeds your taken odds, and the higher price then flows through every affected line. Example: a £1 win single at 4/1 (5.00) that drifts to 6/1 (7.00) pays £7.00 instead of £5.00, a 40% uplift; in a double, replacing one 5.00 with 7.00 turns £1 × 5.00 × 3.00 = £15.00 into £1 × 7.00 × 3.00 = £21.00. Calculators show core settlement; apply BOG leg-by-leg where your bookmaker terms say it applies.

Can I place a Lucky 15 with free bet stakes and how do I calculate the return?

If allowed by the bookmaker, SR (stake not returned) free bets pay winnings only on each winning line. To model it, use return = stake × (decimal − 1) for every winning line, not stake × decimal. Example: a £1 unit on a 4.00 winner returns £3.00 with SR (4.00 − 1.00) versus £4.00 with cash; if three lines win at 4.00, the free-bet-funded total is £9.00 instead of £12.00. Many UK operators restrict tokens on full-cover multiples, so check eligibility before staking.

How does partial Cash Out change a Lucky 15 settlement?

Partial Cash Out sells a percentage of the entire bet and scales both remaining stakes and eventual returns by the unsold proportion. If you cash out 40% of a £0.50-unit Lucky 15, the live portion is 60%: each line’s unit becomes £0.30, and any later return is 0.6 × the amount it would have been without cash out. Example: a line that would have paid £20 now pays £12; total outlay recorded is £15 originally, with 40% of its live value realized immediately as cash-out and 60% left to settle.

What’s the difference between a Lucky 15, a Yankee, and a Canadian (Super Yankee)?

A Lucky 15 has 15 lines from 4 selections and includes singles; a Yankee has 11 lines from 4 selections without singles; a Canadian has 26 lines from 5 selections without singles. At £0.50 win-only, costs are £7.50 (Lucky 15), £5.50 (Yankee), and £13.00 (Canadian); at £0.50 each-way they double to £15.00, £11.00, and £26.00 respectively. The inclusion of singles in a Lucky 15 increases resilience because even one winner returns.

Can I include related contingencies in a Lucky 15?

No; UK bookmakers block multiples where outcomes are dependent, so related selections cannot be combined. Typical barred pairs include two markets from the same horse and race that logically overlap, for example Win and Without-Favourite on the same runner, or Scorecast-style football props with the same match result; attempting them results in rejection, or the operator reforming the slip into permitted lines only.

Do ante-post rules change Lucky 15 settlement for racing?

Yes; traditional ante-post bets usually have no Rule 4 and a non-runner is a losing bet, while NRNB markets refund the stake at 1.00 if the horse does not run. Example: in an ante-post Lucky 15, a non-runner leg contributes £0.00 to affected lines; in NRNB the same leg contributes 1.00 as a multiplier, turning, for example, an AB double into a single on B. Always match the market type per selection before computing returns.

How are multiple Rule 4 deductions combined on a Lucky 15?

Rule 4s on the same race add together and are capped at 90p per £1, and they reduce winnings only via adjusted decimal = 1 + (original − 1) × (1 − combined deduction). Example: a 5.00 winner with two withdrawals at 10p and 15p combines to 25p; adjusted = 1 + 4 × 0.75 = 4.00, so a £1 win single pays £4.00 instead of £5.00. If later a further 70p applied, the total would cap at 90p, giving adjusted = 1 + 4 × 0.10 = 1.40.

How are dead-heats for the last place settled when extra places are paid?

You divide the place stake by the number of runners sharing the final qualifying place and settle at place odds. Example: an each-way 1/5 odds, places 1–4 market has a three-way tie for 4th; a £1 place stake at 10/1 has place odds of 10/1 × 1/5 = 2/1 (3.00 decimal), so the return is £1 ÷ 3 × 3.00 = £1.00; two-thirds of the place stake is lost due to the dead-heat split.

Which rounding method should I use to match UK bookmaker payouts to the penny?

Most UK operators round to the nearest penny at settlement, but the rounding point can be per-line or on the final total, creating 1p to 2p differences. Example: three winning lines at £0.333 each round per line to £0.33 + £0.33 + £0.33 = £0.99, but rounding on the total gives £1.00; conversely, a single line at £1.235 rounds to £1.24. If you need exact parity, replicate your bookmaker’s rounding point consistently.

Why does my Lucky 15 calculator result differ from William Hill or Betfred by a few pence?

Small gaps usually come from rounding point, applied promotions, or market settings mismatches. Typical causes include per-line versus total rounding worth 1p to 2p on small stakes, BOG lifting SP on winners which raises affected lines by 10% to 50% when drifts are large, one-winner or all-correct bonuses adding, for example, 10% to winnings, and place-term or Rule 4 differences where a 20p deduction cuts the winnings portion by 20% on that leg and compounds in multiples.

With exactly two selections placed and none winning, how many lines pay on an each-way Lucky 15?

Three place lines pay: two place singles and one place double; all win-part lines lose. If the two placers have place decimals of 2.50 and 3.00 with a £0.50 each-way unit, the returns are £0.50 × 2.50 + £0.50 × 3.00 + £0.50 × 2.50 × 3.00 = £1.25 + £1.50 + £3.75 = £6.50 from those three lines only.

How do I convert negative American odds to fractional for Lucky 15 inputs?

Use fractional = 100/|A| for negative American A and simplify. Examples: −200 converts to 100/200 = 1/2, −150 converts to 100/150 = 2/3 which is commonly expressed as 4/6, and −120 converts to 100/120 = 5/6; the equivalent decimals are 1.50, 1.67, and 1.83 respectively. If you prefer decimals in the calculator, use decimal = 1 + 100/|A| for negative prices.

How can I estimate break-even strike rate and expected value (EV) for a Lucky 15?

Sum EV across the 15 component bets: EV(line) = stake × product of leg probabilities × product of decimals, and break-even occurs when the total EV equals the total stake. With four fair legs at decimal 3.00 each and win probability 1/3, a single’s EV is s × 1/3 × 3.00 = 1.00s, a double’s is s × (1/3)^2 × 3^2 = 1.00s, and likewise for trebles and the fourfold; summing 4 singles + 6 doubles + 4 trebles + 1 fourfold returns 15s against 15s staked. If each leg’s decimal is haircut by 5% on the winnings portion to 2.90, a single’s EV becomes s × 1/3 × 2.90 = 0.967s and the 15-line EV sums to about 14.5s, implying a roughly 3.3% shortfall versus total outlay.

lucky-31.articleHtml:
What is a Lucky 31 and how does the calculator work?

A Lucky 31 is a full-cover bet with singles on five selections. It creates 31 separate lines: every possible single, double, treble, fourfold and the fivefold accumulator. A calculator processes each line independently based on your stake per line, odds format (fractional or decimal), and the result of each selection (win, lose, or void/non-runner). It then sums all winning line returns and shows total return, total stake, and profit or loss. Voids reduce affected multiples (treating the voided leg at odds of 1.00), while losers make that specific line return zero. If placed each-way, the calculator duplicates all lines into win and place parts and applies the relevant place terms.

Bet breakdown: 31 lines from singles to fivefold

A Lucky 31 uses five selections to create every combination from singles to the fivefold. The line count is fixed and does not change with stake size.

  • Singles: 5 lines (one on each selection)
  • Doubles: 10 lines (all two-selection combos)
  • Trebles: 10 lines (all three-selection combos)
  • Fourfolds: 5 lines (all four-selection combos)
  • Fivefold: 1 line (all five selections together)
  • Total: 31 lines
Line typeNumber of linesSelections per lineCombinatorics
Singles515 choose 1 = 5
Doubles1025 choose 2 = 10
Trebles1035 choose 3 = 10
Fourfolds545 choose 4 = 5
Fivefold155 choose 5 = 1
Total31--

How are returns calculated for winners, losers, and voids?

Each line is settled independently and then summed. For a win-only Lucky 31 with unit stake s per line:

  • Convert odds to decimal if needed. For fractional a/b, decimal = 1 + a/b.
  • Winning line return = s × (product of decimal odds of all winning legs in that line).
  • If any leg in a line loses, that line returns £0.00.
  • If a leg is void/non-runner, treat it as decimal 1.00 in that line (the multiple reduces). A void single returns the stake.
  • Total return = sum of all line returns. Profit/Loss = Total return − (31 × s).

Worked example (win-only, £1.00 unit stake per line): five selections A–E with outcomes and decimal odds: A wins @ 3.50 (5/2), B loses @ 2.00 (Evens), C wins @ 4.00 (3/1), D loses @ 5.00 (4/1), E void @ 1.00 (treated as 1.00). Returns are below for all 31 lines.

13DoubleCDLose (D)-0.00

16TrebleABCLose (B)-0.00

19TrebleACDLose (D)-0.00
20TrebleACEVoid reduces to AC3.50 × 4.00 × 1.00 = 14.0014.00

22TrebleBCDLose (B/D)-0.00

27FourfoldABCEVoid to ABC (loses)3.50 × 2.00 × 4.00 × 1.000.00
28FourfoldABDEVoid to ABD (loses)3.50 × 2.00 × 5.00 × 1.000.00
29FourfoldACDEVoid to ACD (loses)3.50 × 4.00 × 5.00 × 1.000.00
30FourfoldBCDEVoid to BCD (loses)2.00 × 4.00 × 5.00 × 1.000.00

Total return in this example = £44.00. Total stake = 31 × £1.00 = £31.00. Profit = £13.00. Note: rounding follows standard bookmaker practice to two decimal places.

How does each-way change win and place returns?

Each-way (EW) doubles a Lucky 31 to 62 lines: 31 win lines and 31 place lines. Your total stake doubles because you are placing two bets on each line. The win part uses full win odds; the place part uses reduced place odds based on the market’s place terms (for example, 1/5 or 1/4 of the win odds, with the number of places determined by the event rules). A winner also counts as “placed” for the place part.

  • Stake: An EW Lucky 31 at s per line stakes 62 × s in total.

Place odds conversion:

  • Fractional: (a/b) at place fraction f becomes (a × f) / b.
  • Decimal: place_decimal = 1 + (win_decimal − 1) × f.

Settlement:

  • Win part: every leg in that line must win to return.
  • Place part: every leg in that line must finish in a place (winners count as placed) to return.
  • Voids: treated as odds 1.00 on both win and place parts (the multiple reduces).

Example A — each-way single (£1 EW, total £2 stake), selection @ 9/2 with 1/5 place terms:

OutcomeWin part (decimal 5.50)Place part (decimal 1.90)Total return (£)Profit/Loss (£)
Wins£1 × 5.50 = 5.50£1 × 1.90 = 1.907.40+5.40
Placed (not 1st)0.00£1 × 1.90 = 1.901.90−0.10

In an each-way Lucky 31, these same win and place settlement rules apply to every line. The calculator adds the win-part returns and place-part returns across all 62 lines and then subtracts the total stake to show your overall profit or loss.

Using the Lucky 31 Calculator: a quick guide

This guide shows exactly how to input your five selections, choose odds formats, set outcomes, and apply each-way and Rule 4 deductions so you can see total return, total stake, and profit for a Lucky 31. The calculator treats each of the 31 lines independently, applies your settings, and aggregates the results with standard UK settlement conventions and two-decimal rounding.

Enter odds, results, and each-way or Rule 4 deductions

Follow these steps to set up and settle a Lucky 31 correctly. The same inputs are applied consistently to every affected line of the bet.

  • Select odds format (fractional, decimal, or American) and stake per line. Remember, a win-only Lucky 31 has 31 lines; each-way doubles to 62 lines.

For each of the five selections, enter the odds and choose a result:

  • Win: the selection won (for EW, this also counts as placed).
  • Lose: the selection lost (that line returns £0 on the relevant part).
  • Void/Non-runner: treated at decimal 1.00; multiples reduce, single returns stake.
  • Dead-heat (if provided): the calculator applies the standard reduction by the number of tied runners for the winning/place portion.
  • Toggle Each-Way (optional). If on, enter the place terms (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4) applicable to the market. The calculator will duplicate all 31 lines into win and place parts and apply reduced place odds to the place part.

Apply Rule 4 deductions (only if your race had a late withdrawal). Enter the deduction as pence in the pound (e.g., 20p = 0.20). The Rule 4 deduction reduces the winnings portion only:

  • Adjusted decimal for settlement = 1 + (original_decimal − 1) × (1 − deduction).
  • Adjusted fractional profit = original_fraction × (1 − deduction).
  • Example: 4/1 with a 20p Rule 4 becomes profit odds 4 × 0.8 = 3.2 (decimal 4.20).

Review totals:

  • Total stake = lines × stake per line (31×s for win-only; 62×s for EW).
  • Total return = sum of all winning line returns (after deductions and place terms).
  • Profit/Loss = Total return − Total stake.

Can I use fractional, decimal, or American odds?

Yes. You can enter odds in fractional (UK), decimal (EU), or American (US) formats. The calculator converts them internally for accurate settlement. Use these standard conversions and probability checks to verify your inputs:

  • Fractional a/b to decimal: 1 + a/b. Implied probability = b / (a + b).
  • Decimal d to American: if d ≥ 2.00, +100 × (d − 1); if d < 2.00, −100 ÷ (d − 1).
  • American +X to decimal: 1 + X/100; American −Y to decimal: 1 + 100/Y.
  • Implied probability from decimal: 1 ÷ d.
