Tip: For each-way markets, convert the win odds first, then apply the place fraction to the profit portion only. For example, 9/2 at 1/5 terms: win decimal 5.50; place decimal = 1 + (5.50 − 1) × 0.2 = 1.90.

Save, clear, and share your calculation

Most calculators include controls to help you manage and communicate your bet breakdown. Here is how they typically work and what to check for:

  • Save/Permalink: Creates a shareable URL encoding your inputs (stake, EW flag, odds, results, deductions). Anyone with the link can load the same calculation state.
  • Copy/Share: Copies the permalink to your clipboard for quick sharing in chat or email. Verify that sensitive notes are not embedded before sharing.
  • Export/Print: Produces a readable summary (often CSV or PDF-ready print view) listing all 31 lines, returns per line, total stake, total return, and profit.
  • Clear/Reset: Wipes all fields and results to start again. Use with care—unsaved changes are lost.
  • Local save: Some tools store your last inputs in your browser (localStorage). If you change device or clear cache, reload from your permalink to restore the state.

Best practice: After saving or sharing, reload the saved link to confirm it reproduces the exact same totals before you rely on it for staking or reconciliation.

How much does a Lucky 31 cost at 20p or 50p stakes?

Your total cost (outlay) for a Lucky 31 depends solely on the unit stake you place per line and whether you choose each-way. A Lucky 31 contains 31 lines in win-only mode; switching to each-way duplicates the bet into 62 lines (win and place parts), so the total outlay doubles. The calculator multiplies your stake per line by the number of lines to display total stake and shows profit or loss after settlement.

Stake and unit stake: total outlay with and without each-way

Define unit stake s as the amount you place on each individual line. The formulas are straightforward and hold for any currency amount:

  • Win-only total stake = 31 × s
  • Each-way total stake = 62 × s (because each line is duplicated into win and place parts)

Examples: at £0.20 per line, a win-only Lucky 31 costs £6.20; each-way costs £12.40. At £0.50 per line, win-only costs £15.50; each-way costs £31.00.

Unit stake (s) per lineLines (win-only)Total stake win-only (£)Lines (each-way)Total stake each-way (£)
£0.1031£3.1062£6.20
£0.2031£6.2062£12.40
£0.5031£15.5062£31.00
£1.0031£31.0062£62.00
£2.0031£62.0062£124.00

Does each-way double the cost for a Lucky 31?

Yes. Each-way turns 31 win-only lines into 62 lines by adding an equal-stake place part alongside the win part for every line. Because you are staking on two parts (win and place) for each of the same 31 combinations, the total stake doubles. This doubling happens regardless of the odds or place terms; place terms affect returns, not the cost.

  • Example at £0.50 per line: win-only costs £15.50; each-way costs £31.00.
  • Example at £0.20 per line: win-only costs £6.20; each-way costs £12.40.
  • Rule 4 deductions, voids, and dead-heats can change returns but never change the total stake already placed.

What is my total liability and how is it shown?

For fixed-odds bookmaker bets like a Lucky 31, your total liability is simply your total stake (the amount you risk and can lose). The calculator shows this clearly as “Total stake,” alongside “Total return” and “Profit/Loss.” Liability here is not the same as exchange lay liability—it does not depend on odds; it equals the money you have staked.

  • Win-only liability (exposure) = 31 × s
  • Each-way liability (exposure) = 62 × s
  • Display conventions: calculators typically show Stake per line, Lines, Total stake, Estimated return based on current inputs, and Profit/Loss = Return − Total stake.

Examples:

  • £0.20 per line: liability is £6.20 (win-only) or £12.40 (each-way).
  • £0.50 per line: liability is £15.50 (win-only) or £31.00 (each-way).

If you use a free bet token (stake not returned), your out-of-pocket liability may be reduced, but the calculator will reflect this only if it offers a “free bet” or “stake not returned” toggle; otherwise liability equals the cash stake you enter.

Is a Lucky 31 right for me compared with other perms?

Full-cover bets let you spread five or more selections across many combinations to balance the chance of a return against total cost and volatility. The Lucky 31 is a five-selection full cover that includes singles (31 lines). Its closest no-singles analogue is the Canadian (also known as a Super Yankee) with 26 lines. For four selections you would typically choose a Lucky 15 (includes singles) or a Yankee (no singles). For three selections a Patent is the full-cover with singles, while six selections are usually covered by a Heinz (no singles). Choosing the right perm depends on how many picks you have, whether you want singles, your typical prices, and your bankroll.

Lucky 31 vs Lucky 15 vs Yankee: when to choose each?

Match the perm to your number of selections and appetite for singles, cost, and volatility. These are common use-cases:

  • Lucky 31 (5 selections, 31 lines incl. singles): Choose when you have five picks and want the safety of singles. You accept a higher outlay than a Canadian (26 lines) in exchange for more frequent small returns.
  • Lucky 15 (4 selections, 15 lines incl. singles): Choose when you have four picks and value having a return even if only one wins. Total cost is moderate compared with five-selection perms.
  • Yankee (4 selections, 11 lines, no singles): Choose when you expect at least two winners among four picks and want a lower stake than a Lucky 15. Suits bettors comfortable without singles.

Cost comparison examples at £0.50 per line (win-only): Yankee £5.50, Lucky 15 £7.50, Lucky 31 £15.50. Each-way doubles each total. If you want to keep total outlay similar across perms, reduce the unit stake as line count rises.

Patent37Yes1£1.4014£2.80

Notes: “Minimum winners for any return” refers to win-only settlement. Each-way doubles line counts and outlay but can produce place-only returns even without a winner, depending on place terms and outcomes.

When is a Patent or Heinz a better fit?

Pick a Patent or Heinz when your number of selections or volatility target sits at either end of the spectrum:

  • Patent (3 selections, 7 lines incl. singles): Best when you have exactly three picks and want the singles “safety net.” One winner can return something, while two or three winners scale up through doubles and the treble. Useful for shorter-priced selections where singles still return meaningful amounts.
  • Heinz (6 selections, 57 lines, no singles): Best for six picks when you prefer lower outlay per line than adding singles would require, and you expect multiple winners. It is high-line-count and high-volatility; staking should be sized accordingly. If you need singles with six selections, consider a Lucky 63 (adds singles to a Heinz), but be aware the cost rises to 63 lines win-only.

If your typical strategy is to seek two or more winners at decent prices, no-singles perms (Yankee, Canadian, Heinz) concentrate stake on multiples. If you value frequent smaller returns and tracking performance selection-by-selection, full-cover with singles (Patent, Lucky 15, Lucky 31, Lucky 63) is usually more appropriate.

Does a Lucky 31 amplify variance and risk?

It depends on your comparison point and how you size the stake:

  • Versus five singles only: A Lucky 31 amplifies variance because most stake is in multiples; payouts can be much larger when several win, but you can also lose the whole stake if results go against you.
  • Versus a Canadian (26 lines, no singles): A Lucky 31 generally reduces payout variance per unit stake because singles add more chances of a small return; however, total exposure is higher (31 vs 26 lines). At the same unit stake s, the Lucky 31 risks 31×s versus 26×s.
  • Stake-normalised view: If you fix a total budget B, you can reduce the unit stake for a higher-line perm so that B is constant (e.g., s = B/31 for a Lucky 31, B/26 for a Canadian). With equal budgets, including singles usually smooths returns but shifts some stake away from high-multiple lines, trimming extreme upside.
  • Each-way effect: EW doubles outlay and can reduce variance further because place parts can return without winners, but your cash at risk also doubles.

In short: singles decrease volatility of outcomes but increase the number of lines (and therefore potential outlay) if your unit stake per line is unchanged. Tailor stake size to your bankroll and objective—steady tracking of picks (include singles) versus concentrated shots at higher multiples (exclude singles).

Brands, bonuses, history, and responsible play

Our Lucky 31 calculator follows standard UK settlement rules so your estimates align with how leading bookmakers settle full-cover bets. It supports each-way terms, Rule 4 deductions, dead-heats, and void reductions commonly applied by major UK brands. Below you will find brand compatibility notes, a short history of how “Lucky” permutations emerged on UK high streets, and essential resources for safe gambling in Great Britain.

Paddy Power, Betfred, Ladbrokes, William Hill compatibility

The calculator is designed to mirror standard settlement conventions used across major UK bookmakers for full-cover bets including Lucky 31. It handles singles through fivefolds, each-way duplication, Rule 4, and dead-heat adjustments in a way that matches widely accepted industry practice. Always check the specific terms for your event (e.g., place fractions and number of places) on your bookmaker’s site before placing a bet.

Notes: “Tattersalls Rule 4” (often shortened to Rule 4) is a standard UK deduction applied to winnings when a horse is withdrawn after pricing. Dead-heat rules proportionally divide either stake or profit depending on the sport and market rules. Place fractions and number of places vary by race type and declared runners; check the racecard and terms at your bookmaker before staking.

Origins of Lucky permutations in UK betting

“Lucky” bets are UK full-cover multiples with singles included, designed to give more frequent small returns while preserving upside when several selections win. They grew out of high-street multiple betting in the mid–20th century and became popular as simple, named packages covering all combinations for a given number of selections.

  • Patent (3 selections, 7 lines): 3 singles, 3 doubles, 1 treble.
  • Lucky 15 (4 selections, 15 lines): 4 singles, 6 doubles, 4 trebles, 1 fourfold.
  • Lucky 31 (5 selections, 31 lines): 5 singles, 10 doubles, 10 trebles, 5 fourfolds, 1 fivefold.
  • Lucky 63 (6 selections, 63 lines): 6 singles, 15 doubles, 20 trebles, 15 fourfolds, 6 fivefolds, 1 sixfold.

Alongside these, no-singles “full-cover” variants also became staples (e.g., Yankee for four selections, Canadian/Super Yankee for five, Heinz for six). The “Lucky” naming convention helped bettors quickly choose a structure that matched their number of picks and appetite for smaller, more frequent returns (via singles) versus concentrating stake on higher multiples for larger but rarer payouts.

Play safely: UK age limits, limits, and support resources

In Great Britain you must be 18 or older to place bets with licensed operators. Set personal limits and treat gambling as entertainment, not income. If betting stops being fun, pause and seek support. The resources below are independent and free to access:

  • UK Gambling Commission (licensing, rules, consumer guidance): [https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk)
  • BeGambleAware (information and help): [https://www.begambleaware.org](https://www.begambleaware.org)
  • GamCare (advice, live chat, treatment): [https://www.gamcare.org.uk](https://www.gamcare.org.uk)
  • National Gambling Helpline (24/7, free): [0808 8020 133](tel:08088020133)
  • GAMSTOP (free UK-wide online self-exclusion): [https://www.gamstop.co.uk](https://www.gamstop.co.uk)
  • Time-outs, deposit limits, and reality checks: enable these tools in your bookmaker account settings.

If you live in Great Britain, only use operators licensed by the UK Gambling Commission. For concerns about an operator, you can find official guidance and contact routes at [https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk). If you or someone you know is at risk, reach out to the National Gambling Helpline at [0808 8020 133](tel:08088020133) or visit [https://www.gamcare.org.uk](https://www.gamcare.org.uk) for confidential support.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do dead-heats affect a Lucky 31 settlement?

In a dead-heat for a winning position, the affected line’s stake is divided by the number of tied runners and the odds stay the same; for place markets, if H horses tie for the final K payable places, the effective stake is multiplied by K/H. Example (win dead-heat): £2 single at 4/1 in a two-way dead-heat returns (2 ÷ 2) × (4 + 1) = £5. Example (place dead-heat): with 1/5 terms and three tied for the final one payable place, a £2 place stake is treated as £2 × (1 ÷ 3) at the place odds; if the place decimal was 1.80, the return is (2 ÷ 3) × 1.80 = £1.20. In multiples, the dead-heat factor simply scales that leg’s contribution: a two-way win dead-heat multiplies the line’s return by 1/2, while a three-for-two tie on the last paying place multiplies it by 2/3.

How are multiple Rule 4 deductions applied across different legs in a Lucky 31?

Each affected leg is adjusted independently on its profit portion before combining lines. If a leg has decimal d and a deduction r (e.g., 0.20 for 20p), its adjusted decimal is 1 + (d − 1) × (1 − r). Worked example: Leg A 7/2 (decimal 4.50) with 20p Rule 4 becomes 1 + (4.50 − 1) × 0.80 = 3.80; Leg C 3/1 (4.00) with 10p becomes 1 + 3 × 0.90 = 3.70. A double using A and C settles at 3.80 × 3.70 = 14.06 per £1 on that line. Legs without deductions remain unchanged.

In what order should I apply place terms, Rule 4, and dead-heat adjustments?

Apply odds transformations in this sequence on each affected leg: calculate place odds if each-way, then apply Rule 4 to the profit portion, then apply dead-heat reduction to the effective stake/return factor. Micro-case: Win odds 9/2 (decimal 5.50) with 1/5 place terms and 20p Rule 4, dead-heat two-way for first. Place decimal first: 1 + (5.50 − 1) × 0.2 = 1.90. Apply Rule 4 on profit: win decimal becomes 1 + 4.50 × 0.8 = 4.60; place decimal becomes 1 + 0.90 × 0.8 = 1.72. Apply dead-heat factor 1/2 to the relevant part(s): a win dead-heat halves the win-part contribution; a place dead-heat halves the place-part contribution.

What happens to returns if two or more selections are void in a Lucky 31?

Voids reduce the affected legs to decimal 1.00 in every line they appear, so combinations compress to smaller multiples and void singles simply return the unit stake. If two legs are void and three run, all lines effectively settle as if built from the three remaining selections: you’ll receive stake back on the two void singles, reduced multiples wherever a void leg appeared, and full settlement on combinations using only the three runners. If all five are void, each line returns exactly the unit stake and total profit is zero.

Can a Lucky 31 be placed with free bet (stake not returned) funds and how is it settled?

If a line uses a stake-not-returned fund, its first winning leg contributes profit only; the initial stake is excluded from the rollover. For a k-leg winning line with decimals d1…dk and free stake s, return equals s × (d1 − 1) × d2 × … × dk. Example: free £1 on a double 4.00 and 3.00 pays £1 × (4 − 1) × 3 = £9; the equivalent cash stake would have paid £12.

What are the typical UK each-way place terms by race type and field size?

Standard industry terms are commonly: handicaps with 16+ runners pay 1/4 the odds for 1–4, handicaps with 12–15 pay 1/4 for 1–3, races with 8–11 runners pay 1/5 for 1–3, and races with 5–7 runners pay 1/5 for 1–2; below 5 runners, each-way is usually not offered. Some meetings offer enhanced places or special terms; always use the exact fraction and places shown for the market you bet.

Are ‘one winner’ or ‘all correct’ Lucky 31 bonuses included in calculator returns?

Unless a dedicated bonus toggle is provided, headline bonuses are not included because they are brand- and market-specific. As a check, compute base returns first, then apply the published bonus rate to the qualifying scenario, for example adding 10% to total returns on all-correct, or enhancing a single winning single to the specified boosted payout; record the adjustment separately to avoid double counting.

How much can rounding change my final total on a 31-line settlement?

Bookmakers settle to two decimal places and differences arise depending on whether they round per line then sum, or sum raw values then round. The practical impact is small: if 31 lines each sit within ±£0.005 of a penny boundary, the total can differ by up to about £0.31 between the two methods; matching your bookmaker’s per-line rounding policy yields the closest replication.

Can I use a Lucky 31 for football, tennis, or golf markets?

Yes; the structure is sport-agnostic as long as each selection is a fixed-odds market that can settle as win, lose, or void. Each-way and Rule 4 are most common in horse and greyhound racing; in football or tennis you’ll usually use win-only markets, and postponements typically result in void legs per the book’s rules.

How are postponed or abandoned events treated within a Lucky 31?

They settle as void legs if the bookmaker’s time window or rescheduling conditions aren’t met, leaving the affected combinations to reduce accordingly. For example, if a match is abandoned and settled void, every line that includes it treats that leg at decimal 1.00, while singles on that leg return unit stake; consult the sport-specific cut-off (often 24–72 hours) in the event rules before marking the outcome.

What is the maximum and minimum possible return from a win-only Lucky 31?

The minimum is £0 if every running leg loses; the maximum occurs when all five win and equals unit stake multiplied by the sum of all non-empty products of the five decimals. Example with equal decimal 3.00 and £1 unit stake: total return is 5×3 + 10×9 + 10×27 + 5×81 + 1×243 = £1,023, reflecting singles, doubles, trebles, fourfolds, and the fivefold.

How many winners are typically needed to break even on a Lucky 31?

It depends on the average odds. With equal decimal d and exactly m winners, the return is s × [C(m,1)d + C(m,2)d² + … + d^m]; break-even occurs when this meets or exceeds the total outlay. Benchmarks with £1 stakes: at decimal 2.00, three winners return 26 ( 31); at decimal 3.00, two winners return 15 ( 31).

How do I enter non-standard fractions like 100/30 or 11/10 correctly?

Convert the fraction to decimal as 1 + a/b and enter the exact price rather than rounding to a nearby popular fraction. Examples: 100/30 becomes 1 + 100/30 = 4.3333… (decimal 4.3333, often shown as 10/3), and 11/10 becomes 1 + 11/10 = 2.10; using precise decimals preserves accuracy when lines multiply.

Are UK taxes or commissions applied to Lucky 31 returns?

For UK-licensed fixed-odds bookmakers, customer winnings are paid tax-free, so no income or betting tax is deducted from returns. If a platform charges commission (e.g., exchanges), that is separate from a Lucky 31 calculator model; the calculator outputs gross returns based on odds and outcomes.

How are place dead-heats with more ties than remaining places calculated?

Use a proportional reduction: if H horses tie for the final K paying places, the line’s place stake is multiplied by K/H and settled at the place odds. Example: with places 1–4 and five horses tying for fourth, the place factor is 1/5; a £2 place component at decimal 1.80 returns (2 × 1/5) × 1.80 = £0.72 for that leg within any affected line.

Do bookmaker maximum payout limits affect a Lucky 31 result from the calculator?

Yes, if the theoretical return exceeds the bookmaker’s cap for that sport and market, the paid amount will be restricted to the limit even though the calculator shows the full theoretical figure. Because limits vary by brand, event grade, and channel, compare your computed total with the operator’s published maximum for that market before staking.

What if my unit stake is below the bookmaker’s minimum, like 5p or 10p?

The calculator will show mathematical returns for any stake, but operators enforce minimum unit stakes, commonly between £0.05 and £0.10 online. If your input is below the book’s minimum per line, the bet may be rejected or auto-adjusted; verify the platform’s minimums to ensure the stake is executable.

How can I compute expected value (EV) for a Lucky 31 with my own probabilities?

For each line, EV equals unit stake × product of decimals × product of your win probabilities for that line; total EV is the sum across all lines, and EV profit equals EV return minus total outlay. If all five picks share decimal d and win probability p and are independent, EV return simplifies to s × [(1 + d p)^5 − 1]; for d = 2.50 and p = 0.38, EV return ≈ s × [(1 + 0.95)^5 − 1] = s × 27.20, implying an EV loss of about 31s − 27.20s = 3.80s, while at p = 0.42 it becomes s × [(1 + 1.05)^5 − 1] ≈ s × 33.39, an EV profit of 2.39s.

How are negative American odds handled inside a Lucky 31?

Convert to decimal using 1 + 100/|Y| for negative prices and 1 + X/100 for positive prices, then proceed normally. Examples: −120 becomes 1 + 100/120 = 1.8333, +140 becomes 1 + 140/100 = 2.40; a double of −120 and +140 settles at 1.8333 × 2.40 = 4.3999 per £1 on that line.

Does the calculator support partial cash-out of lines mid-event?

No; partial cash-out changes stake and odds dynamically on selected lines, creating a new position that depends on the operator’s cash-out algorithm. To audit such scenarios, record the cashed-out value and treat the remaining position as a new, smaller perm on the unresolved legs.

How do non-runner reductions interact with each-way place markets in the same race?

When a deduction is announced for a race, the same Rule 4 rate typically applies to both the win and the linked place market, reducing only the profit portion on each part. Example: at 1/5 terms with win decimal 6.00 and a 15p deduction, adjusted win decimal is 1 + (6 − 1) × 0.85 = 5.25, adjusted place decimal is 1 + [(6 − 1) × 0.2] × 0.85 = 1 + 1.00 × 0.85 = 1.85.

Is a Lucky 31 the same as a five-pick full-cover parlay in US terms?

Functionally yes; it is the full set of all non-empty combinations from five selections, including singles, settled as separate tickets. The key differences are that UK settlement often includes each-way, Rule 4, and racing-specific dead-heat rules; if you omit each-way and racing adjustments, the math aligns with a five-leg full-cover parlay set.

How can I sanity-check inputs before settling a large Lucky 31?

Check three invariants: void singles must return exactly the unit stake, any line containing a losing leg returns zero for that part, and if all five win at decimal d with unit stake s, the total return must equal s × [5d + 10d² + 10d³ + 5d⁴ + d⁵]. For example, with d = 2.20 and s = £0.50, the all-winners return should be £0.50 × (11 + 48.4 + 106.48 + 118.58 + 51.536) ≈ £167.01.

lucky-63.articleHtml:
How do I use the free Lucky 63 calculator?

This tool lets you price a six-selection Lucky 63 in seconds. Enter six odds in your preferred format, choose each-way if required, decide whether you’re entering a stake per line or a total stake, and (optionally) set a result for each selection to see partial-settlement scenarios. A standard Lucky 63 contains 63 bets (6 singles, 15 doubles, 20 trebles, 15 four-folds, 6 five-folds, 1 six-fold). Selecting each-way doubles the number of bets to 126, splitting the stake equally between win and place parts.

  • Select an odds format (fractional, decimal, or American) and enter the odds for all six selections.
  • Choose stake mode: Stake per line (applies to each of the 63 or 126 bets) or Total stake (the tool divides equally across all bets).
  • Toggle Each-Way if you want win and place parts priced separately using the entered place terms.
  • Optionally assign outcomes per selection (Win, Lose, Void/Non-runner, Placed for each-way) to model real-world settlement.
  • Review total return, profit, and an itemised breakdown by bet type (singles through to the six-fold).

Which odds formats can I enter (fractional, decimal, American)?

The calculator accepts all major UK formats: fractional (e.g., 7/4), decimal (e.g., 2.75), and American (e.g., +175 or -125). You can mix formats across selections or switch formats at any time; the tool converts internally to decimal for calculation accuracy while preserving your chosen display format.

Odds formatExample inputEquivalent formatsNotes
Fractional7/4Decimal 2.75; American +175UK default for racing and football; returns include stake.
Fractional4/5Decimal 1.80; American -125Odds-on prices render as negative American odds.
Decimal3.50Fractional 5/2; American +250Decimal includes stake by definition.
American-200Decimal 1.50; Fractional 1/2Negative means stake required to win £100.
American+120Decimal 2.20; Fractional 6/5Positive means profit on a £100 stake.
  • Fractional to decimal: decimal = 1 + (numerator ÷ denominator).

Decimal to American:

  • If decimal ≥ 2.00: American = +100 × (decimal − 1).
  • If decimal < 2.00: American = −100 ÷ (decimal − 1).

Fractional to American:

  • If numerator ≥ denominator: American = +100 × (numerator ÷ denominator).
  • If numerator < denominator: American = −100 × (denominator ÷ numerator).

Stake per line vs total stake: what should I type?

In a Lucky 63, the stake is split equally across all bets (63 bets win-only; 126 bets if each-way). Use Stake per line to mirror how bookmakers settle each component bet. Use Total stake when you know your overall outlay and want the tool to divide it evenly across all bets.

Total stake£31.50No63£0.50£31.50

  • If you enter Total stake, the tool divides by 63 (win-only) or 126 (each-way) to find the per-line stake. Returns are then calculated line-by-line and summed.
  • To match bookmaker slips exactly, prefer Stake per line with amounts that make the total stake a whole-penny multiple of 63 (or 126 for each-way).
  • Each-way doubles the number of bets by creating identical place lines at the specified place terms; the overall outlay also doubles versus win-only at the same per-line stake.

Can the calculator split winners, losers, voids and non-runners?

Yes. For each of the six selections you can set a settlement state, and the calculator will recompute every affected line within the Lucky 63. This mirrors standard UK settlement logic for full-cover bets.

Selection statusHow it affects lines that include the selectionSettlement rule applied
WinLine continues at the entered odds; returns from that leg multiply into the multiple.Decimal multiplier equals the decimal odds (includes stake).
LoseAny line containing the selection loses (win-only). In each-way bets, the win part loses; the place part may still return if the selection placed.Multiplier 0.00 for the losing part.
Void / Non-runnerThe selection is treated as odds of 1.00 in all affected lines; a multiple reduces in fold count (e.g., a treble with a void becomes a double).Multiplier 1.00; stake for that leg is returned in singles and carried through in multiples.
Placed (each-way)Win part settles as Lose; place part settles at the place fraction of the odds according to the declared place terms.Place odds = (win odds − 1) × place fraction + 1 in decimal; fractional place terms vary by event/field size.
  • Mixed outcomes are handled across all 63 (or 126) bets automatically. For example, marking two winners, one placed (each-way), one void, and two losers will produce returns from all surviving singles, doubles, trebles, and higher folds that include only live legs.
  • If your event has special settlement (e.g., dead heats or Rule 4 deductions), use the advanced options provided by the tool to apply those adjustments before calculating returns.

What is a Lucky 63 bet and how are returns worked out?

A Lucky 63 is a full-cover bet on six selections comprising 63 separate lines: all singles, all doubles, all trebles, all four-folds, all five-folds, and the six-fold accumulator. Returns are calculated line-by-line and then summed. For each winning multiple, the unit stake on that line is multiplied by the product of the odds (converted to decimal) of the selections in that line. Voids/non-runners reduce the fold size within affected lines (treated as odds of 1.00), and only surviving lines with all legs settled as “win” (or “place” for the each-way place part) return.

How many bets are in a Lucky 63 and what are they?

The 63 lines come from the number of combinations you can form from six selections: C(6,1) singles, C(6,2) doubles, C(6,3) trebles, C(6,4) four-folds, C(6,5) five-folds, and C(6,6) six-fold. This ensures at least some cover even if only one or two selections win.

Bet typeLegs per line (r)Count formulaNumber of linesExample composition
Singles1C(6,1)6Any one of the 6 selections
Doubles2C(6,2)15All unique pairs from the 6 selections
Trebles3C(6,3)20All unique triplets from the 6 selections
Four-folds4C(6,4)15All unique sets of 4 from the 6 selections
Five-folds5C(6,5)6All unique sets of 5 from the 6 selections
Six-fold6C(6,6)1All 6 selections combined
Total—Σ C(6,r), r=1..663Full-cover including singles

Compared with a Heinz (which has 57 lines and excludes singles), a Lucky 63 includes the six singles for extra cover, which can be valuable when only one pick wins.

  • 6 singles provide baseline cover.
  • 15 doubles and 20 trebles build medium-risk, medium-return exposure.
  • 15 four-folds, 6 five-folds, and the six-fold provide higher-risk, higher-return potential.

Each-way Lucky 63: how does place and win return work?

An each-way (E/W) Lucky 63 doubles the number of lines to 126 by placing equal stakes on two parts of every line: the win part (at full odds) and the place part (at the stated place terms, typically a fraction such as 1/5 or 1/4 of the win odds). Settlement is independent for each part: the win part requires a win; the place part pays if the selection finishes in the designated places.

  • Stake split: Your entered per-line stake is divided equally between win and place parts (e.g., £1 E/W per line = £0.50 win + £0.50 place).

Place odds conversion:

  • Decimal: place_decimal = 1 + (win_decimal − 1) × place_fraction.
  • Fractional: place_fractional = (numerator/denominator × place_fraction) expressed as a new fraction, plus the returned stake in settlement.

Multiples settlement:

  • Win part of a multiple pays only if all legs win.
  • Place part of a multiple pays only if all legs place (not necessarily win).
  • Mixed outcomes (some win, some place) contribute via the place part only; the win part loses for any non-winning leg.
  • Select E/W and set the place fraction and place count per market (per standard terms).
  • The calculator prices 63 win lines and 63 place lines separately, then sums both to a total return and profit.
  • Voids/non-runners apply as 1.00 multipliers in both win and place parts, reducing fold size accordingly.

Historical path: from Yankee and Heinz to the Lucky 63 full cover

Full-cover combination bets evolved to give bettors exposure across many result combinations with a single entry. The core idea is to combine all permutations of selections so that even partial success can return something. Milestones include the Yankee (4 selections), the Canadian/Super Yankee (5 selections), the Heinz (6 selections without singles), and finally the Lucky 63 (6 selections with singles added).

  • Yankee (4 selections, 11 bets): 6 doubles, 4 trebles, 1 four-fold. No singles.
  • Canadian/Super Yankee (5 selections, 26 bets): 10 doubles, 10 trebles, 5 four-folds, 1 five-fold. No singles.
  • Heinz (6 selections, 57 bets): 15 doubles, 20 trebles, 15 four-folds, 6 five-folds, 1 six-fold. No singles. The name references the “57 varieties.”
  • Lucky 63 (6 selections, 63 bets): All Heinz components plus 6 singles, enhancing cover when only one pick wins.

The addition of singles in the Lucky 63 materially changes risk/return: it increases total stake versus a Heinz at the same per-line stake but improves the chance of any return, especially when one or two selections oblige. This trade-off is why many UK bettors prefer the Lucky 63 for mixed-confidence slips or fields with variable prices.

Advanced settings for UK bettors: each-way, Rule 4, dead heats

Advanced settlement factors can materially change the outcome of a Lucky 63. Rule 4 deductions reduce the winnings portion of affected legs when a rival is withdrawn after final declarations. Dead heats split entitlement between tied runners, altering the effective multiplier applied to a leg. These adjustments are applied per leg across all impacted lines (singles through to the six-fold) and in both the win and, where applicable, the place parts of each-way bets.

How do Rule 4 deductions affect Lucky 63 returns?

Rule 4 (Tattersalls) deductions are applied when a selection runs but a rival is withdrawn after final declarations and the market is not fully reformed. The deduction is expressed as pence-in-the-pound (e.g., 10p/£1 = 10%) and is applied to the winnings portion only (the profit), not to the returned stake. In a Lucky 63, any line containing a leg subject to Rule 4 uses an adjusted price for that leg.

  • Adjusted decimal odds formula (win part): adjusted_decimal = 1 + (original_decimal − 1) × (1 − deduction_rate).
  • Place part uses the same logic but with the calculated place decimal (see Each-way section below).
  • If a selection itself is withdrawn, that leg is void (treated as 1.00) and no Rule 4 is applied to that selection.
  • If there are multiple qualifying withdrawals, bookmakers add multiple deductions according to their schedules.
ScenarioPer-line stakeOriginal odds (decimal)Rule 4Adjusted odds (decimal)Return
Single (win)£1.003.5010%3.25£3.25
Double (win), leg A affected; leg B 2.20£1.00A: 3.50, B: 2.2010% on AA: 3.25; B: 2.20£7.15 (3.25 × 2.20)
Single (place), 1/5 place terms from 3.50£1.00 (place part)Place dec = 1.5010%1.45£1.45
  • Computation detail (first row): profit portion at 3.50 is 2.50; 10% deduction reduces profit to 2.25; return = 1 + 2.25 = 3.25.
  • Every multiple within the Lucky 63 that includes the affected selection uses the adjusted odds for that leg, so the effect compounds through higher folds.

Dead heat calculator: how are places and ties settled?

Dead heats split entitlement among tied runners. For win markets, the usual approach is to divide the stake by the number of tied runners and settle at full odds (the “other part” of the stake loses). In decimal terms, this is equivalent to multiplying the leg’s decimal odds by 1 ÷ n where n is the number of tied runners. For place markets, when more runners tie than remaining places, only a fraction of the stake is entitled: factor = remaining_places ÷ number_tied_at_last_place. That entitled fraction is settled at the place odds; the remainder loses.

  • Win dead heat (n-way): effective multiplier = original_decimal ÷ n.

Place dead heat at final paying position:

  • Entitled-stake factor f = remaining_places ÷ tied_runners_at_last_place.
  • Effective place return on stake S: S × f × place_decimal.
  • In multiples, the dead-heat factor applies only to that leg within the line; other legs multiply as normal.
MarketTiePer-line stakeOriginal odds (decimal)Place termsEffective multiplierReturn
Win2-way£1.004.00—4.00 ÷ 2 = 2.00£2.00
Win3-way£1.003.50—3.50 ÷ 3 ≈ 1.1667£1.1667
Place2 tie for 3rd; 3 places paid£1.00 (place part)11.00 (10/1)1/5f = 1 ÷ 2 = 0.5; place dec = 1 + (11−1)×0.2 = 3.0; effective = 0.5 × 3.0 = 1.5£1.50
  • Example (place dead heat): with £0.50 place stake in an each-way line, the return would be £0.50 × 1.5 = £0.75 for that leg before multiplying by other legs in the multiple.

Bookmaker settings: Betfred Lucky 63 terms to watch

Bookmaker house rules determine exactly how settlements and promotions apply. For Betfred and other UK firms, Lucky 63s are full-cover bets (63 lines; singles included). The following checks help ensure your calculator settings reflect typical UK practice; always verify current terms on the bookmaker’s site or betslip.

Each-way availability:

  • Common for horse racing and golf; uncommon for football markets. If placing a football Lucky 63, you will usually price win-only lines.

Place terms:

  • Industry-standard horse racing place terms often apply (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4) depending on race type and field size; enhanced places may be offered on selected races and affect the place odds used in the calculator.

Rule 4 and non-runners:

  • Rule 4 deductions apply to the winnings portion of affected legs for bets struck before the withdrawal; if your selection is the non-runner, that leg is void in all lines.

Dead-heat policy:

  • Win dead heats divide stake equally between tied runners; place dead heats apportion the entitled stake by remaining_places ÷ tied_runners_at_last_place.

Bonuses and concessions:

  • Some bookmakers advertise Lucky 15/31/63 concessions (e.g., “one winner” or “all winners” bonuses). Availability, qualifiers (sports/markets), and percentages vary and may be excluded from multiples; confirm on the day.

Best Odds Guaranteed (BOG):

  • Where offered on UK/Irish racing, BOG policies frequently exclude or limit multiples. Check whether BOG applies to Lucky 63 lines before relying on an SP uplift.

Minimum stakes and rounding:

  • Per-line minimums and rounding to the nearest penny can affect total outlay and exact returns; using “stake per line” in the calculator helps mirror bookmaker rounding.

Lucky 63 vs Heinz: which is better for football?

Both Lucky 63 and Heinz are six-selection full-cover multiples popular with UK bettors. The key practical difference is that a Lucky 63 includes singles (6 extra lines), while a Heinz excludes them. For football, where each-way is rarely offered, these bets are almost always win-only. Choosing between them is mainly about coverage and volatility: Lucky 63 costs more at the same per-line stake but can return something with just one winner; Heinz is cheaper per-line and concentrates stake in combinations that require at least two winners.

Lucky 63 or Heinz: what are the key differences?

Lucky 63 and Heinz share the same doubles through to the six-fold, but only Lucky 63 also includes all six singles. This changes the minimum winners required for any return and the total outlay at a given per-line stake.

Singles includedYes (6)No
Minimum winners for any return1 (via a winning single)2 (via a winning double)
Outlay at £1 per line (win-only)£63£57
Outlay at £1 each-way per line£126 (win + place parts)£114 (if each-way were offered; in football E/W is generally not available)

If you prefer equal total spend, you can normalise stakes by using a slightly higher per-line stake on Heinz so that the total outlay matches a Lucky 63 (or vice versa). The practical effect then boils down to whether you want singles exposure at all.

  • Lucky 63 improves the chance of a small return when only one pick wins.
  • Heinz reduces outlay at the same per-line stake and focuses on lines that require multiple winners.
  • Both bets void affected legs as 1.00 in lines where a selection is a non-runner or a push (e.g., Asian Handicap 0), reducing fold size.

When is a Heinz better value than a Lucky 63?

Heinz can be a better fit when you do not want singles exposure or when your perceived edge only materialises if several selections land. Because Heinz excludes singles, it avoids committing stake to outcomes that return on just one winner.

  • Low-confidence or short-priced singles: If many selections are short odds with little margin, the singles can dilute expected value; excluding them (Heinz) concentrates stake on higher-paying combinations.
  • Promotions focus on accumulators: If a bookmaker’s bonuses, price boosts, or insurance are targeted at accas or trebles+, Heinz may align better with those terms.
  • Risk appetite: If you accept higher variance in pursuit of larger combined payouts, a Heinz avoids the “drag” of singles.
  • Equal total budget scenario: For a fixed bankroll, a Heinz allows a bigger per-line stake on the 57 lines than a Lucky 63 would on 63 lines, potentially improving returns when multiple legs win.
  • Define goals: recovery potential (favour Lucky 63) versus concentration on combos (favour Heinz).
  • Compare expected value per structure using your own probabilities; remember that excluding singles increases variance.
  • Check house rules and promos: if singles concessions do not apply but acca boosts do, Heinz may be preferable.

How do football markets (BTTS, handicaps) fit a Lucky 63?

Football Lucky 63s are almost always win-only. You can combine standard football markets across different matches, but related contingencies from the same match (e.g., Home Win and Home -1 Handicap in the same game) are usually not permitted in traditional multiples unless placed as a same-game builder, which is treated as a single composite selection.

  • BTTS (Both Teams To Score): Settles as win or lose; no push state. Suitable for inclusion as standard legs.
  • Match odds (1X2), Double Chance, Over/Under: Settle as win/lose; abandoned/postponed events may void per house rules.
  • Draw No Bet (DNB): A draw results in a push/void; in a Lucky 63 line that leg is treated as 1.00 (reduces fold size).
  • Asian Handicap 0 (Level ball): Draw = push/void (multiplier 1.00) in all affected lines.
  • Asian quarter lines (e.g., -0.25, +0.25, -0.75, +0.75): Stake is split across adjacent half-lines (e.g., -0.25 = half on 0 and half on -0.5). Settlement can be half-win/half-void or half-lose/half-void; in multiples this behaves like two parallel legs each carrying half the stake.
  • European Handicap (3-way): No push; outcomes are Home/Draw/Away relative to the handicap line. Treated as a standard win/lose market.
  • Same-match combinations: Typically restricted in traditional accas; use a builder product if you need same-event combinations, then include the builder outcome as one leg within the Lucky 63.
  • Use distinct matches to avoid related-contingency rejections.
  • Set push-capable markets (DNB, AH 0) to “Void” when they draw; the line then prices the remaining legs.
  • For quarter-line Asians, model as split stakes across two selections if your calculator does not natively support half-win/half-lose settlement.

Are Lucky 63 bets worth it? Devil’s advocate and smarter staking

A Lucky 63 spreads your stake across 63 lines from six selections. It offers broad coverage (including singles), which can cushion poor runs, but it also commits more stake than a Heinz at the same per-line amount. Whether it is “worth it” depends on your edge (if any), target variance, and how often you expect multiple legs to land. The maths below shows the thresholds and trade-offs clearly.

What’s the strongest argument against a Lucky 63?

The main case against a Lucky 63 is that it commits stake to many lines that still carry the bookmaker’s margin, diluting value if you do not have a genuine edge on each selection.

  • Margin applied 63 times: Without an edge, the expected return is below 100% across all lines; spreading stake widely does not overcome the house margin.
  • Singles can dilute upside: If your strategy relies on multiple legs winning, adding singles uses bankroll on low-paying lines that do not leverage your edge as strongly as higher folds.
  • Promotions often exclude perms: Acca boosts/insurance typically target straight accumulators and may not apply to full-cover bets or to the singles within a Lucky 63.
  • Higher total outlay: At equal per-line stakes, a Lucky 63 costs more than a Heinz (63 vs 57 lines), increasing exposure and potential absolute loss during downswings.
  • Variance management: While singles reduce variance, they can also reduce long-run ROI if they are placed at negative expected value prices.

When is a Lucky 63 worth it? Expected value and hit-rate math

Assume six independent selections with the same average decimal odds D and true win probability p. Define x = p × D (your per-leg expected return multiplier). For a win-only Lucky 63 with £1 per line, the expected return per £1 staked across the bet is E(Lucky63) = [ (1 + x)6 − 1 ] ÷ 63. Heinz (which removes singles) has E(Heinz) = [ (1 + x)6 − 1 − 6x ] ÷ 57. Break-even for both structures occurs at x = 1 (fair odds). Below are worked examples:

x = p × DInterpretationExpected return per £1 (Lucky 63)Expected return per £1 (Heinz)Comment
0.92Moderate negative edge0.7800.765Lucky 63 loses less (singles cushion); still negative EV.
0.96Slight negative edge0.8850.876Both lose; Lucky 63 marginally “less bad.”
1.00Fair odds1.0001.000Break-even for both structures.
1.05Positive edge1.1621.174Heinz slightly higher EV (focus on multiples, no singles).

Takeaways: If you expect only a mild edge or mixed results, Lucky 63’s singles improve capital retention versus Heinz. If you have a strong edge across all six legs (x > 1), concentrating stake in combinations (Heinz or even straighter accas) can out-return a Lucky 63 on a like-for-like budget. Real-world pricing is not uniform, so replace x with your own p and D per leg to model expected outcomes.

  • Rule of thumb: If your average true probability p is higher than 1/D for most legs but you’re unsure several will land together, choose Lucky 63; if you expect clustered wins, Heinz or targeted accas may be more efficient.
  • Voids/pushes (e.g., Draw No Bet or Asian Handicap 0 draws) are treated as 1.00 multipliers, reducing fold size and slightly improving survival of lines.

What safer alternatives beat a Lucky 63 for value?

“Safer” generally means lower variance and better capital efficiency when edge is modest or uncertain. These structures and approaches often outperform a Lucky 63 for bankroll preservation and, in some cases, expected value.

Targeted singles and doubles only:

  • Back only selections with clear positive EV; pair a few into doubles if both are edge-positive. This trims low-value lines and reduces exposure.

Trixie/Patent instead of full-cover sixfolds:

  • Trixie (3 selections: 3 doubles, 1 treble) or Patent (adds 3 singles) offer coverage with fewer lines and lower outlay.

Yankee/Canadian for mid-size perms:

  • Yankee (4 selections, 11 lines) or Canadian/Super Yankee (5 selections, 26 lines) reduce stake count versus a Lucky 63 while preserving multiple routes to a return.

Heinz with weighted staking:

  • If your thesis requires several winners, use a Heinz and reallocate the budget saved from not staking singles to the higher folds.

Use push-friendly markets to manage downside:

  • Draw No Bet or Asian Handicap 0 can void rather than lose on draws, softening variance across multiples.

Leverage genuine, transparent promotions:

  • Where permitted, acca boosts/insurance on trebles+ can lift EV more reliably than staking into 63 unboosted lines. Always confirm current terms and exclusions.

Bottom line: Lucky 63s are best when you want broad cover and expect a mix of outcomes. If your edge is concentrated in fewer, stronger opinions, trimming lines (or switching structure) typically improves long-run value and variance control.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I combine multiple Rule 4 deductions on one selection in a Lucky 63?

Multiply the winnings portion by each remaining percentage, not by adding deductions. Use adjusted_decimal = 1 + (original_decimal − 1) × (1 − d1) × (1 − d2) × …; for example, 3.50 with 15p and 5p deductions becomes 1 + 2.50 × 0.85 × 0.95 = 3.01875 (rounded to 3.02). This compounded rate then applies to every line in the Lucky 63 that contains that leg.

Can I price a Lucky 63 using a free bet stake (stake not returned)?

Yes—treat each winning line as returning profit only, with the stake part removed. For a single line at decimal 3.00 using a free bet, £1 returns £2.00, not £3.00; across a Lucky 63 struck entirely with a free bet token, every winning line’s return is line_stake × (decimal − 1), so a six-fold at 3.00, 2.20, 4.00, 2.50, 3.50, 2.10 would return stake × ((3−1)×(2.2−1)×(4−1)×(2.5−1)×(3.5−1)×(2.1−1)) instead of stake × (3×2.2×4×2.5×3.5×2.1).

How are fractional pennies rounded on Lucky 63 settlements and does it change totals?

UK fixed‑odds bookmakers typically round each settled bet to the nearest penny, often by truncation down, at the end of the line. A return of £0.2375 is usually paid as £0.23, not £0.24; across 63 lines this can trim a few pence in aggregate, which is why using stake per line values that produce whole‑penny returns helps match bookmaker payouts.

Does Best Odds Guaranteed (BOG) apply to Lucky 63 multiples?

Usually no; most major UK firms exclude multiples from BOG or limit BOG to singles only. If BOG is exceptionally applied to a multiple, it affects the settled odds of the qualifying leg(s) only, so a 10% SP uplift on one leg lifts each live line containing that leg by 10% on the winnings portion, not on the returned stake.

How do I enter SP or in‑play odds in the calculator?

Enter the decimal, fractional, or American price you actually took; SP or in‑play labels do not change the math. If SP is unknown pre‑off, leave it blank until confirmed or use an estimate, noting that a 5% change in one leg’s decimal increases the winnings portion of every live line containing it by exactly 5%.

What happens to US spreads/totals that push in a Lucky 63?

A push is treated as a void leg with a 1.00 multiplier, reducing the fold size. For example, a -110 spread that pushes in a treble makes that line settle as a double, so a £1 treble at 1.91, 1.91, and push becomes £1 × 1.91 × 1.91 = £3.6481 (rounded by house rules) for that line.

How should I treat extra time or penalties in football when using the calculator?

Price according to the market rules you bet: 1X2 settles on 90 minutes plus injury time only, while “To qualify” includes extra time and penalties. If the team won after extra time but drew in 90 minutes, a 1X2 leg is a loser; only a “To qualify” leg is a winner, so mark outcomes accordingly before computing the Lucky 63.

How are dead heats handled with enhanced places in each‑way Lucky 63s?

When more runners tie than remaining places, only a fraction of stake is entitled: factor f = remaining_places ÷ tied_runners_at_last_place, then settle that fraction at place odds. With 4 places paid, a three‑way tie for 4th gives f = 1⁄3; for a 10/1 shot at 1/5 terms, place decimal is 1 + (11−1)×0.2 = 3.0, so £1 place part returns £1 × 1⁄3 × 3.0 = £1.00.

What if my total stake doesn’t divide evenly by 63 (or 126 each‑way)?

The exact per‑line stake is total_stake ÷ number_of_lines and can be a repeating decimal, so £20 each‑way across 126 lines is £0.158730… per line for both win and place parts. Accurate calculators carry full precision during multiplication and round only at the end of each line, whereas bookmakers using stake‑per‑line entry avoid these micro‑fractions altogether.

Is a Lucky 63 or a straight 6‑fold accumulator better value on average?

At equal total spend, the 6‑fold has higher expected return when your per‑leg edge is strong, but much higher variance. Using x = p×D, a £63 6‑fold has expected return 63×x⁶, while a £63 Lucky 63 has expected return (1+x)⁶ − 1; at x = 1.05 this is roughly £84.4 for the acca versus about £73 for the Lucky 63, but the Lucky 63 preserves more capital when few legs win.

Do bookmaker maximum payout caps affect Lucky 63 results?

Yes—caps can clip high‑odds perms materially. If a bookmaker caps football multiples at £250,000 and your theoretical Lucky 63 return is £400,000, the effective haircut is 37.5%, so the paid total is £250,000 regardless of the computed figure; this cap applies to the entire bet, not per line, per typical house rules.

Are returns from Lucky 63 bets taxable in the UK and does the tool include any fees?

UK punters do not pay tax on gambling winnings, so returns are gross of tax. The calculator does not include exchange commission or fees; to model exchange commission c on decimal D, use D' = 1 + (D − 1) × (1 − c), for example 2% commission turns 3.50 into 1 + 2.50 × 0.98 = 3.45.

Can I set banker selections in a Lucky 63 calculation?

Bankers are not part of a standard Lucky 63, which prices every combination equally. To simulate bankers, compute the full Lucky 63 then exclude any lines that omit your banker, or use a custom perm where only combinations containing the banker are staked; for six picks with one banker, you would be removing all lines that lack that selection.

What if an event is postponed—how should I reflect that in the Lucky 63?

If the bookmaker voids the leg after a postponement window, mark it Void so its multiplier is 1.00 and the fold reduces. Many UK firms void if the event is not completed within 24–48 hours or if it moves to a different venue, but thresholds vary, so applying Void in the calculator mirrors the typical settlement outcome.

each-way.metaTitle: Each Way Bet Calculator UK • Official Returns 2026

each-way.intro: Work out UK each way returns instantly for horse racing and football. Enter stake, odds, and place terms to see win, place, and total payouts. Supports singles, doubles, trebles, and Lucky 15 with Rule 4 and dead-heat handling.
