Horse racing – Each-way (boundary tie)YesTie spans last paying placeReduce place stake by R = P_avail/T_tied; settle at EW odds£5 EW at 8/1, 1/5 terms; Top 3; tie for 3rd among 2 → R=1/2; place dec 2.6; eff. £2.50 → £2.50 × 2.6 = £6.50 (win part loses)
Golf – WinRareJoint winners declared (no playoff)Divide stake by N; settle at full odds£10 at 20/1, N=2 → eff. £5 → £5 × 21.0 = £105.00
Golf – Top 8 finish (EW place)YesTie for 8th crosses boundaryReduce place stake by R; settle at EW odds£10 EW at 25/1, 1/5; tie for 8th among 3 → R=1/3; place dec 6.0; eff. £3.33 → ≈£19.98
Football – Match 1X2NoDraw is own outcomeN/A – no dead heat—
Football – Top GoalscorerPossibleAward shared; book does not adopt tie-breakersDivide stake by N; settle at full odds£20 at 10/1, 2 joint winners → eff. £10 → £10 × 11.0 = £110.00
Football – Top 4 finishRareOfficial stand. decides via tie-breakersFollow official table (no dead heat)£30 at 2/1; team 4th on goal diff → £30 × 3.0 = £90.00

Remember: apply any non-runner deductions or exchange commission before or after the dead-heat step as specified by your bookmaker or exchange rules. When in doubt, cross-check settlement wording and, if necessary, seek guidance from [IBAS](https://www.ibas-uk.com/).

Betfair Exchange vs bookmakers: will results match?

Often, yes—because both sides apply the same dead‑heat mathematics: when selections share a position, your stake (or lay liability) is proportionally divided, and the winning portion is paid at full odds. However, results can still diverge due to exchange commission, different place terms, separate win/place markets on the Exchange, Rule 4 versus Exchange reduction factors, and rounding or settlement policy differences. For primary references, see [Betfair](https://betting.betfair.com/), [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk/), and [IBAS](https://www.ibas-uk.com/).

What are Betfair dead heat rules and partial voiding?

On the Exchange, dead‑heat settlement is applied by apportioning your matched bet into winning and losing fractions. For back bets, your effective winning stake equals your original stake divided by the number of tied selections; for lay bets, your liability is divided by the same factor. Betfair then applies commission on your net winnings across the market.

Back bets:

  • Win dead heat (N ties): effective winning stake = stake ÷ N; return = effective stake × decimal odds.
  • Place dead heat at boundary: if P_avail places remain and T_tied runners share them, effective stake = stake × (P_avail ÷ T_tied), then settle at the matched place price.

Lay bets:

  • Win dead heat (N ties): effective liability = full liability ÷ N (you pay out only that fraction).
  • Place boundary tie: liability is multiplied by (P_avail ÷ T_tied) for runners tied at the last paying place.

Commission:

  • Betfair charges commission (e.g., 2% default, varies by account) on net winnings, not on stakes. Payout = stake + profit − commission.

About “partial voiding”:

  • In dead‑heats, Betfair does not normally void part of your bet; it proportionally settles it as part‑win/part‑lose using the factors above. The term “partial void” is more accurately “proportional settlement.”
  • Voids on the Exchange typically relate to market cancellations or non‑runner handling (via reduction factors), not to dead‑heats themselves.

How do major UK bookmakers settle dead heats?

UK bookmakers apply near‑universal dead‑heat principles aligned with industry norms (e.g., Tattersalls principles), with brand‑specific terms governing odds, place fractions, and deductions. The core arithmetic matches the Exchange on the backer side.

Win markets (fixed odds or SP):

  • If N share 1st, divide your win stake by N and settle that fraction at full odds. The remaining fraction is treated as a loser.
  • Example: £20 at 4/1, two‑way tie → effective win stake £10 → return £10 × 5.0 = £50.

Each‑way (EW) markets:

  • Place part: if a tie spans the final paying place, reduce the place stake by R = P_avail ÷ T_tied, then settle at EW place odds (e.g., 1/5 or 1/4 of win odds).
  • If the selection finishes wholly within the paying places without a boundary tie, the place stake is not reduced.

Common bookmaker policies:

  • Rule 4 deductions (horse racing) apply before dead‑heat settlement.
  • Best Odds Guaranteed, Extra/Enhanced Places, and specific market rules can alter odds or the number of paid places, but not the dead‑heat arithmetic.
  • Rounding is typically to the nearest penny.

Why might exchange and sportsbook payouts differ?

Even with matching dead‑heat arithmetic, several structural differences can lead to different returns.

Commission vs none:

  • Betfair Exchange deducts commission from net winnings; sportsbooks do not charge commission on settled bets.

Market architecture:

  • Sportsbooks offer each‑way (one bet split into win/place with a fixed place fraction). The Exchange offers separate win and to‑be‑placed markets with independently traded prices.

Place terms:

  • Sportsbooks may offer Extra/Enhanced Places (e.g., Top 6 instead of Top 4) that do not exist on the Exchange, creating different winners.

Price source and timing:

  • Fixed odds vs starting price (SP) vs Betfair Starting Price (BSP) can lead to different odds on the same selection at settlement.

Deductions model:

  • Rule 4 (sportsbooks) vs Exchange reduction factors (Betfair) can yield slightly different adjusted odds after non‑runners.

Rounding and settlement minutiae:

  • Rounding rules and how part‑win/part‑lose components are combined can create small penny‑level differences.
ScenarioStakeDecimal oddsTie detailsSportsbook settlementExchange settlement (2% commission)Reason for any difference
Win dead heat (2‑way)£10 (win)6.0N = 2Eff. stake £5; return £5 × 6.0 = £30.00Eff. stake £5; return £30.00; profit £20.00; commission £0.40; net £29.60Exchange commission on net winnings
Place boundary tie (Top 3), two tied for 3rd£10 (place)3.0 (place)P_avail = 1, T_tied = 2 → R = 1/2Eff. stake £5; return £5 × 3.0 = £15.00Eff. stake £5; return £15.00; profit £5.00; commission £0.10; net £14.90Exchange commission; same dead‑heat maths otherwise
Enhanced places (Sportsbook Top 6 vs Exchange Top 4)£10 (place)—Finishes 5thWins if Top 6 paid (settled per EW terms)Loses if Exchange market is Top 4 onlyDifferent place terms and market definitions

If a settlement looks inconsistent, compare: the official result and tie status, market definition (number of places), applied deductions (Rule 4 or reduction factor), odds source (fixed, SP, or BSP), and whether Exchange commission has been applied. For unresolved disputes, you can escalate via [IBAS](https://www.ibas-uk.com/) under [UKGC](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk/) oversight.

Why do dead heat rules exist, and how did methods evolve?

Dead-heat rules exist to ensure fair and consistent settlement when competitors finish inseparably tied. In British horse racing, the concept predates modern technology and was originally resolved by “run-offs” (a deciding re‑run) or by mutual agreement to divide the prize. With the arrival of photo-finish cameras in the mid‑20th century and the standardisation of bookmaker terms, the industry converged on proportional settlement: divide the relevant stake (or liability) and pay at full odds. Oversight and dispute mechanisms in the UK involve the sport’s governing bodies and independent adjudicators under regulatory supervision.

How did settlement evolve from coin tosses to shared prizes?

Across sports and contests, early tie-breaks sometimes used random devices (drawing lots or coin tosses) when no practical method existed to separate competitors. British horse racing followed a different path: run-offs were the formal mechanism for dead heats, with stakeholders often agreeing to share the prize instead of running again. As betting markets matured and photography improved result accuracy, settlement shifted decisively toward shared outcomes and proportional bet settlement to preserve fairness and consistency.

Pre–photo-finish era (19th–early 20th century):

  • Horse racing: official rules provided for a run-off; owners could agree to divide the stakes and place money.
  • Other sports/contests: ties sometimes resolved by drawing lots; coin tosses existed as tie-breaks in various events, but not as a standard for racing settlement.

Photo-finish adoption:

  • United Kingdom: photo-finish technology was introduced after World War II (first used at Epsom in 1947), reducing—but not eliminating—dead heats.

Modern betting practice:

  • Bookmakers and exchanges use proportional settlement: divide the stake (or lay liability) by the number of tied competitors and settle at full odds; place-part reductions apply at boundary ties.
EraRacing practiceOfficial result handlingCommon betting settlementNotes
19th–early 20th centuryRun-off for dead heats; parties could agree to divideJudge declared dead heat; deciding heat optional by agreementDivide stakes/returns according to number of tied runnersRun-offs declined due to welfare, logistics, and scheduling
Mid‑20th centuryIncreasing use of photographyPhoto-finish introduced in UK; first at Epsom in 1947Proportional settlement becomes the industry normDead heats persist when photos cannot separate runners
Late 20th–21st centuryStandardised rules and T&CsOfficial results published promptly by governing bodiesStake/liability division; place boundary reductionsApplied consistently by major UK bookmakers and exchanges

Today’s approach prioritises transparency and replicability. Operators publish settlement rules in their terms, and bettors can validate outcomes using calculators that mirror the proportional logic.

What dead‑end approaches failed fairness tests?

Several intuitive but flawed ideas have been rejected because they introduce bias, overpay relative to the betting book, or underpay versus customer expectations. Fairness is tested against symmetry (all tied selections treated identically), proportionality (payout scales with tie size), and arbitrage‑resistance (no “free money” from rule quirks).

Pay full odds to all tied winners without adjustment:

  • Fails budget balance and arbitrage tests; total payout can exceed the pool/book’s implied probabilities.

Void tied outcomes entirely:

  • Penalises customers who correctly predicted a joint outcome; violates symmetry and proportionality.

Halve the odds instead of dividing the stake:

  • Can distort returns when combined with each‑way place fractions or complex multiples; stake division is consistent across odds formats and market structures.

Apply reductions after rounding each component separately:

  • Early rounding can create penny-level biases; best practice is to calculate proportionally first, then round the final figure.

Ignore place boundary logic in each‑way markets:

  • Paying full place stakes across boundary ties overpays and breaks parity between tied selections; proportional place‑stake reduction restores fairness.

Who sets settlement standards in the UK (Tattersalls, IBAS, UKGC)?

Settlement is the product of sport‑specific rules, operator terms, and statutory oversight. Historically, the Tattersalls Committee articulated principles for betting disputes in British racing, including dead‑heat handling (run-offs or division). In the modern era, operators set detailed settlement terms consistent with the sport’s official results, and independent bodies adjudicate disputes under regulatory supervision.

Governing bodies for results:

  • British horse racing: official results and queries are governed by the sport’s rules (e.g., steward enquiries) under the [British Horseracing Authority](https://www.britishhorseracing.com/regulation/rules/).

Operator terms and industry custom:

  • Bookmakers and exchanges publish settlement rules in their T&Cs; proportional dead‑heat settlement mirrors long‑standing custom historically associated with Tattersalls principles.

Independent adjudication:

  • [IBAS](https://www.ibas-uk.com/) (Independent Betting Adjudication Service) reviews customer disputes and issues rulings based on evidence and operator rules.

Regulatory oversight:

  • The [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk/) licenses operators and requires fair, transparent terms and effective customer redress mechanisms.

In practice, a fair settlement aligns four elements: the official result from the sport’s authority, the operator’s published rules, proportional dead‑heat mathematics, and the oversight/adjudication framework provided by [IBAS](https://www.ibas-uk.com/) and the [UKGC](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk/). When these are consistent, exchange and sportsbook outcomes remain predictable and auditable.

The other side of the coin: Should you just trust settlement and skip calculators?

Operator settlement is authoritative because it follows published market rules and official results. A dead heat calculator is an audit and planning aid—it cannot override terms and conditions. The most credible argument against using one is that manual inputs and hidden assumptions (places paid, place fractions, commission, Rule 4 or reduction factors, rounding) can produce figures that differ from official settlement. Used carelessly, a calculator may create false disputes or poor hedging decisions. Used correctly, it helps you predict, verify, and budget for outcomes before and after settlement.

What is the strongest argument against using a dead heat calculator?

The strongest argument is authority and context: only the operator’s market definition and settlement rules apply. A calculator—even a precise one—cannot “know” your bookmaker’s specific terms, exchange commission, enhanced places, or non‑runner handling unless you enter them perfectly. Any mismatch (e.g., entering 1/5 place terms when 1/4 applied, or forgetting commission on the Exchange) will yield a different number from the official payout, making the tool look “wrong” when your inputs were incomplete. For many straightforward markets (win-only with clear ties, or 1X2 football where a draw is its own outcome), trusting the published settlement is usually faster and safer.

  • Settlement authority: bookmaking T&Cs and exchange rules govern payouts; calculators are advisory.
  • Input risk: missed enhanced places, wrong place fraction, omitted Rule 4 or Exchange reduction factors, or ignoring commission/rounding causes discrepancies.
  • Non-transferability: some rules are market-specific (e.g., separate Exchange win vs place markets); generic calculators may not mirror every variant.
  • Time cost: for simple, unambiguous results, checking the operator’s settled bet slip is quicker.

When can calculators mislead: which edge cases should you avoid?

Calculators can mislead if you apply the right maths to the wrong market definition. Edge cases generally fall into five buckets: market definition, odds/terms, timing/deductions, multiples/cash‑out, and rounding/commission. If any of these are uncertain, verify the operator’s rules before relying on a calculator output.

Market definition pitfalls:

  • Enhanced/Extra Places each‑way: using standard places when the bookmaker paid extra (or vice versa) changes boundary‑tie reductions.
  • Exchange vs sportsbook: Exchange has separate place markets with their own prices; there is no “EW fraction.”
  • Competitions with tie‑breakers (league tables, group winners): no dead heat if the official ranking breaks the tie.

Odds and terms:

  • Wrong place fraction (1/5 vs 1/4) or mis‑converting fractional to decimal place odds.
  • Ante‑post vs day‑of‑race: terms (places/Rule 4 applicability) can differ.

Timing and deductions:

  • Rule 4 (sportsbooks) vs Exchange reduction factors: applied at different times; forgetting these skews results.
  • Photo‑finish amendments: calculators may not reflect result changes if you calculate too early.

Multiples and cash‑out:

  • Each‑way multiples: per‑leg dead‑heat reductions feed through complex returns; oversimplified calculators can misstate acca outcomes.
  • Partial cash‑out or “Edit Bet”: settlement splits may no longer align with the original stake.

Rounding, fees, and liability:

  • Exchange commission on net winnings vs no commission on sportsbooks.
  • Penny rounding after all calculations; rounding too early introduces bias.
  • Lay liability: treating lay bets like back bets leads to errors (liability, not stake, is apportioned).
Edge caseWhy calculators can misleadWhat to verify before using a calculator
Each‑way with Enhanced/Extra PlacesWrong number of places changes boundary‑tie reductionExact places paid on your bet, not the default for the race
Exchange place marketsNo EW fraction; separate traded price and commission applyMatched odds in the specific place market and your commission rate
Rule 4 vs Exchange reduction factorsAdjustments differ in method and timingWhether a deduction/reduction applied and its value before dead‑heat maths
League standings with tie‑breakersNo dead heat if the competition assigns distinct ranksOfficial standings and tie‑breaker rules for that competition
Each‑way multiplesPer‑leg reductions compound; simple tools can misstate acca totalsPer‑leg settlement results and how returns roll to the next leg
Lay bets (dead heat)Apportioning stake instead of liability gives wrong figuresOriginal lay liability and the number of tied selections
Rounding differencesEarly rounding creates penny biasOperator’s rounding policy (usually to the nearest penny at end)

When does this tool actually save you time and money?

A robust dead heat calculator pays off when the market has genuine complexity or when you need pre‑bet planning. It helps you price edge cases, validate settlement within seconds, and size hedges accurately on the Exchange.

Pre‑bet planning:

  • Estimate worst‑case place returns under boundary ties (e.g., 3‑way tie for 8th in Top‑8 golf) to decide stake size or whether to take Enhanced Places.
  • Model Exchange hedges (back vs lay) under potential dead‑heat scenarios including commission.

Post‑result verification:

  • Audit each‑way settlements where ties occur at the last paying place; confirm that the reduction factor was applied correctly.
  • Check that Rule 4 or Exchange reduction factors and rounding were applied in the correct order.

Operational efficiency:

  • Speed—produce an auditable breakdown you can compare with your bet slip.
  • Consistency—use the same method across horse racing, golf, and football finishing markets.

Risk control for matched betting and trading:

  • Predict lay liability under dead heat to avoid under‑covering positions.
  • Quantify EV impact when ties are likely (e.g., big golf fields with clustered leaderboards).

Bottom line: trust the operator’s rules for final settlement and use the calculator to plan, verify, and document complex cases. That combination gives accuracy without sacrificing speed.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I work out P_avail in boundary ties like “joint second” for Top 3?

Count how many paid places are still open at the point of the tie, then divide by how many are tied. In a Top 3 where three runners are joint second, two places remain (2nd and 3rd), so P_avail = 2 and T_tied = 3, giving a reduction R = 2/3. Example: £15 place at derived place odds 3.0 with R = 2/3 settles as effective stake £10.00 and return £10.00 × 3.0 = £30.00.

How are accumulators affected when a leg finishes in a dead heat for the win?

For that leg, the accumulator’s multiplier becomes decimal_odds ÷ N, because only 1/N of the stake is winning. Example: £10 treble at 3.0, 2.0, 1.8 with a two‑way dead heat on leg 1 settles as £10 × (3.0 ÷ 2) × 2.0 × 1.8 = £54.00, versus £108.00 without a dead heat on leg 1.

How are each‑way doubles or accas settled when a place leg has a boundary dead heat?

Reduce that leg’s place stake by R = P_avail ÷ T_tied, settle at the leg’s place odds, then roll the resulting place return into the next place leg. Example: £5 each‑way double; leg 1 place odds 3.0 with R = 1/3 returns (£5 × 1/3) × 3.0 = £5.00, this £5.00 then goes at leg 2 place odds 2.5 to return £12.50 on the place side.

How do I calculate a Betfair lay bet when the selection dead‑heats for first?

Divide your lay liability by N and remember you still win the losing fraction of the backer’s stake. Example: lay £10 at 5.0, liability £40; two‑way dead heat means you win £5 stake and lose £20 liability, net −£15 before commission (commission applies only to net wins, so £0 here).

Do UK bookmakers round dead‑heat settlements the same way?

Most round to the nearest penny after all reductions and multiplications, not at intermediate steps. If £10 place stake faces R = 1/3 and place odds 3.0, the exact calculation uses £10 ÷ 3 = £3.333… then × 3.0 = £10.00, so holding precision until the end avoids penny drift that would show as £9.99 if rounded too early.

What’s the correct order when Rule 4, each‑way, and a dead heat all apply?

Apply Rule 4 to the win odds first, convert to place odds, then apply dead‑heat reductions, and finally round. Example: £10 each‑way at 10/1 with a 20% Rule 4 becomes 9.0 decimal; place odds at 1/5 become 2.6; if three tie for the last place (R = 1/3), effective place stake is £3.333… and place return is £3.333… × 2.6 = £8.67 after rounding.

Why is “halving the odds” wrong for dead‑heats with fractional prices?

Stake division is correct; halving fractional odds overpays because fractional odds exclude the returned stake. Example: £10 at 5/1 in a two‑way dead heat should return (£10 ÷ 2) × 6.0 = £30.00, but using 2.5/1 on the full £10 incorrectly pays £35.00; that extra £5.00 is an error.

Can a dead heat ever pay the same as a clean place result?

Yes, when the tie is entirely within the paid band so R = 1. In a Top 4, if three runners tie for second, there are three paid places left at that point (2nd–4th), so P_avail = 3 and T_tied = 3, R = 1, and the place part pays in full with no reduction.

How should I enter “Extra Places” each‑way offers to avoid dead‑heat mistakes?

Use the exact places paid on your bet and count P_avail from that number, not from the race’s standard terms. If you took Top 6 and five tie for 6th, P_avail = 1, T_tied = 5, so R = 1/5; the same selection would lose entirely if the market were Top 4 on an exchange.

Can the calculator account for commission, currency, or taxes like Betfair Premium Charge?

It can apply exchange commission at a user‑set rate and round to your currency, but account‑level fees such as Premium Charge are not per‑bet and cannot be computed from a single market. For example, at 2% commission, £20 profit nets £19.60; Premium Charge, if applicable, is assessed later on cumulative weekly profit.

Are dead‑heat rules the same in the US and Australia?

No. US pari‑mutuel pools split the pool among tied outcomes first, then derive dividends per $2, so a two‑way dead heat typically halves the dividend versus a sole winner. Many Australian bookmakers use UK‑style stake division, but tote pools follow pool‑split mathematics; this tool targets UK fixed‑odds and Betfair‑style settlement.

How do I handle Top Goalscorer dead heats with each‑way terms and award tie‑breakers?

If the operator settles on joint winners, the win stake is divided by N and paid at full odds, and the place part follows boundary‑tie rules. Example: £20 each‑way at 10/1, 1/4 terms, three joint winners means win effective stake £6.67 at 11.0 returns £73.37, place odds 3.5 with no boundary tie return £20 × 3.5 = £70.00, total £143.37 before rounding.

How do Rule 4 deductions differ from Betfair reduction factors numerically?

Both scale winnings, but Rule 4 is a percent deduction on the win portion used by sportsbooks, while Betfair’s reduction factor scales the net odds on matched bets. Example: a 20% Rule 4 on 11.0 reduces to 9.0; a 20% exchange reduction on 6.0 reduces to 1 + (6.0 − 1) × 0.8 = 5.0; in both cases apply dead‑heat maths thereafter.

What’s the fastest way to audit a bookmaker dead‑heat settlement against your slip?

Confirm the official finishing positions, compute N or P_avail ÷ T_tied, apply any Rule 4 or exchange reductions before the dead‑heat step, then multiply through and round once. A two‑way win dead heat at 6.0 on £10 should always show £10 × (6.0 ÷ 2) = £30.00 on sportsbooks, while an exchange at 2% nets £29.60 after commission.

How are free bets, bonuses, or stake‑not‑returned promos treated in a dead heat?

Divide the effective stake by N but remember stake‑not‑returned means only winnings are paid. Example: a £10 free bet at 5.0 in a two‑way dead heat has effective free stake £5 and pays £5 × (5.0 − 1) = £20.00, with no stake added back.

How do I compute a lay bet on a place market when there is a boundary tie?

Multiply your lay liability by R = P_avail ÷ T_tied and note you still win the untied fraction of the backer’s stake. Example: lay £50 at 3.5 on Top 3, liability £125; if three tie for 3rd, R = 1/3 so you pay £41.67 liability, receive £33.33 of backer stake, net −£8.34 before any commission on wins.

How precise should I be with repeating decimals such as R = 1/3?

Carry full precision through all steps and round only the final return to the nearest penny; this prevents 1p errors on ties. For £10 with R = 1/3 at place odds 3.0, using exact 10/3 yields £10.00, whereas early rounding to £3.33 would display £9.99.

forecast.intro: Instantly work out forecast returns for UK horse racing. Supports straight and reverse bets, combinations, doubles and accumulators with fractional or decimal odds. Free, no sign-up, mobile ready.

forecast.articleHtml:
How do I use the Forecast Bet Calculator?

Choose your bet type (Straight Forecast or Reverse Forecast), select your preferred odds format (fractional or decimal), enter the odds for your selections, and specify your stake as either a per‑line (unit) stake or a total stake. The calculator determines the number of lines automatically (1 for a Straight Forecast; 2 for a Reverse Forecast; for combinations it follows n×(n−1) lines, where n is the number of selections) and computes estimated returns. For UK horse racing, most Straight Forecasts with fixed‑odds bookmakers are settled to the official Computer Straight Forecast (CSF) dividend; returns shown here are estimates based on the odds and stake you enter, and actual settlement may vary by bookmaker rules.

  • Select bet type: Straight Forecast (finish 1st–2nd in exact order) or Reverse Forecast (covers both possible orders with two lines).
  • Pick odds format: fractional (e.g., 5/2) or decimal (e.g., 3.50). You can mix formats across runners if needed.
  • Enter odds for your runners. For combinations, add all runners you want covered; the calculator builds every ordered pair automatically.
  • Set stake: either enter a unit (per‑line) stake or a total stake. The calculator derives the other value based on the number of lines.
  • Review estimated returns. For Reverse Forecasts, remember only one of the two lines can win.

Which odds formats are supported (fractional, decimal)?

The calculator supports fractional and decimal odds—the two standard formats in UK horse racing. Fractional odds (e.g., 5/2) quote profit relative to the stake, while decimal odds (e.g., 3.50) include the return of the stake. Conversions use: decimal = 1 + (numerator ÷ denominator); implied probability = denominator ÷ (numerator + denominator) for fractional, and 1 ÷ decimal for decimal. Decimal values and probabilities below are rounded to two decimals.

FormatExample inputMeaning (profit on £1)Decimal (incl. stake)Implied probability
Fractional1/5£0.201.2083.33%
Fractional1/2£0.501.5066.67%
Fractional5/4£1.252.2544.44%
Fractional2/1£2.003.0033.33%
Fractional7/2£3.504.5022.22%
Fractional9/4£2.253.2530.77%
Fractional10/3£3.334.3323.08%
Decimal1.20£0.201.2083.33%
Decimal3.50£2.503.5028.57%
  • Fractional returns shown by bookmakers typically exclude the returned stake in the “profit” figure; decimal includes it.
  • Implied probability does not include bookmaker margin/overround.

Stake options: unit, per line, and total stake

Unit stake and per‑line stake are equivalent terms: the amount staked on each individual line (bet). Total stake is the overall outlay across all lines. The calculator lets you enter either a unit stake or a total stake and automatically derives the other value using the number of lines generated by your selection and bet type.

Bet typeSelections (n)LinesExample inputDerived valueTotal stakeNotes
Straight Forecast (SF)21£5 per line—£5Exact 1–2 order only; one line.
Reverse Forecast (RF)22£2 per line—£4Two SF lines: A>B and B>A. Only one can win.
Combination SF36 (n×(n−1))£1 per line—£6All ordered pairs for A, B, C.
Combination RF412 (n×(n−1))£0.50 per line—£6Each unordered pair creates two SF lines.
Reverse Forecast (RF)22£10 total stake£5 per line£10Total split equally across two lines.
Combination SF36£12 total stake£2 per line£12Total divided by number of lines.
  • Lines for Combination Forecasts scale quickly: n×(n−1). For 5 selections, that is 20 lines.
  • Profit/loss for RF is the winning SF return minus the losing line’s stake.

Race Forecast calculator: UK horse racing coverage

This calculator is designed for UK and Irish horse racing across Flat, Jumps (National Hunt), and All‑Weather meetings. It supports Straight Forecasts (SF) and Reverse Forecasts (RF) in races where forecasts are offered—typically events with at least three declared runners. For fixed‑odds bookmakers in Great Britain and Ireland, SF bets are commonly settled using the official Computer Straight Forecast (CSF) dividend published after the race; an RF is simply two SFs in opposite orders, so only the line matching the actual finishing order can win. Because settlement can vary by bookmaker (e.g., deductions for non‑runners or Rule 4, and dead‑heat handling), the calculator provides estimates based on your inputs.

  • Coverage: Great Britain and Ireland; Flat, Handicap, Maiden, Novice, Conditions, Stakes, and Jump races.
  • Eligibility: Forecasts generally require 3+ runners; in events with fewer runners, forecasts are usually not offered.
  • Non‑runners and adjustments: Affected lines are voided or adjusted per bookmaker rules. Use the calculator’s options to reflect non‑runners and typical deductions where applicable.
  • Result dependency: Final SF returns usually track the official CSF; RF outcomes reflect the CSF for the actual 1–2 order at your stake per line.

Straight vs Reverse Forecast: which should I choose?

Straight Forecast (SF) and Reverse Forecast (RF) are two‑runner bets for predicting the first two finishers in a horse race. An SF requires the exact 1–2 order; an RF covers both possible orders by placing two SF lines. In Great Britain and Ireland with fixed‑odds bookmakers, Straight Forecast bets are typically settled to the official Computer Straight Forecast (CSF) dividend published after the race; an RF simply places two SFs, so only the line matching the actual finishing order can return. Your choice hinges on conviction about the exact order versus the desire for cover, balanced against total stake and expected value.

  • Choose a Straight Forecast if you have a strong view on the exact 1–2 order and want to stake a single line.
  • Choose a Reverse Forecast if you expect two runners to dominate but are unsure of the order; note the total stake doubles because there are two lines.
  • Remember: only one line can win in an RF; the other line loses its stake.
  • Availability: forecasts are generally offered in races with at least three declared runners in the UK and Ireland.

What is a Straight Forecast bet?

A Straight Forecast is a bet picking two named horses to finish first and second in the exact order (e.g., “A to beat B”). It consists of one line, and the stake you set applies to that single line. With fixed‑odds bookmakers in Great Britain and Ireland, settlement is commonly to the official CSF dividend that is declared for the winning 1–2 combination; the return equals the CSF dividend to £1 multiplied by your unit stake. If the order is incorrect, the bet loses.

  • Lines: 1 line covering a single order (Horse A 1st, Horse B 2nd).
  • Settlement: usually to the official CSF dividend published after the race in GB/IE fixed‑odds markets.
  • Stake application: your entered stake is per line (one line only).
  • Non‑runner: generally voids the bet because the specified two‑horse combination can no longer occur (check your bookmaker’s rules).
  • Dead‑heats: forecasts are settled per the official procedure; returns reflect the published CSF for results affected by dead‑heats.
  • Not the Tote: on the Tote, the equivalent bet is an Exacta; dividends and settlement mechanics differ from fixed‑odds CSF.

What is a Reverse Forecast bet?

A Reverse Forecast is two Straight Forecasts on the same pair of horses, covering both possible finishing orders (A>B and B>A). It creates two lines; your per‑line stake is applied to each line, so the total stake is doubled. Only the line matching the actual finishing order can win. Settlement for each line is as per a Straight Forecast, typically using the official CSF dividend for the actual 1–2 order.

  • Lines: 2 lines (A>B and B>A).
  • Stake application: total stake = 2 × unit stake (one unit per line).
  • Outcome: only one line can win; the other line’s stake is lost.
  • Non‑runner: if one selection is a non‑runner, both lines are generally void (check bookmaker rules).
  • Dead‑heats: returns follow the published CSF for the finishing result that occurred.

Reverse vs Straight payout examples for horse racing

The examples below illustrate how CSF‑based settlement works. Bookmakers commonly settle Straight Forecasts to the official CSF dividend (quoted as a return “to £1”). Your payout for a winning line equals (CSF dividend × stake per line). Net profit equals payout minus your total stake. Actual dividends vary by race, field size, prices, and finishing order; the figures here are example dividends for clarity.

ScenarioBet typeStake per lineLinesTotal stakeActual 1–2 orderCSF dividend (£ to £1)Payout (£)Net profit (£)
Straight Forecast wins (A>B)Straight Forecast (A>B)£21£2A>B£18.40£36.80£34.80
Straight Forecast loses (wrong order)Straight Forecast (A>B)£21£2B>A—£0.00−£2.00
Reverse Forecast wins at higher dividend (A>B)Reverse Forecast (A/B)£22£4A>B£18.40£36.80£32.80
Reverse Forecast wins at lower dividend (B>A)Reverse Forecast (A/B)£22£4B>A£10.50£21.00£17.00

Interpretation: a Straight Forecast concentrates stake on a single outcome, maximising profit if your exact order is right but returning nothing if the order is wrong. A Reverse Forecast buys order insurance by splitting stake across both orders; total outlay is higher, so net profit is typically lower than an equivalent winning Straight Forecast when the higher CSF happens to land, but the RF still returns if the alternate order wins at a different CSF.

Advanced forecast perms and combos explained

Permutations let you cover multiple forecast outcomes across one or more races. In the UK and Ireland, forecast bets with fixed‑odds bookmakers are typically settled to the official Computer Straight Forecast (CSF) dividend per race. When you combine forecasts across races (doubles, trebles, accumulators), returns are compounded line‑by‑line: the winning line in leg 1 rolls into leg 2 at the declared CSF, and so on. The calculator automates line building, stake allocation per line, and sequential CSF multiplication, so you can see total stakes, potential returns, and risk concentration before placing a bet.

Forecast Doubles and Accumulator calculator guide

A Forecast Double is a multiple across two races, each leg being a Straight Forecast (SF) or a Reverse Forecast (RF). An Accumulator extends this to three or more legs. Settlement is line‑by‑line: for a winning multiple, payout = stake per line × CSF(leg 1) × CSF(leg 2) × ... × CSF(leg n). The number of overall lines equals the product of lines in each leg (SF = 1 line; RF = 2 lines; combination legs have more). Only the exact finishing orders that match a given line in every leg will return.

  • Choose legs: each leg must be a race where forecasts are offered (typically 3+ runners).
  • Select SF (1 line) or RF (2 lines) per leg; the calculator multiplies lines across legs.
  • Enter stake per line or a total stake; the other is derived from the number of lines.
  • Returns are estimated using the CSF “to £1” dividend per winning line in each leg.
BetLegsLines per legTotal linesStake per lineCSF per winning line (example)Payout (calculation)Total stakeNet result
Forecast Double (SF × SF)21 × 11£1.00£12.50; £9.00£112.50 (1 × 12.50 × 9.00)£1.00£111.50

  • Only lines that match the exact winning orders in every leg return; all other lines lose.
  • CSF is a return to £1 stake; multiply by your stake per line to get payout for that line.
  • Accumulators grow risk and potential return rapidly; check line counts and total stakes before placing.

Combination Forecast bet calculator: perms and lines

A Combination Forecast covers all possible straight‑order forecasts among a set of selections in a single race. For n selections, there are n × (n − 1) lines because each ordered pair of two distinct runners is a separate Straight Forecast line. A two‑selection combination is equivalent to a Reverse Forecast (2 lines). The calculator builds these lines automatically and computes per‑line or total stakes.

Selections (n)Unordered pairs C(n,2)Ordered pairs per pairTotal forecast lines n×(n−1)Example unit stakeTotal stake
2122£0.50£1.00
3326£0.50£3.00
46212£0.50£6.00
510220£0.50£10.00
615230£0.50£15.00
721242£0.50£21.00

Banker variants in one race are efficient when you have a standout runner:

  • Banker to finish 1st with k others for 2nd: k lines.
  • Banker to finish 2nd with k others for 1st: k lines.
  • Banker to finish 1st or 2nd with k others: 2k lines.

How are dead-heats, non-runners, and Rule 4 handled?

In Great Britain and Ireland, fixed‑odds bookmakers usually settle Straight Forecasts to the official CSF dividend. That dividend already reflects the declared runners, starting prices, and any dead‑heat outcomes. As a result, most adjustments are handled at the CSF level rather than by applying manual deductions to forecast returns. The calculator mirrors this by treating CSF as authoritative for settlement estimates and by voiding or filtering lines when a selection cannot run.

  • Dead‑heats: If there is a dead‑heat for first or second, forecasts are settled according to the official CSF publication for that result. Where two outcomes are possible (e.g., dead‑heat for second behind a clear winner), the CSF typically publishes separate dividends for each valid 1–2 combination; only lines matching those combinations can return.
  • Non‑runners: Any line containing a non‑runner is normally void. If a race reduces to fewer than three runners, forecast betting for that race is generally void in full. The calculator removes affected lines from the stake and return calculations.
  • Rule 4 (deductions): Rule 4 deductions apply to early‑price fixed‑odds singles and multiples when a horse is withdrawn, but CSF‑settled forecasts are not subject to a separate Rule 4 deduction. Instead, the official CSF for the finished race reflects the final field and starting prices. If your bookmaker explicitly offers non‑CSF forecast prices (unusual), their published rules on deductions would apply.
  • Abandoned/void race: If a leg is void or abandoned, all forecast lines for that leg are void. In multiples, standard practice is that the multiple is settled on the remaining legs with the void leg treated per bookmaker rules (often as 1.00 for that leg); always check house rules.

Important: settlement policies can vary by bookmaker, but in GB/IE fixed‑odds markets the default is CSF settlement for forecasts. Always check the specific rule summary for the operator you use before placing complex perms or cross‑race multiples.

Can I use this with Ladbrokes, Paddy Power, and others?

Yes. The calculator is bookmaker‑agnostic: you can enter prices taken from any UK or Irish fixed‑odds operator, including Ladbrokes, Paddy Power, BetVictor, Unibet, Parimatch, and others. It estimates returns for Straight Forecasts (SF) and Reverse Forecasts (RF) using the odds and stakes you input. Final settlement is always subject to your bookmaker’s rules; in Great Britain and Ireland, SF bets with fixed‑odds firms are typically settled to the official Computer Straight Forecast (CSF) dividend for the winning 1–2 order, while an RF is two SFs in opposite orders.

  • Compatibility: works with fractional or decimal odds copied from any bookmaker slip or racecard.
  • No account link: the calculator does not place bets or read your betting account; it is a planning and settlement‑estimation tool.
  • Settlement basis: uses your inputs to estimate returns; actual payouts follow the operator’s published rules and the official result.
  • Currencies: calculations are unit‑agnostic (e.g., £ or €); results scale with the stake per line you enter.

Forecast bet calculator app: install and offline use

You can install the calculator as a Progressive Web App (PWA) for quick launch and limited offline use. When installed, the core interface loads from your device even without signal, so you can enter odds and stakes on course or in transit. Live prices, results, or CSF dividends still require an internet connection; offline use is for manual data entry and computation only.

PlatformSupported browserInstall stepsOffline behaviourNotes

Android 9+Chrome (or Edge)Open site → Menu (⋮) → Add to Home screen / Install app → AddWorks as a standalone window; manual calculations offline. Network needed for updates/results.First load online is required to cache assets.
DesktopChrome or EdgeOpen site → Install (toolbar icon) or Menu → App → InstallLaunches in its own window; offline manual use where cached. Internet needed for fresh data.Behaves like a lightweight app; uninstall via browser app menu.
  • Updates: the PWA refreshes automatically when you’re next online.
  • Privacy: no bookmaker credentials are requested or stored.

How to enter prices from UK racecards and settle returns

Racecards in the UK and Ireland typically show fractional odds (e.g., 5/2, 11/4) for early prices or forecast tissue; results pages show the official CSF dividend for the winning 1–2 order after the race. For pre‑race estimates, input your available odds and stake per line. To mirror post‑race settlement, use the official CSF dividend “to £1” for the correct order and multiply by your stake per winning line.

  • Identify your pair: choose the two runners you expect to finish 1st and 2nd.

Enter odds:

  • Fractional: type as “numerator/denominator” (e.g., 9/4). The calculator converts to decimal internally.
  • Decimal: type as a number including stake (e.g., 3.25).

Select bet type:

  • Straight Forecast (SF): one line for the exact order (A>B).
  • Reverse Forecast (RF): two lines (A>B and B>A); total stake = 2 × unit stake.
  • Set stake: enter per‑line stake or total stake; the other value is derived from the number of lines.

After the race (optional for precise settlement):

  • Look up the official CSF dividend for the actual 1–2 order.
  • Payout for a winning SF line = CSF × stake per line; an RF returns only on the matching line.
  • Non‑runners: a line containing a non‑runner is normally void; if the race has fewer than three runners, forecast betting is typically void.
  • Dead‑heats: returns track the official published CSF for the recorded result.

Does this work for horse racing only?

The calculator is designed for UK and Irish horse racing, where forecast betting and CSF settlement are standard with fixed‑odds operators. The same line‑building logic (SF = 1 line, RF = 2 lines, combinations = n×(n−1) lines) also applies to other codes that offer forecasts—such as UK greyhound racing—though naming and settlement conventions can differ by sport and operator. It is not intended for Tote Exacta/Trifecta pools (which have their own dividends and mechanics) or for non‑racing sports.

  • Horse racing (GB/IE): supported for Flat, Jumps, and All‑Weather where forecasts are offered.
  • Greyhound racing: mathematical structure is identical; check the event offers forecasts and confirm settlement rules with your operator.
  • Other sports: not applicable (no forecast market).

Why calculators matter: accuracy, pitfalls, and history

Forecast calculators help you plan stakes and estimate returns for Straight Forecasts (SF) and Reverse Forecasts (RF) before you bet. They remove mental arithmetic errors, show how many lines a perm creates, and highlight the trade‑off between coverage and cost. In Great Britain and Ireland, most fixed‑odds bookmakers settle SF bets to the Computer Straight Forecast (CSF) dividend declared after the race; a calculator helps you understand how stake per line interacts with that dividend and how RF or combination perms change exposure.

  • Clarity: instantly see lines, total outlay, and estimated payouts for SF, RF, and combinations.
  • Consistency: standardised stake handling across different bookmakers and odds formats.
  • Risk awareness: reveals how adding selections balloons line count (and cost) in perms.

From paper slips to apps: a short evolution of forecast settling

Before always‑on mobile access, shop staff and results services settled forecast bets manually using declared dividends displayed in betting shops. With online sportsbooks, settlement became automated at scale and CSF dividends are applied programmatically after results are confirmed. Today, calculators and lightweight apps let you simulate complex perms on your phone and understand how CSF‑based settlement and stake allocation will affect your return long before you place a bet.

EraHow forecasts were enteredHow SF bets were settledUser challengesTypical tools
Betting shop (paper slips)Runner names/numbers written on slips; cashier processed betShop applied the published Straight Forecast dividend after the resultManual arithmetic for perms and stakes; risk of slip errorsIn‑shop dividend boards, calculators behind the counter
Early online sportsbooksWeb forms for SF/RF; limited perm buildersAutomatic application of the official CSF after resultsHard to preview line counts and compounded returns across legsDesktop calculators, results feeds
Modern mobile/PWA calculatorsTap‑to‑add runners, select SF/RF, set stake per line or totalPost‑race settlement to CSF remains standard for SF; RF is two SFsPre‑race dividends unknown; need clear stake‑per‑line logicOn‑device calculators, racecard integration, results look‑ups

What's the strongest argument against using calculators?

The strongest argument is that calculators can create an illusion of precision before the race. CSF dividends are only known after the result, and different bookmakers may have specific rules on voids and multiples. If you treat a pre‑race estimate as guaranteed, you could over‑stake or misinterpret risk. Calculators are decision aids, not settlement authorities.

  • Unknown dividends: CSF is declared after the race; pre‑race figures are illustrative only.
  • Operator rules: handling of void legs in multiples, abandoned races, and non‑runners can differ; always check your bookmaker’s rules.
  • Data entry risk: mixing total stake and per‑line stake or mis‑ordering selections can distort outcomes.
  • Over‑coverage: combination forecasts scale as n×(n−1); costs can exceed bankroll quickly.
  • Not pool betting: Tote Exacta/Trifecta use pool dividends, not CSF; a CSF‑based calculator is not suitable for pools.

Mitigations: confirm stake per line vs total stake, sanity‑check line counts, and verify settlement rules with your operator. Where available, cross‑check results and responsible gambling tools provided by your bookmaker and the regulator.

Responsible betting in the UK: limits, age, and support

In the UK you must be 18 or over to gamble. Set deposit and loss limits, consider time‑outs or self‑exclusion if needed, and never chase losses. If gambling stops being fun or you feel out of control, seek help early. Independent support and blocking tools are available and widely recommended by regulators and charities.

  • Minimum age: 18+ for betting in Great Britain.
  • Account controls: deposit limits, reality checks, time‑outs, and self‑exclusion (availability varies by operator).
  • Self‑exclusion services: register with [GAMSTOP](https://www.gamstop.co.uk) to block online gambling accounts with participating operators in Great Britain.
  • Helpline: contact the National Gambling Helpline via [0808 8020 133](tel:+448088020133) (24/7) operated by [GamCare](https://www.gamcare.org.uk).
  • Information and support: see [BeGambleAware](https://www.begambleaware.org) for advice, and the NHS overview at [NHS gambling addiction support](https://www.nhs.uk/live-well/addiction-support/gambling-addiction/).
  • Regulation: learn about rules and consumer protections at the [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk).

Frequently Asked Questions

Can you place an each-way reverse forecast?

No. Forecasts (straight or reverse) are win-only bets on the exact 1–2 order and do not have an each-way variant. Each-way terms apply to single-runner bets with separate win/place parts, which does not exist for a two-runner forecast market.

What happens to a forecast bet if the field cuts to two runners after withdrawals?

It is usually void because forecasts require at least three runners to be a valid market; the stake is returned. In multiples the void leg is typically settled as 1.00, so a £1 SF×SF double where leg 1 returns a CSF of £12.50 and leg 2 becomes void would pay £12.50 (1 × 12.50 × 1.00).

How do I cap spend on a combination forecast to a fixed budget?

Divide your budget by the line count; per-line stake = total budget ÷ lines. Example: 5 selections create 20 lines; a £10 budget gives £0.50 per line (10 ÷ 20). With 7 selections (42 lines), a £21 budget keeps £0.50 per line (21 ÷ 42).

How do I compare the expected value (EV) of a Straight Forecast versus a Reverse Forecast?

Use EV = sum(probability × payout) − total stake. Example: stake per line £1, SF on A over B with CSF D1 = 18.40 and probability p = 0.25 gives EV_SF = 0.25×18.40 − 1 = £3.60. An RF has two £1 lines; if P(A over B) = 0.25 and P(B over A) = 0.20 with D1 = 18.40 and D2 = 10.50, EV_RF = (0.25×18.40 + 0.20×10.50) − 2 = (4.60 + 2.10) − 2 = £4.70.

Do Rule 4 deductions apply to CSF-settled forecasts?

No. CSF-settled forecasts are not subject to a separate Rule 4 deduction; the official CSF already reflects the final field and SPs. For instance, a 30p/£ Rule 4 that would hit early-price singles has a 0p effect on a CSF-settled forecast; settlement follows the published CSF only.

How are dead-heats for second settled on forecasts, in practice?

The official CSF typically publishes separate dividends for each valid 1–2 pairing created by the dead-heat, and only matching lines are paid. Example: result A wins, B and C dead-heat for second; CSF prints A–B = £7.80 and A–C = £6.90 to £1. A £2 reverse forecast on A/B returns £15.60 on the A–B line (2×7.80) and loses £2 on the B–A line, net £13.60.

How are non-runners handled in a one-race combination forecast?

Lines containing a non-runner are void and removed from settlement. Example: 5 selections create 20 lines; if one is a non-runner, lines drop to 12 (for 4 selections). At £0.50 per line your total outlay falls from £10.00 to £6.00; if you keep total stake fixed at £10.00, the derived per-line stake becomes £10.00 ÷ 12 = £0.83.

Can I use the calculator for greyhound racing forecasts?

Yes, the mathematics is identical: a straight forecast is 1 line, a reverse forecast is 2 lines, and combinations follow n×(n−1). Settlement depends on the greyhound operator’s published forecast dividend rather than horse-racing CSF, so always check the applicable rules for that code.

Is the Computer Straight Forecast (CSF) the same as the Tote Exacta dividend?

No. CSF is a fixed-odds dividend quoted to £1 based on the official algorithm and SPs, while a Tote Exacta is a pool dividend determined by the net pool and winning tickets. On the same race the figures can differ markedly, for example a CSF of £18.40 versus a Tote Exacta of £23.90; you cannot infer one from the other.

How should I handle rounding when splitting a total stake across many forecast lines?

Work to the nearest penny per line and expect a small residual when the split is not exact. For example, £10.00 across 12 lines yields £0.83 per line (£9.96 total) with £0.04 unallocated; either accept the £9.96 total, manually add £0.04 to one line, or adjust the per-line stake to £0.84 and reduce the number of lines you place.

Are forecast bet winnings taxable for bettors in the UK and Ireland?

No. For individuals in the UK and Ireland, gambling winnings are not subject to income tax; the tax burden is on operators, not punters. This is a 0% tax rate on personal betting returns under current regimes, but keep records if you need to evidence source of funds.

How do I target a specific payout using an expected CSF dividend?

Stake per winning line = target payout ÷ expected CSF. Example: to aim for about £50 from a line when you expect a CSF near 12.50, stake ≈ £50 ÷ 12.50 = £4.00; remember the actual CSF may differ, so the realised payout can be higher or lower.

What happens to a forecast multiple if one leg is void or abandoned?

The void leg is usually settled as 1.00 and the multiple continues on remaining legs. Example: a £1 SF×SF double with leg 1 CSF = 9.00 and leg 2 void pays £9.00 (1 × 9.00 × 1.00); in an RF×RF double with four lines, the same 1.00 factor applies to each surviving line.

reverse-forecast.articleHtml:
How do you calculate a Reverse Forecast bet?

A Reverse Forecast is two Straight Forecast bets on the same race covering both possible 1–2 orders of your two selections. It creates two lines: Selection A to beat B, and Selection B to beat A. Settlement in UK horse racing and greyhounds is normally based on the official Computer Straight Forecast (CSF) dividend declared for the winning order. Only the line that matches the actual 1–2 order is a winner; the other line loses. Calculations therefore hinge on your unit stake per line and the dividend for the winning order.

What stake do I enter and how is it split between lines?

Enter the stake as a unit-per-line amount when placing a Reverse Forecast. Because the bet comprises two lines, your total outlay is twice the unit stake. Some calculators offer a “Total stake” mode that splits the amount equally across both lines; either way, the logic is identical—two equal stakes across the two orders.

  • Per-line stake mode: if you enter £5, your total stake is £10 (two lines at £5 each).
  • Total stake mode: if you enter £12 total, the calculator assigns £6 to each line (two lines at £6 each).
  • Rounding: stakes are handled to the nearest penny (GBP), ensuring the two lines sum exactly to your total stake.
Input modeEntered amount (£)LinesStake per line (£)Total stake (£)
Per-line3.0023.006.00
Total10.0025.0010.00
Total7.5023.757.50

Which odds formats are supported: fractional, decimal, or American?

The calculator accepts fractional (UK), decimal, and American odds for estimation and comparison. Final settlement for forecasts uses the official CSF dividend, not a direct multiplication of runner odds; odds formats are therefore most useful pre‑race for planning.

  • Fractional to decimal: decimal = (a ÷ b) + 1 for fractional a/b.

American to decimal:

  • For positive American +X: decimal = 1 + (X ÷ 100).
  • For negative American −Y: decimal = 1 + (100 ÷ Y).

Implied probability:

  • From decimal d: p = 1 ÷ d.
  • From fractional a/b: p = b ÷ (a + b).
Format enteredDecimalAmericanImplied probability
4/15.00+40020.00%
5/23.50+25028.57%
1/21.50-20066.67%
11/43.75+27526.67%

Note: Forecast returns are settled to the official CSF dividend for the winning order. Odds conversions above help you compare selections and create pre‑race estimates; they do not replace the declared dividend at settlement.

How are returns, total stake, and profit calculated?

For a Reverse Forecast settled to the official CSF dividend D for the actual 1–2 order, with unit stake per line s:

  • Total stake T = 2 × s (two lines).
  • Return R = s × D (only the winning-order line returns).
  • Profit P = R − T.

If a non‑runner leads to the bet being void before the off, UK bookmakers typically void both lines of a Reverse Forecast and return the stake; in that case R = T and P = 0. If there is a Rule 4 deduction pre‑off, the official CSF dividend will reflect the revised market; when using pre‑race estimation, apply the Rule 4 factor to affected selections before estimating any notional dividend. For dead‑heats, settlement follows the dividend(s) declared for the published result.

ScenarioUnit stake s (£)LinesTotal stake T (£)Official CSF dividend D (£)Return R (£)Profit P (£)Notes
Win (A–B result)5.00210.008.2041.0031.00Only A–B line wins; B–A line loses.
Win (B–A result)2.0024.003.507.003.00Only B–A line wins; A–B line loses.
Lose (not 1–2 in any order)3.0026.00—0.00-6.00No line matches the result.
Void (non‑runner before off)4.0028.00—8.000.00Both lines voided; stake refunded.

Summary: a Reverse Forecast is two equal-stake forecast lines; settlement uses the CSF dividend for the actual 1–2 order. Your calculator output should always show Total stake, Return (including stake), and Profit, with any voids or deductions clearly identified per UK rules.

Horse racing or greyhounds: where does a Reverse Forecast make sense?

A Reverse Forecast (RF) is most useful when you strongly expect two runners to fill first and second but cannot confidently pick the exact order. This scenario appears frequently in UK greyhound races (six-runner fields) and in horse races where the market has a clear top two. Because an RF covers both 1–2 and 2–1, it costs twice a Straight Forecast (SF) stake but buys order insurance. Settlement for bookmaker forecasts in the UK generally uses the official Computer Straight Forecast (CSF) dividend for the winning order.

  • Good fit: small or medium fields with two stand‑out contenders; strong pace/setup edge suggesting a pair will dominate.
  • Less suitable: highly competitive big fields where the top two are hard to isolate; cases where you have a firm view on the exact finishing order (then an SF is more efficient).
  • Greyhounds: RFs are common due to six-dog fields and clearer draw/early speed dynamics.
  • Horses: RFs see value when two market leaders are well clear on ratings, recent form, or sectional times.

Straight vs Reverse Forecast: what’s the difference?

A Straight Forecast (SF) selects the exact 1–2 order; a Reverse Forecast is two SF lines on the same pair, covering both possible orders. The RF doubles your stake but reduces order risk. Only the line that matches the official 1–2 order is settled as a winner at the CSF dividend for that order; the other line loses.

FeatureStraight Forecast (SF)Reverse Forecast (RF)
Selections2 runners in exact order (A first, B second)Same 2 runners, both possible orders (A–B and B–A)
Number of lines12
StakeUnit stake s2 lines at s each (total 2s)
Settlement basisOfficial CSF dividend for the exact A–B resultOfficial CSF dividend for whichever order actually wins
Return (win)s × CSF(A–B)s × CSF(winning order); other line loses
When to useYou have a strong view on orderYou fancy the pair to fill 1–2 but not the order

In both horse racing and greyhounds, bookmakers publish the CSF dividend for the winning order after the result is official. An RF monetises your “paired dominance” view; an SF monetises your “exact order” conviction at lower total stake.

What is a Reverse Forecast Double and how is it settled?

A Reverse Forecast Double combines two RFs in different races into one multiple. Structurally, each RF has two SF lines; multiplying across two races creates four lines in total: (A–B with C–D), (A–B with D–C), (B–A with C–D), (B–A with D–C). You stake the same unit s on each of the four lines, so the total stake is 4s. Settlement multiplies the winning CSF dividends leg‑by‑leg for the matching order in each race.

  • Lines and stake: 4 lines at s each ⇒ total stake T = 4s.
  • Winning condition: Both races must produce a 1–2 comprising your named pair; exactly one of the four lines can win.
  • Return: R = s × CSF(Race 1 winning order) × CSF(Race 2 winning order).
  • Profit: P = R − T.

Example: s = £2, CSF in Race 1 for the winning order = 5.80, CSF in Race 2 for the winning order = 7.40. Total stake T = £8. Return R = 2 × 5.80 × 7.40 = £85.84. Profit P = £85.84 − £8 = £77.84. If either race fails to land the 1–2 with your pair, all four lines lose. If one leg is made void before the off under your bookmaker’s rules, affected lines are void; surviving lines typically settle as singles on the remaining race at its CSF dividend (check the operator’s rules for the exact treatment).

Do non-runners and dead-heats affect payouts?

Yes. Non-runners and dead-heats change settlement mechanics, but the calculator mirrors official outcomes using published dividends and common UK rules. Always verify the specific operator’s terms where you place the bet, as implementations can differ between firms.

Non-runners (pre‑off):

  • Single RF: If one of your two selections becomes a non‑runner before the off, the forecast cannot be formed. Depending on the bookmaker, the RF is usually voided with stakes returned, or converted to a win single on the remaining selection at SP. Check house rules.
  • RF Doubles/Multiples: Lines containing the void leg are void. Remaining leg(s) may settle as singles at the declared CSF for those races per the operator’s multiple‑bet rules.
  • Reserves (greyhounds): If a reserve replaces a withdrawn runner, bets typically stand on the revised field; the CSF dividend will reflect the starters.

Dead-heats:

  • Dead‑heat for second (e.g., A wins; B and C dead‑heat for second): Bookmakers publish CSF dividends for each valid winning forecast (A–B and A–C). If your line matches one of the declared outcomes, you are paid at that dividend.
  • Dead‑heat for first (e.g., A and B dead‑heat for first): The computer dividend algorithm applies the official rules; where relevant CSF dividends are declared, settlement is to those; if no CSF can be declared for an exact order, standard dead‑heat rules on stake/return apply per the operator.
  • Post‑race adjustments: Any stewards’ amendments to the official result are applied to CSF settlement once the result is made official.

Practical takeaway: Your calculator return for RFs is driven by the CSF dividend for the winning order. Non‑runners can void or convert the bet depending on the firm, and dead‑heats are handled via the published forecast dividends or, failing that, the bookmaker’s dead‑heat rules.

How to use our free Reverse Forecast Calculator?

The calculator is designed for UK horse racing and greyhounds to price Reverse Forecasts (RF) and RF Doubles quickly and transparently. Enter your two selections per race, choose odds format (fractional, decimal, or American), set your stake (per line or total), and, if needed, add a second race to build a Double. The tool returns total stake, estimated or official-dividend returns, and profit. Where available, use dead-heat and Rule 4 inputs to mirror official settlement conditions.

Enter runners and odds: quick input tips

Reverse Forecasts cover both possible 1–2 orders of your pair, so you do not need to specify first/second when entering names. Use the odds format you are most comfortable with; the calculator converts between fractional, decimal, and American automatically. For greyhounds, entering trap numbers alongside names helps avoid mix-ups (e.g., “T3 Swift Ace”).

  • Runners: enter the exact racecard names to match the declared result; add saddle/cloth or trap numbers as a cue.
  • Odds entry: fractional for UK familiarity (e.g., 7/2), decimal for quick probability checks (e.g., 4.50), American for comparability (+250).
  • Stake entry: choose per-line stake if you plan a fixed unit per SF line; choose total stake to let the tool split it 50/50.
  • Greyhounds: draws and early pace matter—log trap numbers; horses: note headgear/going if you keep notes in the comments field.
Input areaAccepted formatsExamplesValidation rulesTip
Runner A / Runner BText (letters, spaces, digits)“Chaselot”, “T3 Swift Ace”Non-empty; avoid duplicatesAdd cloth/trap to reduce errors
Odds (fractional)a/b with positive integers7/2, 11/4, 1/2a ≥ 1, b ≥ 1Use UK racecard prices
Odds (decimal)Real number ≥ 1.011.50, 3.75, 5.80Two to three decimalsQuick for probability (1/d)
Odds (American)+X or -Y integer+250, -200|value| ≥ 100Converts to decimal
Stake modePer line or TotalPer line = £3; Total = £10GBP to nearest £0.01RF uses two equal lines
AdjustmentsRule 4, dead-heat flagsRule 4: 10p; DH: tickOptional, when applicableMirrors official terms

Add multiple races: can I chain Doubles?

Yes—adding a second race creates a Reverse Forecast Double. Each RF expands into two SF lines; two races produce four lines in total. You stake the same unit on each line, so your total stake is four times your unit stake. Settlement multiplies the declared CSF dividends from both races for the winning orders. While the calculator can show how line counts grow, most UK bookmakers commonly accept RF Singles and RF Doubles; higher-order forecast multiples (triples/accas) are rarely available online and may be refused—always check house rules before placing.

  • Single RF: 2 lines at stake s each ⇒ total stake 2s.
  • RF Double: 4 lines at stake s each ⇒ total stake 4s.
  • General pattern: with n races, lines = 2ⁿ; total stake = s × 2ⁿ (availability beyond Doubles varies by operator).
  • Return formula (Double): R = s × CSF₁ × CSF₂ for the matching orders; profit = R − 4s.
Races includedLinesTotal stake multiplierWinning conditionReturn (example)
1 (RF Single)22 × sOne race: your pair fills 1–2 in any orderR = s × CSF(order)
2 (RF Double)44 × sBoth races: your pairs land 1–2R = s × CSF₁ × CSF₂

Example: s = £2; Race 1 CSF (winning order) = 6.10; Race 2 CSF (winning order) = 4.80. Total stake = 4 × £2 = £8. Return = £2 × 6.10 × 4.80 = £58.56. Profit = £58.56 − £8 = £50.56.

Is there an app or offline mode for quick calcs?

The calculator is web-first and works on modern mobile and desktop browsers. You can speed access via “Add to Home Screen” on iOS or “Install app” on Android/Chrome. This creates an app-like launcher. Basic inputs and saved settings may be available from your device cache; however, fetching official dividends, live markets, or rule updates requires an internet connection.

  • iOS (Safari): open the calculator, tap Share, choose “Add to Home Screen,” confirm the name, then tap Add.
  • Android (Chrome): open the menu (⋮), tap “Install app” or “Add to Home screen,” then confirm.
  • Desktop (Chrome/Edge): look for the “Install” icon in the address bar to create an app shortcut.
  • Offline scope: you can enter runners and odds manually offline for planning; settlement using CSF needs connectivity to confirm official dividends.

Tip: If you regularly bet at race time, pre-load the page on Wi‑Fi before travelling. This ensures assets are cached and the calculator opens instantly; reconnect to confirm results and any adjustments (e.g., non‑runners, Rule 4) before settling decisions.

Does this calculator match UK bookmaker rules?

Yes—when you enter the official Computer Straight Forecast (CSF) dividend for the winning 1–2 order, our Reverse Forecast (RF) outputs align with standard UK settlement used by major operators. CSF is derived from the Starting Prices (SPs) of the relevant race and is uniformly applied across UK bookmaking for horse racing and greyhounds. Differences between operators generally arise from stake handling (e.g., free bets), acceptance of multiples, rounding, and specific house rules in non‑runner or dead‑heat scenarios. For consumer protection and consistency, UK‑licensed firms operate under the oversight of the [Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk).

Paddy Power and Ladbrokes: will results align?

They should, provided you mirror the official conditions. Both firms settle forecast bets to the published CSF dividend for the winning order, so if you input the same unit stake per line and the same CSF dividend, your calculator return will match bookmaker settlement (before any firm‑specific bonuses or stake types are applied).

  • Settlement basis: CSF dividend for the actual 1–2 order in the race.
  • Stake logic: an RF is two Straight Forecast lines; total stake is twice your per‑line stake.
  • Rounding: UK online settlement is to the nearest penny (GBP); our calculator does the same.
  • Potential sources of variance: use of free bet tokens, boosts, or promos (not reflected by our neutral calculator); post‑race amendments (we pay to the final official dividend you enter).

BetVictor vs Unibet vs Parimatch vs Betano: what differs?

Core settlement—paying RFs to the CSF dividend—is industry‑standard across UK‑regulated brands. Practical differences typically involve availability of RF Doubles online, how void legs in multiples are handled, maximum payout limits, whether free bets/boosts apply to forecast markets, and presentation/rounding details. Always check each operator’s published rules within their help/terms sections before placing multiples or using promotions.

Settlement aspectUK baseline (industry standard)Calculator behaviourNotes
RF compositionTwo SF lines (A–B and B–A) at equal stakesRequires per‑line or total stake; splits 50/50Total stake = 2 × unit stake
Dividend sourceOfficial CSF for the winning 1–2 order (SP‑based)User enters the published CSF dividendApplies to horses and greyhounds

Void (non‑runner pre‑off)RF single usually void; in multiples, voided leg reducesVoids affected lines; remaining legs settle as applicableHouse rules detail exact treatment

RoundingGBP to nearest pennyNearest £0.01Aligns with online settlement
