matched-betting.articleHtml:
How do I use the Matched Betting Calculator (UK)?

This calculator balances a bookmaker back bet with an exchange lay bet to lock in a small qualifying loss or extract value from a free bet. In the UK, exchanges charge commission only on net winnings for a market. Typical base rates are 2% at Smarkets and 5% at Betfair Exchange (rates can vary by account and promotion; always confirm on the exchange’s help pages at [smarkets.com/help](https://smarkets.com/help) and [support.betfair.com](https://support.betfair.com)). Enter decimal odds, your stake, the lay odds, and the exchange commission; choose the bet type (Qualifier or SNR free bet), and the calculator returns the lay stake and liability.

What inputs do I enter: back odds, stake, lay odds, commission?

The core inputs reflect the bookmaker price, your stake, the exchange lay price, and the exchange’s commission. The calculator also needs the bet type because the formula differs for a qualifying bet versus a “stake not returned” (SNR) free bet.

FieldWhere it comes fromExample (UK)Notes
Back odds (decimal)Bookmaker market price3.00UK sites display decimal odds as standard for calculators.
Back stake (£)Your bookmaker bet amount10.00For qualifiers this is real cash; for SNR, this is the free bet size.
Lay odds (decimal)Exchange lay price3.05Use the lay price with sufficient liquidity at your stake size.
Exchange commission (%)Exchange account settings2.00 (Smarkets), 5.00 (Betfair)Commission applies to net winnings in the market; confirm your rate.
ModeOffer termsQualifier or SNRQualifier = cash stake returned. SNR = free bet stake not returned.
Lay stake (output)Calculator result9.90 (balanced, Smarkets 2%)Rounded to currency precision accepted by the exchange.
Liability (output)Calculator result£20.30Amount the exchange withholds if your back selection wins.

Balanced lay stake formulas (commission = c as a decimal, e.g., 0.02):

  • Qualifier: Lay stake = (Back odds × Back stake) ÷ (Lay odds − c)
  • SNR free bet: Lay stake = [(Back odds − 1) × Free bet size] ÷ (Lay odds − c)

Underlay vs overlay: when should you nudge the lay stake?

“Balanced” aims to make outcomes as equal as possible. Nudging the lay stake changes which outcome is more profitable:

  • Underlay (reduce lay stake): Higher profit if the back bet wins; lower profit (or higher loss) if it loses. Also reduces exchange liability.
  • Overlay (increase lay stake): Higher profit if the back bet loses (exchange win); lower profit if it wins. Increases exchange liability.

Example with a qualifying bet (bookmaker back 3.00 at £10, exchange lay 3.05, Smarkets 2%):

StrategyLay stake (£)Liability (£)Profit if back wins (£)Profit if back loses (£)
Balanced9.9020.30-0.30-0.30
Underlay9.5019.48+0.53-0.69
Overlay10.3021.12-1.12+0.09
  • Typical use: Underlay is common for SNR free bets when you prefer a bigger return if the back bet wins at the bookmaker. Overlay can be useful if you prefer the exchange to win (e.g., to limit bookmaker-side wins or simplify rollover planning). Choose based on risk tolerance and offer goals.

Step‑by‑step with Smarkets or Betfair Exchange (UK)

Follow this process to minimise slippage and mismatches between the bookmaker and the exchange.

  • Find a tight match: Identify a market where the bookmaker back odds and exchange lay odds are close with adequate liquidity at your target lay stake.
  • Confirm commission: Check your current exchange commission (e.g., 2% Smarkets, 5% Betfair) via [smarkets.com/help](https://smarkets.com/help) or [support.betfair.com](https://support.betfair.com), and enter it in the calculator.
  • Enter inputs: Type back odds and back stake (or free bet size), the exchange lay odds, and select Qualifier or SNR in the calculator.
  • Review outputs: Note the lay stake and liability. If needed, choose Underlay or Overlay and recheck the projected outcomes.
  • Place the exchange order first if liquidity is thin: Submit the lay at the quoted lay price. If using a limit order, ensure it is fully matched before placing the bookmaker bet. If liquidity is strong, many place the back bet first and lay immediately after.
  • Enter the exact lay stake: On Smarkets and Betfair, the “Stake” box for a lay bet is your lay stake (not liability). Check that the platform shows the expected liability before confirming.
  • Place the bookmaker back bet: Use the same selection and stake used in the calculator. Verify the accepted odds match your input; if the odds move, recalculate before placing.
  • Record and reconcile: Save the event, odds, stakes, commission, and expected profit. After settlement, compare actual P&L with the calculator’s projection.
  • Smarkets specifics: Commission is typically 2% on net market winnings; the bet slip displays “Liability” and “Potential profit” clearly. Partial matches are possible; unmatched portions remain as open lay offers until canceled or matched.
  • Betfair specifics: Standard UK base commission is often 5% on net market winnings (account-dependent). Betfair also supports partial fills; unmatched orders persist at your limit price unless you cancel or edit them.
  • Good practice: If a lay is partially matched at multiple prices, update the calculator with the weighted average lay odds and total matched lay stake to verify expected P&L.

Which mode do you need: 3‑Way, Each Way, Qualifier, or SNR?

Different offer mechanics and settlement rules require different calculator modes. Use 3‑Way when a push/void is possible, Each Way for horse racing bets split into win and place legs, and choose Qualifier vs SNR to reflect whether your stake is returned (cash bet) or not returned (free bet). Correct mode selection ensures lay stakes, liability, and projected P&L are computed under the right assumptions for UK exchanges and bookmakers.

When should I use the 3 way calculator for win/lose/void outcomes?

A 3‑Way calculator produces three projected results: back‑win, back‑lose (i.e., lay‑win), and void/push. Use it whenever the rules reasonably allow an outcome where your original bookmaker bet is canceled and refunded, and the exchange position may also void or be canceled according to market rules.

  • Draw No Bet (football): Home/away wins settle normally; a draw voids the back bet (push). Exchanges typically offer a “Draw No Bet” market that also voids on a draw.
  • Asian Handicap 0 (AH 0): Team wins = back‑win, team loses = back‑lose; a draw = push/void on both sides.
  • Tennis retirements (rule differences): Some bookmakers void if a set is not completed; exchanges (e.g., Betfair) void if the match is not completed under market rules. A 3‑Way setup helps you plan for a potential void branch. Always check rules in advance.
  • Non‑runner or event cancellation: Horse non‑runners or abandoned events commonly void both bookmaker and exchange bets on that selection/market.
  • Confirm settlement rules for both the bookmaker and the exchange on the specific market.
  • Enter back odds, lay odds, stake, and commission as usual.
  • Enable “3‑Way” mode and verify the void branch is aligned to the correct scenario (both sides void). If rules can lead to “book‑only void” or “exchange‑only void,” use a calculator that supports those toggles and review P&L for each path.

Each Way calculator: how are place terms and EW fractions handled?

An Each Way bet is two bets: win and place, each at half the total stake for a 1x EW. The place odds are derived from the win odds using the bookmaker’s EW fraction and stated place terms (e.g., 1/5 odds, 3 places). In matched betting, you typically lay the win selection in the exchange “Win” market and lay the same runner in the exchange “Place” market separately, each with its own lay odds and potentially different liquidity.

  • EW stake split: Total stake £S becomes £S/2 on Win and £S/2 on Place.

Place odds conversion (decimal): Place odds = 1 + (Win odds − 1) × EW fraction.

  • Example: Win odds 10.00 with 1/5 terms → Place odds = 1 + 9 × 0.2 = 2.80.
  • Lay separately: Compute a lay stake for the Win leg using win lay odds and commission, and a lay stake for the Place leg using place lay odds and commission.

Settlement pathways:

  • Runner wins: Win leg settles as a win; Place leg also settles as a place win.
  • Runner places (but not first): Win leg loses; Place leg wins.
  • Runner unplaced: Both legs lose.
  • Edge cases: Dead‑heats in win or place markets apply dead‑heat rules on returns and exchange P&L; recalculate using the official settlement reduction factors.

Qualifying vs SNR free bet: what changes in the formula?

Qualifying bets return the stake from the bookmaker; SNR (“stake not returned”) free bets do not return the nominal stake—only the winnings. Because of that, the balanced lay stake has a different numerator. Commission (c) is the exchange commission expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.02 for 2%).

ModeOutcomes modelledBalanced lay stake (L)LiabilityOutcome profit (Back wins)Outcome profit (Back loses)Typical use
Qualifier (cash)Win / LoseL = S × b ÷ (l − c)(l − 1) × LS × (b − 1) − (l − 1) × L−S + L × (1 − c)Initial qualifying wagers to unlock offers
SNR (free bet)Win / LoseL = S × (b − 1) ÷ (l − c)(l − 1) × LS × (b − 1) − (l − 1) × LL × (1 − c)Risk‑free token bets where stake is not returned
3‑WayWin / Lose / VoidAs per Qualifier or SNR, plus void branch(l − 1) × L (if market stands)S × (b − 1) − (l − 1) × L−S + L × (1 − c)Markets allowing push/void (e.g., DNB, AH 0); Void branch = 0 if both sides void
Each Way (Win leg)Win / LoseLw = (S/2) × b ÷ (lw − c)(lw − 1) × Lw(S/2) × (b − 1) − (lw − 1) × Lw−(S/2) + Lw × (1 − c)Lay in the exchange Win market
Each Way (Place leg)Place win / Place loseLp = (S/2) × p ÷ (lp − c)(lp − 1) × Lp(S/2) × (p − 1) − (lp − 1) × Lp−(S/2) + Lp × (1 − c)Lay in the exchange Place market; p = 1 + (b − 1) × fraction

Variable key: S = stake (for EW, S/2 per leg; for SNR, S is the free bet size). b = bookmaker back odds (decimal). l = lay odds in the exchange for the same market/leg. lw = lay odds in the Win market; lp = lay odds in the Place market. c = exchange commission as a decimal. p = derived place odds = 1 + (b − 1) × fraction (e.g., fraction = 0.2 for 1/5, 0.25 for 1/4).

  • Qualifying bets usually produce a small, near‑equal loss across outcomes; minimise it by finding tight back/lay matches and lower commission.
  • SNR free bets target positive EV; EV generally improves at higher back odds, but liability and variance increase—manage bankroll accordingly.
  • Each Way requires two separate lays; ensure liquidity exists in both Win and Place exchange markets before placing your bookmaker bet.
  • 3‑Way is essential where a push/void is plausible; if rules can cause only one side to void, ensure your calculator models “book‑only void” or “exchange‑only void” specifically.

App, web, or Excel — what’s best for UK matched bettors?

UK matched bettors typically choose between three channels: web calculators (fast and accessible), Excel/Google Sheets (fully custom and offline-capable), and dedicated mobile apps (portable and streamlined). The optimal choice depends on whether you prioritise speed, custom modelling (e.g., 3‑Way, Each Way, SNR), offline use, or integrations. Exchange commission is account-dependent (commonly 2% at Smarkets and 5% at Betfair Exchange in the UK); ensure any tool you use lets you set commission precisely.

Is there a free matched betting calculator app in the UK?

Yes. There are several ways to use a free calculator on mobile in the UK:

  • Web calculators (free): Many UK-focused sites provide free in-browser calculators. On modern iOS/Android browsers you can “Add to Home Screen” to run them like an app (a Progressive Web App, PWA).
  • Third‑party mobile apps: Some iOS/Android apps include matched betting calculators. Availability, feature depth (3‑Way, Each Way), and pricing (free, ads, in‑app purchases) vary by publisher—check the app’s listing and permissions.
  • DIY mobile: A Google Sheets calculator works in the Sheets app; enable offline access in advance via [support.google.com/docs/answer/6388102](https://support.google.com/docs/answer/6388102).

Minimum features to look for in a free app or PWA:

  • Modes: Qualifier, SNR, 3‑Way (Win/Lose/Void), and Each Way (separate Win and Place legs).
  • Commission control: Custom percentage to two decimal places (e.g., 2.00% for Smarkets, 5.00% for Betfair).
  • Precision and rounding: Lay stake and liability shown to 2 dp (or platform-accepted increments), with outcome P&L for each branch.
  • Safety helpers: Clear display of liability, warning if liquidity is likely insufficient, and notes on settlement rules.
ChannelAccessOffline3‑Way / Each WayCommission controlTypical costNotes
Web calculatorBrowser (desktop/mobile)No (unless PWA cached)Often supportedSupported on reputable toolsUsually freeFast to use; can be added as a PWA on iOS/Android
Excel / Google SheetsLocal workbook or cloudYes (Excel desktop); Sheets with offline enabledYes, if modelledFully customExcel requires licence; Sheets free with Google accountMaximum flexibility; ideal for custom reporting
Mobile app (third‑party)iOS/Android installVaries by appVaries by appVaries by appFree, ads, or IAP/subscriptionCheck feature list and privacy policy before use

Decision tip: If you want speed and zero setup, start with a web calculator or PWA. If you want audit trails, batch logging, and custom tweaks (e.g., unusual commission rules, partial matches), build a spreadsheet. If you need portability with saved presets and no browser UI, test a reputable dedicated app.

Can I build my own Excel matched betting calculator?

Yes. A simple workbook can reproduce industry‑standard calculations for Qualifier, SNR, 3‑Way, and Each Way. Below is a minimal structure using decimal odds and commission applied to exchange winnings.

Inputs (single‑selection market):

  • b = Back odds (decimal), l = Lay odds (decimal), S = Stake (£), c = Exchange commission as a decimal (e.g., 0.02 for 2%).

Excel setup (example cell mapping):

  • B2 = b, B3 = l, B4 = S, B5 = commission percent (e.g., 2). In any formula, use c = B5/100.

Qualifier (cash stake returned):

  • Lay stake L: = $B$4 * $B$2 / ($B$3 - ($B$5/100))
  • Liability: = ($B$3 - 1) * L
  • Profit if back wins: = $B$4 * ($B$2 - 1) - ($B$3 - 1) * L
  • Profit if back loses: = -$B$4 + L * (1 - $B$5/100)

SNR (stake not returned) free bet:

  • Lay stake Lsnr: = $B$4 * ($B$2 - 1) / ($B$3 - ($B$5/100))
  • Profit if back wins: = $B$4 * ($B$2 - 1) - ($B$3 - 1) * Lsnr
  • Profit if back loses: = Lsnr * (1 - $B$5/100)
  • 3‑Way (Win/Lose/Void): Use the Qualifier or SNR formulas for L and add a “Void” branch. If both sides void, profit = 0. If rules differ (e.g., book voids but exchange stands), add a branch: P&L = − exchange liability or + exchange net depending on which side stands. Always confirm market rules.

Each Way (horse racing):

  • Split stake: Win stake = S/2; Place stake = S/2.
  • Place odds (decimal): p = 1 + (b − 1) × fraction, where fraction is 0.20 for 1/5, 0.25 for 1/4, etc.
  • Compute separate lays using exchange Win lay odds lw and Place lay odds lp (and same commission c):
  • Win lay Lw: = (S/2) * b / (lw − c) | Place lay Lp: = (S/2) * p / (lp − c)
  • Example (SNR): b = 5.00, l = 5.10, S = 20, c = 0.02 → L ≈ 20×(5−1)/(5.10−0.02) = 80/5.08 ≈ 15.75; Back‑win P ≈ 20×4 − 4.10×15.75 = 15.43; Back‑lose P ≈ 15.75×0.98 = 15.44 (minor rounding differences are normal).
  • Resources: Excel desktop (licence required; see [support.microsoft.com/excel](https://support.microsoft.com/excel)) and Google Sheets (free with Google account; offline via [support.google.com/docs/answer/6388102](https://support.google.com/docs/answer/6388102)).

From spreadsheets to apps: how did calculators evolve?

Matched betting tools have matured alongside UK exchanges and browsers. Early practitioners used manual formulas; modern users benefit from robust, mobile‑friendly interfaces and clearer risk controls.

  • 2008–2012: Spreadsheet era — community‑shared Excel files model Qualifier/SNR, basic Each Way, manual commission inputs, manual logging.
  • 2013–2016: Web calculators — purpose‑built pages add 3‑Way branches, explicit liability display, per‑exchange commission inputs, and clearer rounding.
  • 2017–2020: Mobile and PWA — responsive calculators run smoothly on phones; “Add to Home Screen” enables app‑like experiences without installs; Sheets/Excel mobile improve viability of DIY tools.
  • 2021–present: Refinement — more accurate edge‑case handling (dead‑heats, partial matches), saved presets, and better UX for underlay/overlay decisions; some tools export logs to CSV/Sheets for reconciliation.
  • Best‑practice today: Use calculators that (1) let you set exchange commission precisely, (2) present outcome P&L per branch, (3) handle Each Way as distinct Win/Place legs, and (4) warn about liability and potential rule mismatches. Always verify against official exchange rules pages and keep a ledger for audit.

How does this tool compare to OddsMonkey and Outplayed?

This calculator focuses on accurate lay stakes, liability, and outcome P&L across Qualifier, SNR, 3‑Way, and Each Way modes. Subscription platforms like [oddsmonkey.com](https://www.oddsmonkey.com) and [outplayed.com](https://outplayed.com) bundle calculators with odds‑matching, training, and workflow features. The right choice depends on whether you want a lightweight, free calculator or an all‑in‑one toolkit with discovery, tracking, and education.

OddsMonkey vs Outplayed vs a free calculator: who is it for?

Match your tool to your experience level, volume, and need for discovery and coaching.

Free calculator (web/PWA/Excel):

  • Best for: Users who already know how to find tight back/lay prices and only need precise stakes and P&L.
  • Strengths: Zero cost, fast, flexible; works with any bookmaker/exchange; easy to verify calculations.
  • Trade‑offs: No integrated odds feed, offer discovery, or logging; entirely manual workflow.

OddsMonkey (subscription):

  • Best for: Beginners to intermediate users wanting discovery tools (Oddsmatcher), tutorials, and multiple calculators (e.g., Each Way, Acca, Dutching).
  • Strengths: Broad toolset, structured training, community resources, and calculators covering common scenarios.
  • Trade‑offs: Subscription cost; you still confirm bets and settlement rules at the bookmaker/exchange.

Outplayed (subscription):

  • Best for: Users who value guided offer walkthroughs, Each Way/Acca matching tools, and consolidated tracking.
  • Strengths: Discovery plus calculators, consistent workflows, and education content.
  • Trade‑offs: Subscription cost; you remain responsible for verifying odds, liquidity, and market rules.

Decision rule of thumb: If you already source your own odds and keep a ledger, a free calculator can be sufficient. If you want an integrated workflow—finding, valuing, and logging offers with fewer manual steps—consider a subscription platform.

Do exchange commissions differ across Smarkets, Betfair, Matchbook?

Yes. In the UK, major exchanges charge commission on net winnings in a market, not on losses. Your effective rate can vary by account, market, region, or promotion. Always confirm on the exchange’s official help pages before placing bets.

ExchangePublished standard commission (UK)Applied toNotesOfficial help
Smarkets2%Net winnings per marketOccasional promos may alter rates for some users; confirm your account rate.smarkets.com/help
Betfair Exchange5% base rateNet winnings per marketDiscounts and special charges can apply to certain accounts; check your base rate and any additional charges.support.betfair.com
Matchbook2%Net winnings per marketRates may vary by market or promotion; verify current terms in your region.help.matchbook.com

Calculator tip: Enter your actual account rate (e.g., 2.00% or 5.00%) and recalc if it changes. Commission is taken from exchange winnings only; if the exchange leg loses, there is no commission on that market.

What about integrations: odds auto‑fill, 3‑way, Each Way, accas?

Free calculators focus on accurate maths; subscription platforms typically add discovery and workflow integrations. Common capabilities include:

Odds auto‑fill and discovery:

  • Oddsmatching tools scrape bookmaker prices and compare to exchange lay odds, passing selections to a calculator with fields pre‑filled.
  • Deep links to markets reduce navigation time; final bet placement and confirmation always occur on the bookmaker/exchange site.

Calculator coverage:

  • 3‑Way: Explicit Win/Lose/Void branches for push‑prone markets (e.g., DNB, AH 0).
  • Each Way: Separate Win and Place legs with derived place odds via EW fraction; support for dead‑heat scenarios varies by tool.
  • Accumulators (accas): Dedicated calculators model sequential laying or lay‑at‑end approaches; tools often help track leg outcomes and implied liability.

Workflow helpers:

  • Presets for commission by exchange (e.g., Smarkets 2%, Betfair 5%, Matchbook 2%).
  • Logging and export (CSV/Sheets) for reconciliation.
  • Offer trackers, reminders, and onboarding tutorials for new users.

Regardless of platform, always verify exchange liquidity, market rules (voids, dead‑heats, retirements), and your commission rate on the official help pages: [smarkets.com/help](https://smarkets.com/help), [support.betfair.com](https://support.betfair.com), and [help.matchbook.com](https://help.matchbook.com). Subscriptions can speed up discovery and reduce manual steps, while a free calculator remains a precise, lightweight option if you already source suitable odds and keep careful records.

Risks, limits, and the other side of the coin: is using a matched betting calculator always safe?

A calculator can get the maths right and still produce the wrong outcome in practice if execution, rules, or account status change. The strongest risk is operational: odds and liquidity move, settlement rules differ by market, and bookmakers can restrict (“gub”) accounts. Treat calculators as planning tools, not guarantees. Verify rules, allow buffers for slippage, and follow safer gambling and bankroll controls.

What is the strongest argument against matched betting calculators?

The most compelling counter‑argument is that calculators cannot protect you from execution and settlement risk. They balance outcomes under fixed inputs and uniform rules, but real betting is dynamic: prices update, orders can partially match, commissions vary by account, and markets settle under rule sets (voids, dead‑heats, Rule 4 deductions) that may diverge between bookmaker and exchange. Human error (misclicks, wrong market, stake rounding) compounds this. As a result, a “correct” lay stake can still yield unexpected losses if any real‑world assumption breaks.

Failure modeWhat actually happensLikely P&L effectHow to mitigateWhere to verify
Odds move between back and layBack is struck; lay price drifts before your order matchesWider qualifying loss or missed valuePre‑check liquidity; place lay first in thin markets; use limitssmarkets.com/help, support.betfair.com
Partial or no match at the exchangeYour lay is only partly filled, leaving exposureUnhedged risk; liability higher than plannedUse sizes within queue depth; monitor fills; cancel/re‑price quicklysmarkets.com/help, support.betfair.com
Rule mismatch (voids, retirements, dead‑heats)Bookmaker and exchange settle differently on the same eventAsymmetric P&L; possible unexpected lossRead market rules before betting; avoid ambiguous marketssupport.betfair.com
Commission not set correctlyActual commission differs from calculator inputOutcome imbalance; smaller profit or larger lossConfirm account rate and promos; save presets per exchangesmarkets.com/help
Bookmaker limitation (“gubbing”)Stake limits, price restrictions, promo exclusionHarder to place the “back”; workflow frictionBroaden activity profile; moderate stakes; diversify operatorsgamblingcommission.gov.uk

Bankroll management and gubbing: how do you stay under the radar?

Bankroll controls limit the impact of slippage and outlier events. “Under the radar” behaviour reduces the chance of bookmaker restrictions. Neither removes risk entirely, but both materially reduce tail losses and workflow friction.

Capital allocation:

  • Separate bookmaker float and exchange float. Ensure exchange balance covers maximum projected liability plus a safety buffer (e.g., an extra 10–20% for odds drift).
  • Liability formula: Liability = (Lay odds − 1) × Lay stake. Plan exposure across overlapping events so total liability stays within limits.

Sizing and sequencing:

  • Keep stake sizes proportionate to market liquidity; avoid thin side markets unless you can place the lay first and watch fills.
  • Avoid cascading exposure across many legs without contingency funds (e.g., accas with sequential laying).

Execution hygiene:

  • Double‑check selection, market (Win vs Place), odds format (decimal), and commission before confirming.
  • Recalculate if any price or stake changes on the slip; match exchange odds to at least two decimal places where supported.

Staying off restriction lists (“gubbing”):

  • Avoid only betting on promotions; mix in organic markets and casual timings.
  • Vary stake sizes and markets; occasional multiples/Each Way mirrors typical recreational behaviour.
  • Do not consistently snipe palpable errors or late steam; follow bookmaker T&Cs.

Record‑keeping:

  • Maintain a ledger of date, event, market, odds, stakes, commission, expected and actual P&L.
  • Reconcile after settlement; investigate any mismatch (dead‑heat, R4 deduction, partial matches).

Legal, tax, and responsible gambling in the UK: what should you know?

In Great Britain, bet only with operators licensed by the Gambling Commission. As a consumer, check operator licence status and follow safer gambling practices. For taxation, gambling winnings for individuals are not subject to UK Income Tax; duties are levied on operators, not players. If you provide services (e.g., sell tips, run communities), that income is taxable as business income. Laws and guidance can change; always consult official sources.

Licensing and consumer advice:

  • Verify operator licence and read consumer guidance at the Gambling Commission: [gamblingcommission.gov.uk](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk).

Tax position for players:

  • HMRC guidance indicates betting/gambling winnings are not taxable as trading profits for punters: see HMRC BIM22017 at [gov.uk/hmrc-internal-manuals/business-income-manual/bim22017](https://www.gov.uk/hmrc-internal-manuals/business-income-manual/bim22017).

Responsible gambling support:

  • National Gambling Helpline (24/7): [0808 8020 133](tel:08088020133) (operated by GamCare). Live chat: [gamcare.org.uk/get-support/talk-to-us/](https://www.gamcare.org.uk/get-support/talk-to-us/).
  • Information and tools (limits, time‑outs, self‑exclusion) via operators and charities such as [begambleaware.org](https://www.begambleaware.org).

Practical safeguards:

  • Use deposit limits, reality checks, and self‑exclusion tools provided by licensed operators.
  • Never treat matched betting as guaranteed income; only stake money you can afford to lose.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is an acceptable qualifying loss in UK matched betting and how do I measure it?

Target a loss per qualifier expressed as a percentage of back stake; 1–3% is typical depending on spread and commission. You measure it as absolute loss divided by the bookmaker stake, using the calculator’s two outcome profits which should match for a balanced qualifier. For example, with £10 at 3.00 backed and 3.05 laid at 2% commission, the balanced lay stake is £9.90 and the projected loss is about £0.30, i.e., 3.0% of stake. If you can reduce the spread to 3.00/3.02 at 2% commission, the loss falls to £0.20 on £10, i.e., 2.0%.

How much of an SNR free bet can I retain, and what odds maximise retention?

With equal back/lay odds and no commission, the retention fraction is (b−1)/b; higher back odds increase retention. Commission trims this slightly. At b = 4.0 with equal lay odds and 2% commission on the exchange, a £20 SNR free bet yields a balanced lay of about £15.08 and profit near £14.77, i.e., roughly 73.9% retention. At b = 5.0 on the same terms, retention rises toward about 80% without commission and approximately 78% with a 2% exchange fee.

How large should my exchange balance be to cover liability safely?

You must at least cover your maximum calculated liability; many keep a margin above that to absorb price moves or partial matches. Liability is (Lay odds − 1) × Lay stake and is reserved as soon as the lay is matched. Example: SNR free bet £25 at b = 5.0, laid at l = 5.10 with 2% commission gives L ≈ 19.69 and liability ≈ 4.10 × 19.69 = £80.71. Holding £95–£100 instead of exactly £80.71 materially reduces the chance of getting stuck if the market drifts before you can adjust.

How do I handle partial matches at multiple lay prices without breaking my hedge?

Aggregate matched lay stakes and compute a weighted average lay price, then recompute P&L using L = sum of lay stakes and l_eff = sum(Li × oi) ÷ sum(Li). This preserves accuracy across fills. If you needed L ≈ 10 and were matched £6 at 3.04 and £4 at 3.08, then L = 10 and l_eff = (6×3.04 + 4×3.08)/10 = 3.056. For a £10 qualifier at 3.00 back with 2% commission, your back‑win profit becomes about −£0.56 and back‑lose about −£0.20, revealing a small overlay from price slippage.

How do I adjust the calculator for Rule 4 deductions on horse racing?

Reduce the bookmaker’s back odds before calculating by applying the Rule 4 percentage to the win component. Use b_adj = 1 + (b − 1) × (1 − R4), where R4 is the decimal deduction (e.g., 0.20 for 20p in the pound). Example: If b = 5.00 and a 20p Rule 4 applies, b_adj = 1 + 4 × 0.80 = 4.20. Enter 4.20 as your back odds, then compute lay stake and liability as usual.

What happens if the bookmaker voids but the exchange lay still stands?

You are left with an unhedged exchange position; your P&L equals the exchange result alone. If the selection wins, you lose the full lay liability; if it loses, you win the lay stake net of commission. Using a common qualifier setup with L ≈ £9.90 at l = 3.05 and 2% commission, a book‑only void means a win at the exchange costs about £20.30 liability, while a loss at the exchange pays about £9.70 after commission. This asymmetry is why verifying both sides’ settlement rules is critical.

Are Betfair Premium Charges or other extra fees included, and how should I model them?

Standard calculators apply a single commission rate to exchange winnings; they do not model weekly Premium Charges. If you are subject to a higher effective take, approximate by increasing the commission input to your average rate. For instance, if your effective exchange take averages 20%, set commission to 20%. A £20 SNR at b = 5.0, l = 5.10 with c = 0.20 yields L ≈ 16.33 and profit ≈ £13.06 per outcome, i.e., about 65.3% retention instead of roughly 77% at 2%.

How should I round lay odds and stakes to match exchange increments without distorting P&L?

Round odds to the nearest tick shown on the exchange and stakes to the minimum currency increment allowed, then read the liability on the slip and reconcile it to the calculator’s output. Small rounding changes should be verified against displayed liability before confirming. If your balanced lay is £19.69 at 5.10 but you must round to £19.70, liability changes by about 4.10 × £0.01 = £0.041. The difference is small per bet, but consistent reconciliation prevents cumulative drift in your ledger.

How do I convert fractional odds to decimal for calculator inputs quickly?

Add 1 to the fractional ratio expressed as a decimal: Decimal = 1 + (Numerator ÷ Denominator). This aligns your bookmaker price with exchange‑style inputs. For example, 11/4 becomes 1 + 11/4 = 3.75, and 6/5 becomes 1 + 6/5 = 2.20. Enter these decimal odds along with your stake, lay odds, and commission.

Underlay vs overlay: how can I size the nudge quantitatively?

Each £1 reduction in lay stake improves back‑win profit by approximately (l − 1) and reduces back‑lose profit by approximately (1 − c). The reverse holds for an overlay of the same size. At l = 3.05 with 2% commission, reducing L by £0.40 lifts back‑win profit by about 2.05 × 0.40 = £0.82 and lowers back‑lose profit by about 0.98 × 0.40 = £0.39. From a balanced qualifier at roughly −£0.30 both ways, that underlay yields about +£0.52 if the back wins and about −£0.69 if it loses.

Can I use the calculator for exchange‑to‑exchange hedging or back‑back dutching?

Yes. Treat the first exchange back as the bookmaker side and adjust its odds for back‑side commission using b_eff = 1 + (b − 1) × (1 − c_back), then use the usual formulas with the second exchange as the lay side. Example: Back on Exchange A at b = 4.10 with 2% back commission gives b_eff ≈ 4.038. Laying on Exchange B at l = 4.00 with 2% lay commission for a £50 qualifier yields L ≈ 50 × 4.038 ÷ (4.00 − 0.02) = £50.73, producing about a £0.29 balanced loss, i.e., roughly 0.6% of stake.

How do I estimate the expected value (EV) of a free bet before placing it?

For a balanced SNR, the two outcome profits are nearly equal, so EV is approximately that per‑outcome profit. Higher back odds generally increase EV, but exchange commission reduces it. With S = £20, b = 5.00, l = 5.10, and c = 2%, L ≈ 15.75 and the per‑outcome profit is about £15.44, implying a retention of roughly 77.2% of the free bet’s face value. Changing to b = 4.00 on similar terms drops retention toward about 74%.

Do UK exchanges charge commission when the lay bet loses?

No. UK exchanges charge commission on net winnings in a market; if your lay loses, there is no commission on that market. If your lay wins, commission is applied to the lay stake won according to your account rate. For instance, at 2% commission with L = £10, a lay‑win pays about £9.80, while a lay‑loss pays nothing and you forfeit the calculated liability. Always verify your personal rate on the exchange’s help pages.

Smarkets 2% vs Betfair 5%: which produces a smaller qualifying loss at similar prices?

Often the lower commission wins even if the lay odds are slightly worse, but you should compute both options. Compare balanced losses side by side using your actual rates. On a £10 qualifier at b = 3.00, Smarkets at l = 3.05 and 2% yields L ≈ £9.90 and a loss ≈ £0.30. Betfair at l = 3.00 and 5% gives L ≈ £10.17 and a loss ≈ £0.34. Despite the better lay price, the higher commission made Betfair about £0.04 worse on this stake.

How do I model dead‑heats on Each Way bets across book and exchange legs?

Apply a dead‑heat factor k/n to the stake or return for the affected leg, then recompute P&L on both the bookmaker and the exchange place or win markets. The unaffected leg settles normally. If a £10 EW (i.e., £5 place stake) at b = 10.00 with 1/5 terms places in a two‑way dead‑heat for the final place, the place leg return is halved: standard place return would be £5 × 2.80 = £14; with a two‑way dead‑heat it becomes £7. On the exchange place lay, both potential profit and liability for that leg are similarly halved, so recalculate using 50% of the original matched lay for that settlement.

dutch.metaDescription: Free Dutching Calculator for UK bettors. Split stakes for equal returns across multiple outcomes; supports fractional and decimal odds, commission and rounding. 2026
dutch.intro: Use our free Dutching Calculator to split your total stake across multiple selections for an equal-profit outcome. Enter fractional, decimal or American odds, include exchange commission, and get a precise staking plan with rounding options. Optimised for UK markets and football, horse racing, and more. Bet responsibly and always confirm prices before placing bets.
dutch.howItWorks: A Dutching Calculator allocates your total stake across two or more mutually exclusive outcomes so that your total return (and therefore profit) is the same whichever selection wins. It does this by weighting each stake inversely to the outcome’s effective payout multiplier (decimal odds adjusted for any exchange commission). This lets you plan exact stakes ahead of placing bets with UK bookmakers or betting exchanges while accounting for rounding and commissions.
dutch.articleHtml:
How does a Dutching Calculator work?

A Dutching Calculator allocates your total stake across two or more mutually exclusive outcomes so that your total return (and therefore profit) is the same whichever selection wins. It does this by weighting each stake inversely to the outcome’s effective payout multiplier (decimal odds adjusted for any exchange commission). This lets you plan exact stakes ahead of placing bets with UK bookmakers or betting exchanges while accounting for rounding and commissions.

What inputs do you need (odds, stake, commission)?

To produce a precise staking plan, you should provide the following information for each bet you intend to place. These inputs ensure the calculator can equalise returns and highlight any expected loss (overround) or guaranteed profit (arb) before you commit funds.

  • Total stake (S): the total amount you want to distribute across all selections (e.g., £100).
  • Selections and odds: the decimal, fractional, or American odds for each mutually exclusive outcome (e.g., Home, Draw, Away in football).
  • Commission per selection (optional): the exchange commission rate (as a percentage) charged on net winnings for that selection; bookmakers typically have 0% commission on winnings.
  • Odds format: choose fractional or decimal (most common in the UK); American is often supported for convenience.
  • Rounding mode: how you want stakes rounded (e.g., to the nearest £0.01, £0.05, or £0.10) to match operator stake limits.
  • Constraints (optional): any minimum/maximum stakes or step sizes imposed by specific firms.
InputPurposeTypical UK Example
Total stake (S)Sets the total capital to allocate across selections£100.00
Odds per selectionDetermines each selection’s payout multiplierHome 2.50, Draw 3.40, Away 3.10 (decimal)
Commission (per selection)Adjusts payout on exchanges where a % is taken from net winnings0% at bookmaker; 2% at exchange (example)
Odds formatCalculator accepts fractional/decimal/American and converts internallyFractional 6/4 ↔ Decimal 2.50
RoundingEnsures stakes meet operator increments and sum to SRound to nearest £0.01

How are stakes split to target equal profit?

The calculator equalises total return K (stake plus profit) across all outcomes. Without commission, stakes are proportional to 1/Oi (where Oi is decimal odds). With exchange commission ci (expressed as a decimal, e.g., 0.02 for 2%), stakes are proportional to 1/Ei, where the effective payout multiplier is Ei = Oi − ci(Oi − 1). This accounts for commission on profit only (stake is not commissioned).

Compute the effective multiplier for each selection:

  • No commission: Ei = Oi
  • With commission: Ei = Oi − ci(Oi − 1)
  • Sum the reciprocals: H = Σ (1 / Ei)
  • Equalised return (constant across outcomes): K = S / H
  • Stake for selection i: si = K / Ei
  • Resulting profit (same whichever wins): P = K − S

Worked example (three football outcomes, S = £100):

SelectionDecimal odds OiCommission ciEffective multiplier EiStake si (£)Return if wins (≈K)Profit P = K − S
Home2.500%2.5039.17£97.93≈ −£2.07 (overround example)
Draw3.400%3.4028.80£97.93
Away (exchange)3.102%3.05832.03£97.93

Notes:

  • Here H = 1/2.50 + 1/3.40 + 1/3.058 ≈ 1.0211, so K ≈ £97.93 and P ≈ −£2.07 (a small expected loss due to market overround). Positive P indicates an arbitrage.
  • Without commission (all at bookmakers), the same method reduces to si ∝ 1/Oi.
  • Rounding to currency increments can slightly alter K; the calculator rebalances so stakes sum to S.

Which odds formats are supported in the UK?

UK-facing calculators typically support fractional and decimal odds by default and often accept American odds. All three are convertible, and the tool internally standardises to decimal for calculations.

FormatExampleMeaningConvert to DecimalImplied Probability
Fractional (a/b)5/2Win £a for every £b staked (stake returned separately)O = 1 + a/b → 1 + 5/2 = 3.50b / (a + b) → 2 / (5 + 2) ≈ 0.2857 (28.57%)
Decimal (O)3.50Total return per £1 staked (includes stake)— (already decimal)1 / O → 1 / 3.50 ≈ 0.2857 (28.57%)
American (+m)+250Profit £m on a £100 stakeO = 1 + m/100 → 1 + 250/100 = 3.50100 / (m + 100) → 100 / 350 ≈ 0.2857 (28.57%)
American (−m)−150Stake £m to profit £100O = 1 + 100/m → 1 + 100/150 ≈ 1.6667m / (m + 100) → 150 / 250 = 0.60 (60%)

In the UK, fractional and decimal formats are most common with bookmakers and exchanges respectively. For Dutching, the calculator converts any input to decimal, applies commission where relevant, and then computes equalised stakes and returns.

Which markets can you Dutch effectively?

Dutching works best where the available selections are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive, meaning exactly one selection will win and all possible results are fully covered. In the UK, that usually includes football 1X2 (win–draw–win), tennis match winner, and horse racing win-only markets. Markets where multiple selections can win simultaneously (for example, place markets) or where your chosen set does not cover every possible outcome make strict equal-return Dutching impossible or fragile. Always check market rules, settlement, and any commission before placing bets.

Market (UK)Mutually Exclusive?Typical OutcomesCan You Dutch Directly?Notes
Football 1X2 (win–draw–win)YesHome, Draw, AwayYesCover all three outcomes; include commission if using an exchange.
Football Correct ScoreYes, if exhaustiveDiscrete scores plus “Any Other…” (varies by market)ConditionalOnly safe if every possible score is covered (check “Any Other…” selections).
BTTS (Both Teams to Score)YesYes, NoYesTwo-outcome market; straightforward Dutching.
Over/Under (single line)YesOver X.5, Under X.5YesDo not mix multiple lines (e.g., Over 2.5 and Over 3.5 are not exclusive).
Horse Racing Win-onlyYesOne winnerYesClassic Dutching use case across multiple runners.
Horse Racing Each-Way (combined win+place)NoWin also triggers place payoutNo (not strictly)Overlapping returns break equalisation; treat win and place parts separately.
Horse Racing Place-onlyNo (multiple place winners)Top k placesNo (not strictly)More than one pick can place; cannot guarantee equal returns.
Accumulator (multiples across events)Yes, if every combo is exclusiveProduct of leg outcomesConditionalMust cover every exclusive combo; combinations grow exponentially.
Draw No BetYesHome, Away (draw void)YesTwo-outcome market; settlement rules for voids apply.
Tennis Match WinnerYesPlayer A, Player BYesCheck retirement rules; some bookmakers settle differently.

Before Dutching any market, confirm that your selection set truly partitions all outcomes, that settlement rules (e.g., voids, retirements, dead-heats) are understood, and that stake limits and maximum payouts will not distort your plan.

Can you Dutch football win–draw–win or correct score?

Yes. Football 1X2 (win–draw–win) is a textbook Dutching market because exactly one of the three outcomes will settle as a winner. You simply input the three prices (adjusting for any exchange commission), and the calculator weights stakes so the total return is equalised. Correct score can also be Dutched, but only if your selection set is exhaustive: you must include every listed discrete score plus any “Any Other…” selections that capture unlisted scorelines (market conventions vary by bookmaker or exchange). Missing even a single reachable scoreline invalidates the hedge.

  • 1X2 checklist: cover Home, Draw, Away; confirm settlement (90 minutes only unless otherwise stated); apply commission if using an exchange.
  • Correct score checklist: include all listed discrete scores and any “Any Other Home Win/Draw/Away” selections required by that market to cover high-score outcomes.
  • Risk controls: place all legs quickly to reduce slippage; watch for stake limits and price changes that can shift the equalised return.

Accumulator dutching: is it possible and how to approach?

It is possible in principle, but rarely practical. To Dutch accumulators you must cover every mutually exclusive combination of your chosen legs, then allocate stakes across those accumulator lines so the return is equal. For two 1X2 matches, that’s 3 × 3 = 9 exclusive acca lines; for three matches it becomes 27, and so on—an exponential growth that multiplies overround, liquidity risk, and operational complexity.

  • Enumerate exclusive scenarios: list every combination of leg outcomes (e.g., H1/D1/A1 × H2/D2/A2).
  • Price each combo: multiply decimal odds per leg (adjust for commission where applicable) to get each accumulator price.
  • Allocate stakes: apply the standard Dutching formula across those acca prices.
  • Execution: place all accas without delay; check max-payout caps and correlated-market restrictions (many firms disallow certain same-game combos).
  • Practical alternative: Dutch singles within each event (e.g., 1X2) rather than trying to Dutch across multiple events via accas.
  • Permutation bets (Trixie/Patent/Yankee) distribute risk but are not strict Dutching and will not produce equal returns across all scenarios.

Does Dutching work for each-way horse racing?

Strict Dutching (equal return regardless of which selection wins) does not work for each-way as a single combined bet. An each-way bet splits your stake into a win part and a place part; if your horse wins, both parts pay, creating overlapping returns that break the “one winner only” requirement. Place markets also allow multiple runners to succeed simultaneously, so backing several runners to place cannot be Dutched to an exact equal return.

  • Win-only Dutching: valid. Choose multiple runners in the win market and equalise returns normally.
  • Approximating each-way coverage: treat the win and place components separately—Dutch the win market; then size place bets for coverage, accepting that returns will vary if more than one selection places.
  • Place terms: bookmakers commonly quote place fractions such as 1/5 or 1/4 of the win odds and pay a set number of places depending on race type and field size (terms vary by race and firm).
  • Converting to place odds (for planning): decimal place odds = 1 + (Owin − 1) × f, where f is the place fraction (e.g., 0.20 for 1/5).
  • Dead-heats and reductions: settlement rules can alter returns; always check race terms, potential Rule 4 deductions, and maximum-payout limits.

If your objective is a guaranteed equal return, stick to win-only Dutching on horse races. If you use each-way for coverage, model scenarios and aim for an acceptable minimum return rather than an exact equalised figure.

Free, app, and Excel: which Dutching Calculator is best?

A Dutching Calculator can be used in three common ways: a free web tool in your browser, a dedicated mobile or desktop app, or a custom spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets). The “best” option depends on how often you calculate stakes, whether you need offline access, and how much control you want over rounding, commissions, and scenario modelling. Below is a practical overview to help you choose.

Is there a free, no-sign-up Dutching tool?

Yes. Many UK-facing web calculators allow you to compute Dutching stakes for free without creating an account. These typically work in any modern browser and support decimal and fractional odds, optional exchange commission, and stake rounding to currency increments. Before using any free tool, verify the following:

  • Security: the site uses HTTPS and loads in a modern browser without warnings.
  • Odds formats: accepts decimal and fractional (American often optional), and converts consistently.
  • Commission: allows per-selection commission (for exchange bets) and applies it only to winnings.
  • Rounding: supports rounding stakes to £0.01, £0.05, or £0.10 and still sums to your total stake.
  • Transparency: shows the implied overround/arb and expected profit or loss after commission.
  • Usability: no forced sign-up, limits, or paywalls for basic calculations.

App vs web vs Excel: which should you choose?

Choose based on frequency, environment (online/offline), and custom modelling needs. The comparison below summarises typical trade-offs UK bettors face when planning Dutching stakes.

OptionTypical CostWorks OfflineUpdatesCustomisationCommission & RoundingPrivacy & ControlPlatform SupportBest For
Web calculatorOften freeNo (requires internet)Automatic by providerLimited to provided fieldsCommonly supportedData stays in browser sessionAny modern browserQuick, ad‑hoc calculations
Mobile/desktop appFree or low one‑time feeOften yes after installVia app store or installerModerate (presets, profiles)Usually supportedLocal storage; user-managediOS/Android/Windows/macOSFrequent field use, low latency
Excel (custom sheet)Excel licence or 365 planYesUser maintains formulasHigh (full control)Fully customisableLocal files; versionableWindows/macOS (Excel)Advanced modelling & logs

Rule of thumb: use a web tool for speed and convenience; an app if you need reliable offline access or device-native features; Excel if you want auditability, batch planning, and fully transparent formulas you can adapt.

How to use or build a Dutching Excel template?

The core of a Dutching sheet is straightforward: compute an effective payout multiplier for each selection (odds adjusted for commission), sum their reciprocals, solve for the constant equalised return, and allocate stakes accordingly. The steps below outline a transparent, auditable template using decimal odds and per-selection commission.

Set up headers in row 1:

  • A1: Selection
  • B1: Decimal Odds (O)
  • C1: Commission (c)
  • D1: Effective Multiplier (E)
  • E1: 1/E
  • F1: Stake (£)
  • G1: Return if Wins (£)

Inputs:

  • Enter each selection’s decimal odds in column B (e.g., rows 2–6).
  • Enter per-selection commission c in column C as a decimal (e.g., 0.02 for 2%; use 0 for bookmakers).
  • Set total stake S in a dedicated cell, e.g., H2 (label H1 as “Total Stake”).
  • Optional: Set rounding increment in H3 (e.g., 0.01 for penny rounding) and label H3 as “Rounding Inc.”

Formulas (copy down as needed):

  • D2 (Effective multiplier): =B2 - C2*(B2-1)
  • E2 (Reciprocal): =1/D2

Harmonic sum and equalised return:

  • H4 (label “H = Σ(1/E)”): =SUM(E2:E6) [adjust E6 to your last row]
  • H5 (label “K = Equalised Return”): =H2/H4
  • H6 (label “Profit = K − S”): =H5 - H2

Stake allocation:

  • F2 (stake for row 2, with rounding): =ROUND($H$5/D2, ROUND(LOG10(1/$H$3),0)) if H3 holds a power-of-10 increment (e.g., 0.01). Simpler: =MROUND($H$5/D2, $H$3) in Excel supporting MROUND.
  • Copy F2 down to all but the last active row.

For the last active row only (say row 6), set the stake as the remainder to ensure stakes sum exactly to S:

  • F6: =$H$2 - SUM(F2:F5)
  • G2 (return if wins): =F2*D2 (copy down). This should be approximately equal to K in H5 for all rows; minor differences arise from rounding.

Quality checks:

  • Total staked: =SUM(F2:F6) should equal S (H2).
  • Variance check: MAX(G2:G6) − MIN(G2:G6) should be small; reduce rounding increment if variance is too high.
  • Overround/arb: Profit (H6) negative implies an expected loss due to overround; positive implies an arbitrage across your prices.

Optional features:

  • Fractional-to-decimal helper: If fractional odds a/b are entered in helper cells, decimal = 1 + a/b.
  • Dynamic range: Convert the grid to an Excel Table so Σ and copying automatically adjust to added rows.
  • Per-operator constraints: Add MIN/MAX checks and flag when a calculated stake breaches a limit.

With this template, you retain full visibility over every calculation: effective multipliers E = O − c×(O−1), the harmonic sum of reciprocals, the equalised return K, and any deviation introduced by rounding or stake limits. Save separate versions for football (3 outcomes) and horse racing (variable runners) to streamline routine use.

Step-by-step: How to use our Dutching Calculator safely

Use Dutching only on markets where outcomes are mutually exclusive and fully cover the result set. Enter prices carefully, account for any exchange commission on winnings, and apply sensible rounding so the final stakes meet operator increments while still summing to your total stake. Always verify the expected equalised return before placing bets, and follow UK responsible gambling guidance from sources such as the [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk) and [BeGambleAware](https://www.begambleaware.org).

How do you enter prices, set target and review stakes?

The calculator equalises the total return across all selections. You can drive the calculation either by setting a fixed total stake or, where supported, targeting a desired equalised return/profit. Follow these steps:

Choose odds format:

  • Use decimal for accuracy (or enter fractional and convert internally).
  • If any selection is on an exchange, add the commission rate for that selection (as a percent of net winnings).

Set target:

  • Total stake S: typical default. The tool solves for the equalised return K and individual stakes si.
  • Target return or profit (if offered): enter K or P (profit), and the tool computes S and si accordingly.

Enter market prices:

  • Add each mutually exclusive selection (e.g., Home/Draw/Away).
  • For exchange legs, enter the commission (e.g., 2%). For bookmaker legs, use 0%.

Set rounding:

  • Choose an increment (e.g., £0.01, £0.05, £0.10) that your bookies/exchanges accept.
  • The calculator rounds stakes and adjusts the final leg to ensure stakes sum to S.

Review the plan:

  • Check the “Returns if wins” column—values should be equal (or very close after rounding).
  • Confirm the displayed expected profit/loss after commission (detecting overround or arbitrage).
SelectionDecimal odds OCommission cEffective multiplier E = O − c×(O−1)Stake exact (£)Stake rounded (£0.01)Return if wins (rounded)
Home2.500%2.5039.1739.17£97.93
Draw3.400%3.4028.8028.80£97.92
Away (exchange)3.102%3.05832.0532.05£97.93
Example with S = £100 total stake. Equalised return K ≈ £97.93 (small expected loss due to market overround and commission). Minor differences arise from rounding to the nearest penny.

Tip: If penny rounding produces too much return variance, switch to £0.01 for most legs and let the final leg absorb the remainder so that total stakes still equal S.

What is the workflow for placing multiple bets?

Execution risk (price changes, stake limits, partial fills) is the main threat to an equalised plan. Use a disciplined, time-efficient workflow:

Pre-check:

  • Confirm market rules (e.g., 90 minutes for football 1X2; settlement time; dead-heat rules).
  • Note any max payout or per-bet limit that could cap your calculated stake.

Quote and lock inputs:

  • Refresh prices on all operators to reduce stale quotes.
  • Enter odds and commission, select rounding, and generate stakes.

Place orders efficiently:

  • Prepare betslips on each operator first, then submit in quick succession.
  • If a price moves before confirmation, pause and recalculate stakes with the new price.

Verification:

  • Check accepted stakes and final prices in bet history.
  • Ensure the sum of accepted stakes matches S; if not, rebalance immediately.

Record-keeping:

  • Log each selection, operator, odds, commission, stake, and expected return.
  • Store confirmations to audit performance over time.

How should you manage bankroll, limits and rounding?

Responsible bankroll management protects you from variance and operational surprises like limits or partial fills. Align stake size with bankroll, keep returns realistic, and ensure rounding preserves your hedge.

Bankroll sizing:

  • Use a fixed staking policy (e.g., limit any one Dutching plan to a small fraction of bankroll, such as 0.5%–2%).
  • Avoid chaining multiple large plans simultaneously; cumulative exposure can exceed comfort quickly.

Operator limits and payout caps:

  • Check per-bet min/max and overall payout caps before committing.
  • If a calculated stake exceeds a limit, reduce S or split across additional operators and recalc.

Rounding control:

  • Choose £0.01 rounding for precision; coarser increments (e.g., £0.05/£0.10) increase return variance.
  • Assign the final leg as “remainder” to force the sum of stakes to equal S exactly.

Volatility and slippage:

  • High-volatility markets require faster execution; consider using an exchange for better fill control.
  • If a leg is partially filled, either top up at the new price and recalc, or rebalance other legs.

Safeguards and support:

  • Use deposit limits, time-outs and reality checks offered by licensed UK operators.
  • For advice and support, visit [BeGambleAware](https://www.begambleaware.org) or review guidance from the [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk).

The other side of the coin: When is Dutching not worth it?

Dutching can be mathematically precise but becomes poor value or operationally risky when the market overround is large, when stake limits prevent you from placing the calculated amounts, or when prices move while you are executing. In these cases, the equalised return falls below your total stake, or your hedge is broken and you are exposed to a specific outcome. Always weigh frictional costs (commission, rounding, withdrawal fees), execution risk (slippage, partial fills), and operator rules before committing funds.

What risks come from overrounds and stake restrictions?

Overround is the built-in margin in a market: if you sum implied probabilities from all outcomes (using decimal odds), values above 100% indicate a negative expectation for backers. Even if you split stakes optimally, a positive overround guarantees an expected loss after commission. Stake restrictions and payout caps can also prevent you from placing the stake size required to maintain equal returns, leaving you under-hedged on some outcomes.

Overround impact:

  • Overround (H) = Σ(1/Oi) using decimal odds Oi. H = 1 is fair, H > 1 implies an expected loss; commission further worsens H via lower effective multipliers.
  • Equalised return K = S/H, so with H > 1 and stake S = £100, you necessarily have K < £100 (a loss before any other costs).

Stake restrictions:

  • If a bookmaker caps your stake below the calculator’s requirement (e.g., you need £32.03 but are limited to £25.00), the affected outcome’s return falls below K, creating a hole in the hedge.
  • Max-payout limits can also bind when long prices are involved; always check per-bet and per-day caps before placing.

Commission and fees:

  • Exchanges typically charge a percentage on net winnings (commonly around 2%–5%), which reduces the effective payout multiplier E = O − c×(O−1).
  • Payment and withdrawal fees, if any, reduce net ROI and should be factored into your plan.

How do odds moves, slippage and partial fills hurt ROI?

Any change in an input price between calculation and placement alters the harmonic sum H and therefore the equalised return K. Slippage (worse price than quoted) and partial fills (especially on exchanges) can convert a near-breakeven plan into a clearly negative one, or break the hedge entirely on one outcome. Recalculate immediately after any change and before submitting remaining bets.

Scenario (S = £100)HomeDrawAway (commission)H = Σ(1/E)K = £100/HP = K − S
Base2.503.403.10 at 2% → E = 3.058≈ 1.02123≈ £97.92≈ −£2.08
Away shortens2.503.402.90 at 2% → E = 2.862≈ 1.04398≈ £95.79≈ −£4.21
Away drifts2.503.403.30 at 2% → E = 3.254≈ 1.00144≈ £99.86≈ −£0.14

Operational pitfalls:

  • Slippage: submitting after a move means your stakes no longer equalise returns; redo the calculation before confirming.
  • Partial fills: if an exchange fills only part of a stake at the target price, either top up at the live price and recalc, or immediately rebalance other legs.
  • Latency: prepare all betslips across operators first, then submit in quick succession to reduce exposure to price changes.

What responsible gambling rules should you follow?

Use Dutching tools to plan, not to chase losses. In the UK, bet only with licensed operators, set hard limits in advance, and seek support if gambling ceases to be controllable or enjoyable. The following resources provide authoritative guidance and tools for safer gambling and self-exclusion.

Know the rules:

  • Gamble only with operators licensed by the [UK Gambling Commission](https://www.gamblingcommission.gov.uk).
  • Understand settlement, dead-heat, voids, Rule 4 deductions (horse racing), and maximum payout policies.

Set controls:

  • Use deposit, loss and session limits, time-outs, and reality checks available at UK-licensed sites.
  • If needed, self-exclude via [GAMSTOP](https://www.gamstop.co.uk) (free UK online self-exclusion service).

Get support:

  • National Gambling Helpline (free, 24/7): [0808 8020 133](tel:08088020133) (operated by GamCare).
  • Advice and resources: [BeGambleAware](https://www.begambleaware.org) and [GamCare](https://www.gamcare.org.uk).

Personal guidelines:

  • Never bet when impaired or under time pressure; avoid chasing losses.
  • Limit any single plan to a small fraction of bankroll; keep records and review outcomes objectively.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I check if my Dutching prices create an arbitrage?

You have an arbitrage if the harmonic sum H = Σ(1/Ei) is below 1, where Ei are effective decimal odds after commission. Example: two-outcome market with O1 = 2.10 and O2 = 1.95 at 0% commission gives H = 1/2.10 + 1/1.95 = 0.4762 + 0.5128 = 0.9890; staking £100 yields K = £100/H ≈ £101.11 and a risk-free profit P ≈ £1.11.

Can I mix bookmaker and exchange bets in one Dutch?

Yes, treat each selection’s effective odds separately by applying its own commission and then equalise returns. For instance, Obook = 3.40 (0% c) stays E = 3.40, while Oexch = 3.10 at 2% becomes E = 3.10 − 0.02×(3.10−1) = 3.058; this change alone raises H by about 1/3.058 − 1/3.10 ≈ 0.0015, trimming a £100 equalised return by roughly £0.15.

How do I include a free bet (stake not returned) in a Dutching plan?

Model a free bet with effective odds E = O − 1 because the stake is not returned. Example: a £25 free bet at O = 5.0 has E = 4.0, so its return contribution is 25×4 = £100; cash legs use E = O (or O − c×(O−1) on exchanges), and you then split stakes so each outcome returns the same K.

What happens to a Dutch if one leg is voided or a match is postponed?

If a leg is voided, its stake is returned and your equalisation breaks; you must remove that selection, recompute H with the remaining Ei and rebalance stakes. Example: with two legs staked £60 at 2.10 and £40 at 2.90, voiding the £40 leg leaves an unhedged £60 exposure; without recalculation your win and loss scenarios diverge by the entire £40.

How do dead-heats and Rule 4 deductions affect a dutched horse-racing plan?

A dead-heat for k horses divides your win stake by k, making the effective multiplier approximately Edeadheat = O/k, so a 2-way tie at O = 6.0 returns s×(6.0/2) = 3.0s instead of 6.0s. A Rule 4 deduction of, say, 20p in the pound scales the profit part by 0.80, giving adjusted E ≈ 1 + (O−1)×0.80; at O = 6.0 this becomes 1 + 5×0.80 = 5.0.

What rounding increment keeps equal returns within a tight tolerance?

Rounding each stake to step Δ changes its return by at most about (Emax×Δ)/2 for the selection with the highest effective odds Emax. To keep return variance under £0.50 when Emax = 6.0, choose Δ ≤ 0.50×2/6 ≈ £0.17; using £0.10 caps per-leg deviation near £0.30, and assigning the final leg as the remainder forces the stakes to sum exactly to S.

How many selections can I Dutch practically and what minimum stake do I need?

You need total stake S at least equal to n×min_stake to clear operator minimums across n legs; with a £0.10 minimum, S must be ≥ £1.00 for 10 legs and ≥ £5.00 for 50 legs. Execution time and slippage risk scale roughly with n, and each extra leg adds 1/Ei to H, which can turn a marginal edge into a loss if prices are not strong.

Is Dutching viable in in-play markets with fast price moves?

It is fragile unless you can submit all legs within a few seconds; even a one-tick move can erase your edge. Example: dropping from 3.00 to 2.96 increases that leg’s 1/E by about 0.3333 − 0.3378 = −0.0045, which for a three-way book typically cuts a £100 equalised return by ~£0.45; two similar moves can flip a 0.5% edge negative.

How do I Dutch to a target profit instead of a fixed stake?

When aiming for profit P, first compute H = Σ(1/Ei); then K = P/(1−H) and S = K×H = P×H/(1−H). Example: with H = 0.98 and target P = £5, the equalised return is K = £250 and the total stake is S = £245; this is only feasible if H

How do I get the combined price for “any of my selections to win”?

The combined break-even decimal price is 1/H where H = Σ(1/Ei); your equalised return ratio is K/S = 1/H. Example: if H = 0.989, the group price is 1/0.989 ≈ 1.011, implying a 1.1% edge; if H = 1.021, the group price is ≈ 0.979, signalling an expected 2.1% loss before other costs.

Is Dutching legal in the UK and are there taxes on winnings?

Using a calculator to plan stakes is legal; you must be 18+ and bet with UK-licensed operators. As of 2026, individuals do not pay income or capital gains tax on gambling winnings in the UK, while operators pay gambling duties.

What’s the practical difference between Dutching, hedging, and laying the field?

Back-Dutching splits stakes to equalise returns across outcomes, hedging adjusts positions to control downside, and laying the field risks a fixed liability against multiple outcomes. A back-Dutch on a set is algebraically equivalent to one lay on the complement with lay odds L = K/P and lay stake P, so liability (L−1)×P equals your total staked S.

How do exchange commission and partial fills change the equalised return?

Commission reduces effective odds by c×(O−1), so O = 4.0 at 2% becomes E = 3.94, cutting return by £0.06 per £1 stake. If half your intended stake fills at 3.90 instead, E ≈ 3.842 for that portion, adding about 0.5×(1/3.842−1/3.94) ≈ 0.00325 to H and trimming a £100 equalised return by roughly £0.32.

What quick checks confirm my Dutching plan is internally consistent?

Verify that sum of stakes equals S exactly, that each leg’s rounded return lies within about (Emax×Δ)/2 of K, and that P computed two ways matches: P = K − S and P = S×(1/H − 1). On a correct setup these numbers agree within rounding tolerance, typically a few pence for £0.01 steps.

Can I replace multiple back bets with one lay bet to simplify execution?

Yes, if a single exchange market cleanly represents the complement event, you can lay it at L = K/P with lay stake P and liability S for the same economics. Example: with H = 0.98 targeting P = £5, K = £250 and S = £245, so a single lay at 50.0 for £5 has £245 liability and pays £5 if the laid event does not occur.

How should I treat a selection priced in American odds when mixing with UK odds?

Convert first, then Dutch: for +m use O = 1 + m/100 and for −m use O = 1 + 100/m, then apply commission to get E = O − c×(O−1). Example: −150 converts to O = 1 + 100/150 = 1.6667; at 2% exchange commission the effective odds are E ≈ 1.6667 − 0.02×0.6667 = 1.6533.

What’s the minimum total stake to respect operator stake floors across many legs?

Your total must be at least n times the highest minimum among your operators. If several legs must be placed at firms with a £0.50 minimum while others allow £0.10, and you have 12 legs with 5 at £0.50, the hard lower bound is 5×£0.50 + 7×£0.10 = £3.50; anything below this cannot be executed as calculated.

kelly-criterion.metaTitle: Kelly Criterion Calculator Stake Optimiser Official 2026
kelly-criterion.metaDescription: Free Kelly Criterion Calculator for UK bettors and traders. Find optimal stake size, apply half-Kelly, set caps, view bankroll %, and export to Excel/CSV. Works for sports, poker, forex, and crypto.
kelly-criterion.intro: Compute optimal stake size for sports betting, poker, forex, and crypto using the Kelly Criterion. Supports UK fractional and decimal odds, half-Kelly, caps, and bankroll % outputs. Free, no sign-up — export to Excel or CSV.
kelly-criterion.howItWorks: The Kelly Criterion computes the optimal fraction of your bankroll to stake on a bet with known odds and your estimated win probability. For binary outcomes with net odds b (profit per 1 staked if you win), the full-Kelly fraction f* equals (b × p − (1 − p)) / b. The calculator accepts decimal or fractional odds, converts them to b, applies any commission on winnings (if relevant), and returns stake size as a percentage of bankroll and in cash. If f* is negative, the bet should be skipped because the expected growth is negative. - Choose odds format: decimal d or fractional a/b. The net odds are b = d − 1 (decimal) or b = a/b (fractional). - Estimate your win probability p (between 0 and 1). Optionally enter commission c on net winnings (exchanges), so effective decimal odds are d_eff = 1 + (d − 1) × (1 − c). - Enter your bankroll B (e.g., in GBP). Optionally set a cap (e.g., 5%) and a fraction-of-Kelly (e.g., 0.5 for half-Kelly) for risk control. - Calculate f* using b (or d_eff): full Kelly for decimal odds is f* = (d × p − 1) / (d − 1). The cash stake is Stake = B × f* × Kelly fraction (e.g., 0.5 for half-Kelly), subject to your cap. - Interpret outputs: a positive f* is your optimal bankroll percentage; a negative f* indicates no bet.
kelly-criterion.articleHtml:
How do you use the Kelly Criterion Calculator for betting?

The Kelly Criterion computes the optimal fraction of your bankroll to stake on a bet with known odds and your estimated win probability. For binary outcomes with net odds b (profit per 1 staked if you win), the full-Kelly fraction f* equals (b × p − (1 − p)) / b. The calculator accepts decimal or fractional odds, converts them to b, applies any commission on winnings (if relevant), and returns stake size as a percentage of bankroll and in cash. If f* is negative, the bet should be skipped because the expected growth is negative.

  • Choose odds format: decimal d or fractional a/b. The net odds are b = d − 1 (decimal) or b = a/b (fractional).
  • Estimate your win probability p (between 0 and 1). Optionally enter commission c on net winnings (exchanges), so effective decimal odds are d_eff = 1 + (d − 1) × (1 − c).
  • Enter your bankroll B (e.g., in GBP). Optionally set a cap (e.g., 5%) and a fraction-of-Kelly (e.g., 0.5 for half-Kelly) for risk control.
  • Calculate f* using b (or d_eff): full Kelly for decimal odds is f* = (d × p − 1) / (d − 1). The cash stake is Stake = B × f* × Kelly fraction (e.g., 0.5 for half-Kelly), subject to your cap.
  • Interpret outputs: a positive f* is your optimal bankroll percentage; a negative f* indicates no bet.

Enter odds and edge: what inputs do you need?

To size a stake, the calculator needs your odds, your assessed probability (or fair odds), bankroll, and any constraints. The “edge” can be entered directly or implied from your probability versus the book’s break-even probability. For decimal odds d (or effective d_eff if commission applies), the edge as expected return per 1 staked is ROI = d × p − 1. The break-even probability is p0 = 1 / d. The probability edge is p − p0. Both describe the same advantage from different angles.

FieldAllowed formatsSymbolHow it’s used
OddsDecimal (e.g., 2.50) or Fractional (e.g., 3/2)d or a/bConvert to net odds b. Decimal: b = d − 1. Fractional: b = a/b.
Commission (optional)Percentage on net winnings (e.g., 2%)cEffective decimal odds: d_eff = 1 + (d − 1) × (1 − c).
Your win probabilityValue from 0 to 1 (e.g., 0.44)pCore input for Kelly; q = 1 − p.
Edge (optional alternative)ROI or probability edgeROI or p − p0ROI = d × p − 1; p0 = 1 / d. Entering either lets the calculator back out p.
BankrollCurrency amount (e.g., £2,000)BStake = B × f*, after any Kelly fraction (half, quarter) and caps.
Kelly fraction1.0 (full), 0.5 (half), 0.25 (quarter)kStake multiplier for risk control: Stake = B × f* × k.
Stake cap (optional)Max % of bankroll (e.g., 5%)f_maxStake% = min(f* × k, f_max).
Worked exampleValueNotes
Bankroll B£2,000Stake is a fraction of this
Odds (decimal)d = 2.50Net odds b = 1.50

Decimal vs fractional odds: does the formula change?

No. Kelly uses net odds b and your probability p, so the core formula is unchanged. You only convert your input odds to b. For decimal odds, b = d − 1. For fractional odds a/b, b = a/b. Break-even probability is p0 = 1 / d = 1 / (1 + b). The full-Kelly fraction can be written as f* = (b × p − (1 − p)) / b, which is algebraically identical to f* = (d × p − 1) / (d − 1).

Kelly variations and risk control

Kelly staking maximises the long-run growth rate of your bankroll under specific assumptions (accurate probabilities, independent bets, binary outcomes). In practice, bettors temper Kelly with fractional staking, caps, and minimums to manage drawdowns and model error. This section explains half-Kelly and other practical controls so you can trade off growth versus volatility with clear, data-backed choices.

What is half-Kelly and when should you use it?

Half-Kelly is fractional Kelly where you stake k × f* with k = 0.5, and f* is the full-Kelly fraction. It keeps the Kelly logic but halves the aggressiveness. This reduces drawdown depth and the penalty for overestimating your edge, while typically retaining a large share of Kelly’s growth rate (rule‑of‑thumb: around 70–80% for small edges and moderate odds).

  • Definition: fractional Kelly uses k ∈ (0,1]; stake fraction = k × f*, where f* = (b × p − (1 − p)) / b and b is net odds (decimal odds d minus 1).
  • Why it helps: model error is common. If your true edge is smaller than estimated, full-Kelly can be too aggressive. Halving the stake reduces downside from misestimation and correlations between bets.

Typical use cases for half-Kelly:

  • Uncertain edge estimates (small samples, new models, changing markets).
  • Positively correlated selections (same league, same model features).
  • Capital constraints and psychological drawdown tolerance.
  • Operational frictions (limits, inability to rebalance continuously).
  • Compute full-Kelly f* from your odds and probability.
  • Choose k (common: 0.25 to 0.75). Many start at 0.5 and adjust as evidence accumulates.
  • Apply caps (e.g., 1–5% of bankroll) to bound exposure on long shots or concentrated slates.

Bankroll management: caps, minimums, and staking plans

Even with fractional Kelly, you should bound risk with explicit limits and a coherent staking plan. Caps avoid oversized stakes on volatile edges; minimums reduce overtrading on tiny edges where slippage and error can dominate. Plans should specify how stakes adapt as bankroll and model confidence change.

Caps (maximum stake as % of bankroll)

  • Formula: f_final = min(k × f*, f_max). Common f_max ranges: 1–2% (conservative), 3–5% (moderate).
  • Rationale: protects against tail risk on high-variance or correlated bets.

Minimums (floor to avoid noise trading)

  • Formula: if k × f* < f_min then stake 0 (or a recording unit only).
  • Typical f_min: 0.25–0.50% when execution costs or estimate error are meaningful.

Staking plans

  • Flat stake: fixed cash per bet. Simple, low variance; ignores edge size.
  • Proportional (percentage-of-bankroll): stake a fixed % each bet. Tracks bankroll, but not edge size.
  • Fractional Kelly (recommended): scales with both edge and bankroll; add caps/minimums.
  • Unit-based: define 1 unit (e.g., 1% bankroll) and map edges to units (e.g., 0.5–5 units).
  • Avoid Martingale: increases exposure after losses and can escalate risk without improving EV.

Recalculation cadence

  • Update stakes as bankroll changes (daily/weekly) to stay proportional.
  • Re‑estimate model uncertainty regularly; lower k when variance or correlation rises.

Volatility vs growth: sensitivity to edge and odds

Kelly optimises expected log growth, not short‑term returns. Reducing k lowers variance faster than it lowers growth. The table below shows this trade‑off on a concrete bet (decimal odds d = 2.50, net odds b = 1.50, win probability p = 0.44; full-Kelly f* = 6.67%).

PlanApplied fraction fMean simple return per betExpected log growth per betGeometric growth per betStd dev of simple return per bet
Full-Kelly0.06670.667%0.003289 nats0.329%8.273%
Half-Kelly0.03330.333%0.002484 nats0.249%4.137%
Quarter-Kelly0.01670.167%0.001453 nats0.145%2.068%

Half-Kelly retains a substantial share of growth while cutting volatility roughly in half. Sensitivity to inputs is also clear from the Kelly formula. Let d be decimal odds, b = d − 1, p your win probability, q = 1 − p, f* = (d × p − 1) / (d − 1) = (b × p − q) / b. Then:

  • Probability sensitivity: ∂f*/∂p = d / (d − 1). Example: at d = 2.50, a +1 percentage point change in p shifts f* by +1.667 percentage points of bankroll.
  • Odds sensitivity: ∂f*/∂d = q / (d − 1)^2. Better odds increase f*; the effect is larger when d is close to 1 (shorter prices are less sensitive).

Edge misestimation example (d = 2.50):

  • Estimated p = 0.44 → f* = 6.67%.
  • True p = 0.42 → f* = 3.33% (halved). Using half-Kelly would have limited overbetting.
  • Correlation note: if bets are positively correlated, effective variance is higher than single-bet variance; consider lowering k and tightening caps.
  • Execution: long shots (large b) amplify variance; even with positive EV, a cap (e.g., 2–3%) can materially reduce drawdown risk.

Where can you apply Kelly: sports, poker, forex, crypto?

The Kelly framework applies anywhere you can quantify edge (win probability or expected return) and payout ratio. In sports betting, closing line value (CLV) helps translate price improvement into an estimated edge. In poker, tournament and cash-game returns can be mapped to Kelly using ROI and variance. In forex and crypto, Kelly sizes directional trades from win probability and reward-to-risk, or allocates capital to a volatile asset using expected excess return and variance.

How does Kelly interact with CLV in sports betting?

CLV measures how your entry price compares to the closing price. If the close reflects the true probability, you can convert CLV into an EV estimate and then into a Kelly stake. With decimal odds d_entry and closing decimal odds d_close (on the same selection), the EV per £1 staked is approximately EV ≈ d_entry × (1 / d_close) − 1. The corresponding full‑Kelly fraction is f* = (d_entry × (1 / d_close) − 1) / (d_entry − 1). This assumes the closing price is an unbiased estimator of fair odds and ignores vig unless you explicitly remove it.

  • Compute p_close = 1 / d_close (or remove vig first for multi‑way markets; see notes).
  • Estimate EV: EV = d_entry × p_close − 1.
  • Compute full‑Kelly fraction: f* = (d_entry × p_close − 1) / (d_entry − 1).
  • Apply fractional Kelly (e.g., half‑Kelly) and caps to manage variance.
